D: Original: The Analects of Confucius (James Legge translation)
D: Refashioned: The Analects of Confucius — Refashioned by Zzy

D: Original: Book 1: Hsio R (Learning)

The Master said, 'Is it not pleasant to learn with a constant perseverance and application?
'Is it not delightful to have friends coming from distant quarters?'
'Is he not a man of complete virtue, who feels no discomposure though men may take no note of him?'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "Isn't there deep satisfaction in learning and consistently applying what you learn?
"Isn't it a delight when friends arrive from far away?
"And isn't it the mark of a truly developed person to remain untroubled when others fail to recognize your worth?"
D: Elegant: To learn and practice — joy. To welcome distant friends — delight. To go unrecognized without resentment — virtue.
D: Distilled: The fulfilled life is built on learning, friendship, and equanimity in obscurity.
Notes: Opening passage establishes three pillars: learning (Xue), fellowship, and inner composure. The 'man of complete virtue' is the Junzi — the cultivated person who is the goal of Confucian education. Connects to FUALL's Paideia concept: education as character formation, not credential acquisition. The third line parallels Socratic indifference to reputation.

D: Original:
The philosopher Yu said, 'They are few who, being filial and fraternal, are fond of offending against their superiors. There have been none, who, not liking to offend against their superiors, have been fond of stirring up confusion.
'The superior man bends his attention to what is radical. That being established, all practical courses naturally grow up. Filial piety and fraternal submission!-- are they not the root of all benevolent actions?'
D: Refashioned:
The philosopher You Ruo said, "It is rare for someone who honors their parents and respects their elders to defy those in authority. And no one who respects authority has ever been inclined to sow disorder.
"The cultivated person focuses on fundamentals. Get the root right, and everything else grows from it. Honoring your parents and respecting your elders — isn't that the very root of benevolence?"
D: Elegant: Those who honor their parents rarely defy rightful authority. Those who respect authority never foment chaos. Virtue begins at home — filial devotion is the root from which all goodness grows.
D: Distilled: Respect for family is the foundation of social order and the root of all benevolence.
Notes: You Ruo (Youzi), one of Confucius's senior disciples. Introduces Xiao (filial piety) as the root of Ren (benevolence). The argument: moral order radiates outward from the family unit. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern — when foundational social bonds decay, institutional order follows. The family as the first 'educational institution' in the Confucian system.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Fine words and an insinuating appearance are seldom associated with true virtue.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Smooth talk and a flattering manner are rarely signs of genuine virtue."
D: Elegant: Eloquence and charm are poor proxies for character.
D: Distilled: Polished words and ingratiating behavior usually mask a deficit of real virtue.
Notes: Ren (benevolence/virtue) contrasted with superficial social performance. Recurs at 5.24. Parallels Socrates' suspicion of the Sophists — those who taught persuasion over truth. Connects to FUALL's distinction between substance and presentation in the Ideational domain.

D: Original: The philosopher Tsang said, 'I daily examine myself on three points:-- whether, in transacting business for others, I may have been not faithful;-- whether, in intercourse with friends, I may have been not sincere;-- whether I may have not mastered and practised the instructions of my teacher.'
D: Refashioned: The philosopher Zengzi said, "Each day I examine myself on three points: Have I been faithful in my dealings on behalf of others? Have I been sincere with my friends? Have I practiced what my teacher taught me?"
D: Elegant: Three daily questions: Was I faithful in my duties? Sincere with my friends? Did I practice what I was taught?
D: Distilled: Daily self-examination on faithfulness, sincerity, and applied learning is the discipline of moral growth.
Notes: Zengzi (Tsang Shen, 505-435 BCE) — one of Confucius's most important disciples, traditionally credited with authoring the Great Learning (Da Xue). The three examinations concern Xin (faithfulness), sincerity in friendship, and the practice of received teaching — the transmission chain from teacher to student. Connects to FUALL's Academy model.

D: Original: The Master said, To rule a country of a thousand chariots, there must be reverent attention to business, and sincerity; economy in expenditure, and love for men; and the employment of the people at the proper seasons.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "To govern a state of any real size requires: reverent attention to duty, trustworthiness, fiscal restraint, genuine care for the people, and calling on them to serve only at the right times."
D: Elegant: Govern with seriousness, honesty, economy, compassion, and respect for people's time.
D: Distilled: Good governance requires attentive duty, trustworthiness, fiscal discipline, care for people, and respect for their time.
Notes: Five principles of governance. 'A thousand chariots' = a mid-sized feudal state. Xin (trustworthiness) appears explicitly. 'Employment of the people at the proper seasons' — not conscripting farmers during planting or harvest. Connects to FUALL's power domains: governance as stewardship, not extraction.

D: Original: The Master said, 'A youth, when at home, should be filial, and, abroad, respectful to his elders. He should be earnest and truthful. He should overflow in love to all, and cultivate the friendship of the good. When he has time and opportunity, after the performance of these things, he should employ them in polite studies.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "A young person should be devoted to their parents at home and respectful to elders outside it. They should be earnest and truthful, love widely, and seek the friendship of good people. Only after fulfilling these duties should they turn to study and the arts."
D: Elegant: First, honor your parents. Then, respect your elders. Be earnest, truthful, and generous. Seek good company. Only then — study.
D: Distilled: Character formation precedes intellectual cultivation — virtue first, then learning.
Notes: Xiao (filial piety) and Ti (fraternal respect) as prerequisites for formal education. The sequence matters: moral formation before intellectual training. 'Polite studies' = the six arts (Liu Yi): ritual, music, archery, charioteering, calligraphy, mathematics. Connects to FUALL's Paideia: education as whole-person formation, not skills acquisition.

D: Original: Tsze-hsia said, 'If a man withdraws his mind from the love of beauty, and applies it as sincerely to the love of the virtuous; if, in serving his parents, he can exert his utmost strength; if, in serving his prince, he can devote his life; if, in his intercourse with his friends, his words are sincere:-- although men say that he has not learned, I will certainly say that he has.'
D: Refashioned: Zixia said, "If someone redirects the energy they spend on appearances toward cultivating virtue; if in serving their parents they give everything they have; if in serving their country they would give their life; if in their dealings with friends they keep their word — then even if others say this person has no formal education, I would call them truly learned."
D: Elegant: Trade vanity for virtue, serve your parents with your whole strength, your country with your life, your friends with your word — and you are educated, whatever the world may say.
D: Distilled: True learning is demonstrated through devoted action, not credentials.
Notes: Zixia (Bu Shang) — one of the younger disciples, known for literary scholarship. His argument inverts the common assumption: learning is proven by conduct, not by formal study. This is a radical claim about what 'education' means. Connects to FUALL's critique of credentialism and the distinction between knowledge and wisdom.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'If the scholar be not grave, he will not call forth any veneration, and his learning will not be solid.
'Hold faithfulness and sincerity as first principles.
'Have no friends not equal to yourself.
'When you have faults, do not fear to abandon them.'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "If a scholar lacks gravitas, they will command no respect, and their learning will be shallow.
"Make faithfulness and sincerity your first principles.
"Do not befriend those who pull you downward.
"When you recognize a fault in yourself, do not hesitate to correct it."
D: Elegant: Without seriousness, no respect; without respect, no deep learning. Hold to faithfulness. Keep worthy company. When you find fault in yourself — fix it immediately.
D: Distilled: Serious character earns respect, which deepens learning; faithfulness, good company, and self-correction complete the foundation.
Notes: Xin (faithfulness) as a 'first principle.' The admonition about friends recurs at 9.24 — nearly verbatim. 'Have no friends not equal to yourself' is frequently debated: likely means 'do not keep company that degrades your character,' not an instruction in social snobbery. Connects to FUALL's concept of the Agora — the quality of discourse depends on the quality of participants.

D: Original: The philosopher Tsang said, 'Let there be a careful attention to perform the funeral rites to parents, and let them be followed when long gone with the ceremonies of sacrifice;-- then the virtue of the people will resume its proper excellence.'
D: Refashioned: The philosopher Zengzi said, "When care is taken to honor the dead properly — through funeral rites and ongoing ancestral remembrance — the moral character of the people will naturally deepen."
D: Elegant: Honor the dead with care, remember them with devotion, and the living will grow in virtue.
D: Distilled: A society that reverently honors its dead cultivates moral depth in its living.
Notes: Zengzi on Li (ritual propriety) in its most solemn form: funerary and ancestral rites. The claim is that ritual attention to death shapes moral consciousness. The 'ceremonies of sacrifice' = ancestral worship, central to Chinese social religion. Connects to FUALL's Temple institution — ritual as moral infrastructure.

D: Original:
Tsze-ch'in asked Tsze-kung, saying, 'When our master comes to any country, he does not fail to learn all about its government. Does he ask his information? or is it given to him?'
Tsze-kung said, 'Our master is benign, upright, courteous, temperate, and complaisant, and thus he gets his information. The master's mode of asking information!-- is it not different from that of other men?'
D: Refashioned:
Ziqin asked Zigong, "When our Master arrives in any country, he invariably learns everything about its government. Does he ask for this information, or do people simply offer it to him?"
Zigong said, "Our Master is kind, upright, courteous, temperate, and deferential — and that is how he learns. His way of seeking information is quite unlike that of other men."
D: Elegant: How does the Master learn every state's affairs? Not by interrogation but by being the kind of person people want to confide in — kind, upright, courteous, temperate, deferential.
D: Distilled: The deepest intelligence-gathering is not asking but being the person others trust enough to tell.
Notes: Zigong (Tsze-kung, Duanmu Ci) — Confucius's most commercially successful and diplomatically skilled disciple. Ziqin (Ch'in) — possibly a disciple of Zigong's. The five qualities listed (wen, liang, gong, jian, rang) form a model of Ren in social practice. Connects to FUALL's Ideational domain: influence through character rather than force.

D: Original: The Master said, 'While a man's father is alive, look at the bent of his will; when his father is dead, look at his conduct. If for three years he does not alter from the way of his father, he may be called filial.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "While a man's father is alive, observe his intentions. After his father has died, observe his conduct. If for three years he does not depart from his father's way, he may be called truly filial."
D: Elegant: Before the father dies, watch the son's aspirations. After, watch his actions. Three years of consistency proves devotion.
D: Distilled: Filial piety is proven not by grief but by sustained adherence to what was good in a father's example.
Notes: Xiao (filial piety) tested by time, not sentiment. The 'three-year' period corresponds to the traditional mourning period. This passage recurs nearly verbatim at 4.20. The principle: character is revealed by what you do when no one is compelling you. Connects to FUALL's transmission concept — maintaining the received Dao across generations.

D: Original:
The philosopher Yu said, 'In practising the rules of propriety, a natural ease is to be prized. In the ways prescribed by the ancient kings, this is the excellent quality, and in things small and great we follow them.
'Yet it is not to be observed in all cases. If one, knowing how such ease should be prized, manifests it, without regulating it by the rules of propriety, this likewise is not to be done.'
D: Refashioned:
The philosopher You Ruo said, "In practicing the rules of propriety, what matters most is natural ease. This is what made the ways of the ancient kings excellent — it guided them in matters great and small.
"Yet ease alone is not enough. If someone pursues naturalness without grounding it in propriety, that too goes wrong."
D: Elegant: Ritual should feel natural, not forced. But naturalness without ritual becomes chaos. The balance is the art.
D: Distilled: Propriety without ease becomes rigid; ease without propriety becomes disorder — the goal is harmony between the two.
Notes: Li (ritual propriety) and its relationship to naturalness. You Ruo argues for the Golden Mean — neither stiff formalism nor undisciplined spontaneity. Connects to FUALL's concept of Right: structure that enables rather than constrains. Also connects to Aristotle's doctrine of the mean — virtue as the balance between extremes.

D: Original: The philosopher Yu said, 'When agreements are made according to what is right, what is spoken can be made good. When respect is shown according to what is proper, one keeps far from shame and disgrace. When the parties upon whom a man leans are proper persons to be intimate with, he can make them his guides and masters.'
D: Refashioned: The philosopher You Ruo said, "When agreements are grounded in what is right, promises can be kept. When respect is guided by what is proper, one avoids shame. When the people you rely on are worthy of trust, they can serve as your guides."
D: Elegant: Agreements rooted in justice can be honored. Respect guided by propriety avoids disgrace. Worthy companions become true guides.
D: Distilled: Commitments should be grounded in righteousness, respect in propriety, and trust in the character of those you rely on.
Notes: Yi (righteousness) as the foundation of reliable agreements. Li (propriety) as the guard against disgrace. The third principle: choose your dependencies wisely. Connects to FUALL's framework: Right (Yi) provides the standard by which social conduct (Li) is judged.

D: Original: The Master said, 'He who aims to be a man of complete virtue in his food does not seek to gratify his appetite, nor in his dwelling place does he seek the appliances of ease; he is earnest in what he is doing, and careful in his speech; he frequents the company of men of principle that he may be rectified:-- such a person may be said indeed to love to learn.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "The person who aspires to be a Junzi does not chase fine food or comfortable living. They are diligent in their work and careful with their words. They seek out people of principle to correct and improve themselves. Such a person can truly be said to love learning."
D: Elegant: The cultivated person doesn't chase comfort or luxury. They work hard, speak carefully, and seek correction from those with principle. That is what it means to love learning.
D: Distilled: Loving learning means choosing discipline over comfort, careful speech over careless talk, and principled company over easy company.
Notes: Junzi (the cultivated/superior person) — the aspirational model of Confucian education. The passage defines the Junzi not by achievement but by orientation: toward discipline, precision, and self-correction. Connects to FUALL's Paideia and the Academy institution — learning as a lifelong moral discipline, not a credential to acquire.

D: Original:
Tsze-kung said, 'What do you pronounce concerning the poor man who yet does not flatter, and the rich man who is not proud?' The Master replied, 'They will do; but they are not equal to him, who, though poor, is yet cheerful, and to him, who, though rich, loves the rules of propriety.'
Tsze-kung replied, 'It is said in the Book of Poetry, "As you cut and then file, as you carve and then polish."-- The meaning is the same, I apprehend, as that which you have just expressed.'
The Master said, 'With one like Ts'ze, I can begin to talk about the odes. I told him one point, and he knew its proper sequence.'
D: Refashioned:
Zigong asked, "What do you think of a poor person who does not grovel, or a rich person who is not arrogant?" The Master said, "That is decent. But they are not equal to the poor person who is genuinely cheerful, or the rich person who truly loves propriety."
Zigong said, "The Book of Poetry says, 'As you cut and file, as you carve and polish' — is this what you mean?" The Master said, "Now I can begin discussing the Odes with you, Ci. I tell you one thing, and you see what follows."
D: Elegant: Not groveling in poverty and not preening in wealth — decent enough. But the poor person who finds joy and the rich one who loves propriety have gone further. That's the difference between resisting vice and cultivating virtue.
D: Distilled: Merely avoiding the vices of your station falls short — true virtue means cultivating positive qualities regardless of circumstance.
Notes: Zigong's intellectual agility pleases Confucius — he can extrapolate from a single point. The Book of Poetry (Shi Jing) reference is Ode 55 (Wei Feng), about the refinement of jade. The metaphor: character, like jade, requires active polishing, not merely avoiding damage. Connects to FUALL's distinction between negative virtue (avoiding wrong) and positive virtue (actively cultivating Right).

D: Original: The Master said, 'I will not be afflicted at men's not knowing me; I will be afflicted that I do not know men.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Do not worry that others don't recognize you. Worry that you don't yet recognize others."
D: Elegant: Don't lament being unknown. Lament failing to know others.
D: Distilled: The real failure is not being unrecognized but being unable to see the worth in others.
Notes: Bookend to 1.1's third line — returns to the theme of equanimity about recognition. The inversion: stop worrying about your reputation and start developing the discernment to understand others. Connects to FUALL's Zhi (wisdom) — the capacity to perceive Reality clearly, including the reality of other people.

D: Original: Book 2: Wei Chang (Government)
The Master said, 'He who exercises government by means of his virtue may be compared to the north polar star, which keeps its place and all the stars turn towards it.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "A leader who governs through personal virtue is like the North Star — they hold their position, and everything else naturally orients around them."
D: Elegant: Govern by virtue, and you become the pole star — still, while all else turns toward you.
D: Distilled: Virtuous leadership creates natural authority that draws others into alignment without force.
Notes: Opens Book 2 (Government) with Confucius's central political claim: moral authority is more effective than coercive power. The North Star (Polaris) metaphor: the leader who embodies Dao doesn't need to chase compliance. Connects to FUALL's 4 Domains of Power — the argument that ideational authority (earned through virtue) is superior to physical, resource, or social coercion.

D: Original: The Master said, 'In the Book of Poetry are three hundred pieces, but the design of them all may be embraced in one sentence-- "Having no depraved thoughts."'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "The Book of Poetry contains three hundred pieces, but the essence of them all can be captured in one phrase: 'Think no crooked thoughts.'"
D: Elegant: Three hundred poems, one lesson: think without crookedness.
D: Distilled: All great literature points toward the same thing — sincerity of thought.
Notes: Shi Jing (Book of Poetry/Book of Songs) — one of the Five Classics, compiled c. 600 BCE, containing 305 poems. Confucius reportedly selected and edited the collection. The phrase 'having no depraved thoughts' (si wu xie) comes from Ode 297 (Lu Song). Connects to FUALL's concept of the Academy — canonical texts as vehicles for moral formation.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'If the people be led by laws, and uniformity sought to be given them by punishments, they will try to avoid the punishment, but have no sense of shame.
'If they be led by virtue, and uniformity sought to be given them by the rules of propriety, they will have the sense of shame, and moreover will become good.'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "If you lead people with laws and keep them in line with punishments, they will try to avoid penalties but will have no sense of shame.
"If you lead with virtue and guide them through propriety, they will develop a sense of shame and moreover will genuinely improve."
D: Elegant: Rule by punishment, and people learn only to evade it. Lead by virtue and propriety, and they learn to hold themselves to a standard.
D: Distilled: Laws compel compliance; virtue cultivates conscience — and only conscience produces genuine goodness.
Notes: Central Confucian political philosophy: Li (ritual propriety) and De (virtue) are more effective governance tools than Fa (law/punishment). This directly opposes the Legalist school (Han Fei, Shang Yang) which argued for governance through strict law and punishment. Connects to FUALL's Right pillar — the argument that shared moral norms are more durable than coercive enforcement.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'At fifteen, I had my mind bent on learning.
'At thirty, I stood firm.
'At forty, I had no doubts.
'At fifty, I knew the decrees of Heaven.
'At sixty, my ear was an obedient organ for the reception of truth.
'At seventy, I could follow what my heart desired, without transgressing what was right.'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "At fifteen, I committed myself to learning.
At thirty, I had found my footing.
At forty, I was no longer confused by competing claims.
At fifty, I understood what was beyond my control.
At sixty, I could hear any perspective without being thrown off balance.
At seventy, I could follow my instincts without overstepping what was right."
D: Elegant: Fifteen: devoted to learning. Thirty: steady. Forty: clear. Fifty: reconciled to fate. Sixty: open to all truths. Seventy: free — desire and duty finally aligned.
D: Distilled: Moral maturity is a lifelong arc from committed learning to the point where instinct and virtue become indistinguishable.
Notes: Confucius's spiritual autobiography — one of the most famous passages in Chinese literature. 'The decrees of Heaven' (Tian Ming) = what is given, not chosen — fate, natural endowment, the limits of human agency. The trajectory: discipline → stability → clarity → acceptance → receptivity → freedom. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom framework — the integration of Reality (acceptance of what is), Reason (clarity), and Right (ethical conduct).

D: Original:
Mang I asked what filial piety was. The Master said, 'It is not being disobedient.'
Soon after, as Fan Ch'ih was driving him, the Master told him, saying, 'Mang-sun asked me what filial piety was, and I answered him,-- "not being disobedient."'
Fan Ch'ih said, 'What did you mean?' The Master replied, 'That parents, when alive, be served according to propriety; that, when dead, they should be buried according to propriety; and that they should be sacrificed to according to propriety.'
D: Refashioned:
Meng Yi asked about filial piety. The Master said, "It means not being disobedient."
Later, as Fan Chi was driving the Master's chariot, Confucius told him, "Meng Yi asked me about filial piety, and I said: 'Not being disobedient.'"
Fan Chi asked, "What did you mean?" The Master said, "When your parents are alive, serve them according to propriety. When they die, bury them according to propriety. Honor their memory according to propriety."
D: Elegant: Filial piety is not disobedience — but not mere obedience either. It means serving the living and honoring the dead according to what is proper, not merely what is convenient.
D: Distilled: True filial devotion means honoring your parents through right conduct in life, proper rites in death, and reverent remembrance after.
Notes: Meng Yi (Mang I) — head of the Mengsun clan, one of the Three Families dominating Lu. Confucius gives him a deliberately terse answer, then unpacks it privately. The 'disobedience' is not to parents but to Li (propriety) — the key insight. Xiao (filial piety) is not blind obedience but conduct guided by principle. Connects to FUALL's Temple institution — ritual as the practical mechanism of Right.

D: Original: Mang Wu asked what filial piety was. The Master said, 'Parents are anxious lest their children should be sick.'
D: Refashioned: Meng Wu asked about filial piety. The Master said, "Give your parents no cause for worry beyond concern for your health."
D: Elegant: The only anxiety your parents should have about you is whether you are well.
D: Distilled: True filial piety means living so well that your parents' only worry is your health.
Notes: Meng Wu (Mang Wu) — son of Meng Yi (2.5). The answer is tailored to the questioner, as Confucius typically does. The implication: if you live rightly, your parents have nothing to fear except what they cannot control — illness and mortality. Xiao adapted to circumstance.

D: Original: Tsze-yu asked what filial piety was. The Master said, 'The filial piety of now-a-days means the support of one's parents. But dogs and horses likewise are able to do something in the way of support;-- without reverence, what is there to distinguish the one support given from the other?'
D: Refashioned: Ziyu asked about filial piety. The Master said, "These days people think filial piety just means providing for their parents' material needs. But we provide food and shelter for dogs and horses too. Without genuine reverence, what's the difference?"
D: Elegant: You feed your parents — but you feed your animals, too. Without reverence, what distinguishes the one from the other?
D: Distilled: Material support without genuine reverence is not filial piety — it is maintenance.
Notes: Ziyu (Tsze-yu, Yan Yan) — disciple known for his literary gifts. Confucius's sharpest statement on Xiao: the distinction between care and reverence. Mere provision is animal behavior; human obligation requires emotional and spiritual depth. Connects to FUALL's hierarchy of values — material sufficiency is necessary but not sufficient for Right conduct.

D: Original: Tsze-hsia asked what filial piety was. The Master said, 'The difficulty is with the countenance. If, when their elders have any troublesome affairs, the young take the toil of them, and if, when the young have wine and food, they set them before their elders, is THIS to be considered filial piety?'
D: Refashioned: Zixia asked about filial piety. The Master said, "The hardest part is maintaining the right attitude. If the young do the heavy work and serve food and wine to their elders, is that really filial piety? The demeanor — that is what matters."
D: Elegant: Doing the labor and serving the food is easy. Keeping a genuinely warm countenance while doing it — that is the real test of devotion.
D: Distilled: Filial piety is tested not by what you do for your parents but by the spirit in which you do it.
Notes: Zixia (Bu Shang). Four consecutive passages (2.5-2.8) on Xiao, each tailored to a different questioner. The 'difficulty with the countenance' (se nan) — maintaining genuine warmth rather than dutiful resentment. Connects to FUALL: the difference between performing a virtue and embodying it.

D: Original: The Master said, 'I have talked with Hui for a whole day, and he has not made any objection to anything I said;-- as if he were stupid. He has retired, and I have examined his conduct when away from me, and found him able to illustrate my teachings. Hui!-- He is not stupid.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "I have spent whole days talking with Yan Hui, and he never raised a single objection — as if he were slow-witted. But when I observed his private conduct afterward, I found him fully capable of applying and illustrating my teachings. Hui is not slow at all."
D: Elegant: Hui never objects, never questions — he seems slow. But watch what he does with what he hears. Hui is not slow at all.
D: Distilled: Deep understanding shows itself not in argument but in application.
Notes: Yan Hui (Yan Yuan, 521-481 BCE) — Confucius's favorite disciple, who died young (see 6.2, 11.8-10). His receptive silence is not passivity but depth. Confucius initially misjudges appearance for substance — then corrects himself. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom concept: understanding proven by conduct, not by verbal display.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'See what a man does.
'Mark his motives.
'Examine in what things he rests.
'How can a man conceal his character?
How can a man conceal his character?'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "Observe what a person does.
Examine their motives.
Note what gives them satisfaction.
How then can anyone hide their true character? How can anyone hide their true character?"
D: Elegant: Watch what they do. Ask why. See what satisfies them. Character cannot hide.
D: Distilled: A person's true character is revealed by the intersection of their actions, motives, and sources of contentment.
Notes: A method for reading character — three layers of observation: actions (visible), motives (inferred), and what brings them rest/satisfaction (deepest). The repetition of the final line emphasizes certainty. Connects to FUALL's Reality pillar — seeing things as they actually are, including people. Parallels Socratic elenchus as a method of revealing character through questioning.

D: Original: The Master said, 'If a man keeps cherishing his old knowledge, so as continually to be acquiring new, he may be a teacher of others.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "A person who keeps their existing knowledge alive while continually acquiring new understanding — that person can teach others."
D: Elegant: Renew the old, pursue the new — then you can teach.
D: Distilled: The teacher is someone who keeps old knowledge alive while always acquiring new understanding.
Notes: Wen gu zhi xin — 'reviewing the old to know the new.' One of the most quoted Confucian educational principles. The teacher is not merely a repository but a living synthesizer. Connects to FUALL's Academy institution and the transmission concept — the teacher's role is to make inherited wisdom relevant to present circumstances.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The accomplished scholar is not a utensil.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "The truly educated person is not a mere tool."
D: Elegant: The cultivated person is not a utensil — useful for one thing, blind to all others.
D: Distilled: True education produces versatile judgment, not narrow specialization.
Notes: Junzi bu qi — the Junzi is not a vessel/utensil. A qi (utensil) has a single fixed function. The Junzi should be capable of adaptive judgment across domains. This is the Confucian case against over-specialization. Connects directly to FUALL's Guilds institution and the critique of modern education as vocational training rather than whole-person formation.

D: Original: Tsze-kung asked what constituted the superior man. The Master said, 'He acts before he speaks, and afterwards speaks according to his actions.'
D: Refashioned: Zigong asked what makes a cultivated person. The Master said, "They act first and speak about it afterward."
D: Elegant: The Junzi acts before speaking, then lets the actions speak.
D: Distilled: Let your conduct precede your claims — say what you have done, not what you will do.
Notes: Junzi definition continued. Xian xing qi yan — 'first act, then speak.' The principle inverts the common pattern of promises followed by excuses. Connects to 4.22 and 4.24 — the Confucian emphasis on speech as a moral act that must be backed by conduct.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The superior man is catholic and no partisan. The mean man is partisan and not catholic.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "The cultivated person is inclusive, not factional. The small-minded person is factional, not inclusive."
D: Elegant: The Junzi unites without excluding. The small person excludes without uniting.
D: Distilled: Breadth of sympathy without partisan loyalty defines the cultivated person; the reverse defines the petty one.
Notes: Zhou er bu bi (inclusive, not partisan) vs. bi er bu zhou (partisan, not inclusive). One of the sharpest Junzi/Xiaoren (superior/petty person) contrasts. Connects to FUALL's political spectrum framework — the critique of partisan identity as a substitute for principled judgment.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Learning without thought is labour lost; thought without learning is perilous.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Learning without thinking is wasted effort. Thinking without learning is dangerous."
D: Elegant: Study without thought is blind. Thought without study is reckless.
D: Distilled: Learning and thinking are inseparable — one without the other is either wasted effort or dangerous speculation.
Notes: Xue er bu si ze wang, si er bu xue ze dai — one of the most quoted passages in the Analects. Learning (Xue) without reflection (Si) produces rote knowledge; reflection without learning produces unfounded speculation. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom = Reality + Reason + Right: all three must be integrated. Also connects to the Academy/Lab distinction — pure theory vs. pure empiricism are both insufficient.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The study of strange doctrines is injurious indeed!'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Pursuing strange and unorthodox doctrines does real damage."
D: Elegant: Chasing exotic doctrines does more harm than good.
D: Distilled: Intellectual novelty-seeking that abandons proven foundations is genuinely harmful.
Notes: Yi duan — 'heterodox/strange doctrines.' Confucius is conservative in the specific sense: he values the proven transmission of the Zhou cultural inheritance over untested innovations. This is not anti-intellectual but anti-faddish — prioritize what has been tested by time. Connects to FUALL's transmission theme and the danger of abandoning foundational wisdom for novelty.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Yu, shall I teach you what knowledge is? When you know a thing, to hold that you know it; and when you do not know a thing, to allow that you do not know it;-- this is knowledge.'
D: Refashioned:
Zizhang was studying with an eye toward securing an official position.
The Master said, "Listen widely and set aside what is doubtful; speak cautiously about the rest — then you will give few occasions for blame. Observe widely and set aside what seems risky; act cautiously on the rest — then you will have few occasions for regret. Few occasions for blame in your words, few occasions for regret in your conduct — and the position will take care of itself."
D: Elegant: Hear much, speak cautiously on what you trust. See much, act cautiously on what you understand. Minimize blame and regret, and the career follows.
D: Distilled: The surest path to professional success is disciplined caution: speak only what you can defend and act only on what you understand.
Notes: Zizhang (Zhuan Sun Shi) — ambitious disciple, focused on career. Confucius redirects his ambition: don't pursue the position, pursue the conduct that earns it. The advice is practical epistemology: filter your inputs, reserve judgment on the uncertain, and act only on the reliable. Connects to FUALL's Reason pillar — careful judgment as the basis of right action.

D: Original:
Tsze-chang was learning with a view to official emolument.
The Master said, 'Hear much and put aside the points of which you stand in doubt, while you speak cautiously at the same time of the others:-- then you will afford few occasions for blame. See much and put aside the things which seem perilous, while you are cautious at the same time in carrying the others into practice:-- then you will have few occasions for repentance. When one gives few occasions for blame in his words, and few occasions for repentance in his conduct, he is in the way to get emolument.'
D: Refashioned:
Zizhang was studying with an eye toward securing an official position.
The Master said, "Listen widely and set aside what is doubtful; speak cautiously about the rest — then you will give few occasions for blame. Observe widely and set aside what seems risky; act cautiously on the rest — then you will have few occasions for regret. Few occasions for blame in your words, few occasions for regret in your conduct — and the position will take care of itself."
D: Elegant: Hear much, speak cautiously on what you trust. See much, act cautiously on what you understand. Minimize blame and regret, and the career follows.
D: Distilled: The surest path to professional success is disciplined caution: speak only what you can defend and act only on what you understand.
Notes: Zizhang (Zhuan Sun Shi) — ambitious disciple, focused on career. Confucius redirects his ambition: don't pursue the position, pursue the conduct that earns it. The advice is practical epistemology: filter your inputs, reserve judgment on the uncertain, and act only on the reliable. Connects to FUALL's Reason pillar — careful judgment as the basis of right action.

D: Original: The Duke Ai asked, saying, 'What should be done in order to secure the submission of the people?' Confucius replied, 'Advance the upright and set aside the crooked, then the people will submit. Advance the crooked and set aside the upright, then the people will not submit.'
D: Refashioned: Duke Ai asked, "How do I secure the people's loyalty?" Confucius replied, "Elevate the honest and set aside the corrupt, and the people will follow you. Elevate the corrupt and set aside the honest, and they will not."
D: Elegant: Promote the upright, remove the crooked — and the people follow. Reverse it, and they won't.
D: Distilled: Public loyalty follows from visible justice in who a leader elevates and who they remove.
Notes: Duke Ai of Lu (r. 494-468 BCE) — nominal ruler during Confucius's later years, largely powerless against the Three Families. Confucius gives advice so obvious it functions as rebuke. Connects to FUALL's power domains — the Social domain depends on perceived legitimacy, which is destroyed by corrupt appointments.

D: Original: Chi K'ang asked how to cause the people to reverence their ruler, to be faithful to him, and to go on to nerve themselves to virtue. The Master said, 'Let him preside over them with gravity;-- then they will reverence him. Let him be filial and kind to all;-- then they will be faithful to him. Let him advance the good and teach the incompetent;-- then they will eagerly seek to be virtuous.'
D: Refashioned: Ji Kang asked how to make the people reverent, loyal, and eager to improve. The Master said, "Preside over them with dignity — then they will be reverent. Be devoted and kind to all — then they will be loyal. Promote the competent and train those who are not — then they will strive to improve."
D: Elegant: Show dignity, and they will revere you. Show devotion, and they will follow you. Promote merit and train the rest, and they will strive.
D: Distilled: People mirror their leaders: dignity inspires reverence, kindness inspires loyalty, and investing in growth inspires ambition.
Notes: Ji Kang (Chi K'ang) — head of the Ji family, the most powerful of the Three Families in Lu. Confucius answers by redirecting: you want the people to change? Change yourself first. De (virtue) as governance method. Connects to FUALL's concept of moral authority in the Ideational domain.

D: Original:
Some one addressed Confucius, saying, 'Sir, why are you not engaged in the government?'
The Master said, 'What does the Shu-ching say of filial piety?-- "You are filial, you discharge your brotherly duties. These qualities are displayed in government." This then also constitutes the exercise of government. Why must there be THAT-- making one be in the government?'
D: Refashioned:
Someone asked Confucius, "Why are you not in government?"
The Master said, "The Book of Documents says of filial piety: 'Be filial, discharge your duties to your siblings — these qualities extend into governance.' To practice these virtues is itself to govern. Why must one hold a formal office to be governing?"
D: Elegant: Being filial and brotherly is itself governance. Office is not the only stage on which you can shape the world.
D: Distilled: Governance is not limited to holding office — anyone who models right conduct in their relationships is already governing.
Notes: Shu Jing (Book of Documents) cited — one of the Five Classics. Confucius redefines 'government' beyond office-holding. Moral influence within the family and community is itself a political act. Connects to FUALL's framework: the Ideational domain operates through character and example, not just institutional position.

D: Original: The Master said, 'I do not know how a man without truthfulness is to get on. How can a large carriage be made to go without the cross-bar for yoking the oxen to, or a small carriage without the arrangement for yoking the horses?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "I cannot imagine how someone without trustworthiness gets through life. It would be like a cart without the pin that connects it to the ox, or a carriage without the bar that yokes the horses. How does it move at all?"
D: Elegant: A person without trustworthiness is a cart without a yoke-pin — it looks complete, but it cannot move.
D: Distilled: Trustworthiness is the essential connector that makes all human endeavor functional.
Notes: Xin (trustworthiness/faithfulness) — one of the Five Constant Virtues. The metaphor is mechanical: Xin is the structural element that makes social cooperation possible. Without it, nothing connects. Connects to FUALL's concept of social trust as infrastructure — when Xin erodes, institutions hollow out.

D: Original:
Tsze-chang asked whether the affairs of ten ages after could be known.
Confucius said, 'The Yin dynasty followed the regulations of the Hsia: wherein it took from or added to them may be known. The Chau dynasty has followed the regulations of Yin: wherein it took from or added to them may be known. Some other may follow the Chau, but though it should be at the distance of a hundred ages, its affairs may be known.'
D: Refashioned:
Zizhang asked whether we could know the affairs of future ages.
Confucius said, "The Yin dynasty built on the Xia's system — what they added or removed can be known. The Zhou dynasty built on the Yin's — what they changed can also be known. Whoever follows the Zhou, even a hundred generations hence, their direction can be anticipated."
D: Elegant: Each dynasty inherits, adapts, and passes on. Study the pattern of what was kept and what was changed, and you can see a hundred generations ahead.
D: Distilled: The future is legible through the patterns of what each generation preserves, adapts, and discards from the last.
Notes: The Three Dynasties: Xia (c. 2070-1600 BCE), Shang/Yin (c. 1600-1046 BCE), Zhou (c. 1046-256 BCE). Confucius's method: study institutional transmission across generations to understand historical direction. This is an argument for the study of history as a predictive discipline. Connects directly to FUALL's erosion pattern — civilizations follow legible patterns of inheritance and decay.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'For a man to sacrifice to a spirit which does not belong to him is flattery.
'To see what is right and not to do it is want of courage.'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "To sacrifice to a spirit that is not your own to honor is flattery.
"To see what is right and not to do it is cowardice."
D: Elegant: Worshipping what isn't yours to worship is flattery. Seeing the right thing and not doing it is cowardice.
D: Distilled: Two fundamental failures: false piety toward what you should not worship, and cowardice before what you know is right.
Notes: Two distinct maxims paired. The first concerns Li (propriety in ritual) — sacrificing to spirits outside your ancestral lineage is not devotion but social climbing. The second is a direct statement about Yi (righteousness) and moral courage. Connects to FUALL's Right pillar — knowing the right thing is insufficient without the courage to act on it.

D: Original: Book 3: Pa Yih (Eight Rows of Dancers)
Confucius said of the head of the Chi family, who had eight rows of pantomimes in his area, 'If he can bear to do this, what may he not bear to do?'
D: Refashioned: Confucius said of the head of the Ji family, who used eight rows of dancers in his courtyard — a privilege reserved for the Son of Heaven — "If he is capable of this transgression, what transgression is beyond him?"
D: Elegant: He claims the emperor's ritual for himself. If he can stomach that, there is nothing he won't do.
D: Distilled: When powerful people violate the symbolic boundaries of authority without consequence, no boundary is safe.
Notes: Opens Book 3 (Ritual). The Ba Yi (Eight Rows) — 64 dancers in 8 rows, reserved exclusively for the Zhou king. The Ji family (Chi), as mere ministers of Lu, were entitled to only 4 rows. Confucius is outraged not at a dance performance but at a deliberate usurpation of ritual hierarchy. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: institutional decay begins when symbolic boundaries are violated without consequence.

D: Original: The three families used the YUNG ode, while the vessels were being removed, at the conclusion of the sacrifice. The Master said, '"Assisting are the princes;-- the son of heaven looks profound and grave:"-- what application can these words have in the hall of the three families?'
D: Refashioned: The Three Families used the Yong ode during the conclusion of their sacrifices. The Master said, "The ode says, 'Assisting are the princes; the Son of Heaven looks profound and grave.' What possible application do these words have in the halls of the Three Families?"
D: Elegant: They chant hymns meant for the emperor in their private halls. The words themselves indict them — if only they listened.
D: Distilled: Performing rituals above your station doesn't elevate you — it exposes your pretension.
Notes: The Three Families (Mengsun, Shusun, Jisun) — powerful clans who effectively controlled the state of Lu, usurping ducal prerogatives. The Yong ode (Psalm 282 of the Shi Jing) describes the emperor's sacrifice attended by feudal lords. The families' use of it is doubly absurd — the text describes a hierarchy they are actively violating. Connects to FUALL: ritual (Li) loses meaning when divorced from the reality it represents.

D: Original: The Master said, 'If a man be without the virtues proper to humanity, what has he to do with the rites of propriety? If a man be without the virtues proper to humanity, what has he to do with music?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "If a person lacks basic human compassion, what use are rituals to them? If a person lacks basic human compassion, what use is music to them?"
D: Elegant: Without humanity, ritual is empty performance. Without humanity, music is just noise.
D: Distilled: Ritual and music are only meaningful when grounded in genuine human compassion.
Notes: Ren (benevolence/humaneness) as the prerequisite for Li (ritual) and Yue (music). Without the inner virtue, the outer forms are hollow. This is Confucius's guard against formalism — ritual is not the point; the virtue it expresses is the point. Connects to FUALL's Temple institution: the danger of ritual becoming mechanical rather than transformative.

D: Original:
Lin Fang asked what was the first thing to be attended to in ceremonies.
The Master said, 'A great question indeed!
'In festive ceremonies, it is better to be sparing than extravagant. In the ceremonies of mourning, it is better that there be deep sorrow than a minute attention to observances.'
D: Refashioned:
Lin Fang asked what is most essential in ritual.
The Master said, "A great question indeed!
In celebrations, it is better to be restrained than extravagant. In mourning, it is better to feel genuine grief than to observe every detail of protocol perfectly."
D: Elegant: Restraint over extravagance in celebration. Grief over precision in mourning. Ritual's essence is sincerity, not spectacle.
D: Distilled: The heart of ritual is sincerity — genuine feeling matters more than elaborate performance.
Notes: Lin Fang — a man of Lu, not a regular disciple. Confucius's enthusiasm ('A great question!') signals this touches something fundamental. Li (ritual) stripped to its core: internal sincerity over external display. The principle applies broadly: substance over form in all institutional life. Connects to FUALL's Temple institution — ritual must remain connected to genuine meaning.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The rude tribes of the east and north have their princes, and are not like the States of our great land which are without them.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Even the barbarian tribes of the north and east have their rulers and maintain order. They are not like the states of our great land, which have lost theirs."
D: Elegant: The 'barbarians' at least have functioning leadership. Can we say the same of ourselves?
D: Distilled: A civilization that has abandoned its own governing principles has fallen below the standard of those it considers uncivilized.
Notes: A biting comparison: the Yi and Di peoples (northern and eastern 'barbarians') maintain social order, while the Chinese states — theoretically the pinnacle of civilization — have descended into factional chaos. Confucius uses the 'uncivilized' as a mirror. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: civilizations that abandon their own foundations can be surpassed by those they once looked down upon.

D: Original: The chief of the Chi family was about to sacrifice to the T'ai mountain. The Master said to Zan Yu, 'Can you not save him from this?' He answered, 'I cannot.' Confucius said, 'Alas! will you say that the T'ai mountain is not so discerning as Lin Fang?'
D: Refashioned: The head of the Ji family was about to perform a sacrifice at Mount Tai — a privilege reserved for the ruler of Lu. The Master said to Ran Qiu, "Can you not stop him?" Ran Qiu replied, "I cannot." Confucius said, "Do you really think Mount Tai has less discernment than Lin Fang?"
D: Elegant: The Ji chief usurps a royal sacrifice. If even a commoner like Lin Fang can see the error, do you think Heaven is blind?
D: Distilled: Heaven recognizes illegitimate ritual even when no one else will call it out.
Notes: Mount Tai — the most sacred mountain in China, site of the Feng and Shan sacrifices that only the Son of Heaven could perform. The Ji family's sacrifice is another ritual usurpation (continuing from 3.1-2). Ran Qiu (Zan Yu) — Confucius's disciple who served the Ji family. Lin Fang asked about ritual's essence in 3.4. The rebuke: if a simple questioner understands ritual better than you do, your transgression is inexcusable.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The student of virtue has no contentions. If it be said he cannot avoid them, shall this be in archery? But he bows complaisantly to his competitors; thus he ascends the hall, descends, and exacts the forfeit of drinking. In his contention, he is still the Chun-tsze.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "The cultivated person has no need for contention. If pressed, the closest example would be archery — but even there, competitors bow respectfully to each other before ascending the platform and drink together afterward. Even in competition, the Junzi remains the Junzi."
D: Elegant: The Junzi does not compete. If forced to, even in competition he bows before, drinks after, and never loses himself.
D: Distilled: True character is not suspended during competition — the cultivated person maintains their standards especially when contending.
Notes: Archery (She) — one of the Six Arts and a ritual activity with strict ceremonial protocols. The Junzi competes without aggression; the contest is with oneself, not against the other. Connects to FUALL: competition within the Ideational domain should elevate all parties, not degrade them.

D: Original:
Tsze-hsia asked, saying, 'What is the meaning of the passage-- "The pretty dimples of her artful smile! The well- defined black and white of her eye! The plain ground for the colours?"'
The Master said, 'The business of laying on the colours follows (the preparation of) the plain ground.'
'Ceremonies then are a subsequent thing?' The Master said, 'It is Shang who can bring out my meaning. Now I can begin to talk about the odes with him.'
D: Refashioned:
Zixia asked, "What do these lines from the Odes mean: 'The dimples of her smile, the black and white of her eyes, the plain ground for the colors'?"
The Master said, "The painting comes after the plain ground is prepared."
"So ceremonies are built on a foundation?" The Master said, "Shang, you are the one who opens my meaning. Now I can discuss the Odes with you."
D: Elegant: Colors go on after the ground is prepared. Ceremony is built on something deeper. Only the student who sees this is ready for the Odes.
D: Distilled: Ritual and beauty are surfaces — they require a foundation of substance beneath them to have meaning.
Notes: Zixia (Bu Shang, style name Shang). The Odes reference is to a beauty whose cosmetics enhance a natural foundation. Confucius draws the analogy: Li (ritual) is the 'coloring' applied to a foundation of Ren (benevolence). Without the foundation, the decoration is meaningless. Zixia earns the same compliment Zigong received in 1.15. Connects to FUALL: form (Li) depends on substance (Ren) — institutions without underlying values are empty.

D: Original: The Master said, 'I could describe the ceremonies of the Hsia dynasty, but Chi cannot sufficiently attest my words. I could describe the ceremonies of the Yin dynasty, but Sung cannot sufficiently attest my words. (They cannot do so) because of the insufficiency of their records and wise men. If those were sufficient, I could adduce them in support of my words.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "I could describe the ceremonies of the Xia dynasty, but the state of Qi cannot verify my account. I could describe the ceremonies of the Yin dynasty, but the state of Song cannot verify my account. In both cases, the records and wise men are insufficient. If they were sufficient, I could prove my claims."
D: Elegant: I know the ancient rites, but the evidence to prove it has been lost. Without records and living memory, even truth cannot be verified.
D: Distilled: Knowledge without evidence cannot be transmitted — when records and knowledgeable people vanish, even truth becomes unverifiable.
Notes: Qi and Song — states descended from the Xia and Shang/Yin dynasties respectively, expected to preserve ancestral traditions. Confucius laments the loss of institutional memory. Connects directly to FUALL's transmission theme: wisdom requires both records (texts) and living carriers (teachers). When either is lost, the chain breaks. Also connects to the Academy and News institutions — preservation of knowledge across time.

D: Original: The Master said, 'At the great sacrifice, after the pouring out of the libation, I have no wish to look on.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "At the great Di sacrifice, after the initial libation is poured, I have no wish to watch what follows."
D: Elegant: After the opening libation, the ceremony degenerates. I cannot bear to watch.
D: Distilled: When sacred rituals are performed without genuine understanding, they become painful to witness.
Notes: The Di (great sacrifice) — the most important state ceremony in Lu, theoretically led by the Duke but by this period largely controlled by the Three Families. Confucius implies the ceremony is so degraded after the opening that it is no longer worth observing. Connects to FUALL: institutional forms persist long after the substance has drained out of them.

D: Original: Some one asked the meaning of the great sacrifice. The Master said, 'I do not know. He who knew its meaning would find it as easy to govern the kingdom as to look on this;-- pointing to his palm.
D: Refashioned: Someone asked the meaning of the great Di sacrifice. The Master said, "I do not know. Someone who truly understood its meaning would find governing the world as easy as looking at this" — and he pointed to his palm.
D: Elegant: Understand the great sacrifice — truly understand it — and the world becomes as legible as the lines on your palm.
D: Distilled: Deep understanding of why we ritualize our highest values would make the art of governance transparent.
Notes: Confucius claims ignorance — likely diplomatic, since criticizing the Di sacrifice directly would be politically dangerous. But the implication is profound: whoever truly grasps the meaning of sacred ritual has grasped the principle of governance itself. Li (ritual) and Zheng (governance) share a common root. Connects to FUALL's Temple institution — ritual as the foundational technology of social order.

D: Original:
He sacrificed to the dead, as if they were present. He sacrificed to the spirits, as if the spirits were present.
The Master said, 'I consider my not being present at the sacrifice, as if I did not sacrifice.'
D: Refashioned:
He performed sacrifices to the dead as if they were present. He sacrificed to the spirits as if the spirits were there before him.
The Master said, "If I am not fully present at the sacrifice, it is as though I did not sacrifice at all."
D: Elegant: Sacrifice without presence is no sacrifice. Be there — fully — or do not go.
D: Distilled: Ritual performed without full presence and sincerity is not ritual at all.
Notes: The key phrase is 'as if' (ru) — Confucius does not make claims about whether spirits literally exist. What matters is the quality of presence and attention. Ji ru zai — 'sacrifice as if present.' This is Confucian agnosticism about metaphysics combined with absolute commitment to the moral function of ritual. Connects to FUALL's Temple: ritual works regardless of theological claims because its power is in the human disposition it cultivates.

D: Original:
Wang-sun Chia asked, saying, 'What is the meaning of the saying, "It is better to pay court to the furnace than to the south-west corner?"'
The Master said, 'Not so. He who offends against Heaven has none to whom he can pray.'
D: Refashioned:
Wangsun Jia asked, "What does the saying mean, 'It is better to court the kitchen god than the god of the southwest corner'?"
The Master said, "That is wrong. If you offend against Heaven itself, there is no one to whom you can pray."
D: Elegant: They say: flatter the useful god, not the honored one. But if you offend Heaven, no amount of flattery will save you.
D: Distilled: No amount of strategic piety can compensate for a genuine offense against what is right.
Notes: Wangsun Jia — a powerful minister of Wei, testing Confucius with a political allegory. The 'kitchen god' (Zao) = the minister with real power; the 'southwest corner god' (Ao) = the nominal ruler. Wangsun is suggesting Confucius should court him rather than the Duke of Wei. Confucius refuses the frame entirely: there is a higher authority than political convenience. Connects to FUALL: principled action cannot be replaced by strategic networking.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Chau had the advantage of viewing the two past dynasties. How complete and elegant are its regulations! I follow Chau.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "The Zhou dynasty had the advantage of studying two previous dynasties. How rich and elegant is its culture! I follow Zhou."
D: Elegant: Zhou inherited from two dynasties and built something magnificent. I follow Zhou.
D: Distilled: The Zhou achieved cultural greatness by building on the accumulated wisdom of its predecessors.
Notes: Zhou dynasty (c. 1046-256 BCE) — the dynasty Confucius considered the peak of Chinese civilization, particularly under King Wen, King Wu, and the Duke of Zhou. The key claim: cultural achievement is cumulative. Zhou succeeded because it inherited and refined the legacy of Xia and Shang. Connects directly to FUALL's transmission concept — civilization advances through inheritance and refinement, not through starting from scratch.

D: Original: The Master, when he entered the grand temple, asked about everything. Some one said, 'Who will say that the son of the man of Tsau knows the rules of propriety! He has entered the grand temple and asks about everything.' The Master heard the remark, and said, 'This is a rule of propriety.'
D: Refashioned:
When the Master entered the grand temple, he asked about everything. Someone said, "Who claims that the son of the man from Zou knows the rules of propriety? He enters the grand temple and asks about everything!"
The Master heard this and said, "Asking is itself a rule of propriety."
D: Elegant: They mocked him for asking about everything in the temple. He replied: asking is the ritual.
D: Distilled: Genuine inquiry is not a sign of ignorance but an act of reverence — asking is itself a form of propriety.
Notes: The 'man of Zou' — Confucius's father, Shu Lianghe, was from the town of Zou in Lu. The criticism implies Confucius's humble origins disqualify him from ritual knowledge. His response redefines Li: asking questions is not a departure from propriety but an expression of it. Connects to FUALL's Academy: the posture of inquiry is more valuable than the pretense of already knowing.

D: Original: The Master said, 'In archery it is not going through the leather which is the principal thing;-- because people's strength is not equal. This was the old way.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "In archery, the important thing is not piercing the target — because people's strength differs. This was the ancient understanding."
D: Elegant: Archery is not about raw power — it never was. What matters is form, intention, discipline.
D: Distilled: Excellence is measured by mastery and intention, not brute force.
Notes: Archery (She) as moral analogy — one of the Six Arts. The ancient rule: archery tests skill, focus, and character, not physical strength. Connects to FUALL: merit should be judged by the quality of one's effort and judgment, not by raw capacity or output.

D: Original:
Tsze-kung wished to do away with the offering of a sheep connected with the inauguration of the first day of each month.
The Master said, 'Ts'ze, you love the sheep; I love the ceremony.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "When someone serves their ruler with full observance of propriety, people call it flattery."
D: Elegant: Observe every rule of propriety in serving your ruler, and people will call you a flatterer. That says more about them than about you.
D: Distilled: In a degraded culture, genuine propriety is mistaken for sycophancy.
Notes: A lament about cultural decline: when most people have abandoned proper conduct, those who maintain it are viewed with suspicion. The diagnosis: the baseline has shifted so far that sincere Li (propriety) looks like excess. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern — when standards decay, anyone who maintains them appears abnormal.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The full observance of the rules of propriety in serving one's prince is accounted by people to be flattery.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "When someone serves their ruler with full observance of propriety, people call it flattery."
D: Elegant: Observe every rule of propriety in serving your ruler, and people will call you a flatterer. That says more about them than about you.
D: Distilled: In a degraded culture, genuine propriety is mistaken for sycophancy.
Notes: A lament about cultural decline: when most people have abandoned proper conduct, those who maintain it are viewed with suspicion. The diagnosis: the baseline has shifted so far that sincere Li (propriety) looks like excess. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern — when standards decay, anyone who maintains them appears abnormal.

D: Original: The Duke Ting asked how a prince should employ his ministers, and how ministers should serve their prince. Confucius replied, 'A prince should employ his minister according to according to the rules of propriety; ministers should serve their prince with faithfulness.'
D: Refashioned: Duke Ding asked how a ruler should employ his ministers, and how ministers should serve their ruler. Confucius replied, "A ruler should employ his ministers according to propriety. Ministers should serve their ruler with faithfulness."
D: Elegant: The ruler owes propriety to his ministers. The ministers owe faithfulness to their ruler. The contract is mutual.
D: Distilled: Governance is a reciprocal obligation: rulers owe propriety to those who serve, and servants owe faithfulness to those who lead.
Notes: Duke Ding of Lu (r. 509-495 BCE). Confucius served under him as Minister of Crime. The reciprocity principle: authority and obedience are not one-directional. Li (propriety) flowing downward, Xin (faithfulness) flowing upward. Connects to FUALL's power domains — legitimate authority requires mutual obligation, not just top-down command.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The Kwan Tsu is expressive of enjoyment without being licentious, and of grief without being hurtfully excessive.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "The Guan Ju ode expresses joy without becoming licentious, and sorrow without becoming excessive."
D: Elegant: The Guan Ju achieves joy without excess and grief without collapse — the emotional golden mean.
D: Distilled: The highest art expresses feeling fully while maintaining composure — neither repressed nor unrestrained.
Notes: Guan Ju (Guan Sui) — the first ode in the Book of Poetry, a love poem about a nobleman seeking a virtuous wife. Confucius praises its emotional balance. The aesthetic principle mirrors the ethical one: the Doctrine of the Mean (Zhong Yong). Connects to FUALL: Aristotle's Golden Mean and Confucius's Zhong Yong — parallel discoveries in Greek and Chinese thought.

D: Original:
The Duke Ai asked Tsai Wo about the altars of the spirits of the land. Tsai Wo replied, 'The Hsia sovereign planted the pine tree about them; the men of the Yin planted the cypress; and the men of the Chau planted the chestnut tree, meaning thereby to cause the people to be in awe.'
When the Master heard it, he said, 'Things that are done, it is needless to speak about; things that have had their course, it is needless to remonstrate about; things that are past, it is needless to blame.'
D: Refashioned:
Duke Ai asked Zai Wo about the altars of the spirits of the land. Zai Wo said, "The Xia planted pines around them, the Yin planted cypresses, and the Zhou planted chestnut trees — meaning to make the people tremble with awe."
When the Master heard this, he said, "What is done is done — there is no point discussing it. What has run its course need not be protested. What is past should not be blamed."
D: Elegant: The past is past. What is done cannot be undone, what has run its course need not be protested, and old wrongs need not be relitigated.
D: Distilled: Wisdom includes knowing when to stop relitigating the past and focus on what can still be shaped.
Notes: Zai Wo (Tsai Yu) — a disciple who frequently drew Confucius's criticism (see 5.9). His explanation of the chestnut tree is a veiled threat: the character for 'chestnut' (li) sounds like 'tremble' (li). Confucius shuts down this line of reasoning — using ritual symbolism to justify intimidation perverts Li. Connects to FUALL: the danger of weaponizing tradition for political purposes.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'Small indeed was the capacity of Kwan Chung!'
Some one said, 'Was Kwan Chung parsimonious?' 'Kwan,' was the reply, 'had the San Kwei, and his officers performed no double duties; how can he be considered parsimonious?'
'Then, did Kwan Chung know the rules of propriety?' The Master said, 'The princes of States have a screen intercepting the view at their gates. Kwan had likewise a screen at his gate. The princes of States on any friendly meeting between two of them, had a stand on which to place their inverted cups. Kwan had also such a stand. If Kwan knew the rules of propriety, who does not know them?'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "How limited was Guan Zhong's capacity!"
Someone said, "Was Guan Zhong frugal?" The Master replied, "Guan Zhong maintained three separate residences and never had his staff multitask — how is that frugal?"
"Then did Guan Zhong understand propriety?" The Master said, "Rulers have a screen at their gates — Guan Zhong had one too. Rulers have a stand for their cups at state banquets — Guan Zhong had one of those as well. If Guan Zhong understood propriety, then who doesn't?"
D: Elegant: They call Guan Zhong great. But he lived like a king, spent like a king, and decorated like a king — while serving as a minister. If that's propriety, the word means nothing.
D: Distilled: Ability without propriety is not greatness — adopting the symbols of authority you have not earned reveals a failure of character.
Notes: Guan Zhong (Guan Yiwu, d. 645 BCE) — legendary minister of Duke Huan of Qi, credited with making Qi the first hegemon. Confucius acknowledges his political achievements elsewhere (14.17) but here criticizes his personal conduct. The San Gui (three residences) and ritual usurpations parallel the Ji family's violations in 3.1-2. Connects to FUALL: competence without ethical constraint is dangerous. Power must be governed by Li.

D: Original: The Master instructing the grand music-master of Lu said, 'How to play music may be known. At the commencement of the piece, all the parts should sound together. As it proceeds, they should be in harmony while severally distinct and flowing without break, and thus on to the conclusion.'
D: Refashioned: The Master instructed the grand music master of Lu: "Music works like this: all parts begin together. As the piece proceeds, the parts harmonize while remaining distinct, flowing continuously without interruption to the conclusion."
D: Elegant: Begin in unison. Proceed in harmony — each voice distinct, yet none breaking from the whole. That is music.
D: Distilled: Music, like any good order, requires unity at the start, harmonious distinction in the middle, and continuity to the end.
Notes: Confucius on Yue (music) — one of the Six Arts and, alongside Li (ritual), one of the two pillars of Zhou civilization. The description is both technical and metaphorical: the ideal society, like ideal music, achieves harmony through distinct but coordinated parts. Connects to FUALL's concept of ordered liberty — pluralism within a shared framework.

D: Original: The border warden at Yi requested to be introduced to the Master, saying, 'When men of superior virtue have come to this, I have never been denied the privilege of seeing them.' The followers of the sage introduced him, and when he came out from the interview, he said, 'My friends, why are you distressed by your master's loss of office? The kingdom has long been without the principles of truth and right; Heaven is going to use your master as a bell with its wooden tongue.'
D: Refashioned: The border warden at Yi requested an audience with the Master, saying, "Whenever men of great virtue have passed through here, I have never been denied the privilege of meeting them." The disciples introduced him. When he came out, he said to them, "Friends, do not be distressed by your Master's loss of office. The world has long been without the Way. Heaven is going to use your Master as a bell — to wake people up."
D: Elegant: The world has lost the Way. Heaven will use your Master as a bell with a wooden tongue — to sound the alarm.
D: Distilled: When civilization loses its moral direction, the philosopher's role is not to govern but to sound the alarm.
Notes: The border warden of Yi — an anonymous official with deep insight. 'A bell with its wooden tongue' (mu duo) — a wooden-tongued bell used for public proclamations. The warden sees Confucius's political failure as Heaven's intention: he was meant to teach, not to rule. Dao (the Way) being absent from the world is a recurring Confucian theme. Connects to FUALL's framework: when institutions fail, the transmission of wisdom through individuals becomes the critical function — the role of the Academy and Temple.

D: Original: The Master said of the Shao that it was perfectly beautiful and also perfectly good. He said of the Wu that it was perfectly beautiful but not perfectly good.
D: Refashioned: The Master said of the Shao music that it was perfectly beautiful and perfectly good. He said of the Wu music that it was perfectly beautiful but not perfectly good.
D: Elegant: The Shao: beauty and goodness united. The Wu: beauty without complete goodness.
D: Distilled: True greatness unites beauty with moral goodness — beauty alone is incomplete.
Notes: Shao — the music of the legendary Emperor Shun, who received the throne through virtue (abdication/merit). Wu — the music of King Wu of Zhou, who took the throne by military conquest. Both achieved great things, but Shun's succession was peaceful while Wu's was violent. Confucius judges art by its moral content, not just its aesthetic quality. Connects to FUALL: the distinction between power achieved through the Ideational domain (virtue) vs. the Physical domain (force).

D: Original: The Master said, 'High station filled without indulgent generosity; ceremonies performed without reverence; mourning conducted without sorrow;-- wherewith should I contemplate such ways?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Authority exercised without generosity; rituals performed without reverence; mourning conducted without sorrow — how can I bear to look at such things?"
D: Elegant: Power without compassion, ritual without reverence, mourning without grief — I cannot bear to witness it.
D: Distilled: Forms without feeling are intolerable — authority, ritual, and mourning all require the inner states they are supposed to express.
Notes: Closing passage of Book 3. Confucius summarizes the book's theme: the crisis of form without substance. Li (ritual) has become disconnected from Ren (benevolence). The institutions persist but the animating virtue has drained out. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: hollowed-out institutions that maintain their external forms while losing their internal meaning.

D: Original: Book 4: Le Jin (Virtue)
The Master said, 'It is virtuous manners which constitute the excellence of a neighborhood. If a man in selecting a residence, do not fix on one where such prevail, how can he be wise?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "It is the presence of virtuous people that makes a community excellent. If someone chooses to live where such people are not found, how can they be called wise?"
D: Elegant: A neighborhood's excellence is its people. Choose to live among virtue — or forfeit the claim to wisdom.
D: Distilled: Wisdom includes choosing to live where virtue is practiced, because environment shapes character.
Notes: Opens Book 4 (Virtue/Ren). Li ren wei mei — 'virtuous community is beautiful.' The claim: environment is not ethically neutral. Where you choose to place yourself reflects and shapes your character. Connects to FUALL's Agora concept — the quality of public discourse depends on the quality of the community.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Those who are without virtue cannot abide long either in a condition of poverty and hardship, or in a condition of enjoyment. The virtuous rest in virtue; the wise desire virtue.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Those without virtue cannot endure either poverty or prosperity for long. The virtuous find peace in virtue itself. The wise pursue virtue because they understand its value."
D: Elegant: Without virtue, neither hardship nor comfort can be sustained. The virtuous rest in virtue. The wise desire it.
D: Distilled: Virtue provides the stability that neither poverty nor prosperity can offer on their own.
Notes: Two orientations toward Ren: the virtuous person rests in it naturally (an ren); the wise person desires it for its benefits (li ren). Both are valid, but the first is higher. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom framework — understanding that virtue is not instrumental but constitutive of the good life.

D: Original: The Master said, 'It is only the (truly) virtuous man, who can love, or who can hate, others.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Only the truly virtuous person can love rightly and hate rightly."
D: Elegant: Only virtue qualifies you to love or hate justly.
D: Distilled: Right judgment about what to love and what to hate requires genuine virtue as its foundation.
Notes: Ren (benevolence) as the prerequisite for sound moral judgment. Without inner virtue, both love and hatred are distorted by self-interest. Connects to FUALL's Right pillar — ethical judgment requires a calibrated moral sense, not just strong feeling.

D: Original: The Master said, 'If the will be set on virtue, there will be no practice of wickedness.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "If your will is genuinely set on virtue, you will do no evil."
D: Elegant: Set your will on virtue, and wrongdoing becomes impossible.
D: Distilled: A person genuinely committed to virtue will not commit evil — intention shapes action.
Notes: Zhi yu ren — 'will set on Ren.' The claim is about orientation, not perfection: a person sincerely directed toward virtue will not willfully do wrong. Connects to FUALL: the fundamental question is not what rules to follow but what to orient yourself toward.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'Riches and honours are what men desire. If it cannot be obtained in the proper way, they should not be held. Poverty and meanness are what men dislike. If it cannot be avoided in the proper way, they should not be avoided.
'If a superior man abandon virtue, how can he fulfil the requirements of that name?
'The superior man does not, even for the space of a single meal, act contrary to virtue. In moments of haste, he cleaves to it. In seasons of danger, he cleaves to it.'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "Everyone desires wealth and status. But if they cannot be obtained through right conduct, they should be refused. Everyone dislikes poverty and obscurity. But if they cannot be avoided through right conduct, they should be accepted.
