D: Original: The Master having visited Nan-tsze, Tsze-lu was displeased, on which the Master swore, saying, 'Wherein I have done improperly, may Heaven reject me, may Heaven reject me!'
D: Refashioned: After the Master visited Nanzi, Zilu was displeased. The Master swore, saying, "If I have done anything improper, may Heaven strike me down! May Heaven strike me down!"
D: Elegant: Zilu disapproved of the visit. The Master swore: 'If I acted wrongly, let Heaven destroy me.'
D: Distilled: When your integrity is questioned, the strongest defense is not argument but a willingness to submit to the highest judgment.
Notes: Nanzi — the notorious wife of Duke Ling of Wei, reputed for her beauty and scandalous behavior. Zilu suspects impropriety. Confucius's oath — tian yan zhi ('Heaven reject me') — is an extraordinary appeal to cosmic judgment. The incident reveals the tension between maintaining propriety and engaging with the politically necessary. Connects to FUALL: moral leaders must sometimes navigate situations that appear compromising.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Perfect is the virtue which is according to the Constant Mean! Rare for a long time has been its practise among the people.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Perfect is the virtue of the Constant Mean! It has been rare among the people for a long time now."
D: Elegant: The Constant Mean — the highest virtue — has been all but lost among the people.
D: Distilled: The balanced middle path is the highest virtue, and its rarity is the clearest sign of moral decline.
Notes: Zhong yong zhi wei de ye, qi zhi yi hu, min xian neng jiu yi — the Doctrine of the Mean (Zhong Yong), one of the Four Books of Confucianism. Zhong = center/balance. Yong = constant/ordinary. The ideal is not dramatic virtue but steady, balanced, unremarkable goodness maintained over time. Connects to FUALL: both Aristotle's Golden Mean and the Confucian Zhong Yong identify the balanced center as the hardest and highest achievement.

D: Original:
Tsze-kung said, 'Suppose the case of a man extensively conferring benefits on the people, and able to assist all, what would you say of him? Might he be called perfectly virtuous?' The Master said, 'Why speak only of virtue in connexion with him? Must he not have the qualities of a sage? Even Yao and Shun were still solicitous about this.
'Now the man of perfect virtue, wishing to be established himself, seeks also to establish others; wishing to be enlarged himself, he seeks also to enlarge others.
'To be able to judge of others by what is nigh in ourselves;-- this may be called the art of virtue.'
D: Refashioned:
Zigong said, "Suppose someone could broadly benefit the people and assist everyone in need — what would you say of them? Could they be called perfectly virtuous?" The Master said, "Why stop at virtue? That would be a sage! Even Yao and Shun found that difficult.
"The person of perfect virtue, wishing to stand firm themselves, helps others stand firm. Wishing to advance themselves, they help others advance.
"To judge others by what is close at hand in yourself — that is the method of Ren."
D: Elegant: The sage benefits everyone — even Yao and Shun found that hard. But Ren starts smaller: want to stand firm? Help others stand. Want to advance? Help others advance. Start from yourself, extend to others. That is the art.
D: Distilled: Perfect virtue begins not with grand ambition but with the practice of extending to others what you wish for yourself.
Notes: Neng jin qu pi — 'to take what is near as the analogy.' The method of Ren: start from your own experience and extend it outward. This is Shu (reciprocity) as practical method. Yao and Shun — legendary sage-kings representing the pinnacle of virtuous rule. Even they found universal benevolence difficult. Connects to FUALL's Right pillar: morality is built from the inside out, starting with self-knowledge and extending through empathy.

D: Original: Book 7: Shu R (Transmission)
The Master said, 'A transmitter and not a maker, believing in and loving the ancients, I venture to compare myself with our old P'ang.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "I transmit but do not create. I trust in and love the ancients. In this, I dare to compare myself to our old Peng."
D: Elegant: I transmit — I do not invent. I trust the ancients and love what they built. That is my role.
D: Distilled: The highest intellectual calling is not invention but faithful transmission of inherited wisdom.
Notes: Shu er bu zuo — 'transmit but not create.' The opening of Book 7 (Transmission) states Confucius's self-understanding: he is not a prophet or creator but a transmitter. Old Peng — possibly a legendary historian of the Shang dynasty. This is the most direct statement of the FUALL transmission concept: the primary task is preserving, clarifying, and passing on what has been tested by time, not generating novelty.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The silent treasuring up of knowledge; learning without satiety; and instructing others without being wearied:-- which one of these things belongs to me?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Silently storing up knowledge, learning without tiring, teaching others without growing weary — which of these do I actually achieve?"
D: Elegant: To store knowledge silently, to learn without satiety, to teach without weariness — do I manage even one?
D: Distilled: The ideal teacher silently accumulates wisdom, never tires of learning, and never wearies of teaching — and still considers themselves inadequate.
Notes: Mo er zhi zhi, xue er bu yan, hui ren bu juan — three practices of the ideal teacher/scholar. Confucius's claim of inadequacy is characteristic humility, but also genuine self-assessment: he holds himself to an impossible standard. Connects to FUALL's Academy concept: the teacher is defined by inexhaustible commitment to learning and teaching.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The leaving virtue without proper cultivation; the not thoroughly discussing what is learned; not being able to move towards righteousness of which a knowledge is gained; and not being able to change what is not good:-- these are the things which occasion me solicitude.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "My worries are these: that I leave virtue uncultivated, that I fail to discuss what I have learned thoroughly enough, that knowing what is right I fail to move toward it, and that recognizing what is wrong in myself I fail to change it."
D: Elegant: Four anxieties: uncultivated virtue, undiscussed learning, unacted-upon knowledge of what is right, and unchanged faults.
D: Distilled: The serious person worries not about external failure but about internal neglect — uncultivated virtue, unexamined learning, unapplied knowledge, and uncorrected faults.
Notes: Four sources of Confucius's anxiety — all internal, all about the gap between knowing and doing. De zhi bu xiu (virtue uncultivated), xue zhi bu jiang (learning not discussed), wen yi bu neng xi (unable to move toward righteousness), bu shan bu neng gai (unable to correct faults). Connects to FUALL: the real failures are never external but always in the gap between understanding and practice.

D: Original: When the Master was unoccupied with business, his manner was easy, and he looked pleased.
D: Refashioned: When the Master was at leisure, his manner was relaxed and his expression cheerful.
D: Elegant: At rest, the Master was easy and pleased — discipline did not make him rigid.
D: Distilled: True cultivation produces not rigidity but the capacity for genuine ease and contentment.
Notes: Yan ju shen shen, yao yao ru ye — 'at rest, composed and at ease.' A brief observational note showing that Confucius was not perpetually stern. His discipline coexisted with natural warmth and relaxation. Connects to FUALL: the Junzi ideal includes joy and ease, not just moral seriousness.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Extreme is my decay. For a long time, I have not dreamed, as I was wont to do, that I saw the duke of Chau.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "How far I have declined. For a long time now, I have not dreamed of the Duke of Zhou."
D: Elegant: I have not dreamed of the Duke of Zhou in so long. How far I have fallen.
D: Distilled: When you stop dreaming of your highest ideals, it is a sign that your connection to them is fading.
Notes: Duke of Zhou (Zhou Gong Dan, 11th c. BCE) — younger brother of King Wu, regent for the young King Cheng, architect of Zhou ritual and governance institutions. Confucius's personal hero and model. The dream's absence = spiritual decline. Confucius measures his own decay by his connection to the ideal. Connects to FUALL's transmission concept: the living relationship with inherited ideals must be actively maintained.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'Let the will be set on the path of duty.
'Let every attainment in what is good be firmly grasped.
'Let perfect virtue be accorded with.
'Let relaxation and enjoyment be found in the polite arts.'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "Set your heart on the Way.
Hold firm to virtue.
Rely on benevolence.
Find enjoyment in the arts."
D: Elegant: Aim at the Way. Cling to virtue. Rest in benevolence. Play in the arts.
D: Distilled: The complete life is oriented by the Way, anchored in virtue, sustained by benevolence, and enriched by the arts.
Notes: Zhi yu dao, ju yu de, yi yu ren, you yu yi — four-part formula for the complete life. Dao (direction), De (foundation), Ren (sustaining force), Yi (the six arts: ritual, music, archery, charioteering, calligraphy, mathematics). The sequence matters: orientation first, then grounding, then practice, then cultivation. Connects to FUALL's educational institutions — the arts are not luxuries but essential to full human development.

D: Original: The Master said, 'From the man bringing his bundle of dried flesh for my teaching upwards, I have never refused instruction to any one.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "I have never refused to teach anyone who came to me, even if they could offer nothing more than a bundle of dried meat as tuition."
D: Elegant: Bring dried meat or nothing at all — I have never turned away anyone who came to learn.
D: Distilled: Education should never be refused to those who genuinely seek it, regardless of what they can afford.
Notes: Shu xiu — 'bundle of dried meat,' the traditional minimal gift when seeking a teacher. Confucius's principle: anyone who shows the initiative to seek learning deserves instruction. This is a democratizing claim: access to education should not depend on wealth. Connects to FUALL's Academy concept — education as a right tied to desire, not to status or resources.

D: Original: The Master said, 'I do not open up the truth to one who is not eager to get knowledge, nor help out any one who is not anxious to explain himself. When I have presented one corner of a subject to any one, and he cannot from it learn the other three, I do not repeat my lesson.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "I do not enlighten those who are not eager to learn, nor do I help those who are not struggling to articulate what they understand. If I present one corner of a subject and the student cannot work out the other three, I do not repeat the lesson."
D: Elegant: No eagerness — no enlightenment. No struggle to express — no help. Show one corner; if they can't find the other three, the lesson is over.
D: Distilled: Teaching requires a student who brings their own hunger to learn — the teacher opens doors but the student must walk through them.
Notes: Bu fen bu qi, bu fei bu fa — 'not eager, not enlighten; not struggling, not help.' Ju yi yu, bu yi san yu fan — 'show one corner, if not three corners in return, do not repeat.' This is Confucius's pedagogy: teaching is a response to demonstrated effort, not a service delivered regardless of engagement. Connects to FUALL's Academy concept — education is a partnership between teacher and student, not a one-way delivery.

D: Original:
When the Master was eating by the side of a mourner, he never ate to the full.
He did not sing on the same day in which he had been weeping.
D: Refashioned:
When dining beside a person in mourning, the Master never ate his fill.
On a day when he had wept, he did not sing.
D: Elegant: He ate lightly near mourners and did not sing on days he had wept. Respect and grief were not performances.
D: Distilled: Genuine empathy is expressed through small, unrehearsed acts of restraint — not eating fully near the grieving, not singing on days of sorrow.
Notes: Li (propriety) in its most personal, un-performative form. These are not public displays but private calibrations of behavior. The principle: moral conduct extends to moments when no one is watching or judging. Connects to FUALL: Right conduct is not about compliance with rules but about the internalization of values.

D: Original:
The Master said to Yen Yuan, 'When called to office, to undertake its duties; when not so called, to lie retired;-- it is only I and you who have attained to this.'
Tsze-lu said, 'If you had the conduct of the armies of a great State, whom would you have to act with you?'
The Master said, 'I would not have him to act with me, who will unarmed attack a tiger, or cross a river without a boat, dying without any regret. My associate must be the man who proceeds to action full of solicitude, who is fond of adjusting his plans, and then carries them into execution.'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said to Yan Yuan, "To serve when called to office, and to withdraw when not — only you and I have achieved this."
Zilu asked, "If you were to lead a great army, who would you take with you?"
The Master said, "Not someone who would attack a tiger with bare hands or wade across a river without a boat, and die without regret. I would want someone who approaches action with caution, who plans carefully, and then acts."
D: Elegant: Serve when called; withdraw when not — only Hui and I manage this. And if I led an army, I would not choose the recklessly brave. I would choose the person who plans, worries, and then acts.
D: Distilled: The ideal partner in any serious undertaking is not the fearless but the thoughtful — someone who plans carefully and acts with appropriate caution.
Notes: Zilu's question is a bid for inclusion, but Confucius deliberately excludes his kind of courage (reckless physical bravery). Lin shi er ju, hao mou er cheng — 'approaches affairs with caution, fond of planning and then succeeding.' This is the Confucian preference for strategic wisdom over raw courage. Connects to FUALL: in the Ideational domain, deliberation outranks bravado.

D: Original: The Master said, 'If the search for riches is sure to be successful, though I should become a groom with whip in hand to get them, I will do so. As the search may not be successful, I will follow after that which I love.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "If wealth could be reliably obtained, I would pursue it even as a market gatekeeper. But since it cannot be guaranteed, I will follow what I love."
D: Elegant: If wealth were certain, I'd chase it — even as a groom. But it isn't. So I follow what I love.
D: Distilled: Since wealth cannot be guaranteed by effort alone, the wiser path is to devote yourself to what you genuinely value.
Notes: Zhi ke qiu ye — 'if it could be sought.' Confucius does not condemn wealth (see 4.5) but notes its uncertainty. Given that uncertainty, the rational choice is to invest in what you can control: your character and your learning. Connects to FUALL's Resource domain: wealth is a means, not an end, and an unreliable one at that.

D: Original: The things in reference to which the Master exercised the greatest caution were -- fasting, war, and sickness.
D: Refashioned: The three things the Master treated with the greatest care were: fasting, war, and illness.
D: Elegant: Three things demanded the Master's utmost gravity: fasting, war, and sickness.
D: Distilled: The matters deserving the greatest seriousness are those involving the sacred, the destructive, and the fragile.
Notes: Zhai (fasting/purification before sacrifice), Zhan (war), Ji (illness) — three domains where carelessness has irreversible consequences. Each connects to a different power domain: fasting (Ideational/spiritual), war (Physical), illness (personal vulnerability). Connects to FUALL: certain domains demand heightened attention because the stakes are existential.

D: Original: When the Master was in Ch'i, he heard the Shao, and for three months did not know the taste of flesh. 'I did not think'' he said, 'that music could have been made so excellent as this.'
D: Refashioned: When the Master was in Qi, he heard the Shao music and for three months afterward could not taste his food. He said, "I never imagined music could be made so beautiful."
D: Elegant: He heard the Shao in Qi and lost his appetite for three months. 'I never imagined music could reach this height.'
D: Distilled: Encountering true beauty at its highest level is so overwhelming that it can temporarily displace all other pleasures.
Notes: Shao — the music of Emperor Shun (see 3.25, where it is praised as both beautiful and good). San yue bu zhi rou wei — 'three months, did not know the taste of meat.' Hyperbole expressing total aesthetic absorption. Connects to FUALL: the experience of encountering something that fully integrates beauty and goodness can be transformative.

D: Original:
Yen Yu said, 'Is our Master for the ruler of Wei?' Tsze-kung said, 'Oh! I will ask him.'
He went in accordingly, and said, 'What sort of men were Po-i and Shu-ch'i?' 'They were ancient worthies,' said the Master. 'Did they have any repinings because of their course?' The Master again replied, 'They sought to act virtuously, and they did so; what was there for them to repine about?' On this, Tsze-kung went out and said, 'Our Master is not for him.'
D: Refashioned:
Ran You asked, "Does the Master support the ruler of Wei?" Zigong said, "I will find out."
He went in and asked the Master, "What kind of men were Bo Yi and Shu Qi?" "They were ancient worthies," said the Master. "Did they have regrets?" "They sought to act virtuously and achieved it — what would they have to regret?" Zigong came out and told Ran You, "The Master does not support the ruler of Wei."
D: Elegant: Zigong asked indirectly: did Bo Yi and Shu Qi regret their principled sacrifice? No, the Master said — they acted rightly and achieved what they sought. From this, Zigong concluded: the Master will not support Wei's ruler.
D: Distilled: By praising those who sacrificed position for principle without regret, Confucius revealed that he would not support a ruler who gained power through improper means.
Notes: The ruler of Wei — Duke Chu, whose claim was contested (his father tried to kill his grandfather Nanzi's lover; he fled; the succession was disputed). Bo Yi and Shu Qi — see 5.22. They gave up their inheritance on principle. Zigong's brilliance: he uses an indirect question to determine Confucius's political stance. The logic: if the Master admires those who sacrificed position for righteousness, he will not support someone who gained position through impropriety. Connects to FUALL: political legitimacy requires moral foundation.

D: Original: The Master said, 'With coarse rice to eat, with water to drink, and my bended arm for a pillow;-- I have still joy in the midst of these things. Riches and honours acquired by unrighteousness, are to me as a floating cloud.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "With coarse rice to eat, water to drink, and my arm bent for a pillow — there is still joy in this. Wealth and status gained through unrighteousness are to me like passing clouds."
D: Elegant: Rice, water, a bent arm for a pillow — joy enough. Ill-gotten wealth is a passing cloud.
D: Distilled: Joy from a clear conscience in simple conditions outweighs any wealth or status obtained through unrighteous means.
Notes: Fu gui yu wo ru fu yun — 'riches and honor to me are like floating clouds.' One of the most quoted Confucian passages. The contrast: inner contentment through virtue vs. external comfort through corruption. Parallels 6.9 (Yan Hui's poverty and joy). Connects to FUALL: Wisdom produces a form of contentment that is independent of material circumstance.

D: Original: The Master said, 'If some years were added to my life, I would give fifty to the study of the Yi, and then I might come to be without great faults.' CHAP. XVII The Master's frequent themes of discourse were-- the Odes, the History, and the maintenance of the Rules of Propriety. On all these he frequently discoursed.
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "If I had more years to live, I would give fifty to studying the Yi, and then perhaps I could avoid great mistakes."
The Master's frequent subjects of discourse were the Odes, the Documents, and the observance of the rules of propriety.
D: Elegant: Give me fifty years with the Yi, and perhaps I could live without great fault. Until then — the Odes, the Documents, and propriety sustain me.
D: Distilled: Even a lifetime devoted to the deepest texts may not be enough to achieve moral clarity — but the pursuit itself is the point.
Notes: Yi (the Book of Changes / I Ching) — one of the Five Classics, a divination text that Confucius studied intensively in old age. Tradition holds that he wore out the leather bindings of his copy from repeated reading. The Odes (Shi Jing), Documents (Shu Jing), and Li (rules of propriety) were his primary teaching texts. Connects to FUALL: the canonical texts serve as instruments of lifelong moral formation, not subjects to be 'completed.'

D: Original:
The Duke of Sheh asked Tsze-lu about Confucius, and Tsze-lu did not answer him.
The Master said, 'Why did you not say to him,-- He is simply a man, who in his eager pursuit (of knowledge) forgets his food, who in the joy of its attainment forgets his sorrows, and who does not perceive that old age is coming on?'
D: Refashioned:
The Duke of She asked Zilu about Confucius, and Zilu did not answer.
The Master said, "Why didn't you simply say: He is a person who is so passionate in his pursuit of knowledge that he forgets to eat, so joyful in its attainment that he forgets his troubles, and so absorbed that he does not notice old age approaching."
D: Elegant: Say this of me: he forgets to eat when studying, forgets his sorrows when learning, and does not notice he is growing old.
D: Distilled: The life devoted to learning is so absorbing that physical need, emotional burden, and the passage of time all recede from awareness.
Notes: Duke of She (She Gong) — ruler of the She district in Chu. Zilu's silence may reflect uncertainty about how to describe Confucius to an outsider. Confucius provides his own self-description: defined entirely by the love of learning. Fa fen wang shi, le yi wang you, bu zhi lao zhi jiang zhi — 'so eager he forgets food, so joyful he forgets sorrow, unaware of approaching old age.' Connects to FUALL: the life of learning as a form of eudaimonia.

D: Original: The Master said, 'I am not one who was born in the possession of knowledge; I am one who is fond of antiquity, and earnest in seeking it there.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "I was not born with knowledge. I am simply one who loves antiquity and pursues it diligently."
D: Elegant: I was not born knowing. I simply love the old and pursue it earnestly.
D: Distilled: Wisdom is not innate genius but the product of loving the inherited tradition and diligently pursuing its understanding.
Notes: Wo fei sheng er zhi zhi zhe — 'I am not one born knowing.' Confucius explicitly denies being a sage (born wise) and identifies himself as a learner. Hao gu, min yi qiu zhi — 'fond of antiquity, earnest in seeking.' This is the transmission identity: the learner who loves what was passed down. Connects to FUALL's central thesis: wisdom is transmitted, not generated de novo.

D: Original: The subjects on which the Master did not talk, were-- extraordinary things, feats of strength, disorder, and spiritual beings.
D: Refashioned: The subjects the Master did not discuss were: the supernatural, feats of strength, chaos, and spirits.
D: Elegant: He did not speak of the uncanny, the violent, the chaotic, or the spiritual. His teaching stayed with what humans can know and do.
D: Distilled: Confucius deliberately limited his teaching to the human and practical, avoiding speculation about the supernatural, the violent, and the chaotic.
Notes: Zi bu yu: guai, li, luan, shen — 'the Master did not talk of: strange things, feats of strength, disorder, spirits.' This defines the Confucian intellectual domain: human ethics, governance, learning, and social relations. The transcendent is respected (see 6.20) but not discussed. Connects to FUALL's Reality pillar and the Confucian emphasis on what can be known and acted upon rather than metaphysical speculation.

D: Original: The Master said, 'When I walk along with two others, they may serve me as my teachers. I will select their good qualities and follow them, their bad qualities and avoid them.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "When I walk with two others, they each serve as my teachers. I select their good qualities to follow and their bad qualities to correct in myself."
D: Elegant: Any two companions become my teachers: I follow their strengths and correct their faults in myself.
D: Distilled: Everyone you encounter is a potential teacher — their virtues show you what to emulate, their faults show you what to correct in yourself.
Notes: San ren xing, bi you wo shi yan — 'among three walking, there must be my teacher.' One of the most famous Confucian sayings. The principle: learning is universal and continuous. Every person teaches you something — either what to do or what to avoid. Connects to FUALL's Academy concept: education is not bounded by institutional walls.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Heaven produced the virtue that is in me. Hwan T'ui-- what can he do to me?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Heaven produced the virtue that is in me. What can Huan Tui do to me?"
D: Elegant: Heaven gave me this virtue. What can any man do to me?
D: Distilled: When your virtue comes from a source higher than human authority, no human threat can ultimately reach you.
Notes: Huan Tui — Minister of War in Song who reportedly attempted to kill Confucius by felling a tree where he was teaching. Tian sheng de yu yu — 'Heaven produced virtue in me.' Confucius appeals to a transcendent mandate: his mission is Heaven-given, and no mortal can override it. Connects to FUALL: the Ideational domain ultimately transcends the Physical — virtue cannot be killed by force.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Do you think, my disciples, that I have any concealments? I conceal nothing from you. There is nothing which I do that is not shown to you, my disciples;-- that is my way.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Do you think I conceal things from you, my disciples? I conceal nothing. Everything I do, I share with you. That is my way."
D: Elegant: I hide nothing from you. Everything I do is shown openly. That is my way.
D: Distilled: The genuine teacher conceals nothing — their life itself is the lesson.
Notes: Wu wu xing er bu yu er san zi zhe — 'there is nothing I do that is not shown to you.' Confucius's teaching method: transparency. His life is his curriculum. Connects to FUALL's Academy: the teacher's character, not just their knowledge, is the primary instrument of education.

D: Original: There were four things which the Master taught,-- letters, ethics, devotion of soul, and truthfulness.
D: Refashioned: The Master taught four things: literature, conduct, loyalty, and truthfulness.
D: Elegant: Four subjects: literature, conduct, loyalty, truthfulness.
D: Distilled: The complete curriculum is literature for the mind, conduct for practice, loyalty for relationships, and truthfulness for character.
Notes: Wen (literature/culture), Xing (conduct/behavior), Zhong (loyalty/conscientiousness), Xin (truthfulness/faithfulness). Two intellectual (Wen, Xin) and two practical (Xing, Zhong). Connects to FUALL's educational framework: the full curriculum integrates intellectual and moral formation.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'A sage it is not mine to see; could I see a man of real talent and virtue, that would satisfy me.'
The Master said, 'A good man it is not mine to see; could I see a man possessed of constancy, that would satisfy me.
'Having not and yet affecting to have, empty and yet affecting to be full, straitened and yet affecting to be at ease:-- it is difficult with such characteristics to have constancy.'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "A sage I will never meet. If I could find someone of genuine talent and constancy, that would satisfy me."
The Master said, "A truly good person I will never meet. If I could find someone who maintains constancy, that would satisfy me.
"People who have nothing yet pretend to have, who are empty yet pretend to be full, who are struggling yet pretend to be at ease — for such people, constancy is impossible."
D: Elegant: I'll never meet a sage. I'd settle for someone with constancy. But constancy is impossible for those who pretend — pretend to have, pretend to know, pretend to be at ease.
D: Distilled: Constancy of character is worth more than brilliance, but it is impossible for those who live in pretense.
Notes: Heng (constancy) — the quality Confucius values most realistically. He does not expect sages; he hopes for constancy. The three forms of pretense — pretending to have, to be full, to be at ease — are obstacles to Heng because they are inherently unstable. Connects to FUALL: sustainability of character matters more than peak performance.

D: Original: The Master angled,-- but did not use a net. He shot,-- but not at birds perching.
D: Refashioned: The Master fished with a line but not a net. He shot birds in flight but not those at rest.
D: Elegant: He fished with a line, not a net. He shot birds in flight, never at rest. Fair play, even with animals.
D: Distilled: Even in hunting and fishing, the cultivated person observes limits — taking only what is fair, never exploiting helplessness.
Notes: Diao er bu gang, yi bu she su — 'angled but did not net; shot but not at roosting birds.' The principle of proportionality and fairness extended even to the animal world. The Junzi takes what is needed, not what is available. Connects to FUALL: Right conduct includes restraint in one's power over those who cannot resist.

D: Original: The Master said, 'There may be those who act without knowing why. I do not do so. Hearing much and selecting what is good and following it; seeing much and keeping it in memory:-- this is the second style of knowledge.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "There may be people who act without understanding why. I do not do this. I listen widely, select what is good, and follow it. I observe widely and remember what I see. This is the second level of knowledge."
D: Elegant: Some act without knowing why. I do not. I listen widely, choose the good, observe much, and remember. This is the second kind of knowledge — below the innate, but reliable.
D: Distilled: Acting without understanding is reckless; the disciplined alternative is to listen broadly, select carefully, observe widely, and remember faithfully.
Notes: The 'second style of knowledge' (ci zhi ye) — below innate knowledge (sheng zhi) but above ignorance. Confucius again denies being born wise (see 7.19) and describes his method: empirical observation + selective retention. Connects to FUALL's Reality pillar: knowledge built from careful observation is more reliable than assumed wisdom.

D: Original:
It was difficult to talk (profitably and reputably) with the people of Hu-hsiang, and a lad of that place having had an interview with the Master, the disciples doubted.
The Master said, 'I admit people's approach to me without committing myself as to what they may do when they have retired. Why must one be so severe? If a man purify himself to wait upon me, I receive him so purified, without guaranteeing his past conduct.'
D: Refashioned:
The people of Huxiang were considered difficult to engage with, so when a young man from there came to see the Master, the disciples were doubtful.
The Master said, "I accept a person's approach to me without guaranteeing their past or future conduct. If someone purifies themselves to come to me, I accept that purification. Why be so rigid? Why must we hold people to what they were before?"
D: Elegant: A man from a bad place came to learn. The disciples doubted. The Master said: I accept who they are now, not who they were before. Why must we hold people hostage to their past?
D: Distilled: The willingness to accept someone's present effort without holding them hostage to their past is both generous and necessary for genuine transformation.
Notes: Huxiang — a village with a bad reputation. Confucius's principle: judge the present intention, not the past record. Yu qi jie ye, bu bao qi wang ye — 'accept their purification, do not guarantee their past.' This is a principle of moral second chances. Connects to FUALL: the Academy must be open to anyone who genuinely seeks to learn, regardless of background.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Is virtue a thing remote? I wish to be virtuous, and lo! virtue is at hand.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Is virtue truly far away? The moment I desire virtue, it is here."
D: Elegant: Is virtue distant? I wish for it — and it arrives.
D: Distilled: Virtue is not a distant achievement but an immediate possibility — it is as close as the decision to pursue it.
Notes: Wo yu ren, si ren zhi yi — 'I wish for Ren, and Ren is here.' Ren is not a destination requiring a long journey but a disposition available in any moment. The only prerequisite is genuine desire. Connects to FUALL: the transformation the framework calls for does not require waiting for institutional change — it begins with individual commitment.

D: Original:
The minister of crime of Ch'an asked whether the duke Chao knew propriety, and Confucius said, 'He knew propriety.'
Confucius having retired, the minister bowed to Wu-ma Ch'i to come forward, and said, 'I have heard that the superior man is not a partisan. May the superior man be a partisan also? The prince married a daughter of the house of Wu, of the same surname with himself, and called her,-- "The elder Tsze of Wu." If the prince knew propriety, who does not know it?'
Wu-ma Ch'i reported these remarks, and the Master said, 'I am fortunate! If I have any errors, people are sure to know them.'
D: Refashioned:
The minister of crime of Chen pointed out that Duke Zhao of Lu had married a woman from Wu — from the same surname as his own house — and called her 'The Elder of Wu' to disguise the impropriety. "If the Duke understood propriety, who doesn't?"
When this was reported, Confucius said, "I am fortunate. When I make mistakes, people notice."
D: Elegant: They caught the Duke — and the Master — in a breach of propriety. The Master said: 'I am fortunate. When I make mistakes, people are sure to notice.'
D: Distilled: The mark of genuine humility is welcoming correction — treating the discovery of your errors as a piece of good fortune.
Notes: Duke Zhao of Lu married within his own surname (Ji), violating the exogamy rules of Li (propriety). Confucius had initially defended the Duke, and when corrected, accepted the criticism gracefully. Guo ye ren bi zhi zhi — 'when I err, people are sure to know it.' Connects to FUALL: public accountability is not a burden but a gift — it forces error-correction that private life cannot.

D: Original: When the Master was in company with a person who was singing, if he sang well, he would make him repeat the song, while he accompanied it with his own voice.
D: Refashioned: When the Master heard someone singing well, he would ask them to repeat the song and then join in himself.
D: Elegant: When he heard good singing, he asked for an encore — and sang along.
D: Distilled: The love of beauty expresses itself naturally through appreciation, participation, and shared joy.
Notes: A brief observational note showing Confucius's love of music and his generosity of spirit — he did not compete with good performers but joined them. The impulse to participate rather than merely judge. Connects to FUALL: the Agora at its best is a space of shared excellence, not competitive display.

D: Original: The Master said, 'In letters I am perhaps equal to other men, but the character of the superior man, carrying out in his conduct what he professes, is what I have not yet attained to.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "In matters of learning, I may be the equal of other men. But the character of the Junzi — living out in practice what one professes — that I have not yet achieved."
D: Elegant: In knowledge, I may match others. In living what I teach? Not yet.
D: Distilled: Knowing enough is far easier than living what you know — the gap between learning and embodiment is the real measure of character.
Notes: Gong xing junzi zhe, ze wu wei zhi you de — 'carrying out the Junzi in conduct, I have not yet attained.' Another instance of Confucius's characteristic humility about practice vs. knowledge. Connects to FUALL: the gap between understanding and embodiment is the central challenge of Wisdom.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The sage and the man of perfect virtue;-- how dare I rank myself with them? It may simply be said of me, that I strive to become such without satiety, and teach others without weariness.' Kung-hsi Hwa said, 'This is just what we, the disciples, cannot imitate you in.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "To claim the titles of sage or perfectly virtuous person — how could I presume? At most it may be said of me that I pursue these things tirelessly and teach others without growing weary." Gongxi Hua said, "That is precisely what we, your disciples, cannot match."
D: Elegant: I am no sage. I simply pursue without ceasing and teach without tiring. 'That,' said the disciple, 'is exactly what we cannot do.'
D: Distilled: Tireless pursuit and tireless teaching may not make you a sage, but they are themselves so rare that even disciples cannot match them.
Notes: Gongxi Hua (Chi) — see 5.7. Confucius denies being a sage but claims tireless effort and tireless teaching. His disciple's response: that tirelessness itself is the remarkable quality. Connects to FUALL: the teacher's defining quality is not genius but inexhaustible commitment.

D: Original: The Master being very sick, Tsze-lu asked leave to pray for him. He said, 'May such a thing be done?' Tsze-lu replied, 'It may. In the Eulogies it is said, "Prayer has been made for thee to the spirits of the upper and lower worlds."' The Master said, 'My praying has been for a long time.'
D: Refashioned: When the Master was very ill, Zilu asked permission to pray for him. The Master said, "Is that appropriate?" Zilu replied, "It is. The Eulogies say, 'Prayers are made for you to the spirits above and below.'" The Master said, "Then I have been praying for a long time already."
D: Elegant: They wanted to pray for his recovery. 'I have been praying,' he said, 'my entire life.'
D: Distilled: A life lived with integrity is itself a continuous prayer — no special petition is needed.
Notes: Qiu zhi jiu yi — 'my praying has been for a long time.' Confucius redefines prayer: a virtuous life is itself a form of prayer, making any additional petition unnecessary. This is consistent with his approach to the spirits (6.20): respectful but not supplicatory. Connects to FUALL's Temple concept: ritual and the sacred are expressed through how you live, not through special appeals.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Extravagance leads to insubordination, and parsimony to meanness. It is better to be mean than to be insubordinate.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Extravagance leads to arrogance and excess. Frugality leads to meanness and rigidity. But between the two, it is better to be frugal than extravagant."
D: Elegant: Extravagance breeds arrogance; frugality breeds stinginess. Given the choice, stinginess is the lesser evil.
D: Distilled: Both extravagance and excessive frugality are flaws, but frugality's vices are less dangerous than extravagance's.
Notes: She ze bu sun, jian ze gu — 'extravagant then not deferential (insubordinate), frugal then solid (rigid/mean).' The Zhong Yong (Mean) would be best, but forced to choose an extreme, Confucius prefers the one less likely to lead to social harm. Connects to FUALL: when the ideal center cannot be maintained, err toward restraint rather than excess.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The superior man is satisfied and composed; the mean man is always full of distress.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "The Junzi is at ease and composed. The petty person is perpetually anxious and distressed."
D: Elegant: The Junzi is calm. The petty person is in constant distress.
D: Distilled: Inner peace is the mark of genuine cultivation; perpetual anxiety is the mark of its absence.
Notes: Junzi tan dang dang, xiaoren chang qi qi — 'the Junzi is expansive and at ease; the small person is constantly worried.' The Junzi's composure comes from alignment with Dao; the xiaoren's anxiety comes from dependence on external outcomes. Connects to FUALL: wisdom produces equanimity because it decouples wellbeing from circumstance.

D: Original: The Master was mild, and yet dignified; majestic, and yet not fierce; respectful, and yet easy.
D: Refashioned: The Master was gentle yet dignified, commanding yet not intimidating, respectful yet at ease.
D: Elegant: Gentle but dignified. Commanding but not fierce. Respectful but natural.
D: Distilled: The ideal character holds apparent opposites in balance — gentleness with dignity, authority with warmth, respect with naturalness.
Notes: Wen er li, wei er bu meng, gong er an — three paired qualities. The closing portrait of Book 7 depicts Confucius as the embodiment of the Zhong Yong (Constant Mean) — each quality tempered by its complement. Connects to FUALL: the Golden Mean as the highest expression of character, balancing opposing virtues.

D: Original: Book 8: T'ai-Po

The Master said, 'T'ai-po may be said to have reached the highest point of virtuous action. Thrice he declined the kingdom, and the people in ignorance of his motives could not express their approbation of his conduct.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Tai Bo may be said to have reached the highest point of virtuous conduct. Three times he declined the throne, and the people, unaware of his reasons, could not even express their admiration for his sacrifice."
D: Elegant: Tai Bo gave up the throne three times. The people never even understood what he had done. That is virtue at its peak — invisible.
D: Distilled: The highest virtue may be so selfless that the world never recognizes it was practiced.
Notes: Tai Bo — eldest son of King Tai of Zhou, who ceded the succession to his younger brother so that the line could pass to King Wen (the founder of Zhou's greatness). He reportedly fled to the Wu region. San yi tian xia rang — 'three times yielded the empire.' The people's ignorance is the point: the highest virtue does not seek recognition. Connects to FUALL: transmission concept — some of the most consequential acts of moral leadership are invisible.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'Respectfulness, without the rules of propriety, becomes laborious bustle; carefulness, without the rules of propriety, becomes timidity; boldness, without the rules of propriety, becomes insubordination; straightforwardness, without the rules of propriety, becomes rudeness.
'When those who are in high stations perform well all their duties to their relations, the people are aroused to virtue. When old friends are not neglected by them, the people are preserved from meanness.'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "Respectfulness without propriety becomes anxious fussing. Carefulness without propriety becomes timidity. Boldness without propriety becomes recklessness. Straightforwardness without propriety becomes rudeness.
"When those in high position fulfill their duties to family and community, the people are moved to virtue. When they do not neglect old friends and allies, the people are preserved from meanness."
D: Elegant: Without propriety, respect becomes fussing, care becomes fear, courage becomes recklessness, and honesty becomes rudeness. Every virtue needs its form.
D: Distilled: Every virtue requires the structure of propriety to function properly — without form, respect becomes anxiety, courage becomes recklessness, and honesty becomes cruelty.
Notes: Four virtues (respectfulness, carefulness, boldness, directness) that become four vices when practiced without Li (propriety). Li is the structuring principle that channels natural impulses into functional virtues. Connects to FUALL: Right (moral principle) without practical structure (Li/institutional form) produces dysfunction. Also: leaders' conduct shapes public morality — what they model, the people follow.

D: Original: The philosopher Tsang being ill, he called to him the disciples of his school, and said, 'Uncover my feet, uncover my hands. It is said in the Book of Poetry, "We should be apprehensive and cautious, as if on the brink of a deep gulf, as if treading on thin ice," and so have I been. Now and hereafter, I know my escape from all injury to my person, O ye, my little children.'
D: Refashioned: The philosopher Zengzi, gravely ill, called his disciples and said, "Look at my feet. Look at my hands. The Book of Poetry says, 'Be watchful and careful, as if standing on the edge of a deep abyss, as if treading on thin ice.' I have lived this way. From now on, I know I have escaped harm to my body. O my children."
D: Elegant: Zengzi on his deathbed: 'See my hands and feet — unharmed. I lived as if on thin ice, over an abyss. Now, finally, I am released from that vigilance.'
D: Distilled: A lifetime of moral vigilance, sustained to the very end, is itself the achievement — the body preserved is proof of the discipline.
Notes: Zengzi's deathbed scene. The request to 'see my hands and feet' — demonstrating that his body (received from his parents) has been preserved intact, fulfilling filial duty. Zhan zhan jing jing, ru lin shen yuan, ru lv bo bing — the Shi Jing quote (Ode 195). Xiao (filial piety) includes preserving the body your parents gave you. Connects to FUALL: the Temple institution — ritual vigilance maintained across an entire lifetime.

D: Original:
The philosopher Tsang being ill, Meng Chang went to ask how he was.
Tsang said to him, 'When a bird is about to die, its notes are mournful; when a man is about to die, his words are good.
'There are three principles of conduct which the man of high rank should consider specially important:-- that in his deportment and manner he keep from violence and heedlessness; that in regulating his countenance he keep near to sincerity; and that in his words and tones he keep far from lowness and impropriety. As to such matters as attending to the sacrificial vessels, there are the proper officers for them.'
D: Refashioned:
The philosopher Zengzi, when ill, received a visit from Meng Jing. Zengzi said, "When a bird is about to die, its cries are mournful. When a person is about to die, their words are good.
"There are three principles a person of high rank should consider especially important: in their manner, to keep from violence and carelessness; in their expression, to keep close to sincerity; in their speech, to keep far from coarseness and impropriety. As for managing the details of ceremony, there are officials for that."
D: Elegant: A dying bird cries mournfully; a dying man speaks truth. Three things matter for a leader: deportment free of violence, expression close to sincerity, speech far from coarseness. The details can be delegated.
D: Distilled: What matters in leadership is not managing details but embodying the right disposition — sincere in expression, restrained in manner, dignified in speech.
Notes: Niao zhi jiang si, qi ming ye ai; ren zhi jiang si, qi yan ye shan — 'when birds are about to die, their cries are sad; when people are about to die, their words are good.' Zengzi's deathbed wisdom. The three principles concern personal bearing, not administrative competence. Connects to FUALL: leadership operates primarily in the Ideational domain — what you embody matters more than what you manage.

D: Original: The philosopher Tsang said, 'Gifted with ability, and yet putting questions to those who were not so; possessed of much, and yet putting questions to those possessed of little; having, as though he had not; full, and yet counting himself as empty; offended against, and yet entering into no altercation; formerly I had a friend who pursued this style of conduct.'
D: Refashioned: The philosopher Zengzi said, "Gifted yet learning from the ungifted. Knowledgeable yet consulting those with little knowledge. Having much yet appearing to have nothing. Full yet appearing empty. Wronged yet never retaliating. I once had a friend who conducted himself this way."
D: Elegant: Talented, yet learning from the untalented. Full, yet appearing empty. Wronged, yet never retaliating. I once knew someone like this.
D: Distilled: The highest character combines genuine ability with radical humility — being full while appearing empty, being wronged while refusing retaliation.
Notes: The 'friend' is almost certainly Yan Hui, unnamed out of reverence. The qualities described — intellectual humility, apparent emptiness, non-retaliation — are distinctly Yan Hui's (see 2.9, 6.9). Yi you ruo wu, shi ruo xu — 'having as if not having, full as if empty.' Connects to FUALL: the Daoist influence on Confucian thought — strength expressed through apparent yielding.

D: Original: The philosopher Tsang said, 'Suppose that there is an individual who can be entrusted with the charge of a young orphan prince, and can be commissioned with authority over a state of a hundred li, and whom no emergency however great can drive from his principles:-- is such a man a superior man? He is a superior man indeed.'
D: Refashioned: The philosopher Zengzi said, "The person who can be entrusted with the care of an orphaned prince, given authority over a state of a hundred li, and who cannot be driven from their principles by any crisis — is such a person a Junzi? They are a Junzi indeed."
D: Elegant: Entrust them with an orphan prince. Give them authority over a kingdom. Test them in crisis. If they hold — that is the Junzi.
D: Distilled: The true test of character is maintaining principle under the combined pressure of great responsibility and great crisis.
Notes: Tuo liu chi zhi gu — 'entrusted with a six-chi orphan' (a young heir). Ji bai li zhi ming — 'commissioned with a hundred-li state.' Lin da jie er bu ke duo — 'facing great trials and cannot be swayed.' Three tests: can this person handle (1) vulnerability (orphan), (2) power (state), and (3) adversity (crisis)? Connects to FUALL: character is tested by the combination of power and adversity, not by either alone.

D: Original:
The philosopher Tsang said, 'The officer may not be without breadth of mind and vigorous endurance. His burden is heavy and his course is long.
'Perfect virtue is the burden which he considers it is his to sustain;-- is it not heavy? Only with death does his course stop;-- is it not long?
D: Refashioned:
The philosopher Zengzi said, "The scholar must be broad-minded and resilient. The burden is heavy and the road is long.
"Ren is the burden they have chosen to carry — is it not heavy? Only with death does the road end — is it not long?"
D: Elegant: The burden is heavy — it is Ren itself. The road is long — it ends only with death. A scholar must have broad shoulders and endurance.
D: Distilled: The moral life is a lifelong burden of benevolence carried without relief until death — it demands both strength and endurance.
Notes: Shi bu ke yi bu hong yi — 'the scholar cannot be without breadth and fortitude.' Ren yi wei ji ren — 'Ren is their self-assumed burden.' Si er hou yi — 'only after death does it stop.' Zengzi's statement frames the moral life as an endurance challenge, not a sprint. Connects to FUALL's framework: Wisdom is not an achievement but an ongoing discipline — the road never shortens.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'It is by the Odes that the mind is aroused.
'It is by the Rules of Propriety that the character is established.
'It is from Music that the finish is received.'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "Begin with the Odes to awaken the mind.
Establish character through the rules of propriety.
Complete the process through music."
D: Elegant: The Odes awaken. Propriety establishes. Music completes.
D: Distilled: Education follows a three-stage progression: literature awakens the mind, propriety forms the character, and music completes the person.
Notes: Xing yu shi, li yu li, cheng yu yue — 'aroused by the Odes, established by Li, completed by music.' This is Confucius's curriculum in sequence: (1) emotional/intellectual awakening through poetry, (2) behavioral formation through ritual propriety, (3) aesthetic/spiritual completion through music. Connects to FUALL's educational institutions: Academy (poetry/literature), Temple (ritual/propriety), and Arts (music) as the three stages of Paideia.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The people may be made to follow a path of action, but they may not be made to understand it.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "The people can be led to follow a course of action, but they cannot always be made to understand the reasons for it."
D: Elegant: You can lead the people to act. You cannot always make them understand why.
D: Distilled: Sometimes sound governance requires leading people to act rightly before they can understand the reasons — understanding may follow practice.
Notes: Min ke shi you zhi, bu ke shi zhi zhi — one of the most debated passages in the Analects. Critics read it as authoritarian; defenders argue it means that understanding often follows practice rather than preceding it (the way ritual works). Connects to FUALL: the Temple institution operates on this principle — ritual shapes understanding through practice rather than argument.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The man who is fond of daring and is dissatisfied with poverty, will proceed to insubordination. So will the man who is not virtuous, when you carry your dislike of him to an extreme.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "A brave person who resents their poverty will cause trouble. A person who lacks virtue, if pushed too far by others' contempt, will also cause trouble."
D: Elegant: A bold person who hates their poverty will rebel. A person without virtue, despised too harshly, will do the same.
D: Distilled: Both resentful courage and contempt for the unvirtuous are social dangers — the first breeds rebellion, the second breeds desperation.
Notes: Hao yong ji pin — 'fond of daring and resentful of poverty.' The warning is practical: social order depends on managing both the ambitious poor and the despised outcasts. Excessive contempt for the unvirtuous drives them to destructive action. Connects to FUALL's power domains: the Physical domain (rebellion) is triggered when the Social domain (dignity, inclusion) fails.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Though a man have abilities as admirable as those of the Duke of Chau, yet if he be proud and niggardly, those other things are really not worth being looked at.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Even if someone had abilities as admirable as the Duke of Zhou's, if they were proud and stingy, the rest of their qualities would not be worth considering."
D: Elegant: Give someone the Duke of Zhou's genius. Add pride and stinginess. The genius becomes worthless.
D: Distilled: Pride and stinginess negate even the greatest abilities — character defects can render exceptional talent meaningless.
Notes: Duke of Zhou (Zhou Gong) — Confucius's ideal (see 7.5). Even using his own hero as the example, Confucius insists: ability without humility and generosity is worthless. Jiao (pride) and lin (stinginess/meanness) are sufficient to disqualify. Connects to FUALL: competence in any domain is nullified by fundamental character flaws.

D: Original: The Master said, 'It is not easy to find a man who has learned for three years without coming to be good.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "It is not easy to find someone who has studied for three years without arriving at goodness."
D: Elegant: Three years of genuine study, and goodness is hard to avoid.
D: Distilled: Sustained learning naturally produces moral improvement — the person who studies earnestly for years will almost inevitably become good.
Notes: San nian xue, bu zhi yu gu, bu yi de ye — 'three years studying without arriving at goodness — that is not easy to find.' The optimistic claim: genuine learning naturally leads to moral development. The inverse of modern assumptions that knowledge and virtue are unrelated. Connects to FUALL's Academy concept: intellectual and moral formation are inseparable when education is properly structured.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'With sincere faith he unites the love of learning; holding firm to death, he is perfecting the excellence of his course.
'Such an one will not enter a tottering State, nor dwell in a disorganized one. When right principles of government prevail in the kingdom, he will show himself; when they are prostrated, he will keep concealed.
'When a country is well-governed, poverty and a mean condition are things to be ashamed of. When a country is ill- governed, riches and honour are things to be ashamed of.'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "With sincere faith united with love of learning, hold firm even to death in perfecting your course.
"Do not enter a state that is in danger, nor remain in one that is in chaos. When the Way prevails in the world, show yourself. When it does not, withdraw.
"When good governance prevails, poverty and obscurity are shameful. When governance is corrupt, wealth and honor are shameful."
D: Elegant: Hold to learning, hold to death, hold to the Way. In a just age, be ashamed of poverty. In a corrupt age, be ashamed of wealth.
D: Distilled: In a well-governed society, failing to achieve is shameful; in a corrupt one, succeeding is shameful — your relationship to wealth reveals your relationship to justice.
Notes: Du xin hao xue, shou si shan dao — 'sincere faith, love learning, hold to death, perfect the Way.' The principle: visible prosperity in a corrupt society is evidence of complicity. Visible poverty in a just society is evidence of failure. Your material condition is morally legible only relative to the quality of governance. Connects to FUALL: the ethical status of wealth and status depends entirely on the system that produces them.

D: Original: The Master said, 'He who is not in any particular office, has nothing to do with plans for the administration of its duties.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "If you do not hold a particular office, do not concern yourself with its policies."
D: Elegant: Not your office, not your policies.
D: Distilled: Stay in your lane — focus your effort on what you are actually responsible for, not on roles you do not hold.
Notes: Bu zai qi wei, bu mou qi zheng — 'not in the position, do not plan its governance.' A principle of role discipline: focus your energy on your actual responsibilities. This is not about disengagement from public life but about the discipline of scope. Connects to FUALL: effective institutional function requires people who focus on their roles rather than overstepping them.

D: Original: The Master said, 'When the music master Chih first entered on his office, the finish of the Kwan Tsu was magnificent;-- how it filled the ears!'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "When the music master Zhi first took his position, the finale of the Guan Ju was magnificent — how it filled the ears!"
D: Elegant: When Music Master Zhi began, the Guan Ju soared. How it filled the ears.
D: Distilled: Great institutional moments occur when the right person brings their full ability to a role that matches their gifts.
Notes: Music Master Zhi — chief musician of Lu. The Guan Ju — the first ode of the Shi Jing (see 3.20). Confucius recalls a peak moment of musical excellence — the alignment of talent, role, and performance. Connects to FUALL: institutional excellence depends on placing the right people in the right roles.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Ardent and yet not upright; stupid and yet not attentive; simple and yet not sincere:-- such persons I do not understand.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Passionate yet not honest; simple yet not careful; naively trusting yet not sincere — such people I cannot understand."
D: Elegant: Enthusiastic but dishonest. Simple but careless. Trusting but insincere. I cannot fathom such combinations.
D: Distilled: The most bewildering failure is possessing a natural disposition toward virtue but lacking the corresponding inner quality.
Notes: Kuang er bu zhi, dong er bu yuan, kong kong er bu xin — three contradictions between natural temperament and moral substance. Each person has a natural quality that should produce a corresponding virtue but doesn't. Connects to FUALL: natural disposition without cultivation produces inconsistency — talent is wasted without disciplined development.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Learn as if you could not reach your object, and were always fearing also lest you should lose it.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Learn as though you might never catch up, and as though you were always afraid of losing what you have gained."
D: Elegant: Learn as if you can never catch up. Fear losing what you've gained.
D: Distilled: The right posture toward learning combines the urgency of pursuit with the anxiety of preservation.
Notes: Xue ru bu ji, you kong shi zhi — 'learn as if unable to reach it, yet fearing to lose it.' Two complementary anxieties: the pursuit of what you don't yet know and the preservation of what you do. Both are necessary. Connects to FUALL's transmission concept: knowledge must be both actively pursued and actively preserved.

D: Original: The Master said, 'How majestic was the manner in which Shun and Yu held possession of the empire, as if it were nothing to them!'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "How magnificent was the way Shun and Yu held the empire — as if it were nothing to them!"
D: Elegant: Shun and Yu held the world as if it were nothing. That is majesty.
D: Distilled: The greatest leaders hold power as if it were weightless — authority without attachment is the mark of true sovereignty.
Notes: Shun and Yu — legendary sage-kings. Shun received the throne through abdication (meritocratic succession). Yu founded the Xia dynasty and is credited with flood control. Wei er bu yu — 'possessed but did not possess.' Their greatness lay in holding power without being possessed by it. Connects to FUALL: the Ideational domain ideal — authority exercised without attachment to power itself.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'Great indeed was Yao as a sovereign! How majestic was he! It is only Heaven that is grand, and only Yao corresponded to it. How vast was his virtue! The people could find no name for it.
'How majestic was he in the works which he accomplished! How glorious in the elegant regulations which he instituted!'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "How great was Yao as a ruler! How magnificent! Only Heaven is truly grand, and only Yao matched it. How vast was his virtue — the people could find no words for it. How glorious his accomplishments, how brilliant the institutions he established!"
D: Elegant: Only Heaven is grand — and only Yao matched it. His virtue was beyond words, his institutions beyond improvement.
D: Distilled: The greatest ruler is one whose virtue is so vast that the people cannot even name it — and whose institutions endure beyond their creator.
Notes: Yao — the first of the legendary sage-kings, model of virtuous rule. Wei wei hu, wei tian wei da, wei Yao ze zhi — 'majestic! Only Heaven is great; only Yao corresponded to it.' Confucius's highest political praise: Yao's virtue was proportional to Heaven's grandeur. Connects to FUALL: the ideal of governance is not just effective rule but the creation of enduring institutions (the Zhangwen — 'elegant regulations' that outlast any individual ruler).

D: Original:
Shun had five ministers, and the empire was well-governed.
King Wu said, 'I have ten able ministers.'
Confucius said, 'Is not the saying that talents are difficult to find, true? Only when the dynasties of T'ang and Yu met, were they more abundant than in this of Chau, yet there was a woman among them. The able ministers were no more than nine men.
'King Wan possessed two of the three parts of the empire, and with those he served the dynasty of Yin. The virtue of the house of Chau may be said to have reached the highest point indeed.'
D: Refashioned:
Shun governed with five ministers and the empire was well-ordered.
King Wu said, 'I have ten capable ministers.'
Confucius said, 'Isn't it true that talent is rare? The eras of Yao-Shun and early Zhou had more talent than any other time. Among King Wu's ten, one was a woman. The actual count was nine.
'King Wen possessed two-thirds of the empire, and yet with that power he continued to serve the Yin dynasty. The virtue of the house of Zhou may truly be called the highest.'"
D: Elegant: Talent is rare. Even the greatest eras barely managed ten capable ministers. And King Wen, controlling most of the world, still served the dynasty above him. That is virtue at its peak.
D: Distilled: True greatness is demonstrated not by seizing power when you have it, but by continuing to serve legitimate authority even when you could easily overthrow it.
Notes: Five ministers of Shun, ten of King Wu (one a woman — traditionally identified as the Queen Mother of Wu). King Wen (Wen Wang) — father of King Wu, possessed 2/3 of the empire but never rebelled against the Shang/Yin dynasty. His restraint is the ultimate Confucian political virtue: power held in check by loyalty to legitimate order. Connects to FUALL: the Ideational domain's highest expression is authority that restrains itself.

D: Original: The Master said, 'I can find no flaw in the character of Yu. He used himself coarse food and drink, but displayed the utmost filial piety towards the spirits. His ordinary garments were poor, but he displayed the utmost elegance in his sacrificial cap and apron. He lived in a low mean house, but expended all his strength on the ditches and water-channels. I can find nothing like a flaw in Yu.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "I can find no fault in Yu. He ate coarse food but made lavish offerings to the spirits. He wore poor clothes but displayed the utmost elegance in his sacrificial garments. He lived in a humble dwelling but poured all his energy into public works — the ditches and waterways that protected the people. I can find no fault in Yu."
D: Elegant: Yu lived simply, dressed poorly, ate rough food — but his sacrificial robes were magnificent and his waterways saved the world. No fault can be found in him.
D: Distilled: The ideal leader is personally austere but generous in public service and reverent in sacred duty.
Notes: Yu (Da Yu, Yu the Great) — legendary founder of the Xia dynasty, famed for his flood control engineering. He supposedly spent years away from home, passing his own door three times without entering, to complete the work. The contrast: personal austerity + public magnificence + ritual devotion. Connects to FUALL: the three domains of excellence — humble in the personal, generous in the public, reverent in the sacred.

D: Original: Book 9: Tsze Han (The Master Seldom Spoke)
The subjects of which the Master seldom spoke were-- profitableness, and also the appointments of Heaven, and perfect virtue.
D: Refashioned: The Master seldom spoke of profit, fate, or perfect virtue.
D: Elegant: He rarely spoke of profit, rarely of fate, rarely of complete virtue.
D: Distilled: The wisest teacher is cautious about the subjects most easily misunderstood: profit, fate, and moral perfection.
Notes: Zi han yan li, yu ming, yu ren — 'the Master seldom spoke of: profit, the mandate of Heaven, and Ren.' Opens Book 9. The three topics he avoids discussing are all easily misunderstood or misused. Li (profit) distorts moral reasoning; Ming (fate/mandate) can excuse passivity; Ren (perfect virtue) can become a self-congratulatory label. Connects to FUALL: the most important concepts are also the most dangerous to discuss carelessly.

D: Original:
A man of the village of Ta-hsiang said, 'Great indeed is the philosopher K'ung! His learning is extensive, and yet he does not render his name famous by any particular thing.'
The Master heard the observation, and said to his disciples, 'What shall I practise? Shall I practise charioteering, or shall I practise archery? I will practise charioteering.'
D: Refashioned:
A villager from Daxiang said, "How great is the philosopher Kong! His learning is vast, yet he has not made himself famous for any one particular thing."
The Master heard this and said to his disciples, "What should I specialize in? Shall I take up charioteering? Or archery? I'll take up charioteering."
D: Elegant: They said: 'He knows everything but is famous for nothing in particular.' The Master laughed: 'Then I'll specialize in driving a chariot.'
D: Distilled: The truly learned person resists specialization — their breadth is their strength, even if the world cannot categorize them.
Notes: The villager's comment is a backhanded compliment: Confucius is learned but hasn't made a name in any one field. Confucius's response is ironic — he picks the humblest of the Six Arts (charioteering) as his 'specialty.' The joke underscores his rejection of narrow specialization. Connects to FUALL: 2.12 — the Junzi is not a utensil. Wisdom resists reduction to a single domain.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'The linen cap is that prescribed by the rules of ceremony, but now a silk one is worn. It is economical, and I follow the common practice.
'The rules of ceremony prescribe the bowing below the hall, but now the practice is to bow only after ascending it. That is arrogant. I continue to bow below the hall, though I oppose the common practice.'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "The ritual prescribes a linen cap, but nowadays people use silk. It is more economical, and I follow the common practice.
"The ritual prescribes bowing at the bottom of the hall before ascending, but now people bow only after ascending. That is arrogant. Despite going against common practice, I continue to bow below the hall."
D: Elegant: Silk caps instead of linen — practical, and I accept it. Bowing only after ascending instead of below — arrogant, and I refuse it.
D: Distilled: Adapt to practical changes in ritual without complaint, but resist changes that violate the spirit of humility the ritual was meant to express.
Notes: Two contrasting cases of ritual change: one acceptable (silk vs. linen — a practical economy), one unacceptable (bowing above vs. below — a loss of humility). The principle: distinguish between the form of ritual (which can adapt) and its meaning (which must be preserved). Connects to FUALL: institutional adaptation should preserve core values while allowing practical evolution.

D: Original: There were four things from which the Master was entirely free. He had no foregone conclusions, no arbitrary predeterminations, no obstinacy, and no egoism.
D: Refashioned: The Master was entirely free from four things: foregone conclusions, arbitrary predeterminations, stubbornness, and egoism.
D: Elegant: No preconceptions. No rigidity. No stubbornness. No ego.
D: Distilled: The four intellectual vices to eliminate are prejudgment, inflexibility, obstinacy, and self-centeredness.
Notes: Wu yi, wu bi, wu gu, wu wo — 'no preconceptions, no predeterminations, no obstinacy, no egoism.' Four negations that define intellectual freedom. This is Confucius's epistemology reduced to its essence: openness, adaptability, humility, and selflessness. Connects to FUALL's Reality pillar: seeing things as they are requires the elimination of all four biases.

D: Original:
The Master was put in fear in K'wang.
He said, 'After the death of King Wan, was not the cause of truth lodged here in me?
'If Heaven had wished to let this cause of truth perish, then I, a future mortal, should not have got such a relation to that cause. While Heaven does not let the cause of truth perish, what can the people of K'wang do to me?'
D: Refashioned: The Master was in danger in Kuang. He said, "Since King Wen's death, is not the cause of culture and truth lodged here in me? If Heaven intended to destroy this tradition, then I, a mortal, would not have been given this connection to it. As long as Heaven does not wish to see the Way perish, what can the people of Kuang do to me?"
D: Elegant: If Heaven wanted this tradition to die, it would not have given it to me. Since it did — what can any mob do?
D: Distilled: When you carry a mission larger than yourself, fear of personal danger becomes irrelevant — the mission's source is beyond any human threat.
Notes: Kuang — a town where Confucius was surrounded by hostile forces who mistook him for Yang Huo, a despised official. Wen wang ji mo, wen bu zai zi hu — 'after King Wen's death, is not the culture/tradition here in me?' Confucius claims to be the living vessel of Zhou civilization. Tian zhi jiang sang si wen ye — 'if Heaven wished to destroy this culture.' His courage comes from the sense of being a transmission vessel. Connects directly to FUALL's central transmission concept: the individual becomes the carrier of civilizational wisdom, which is worth more than any individual life.

D: Original:
A high officer asked Tsze-kung, saying, 'May we not say that your Master is a sage? How various is his ability!'
Tsze-kung said, 'Certainly Heaven has endowed him unlimitedly. He is about a sage. And, moreover, his ability is various.'
The Master heard of the conversation and said, 'Does the high officer know me? When I was young, my condition was low, and therefore I acquired my ability in many things, but they were mean matters. Must the superior man have such variety of ability? He does not need variety of ability.'
Lao said, 'The Master said, "Having no official employment, I acquired many arts."'
D: Refashioned:
A high official asked Zigong, "Is your Master a sage? His abilities are so varied!"
Zigong said, "Heaven has indeed endowed him lavishly. He is close to being a sage, and his abilities are indeed various."
The Master heard this and said, "Does the official know me? When I was young, I was poor, and so I learned many practical skills — but they were lowly skills. Does the Junzi need such variety? No."
Lao confirmed, "The Master himself said, 'Not being employed in office, I acquired many arts.'"
D: Elegant: They call his many skills a sign of sagacity. He called them a product of poverty. 'The Junzi,' he said, 'does not need such variety.'
D: Distilled: Diverse practical skills are not signs of wisdom but products of necessity — true cultivation aims higher than versatile competence.
Notes: Confucius distinguishes between his practical abilities (gained through youthful poverty) and genuine sagacity. Duo neng bi shi — 'many abilities, lowly things.' His breadth came from need, not from excellence. The Junzi aspires to something beyond versatile competence. Connects to FUALL: the difference between practical skill (Guilds) and Wisdom — both are valuable, but they are not the same thing.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Am I indeed possessed of knowledge? I am not knowing. But if a mean person, who appears quite empty-like, ask anything of me, I set it forth from one end to the other, and exhaust it.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Do I actually possess knowledge? I do not. But when anyone, however simple, comes to me with a question, I examine it from every angle until I have exhausted the subject."
D: Elegant: Am I knowledgeable? No. But when asked, I turn the question over from every side until nothing remains unexamined.
D: Distilled: True knowledge is not a stock of answers but the method of examining questions thoroughly from all sides.
Notes: Wu you zhi hu zai, wu zhi ye — 'do I have knowledge? I have none.' Another denial of innate wisdom (see 7.19, 7.27). His method: kou qi liang duan er jie — 'tap both ends and exhaust it.' Knowledge is not a possession but a process of thorough examination. Connects to FUALL's Reality pillar: wisdom is a method of inquiry, not a body of facts. Parallels the Socratic method.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The FANG bird does not come; the river sends forth no map:-- it is all over with me!'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "The phoenix does not come. The river sends forth no chart. It is all over for me."
D: Elegant: No phoenix. No river chart. The signs of Heaven's favor have ceased.
D: Distilled: When the cosmic signs of renewal stop appearing, even the sage must face the possibility that the age of restoration has passed.
Notes: Feng niao bu zhi, he bu chu tu — the Feng (phoenix) traditionally appeared at the start of a virtuous dynasty. The He Tu (river chart) was a mystical diagram supposedly carried by a dragon-horse from the Yellow River to Fu Xi, founder of civilization. Their absence = Heaven has withdrawn its mandate for cultural renewal. Confucius's deepest despair. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: the feeling that the transmission may be permanently broken.

D: Original: When the Master saw a person in a mourning dress, or any one with the cap and upper and lower garments of full dress, or a blind person, on observing them approaching, though they were younger than himself, he would rise up, and if he had to pass by them, he would do so hastily.
D: Refashioned: When the Master saw someone in mourning clothes, in ceremonial dress, or who was blind, he would rise in acknowledgment even if they were younger than him. If he had to pass them, he would do so quickly, with visible deference.
D: Elegant: Mourners, officials, the blind — he rose for all of them, even the young. He passed them quickly, with deference.
D: Distilled: Genuine respect is shown through automatic, unselfconscious acts of deference toward those in grief, authority, or vulnerability.
Notes: Three categories requiring automatic deference: the mourning (grief), the formally dressed (authority/duty), and the blind (vulnerability). Confucius's response is physical and immediate — not calculated but reflexive. Li (propriety) internalized to the point of bodily habit. Connects to FUALL's Temple concept: ritual becomes virtue when it becomes reflex.

D: Original:
Yen Yuan, in admiration of the Master's doctrines, sighed and said, 'I looked up to them, and they seemed to become more high; I tried to penetrate them, and they seemed to become more firm; I looked at them before me, and suddenly they seemed to be behind.
'The Master, by orderly method, skilfully leads men on. He enlarged my mind with learning, and taught me the restraints of propriety.
'When I wish to give over the study of his doctrines, I cannot do so, and having exerted all my ability, there seems something to stand right up before me; but though I wish to follow and lay hold of it, I really find no way to do so.'
D: Refashioned:
