Yan Yuan, in admiration of the Master's teaching, sighed and said, "The more I look up at it, the higher it appears. The harder I try to penetrate it, the more solid it becomes. I see it before me, and suddenly it is behind me.
"The Master skillfully leads me on, step by step. He broadens my mind with learning and restrains me with propriety.
"Even when I want to stop, I cannot. I have exhausted all my ability, and still something stands before me, beckoning — but I cannot reach it."
D: Elegant: I look up — it rises. I try to grasp it — it hardens. It's ahead, then behind. He leads me on with learning and restraint, and even when I'm spent, it stands there — just out of reach. I cannot stop.
D: Distilled: The greatest teaching creates a pursuit that cannot be abandoned — a vision of truth that stays always slightly beyond your grasp, compelling you to keep reaching.
Notes: Yan Hui's tribute to Confucius's teaching method: bo wo yi wen, yue wo yi li — 'broadened me with culture, restrained me with Li.' The experience described is of asymptotic approach: you get closer but never arrive. This is the phenomenology of genuine learning — the horizon recedes as you advance. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom concept: true understanding is not a destination but an inexhaustible pursuit. Parallels Plato's account of the ascent toward the Form of the Good.

D: Original:
The Master being very ill, Tsze-lu wished the disciples to act as ministers to him.
During a remission of his illness, he said, 'Long has the conduct of Yu been deceitful! By pretending to have ministers when I have them not, whom should I impose upon? Should I impose upon Heaven?
'Moreover, than that I should die in the hands of ministers, is it not better that I should die in the hands of you, my disciples? And though I may not get a great burial, shall I die upon the road?'
D: Refashioned:
When the Master was very ill, Zilu arranged for the disciples to act as ministers at his funeral, as though he were a nobleman.
During a remission, the Master said, "How long has Zilu been deceiving people! By pretending I have ministers when I do not, whom am I fooling? Heaven?
"And is it not better that I die attended by you, my students, than by pretend ministers? Even without a state funeral, will I die unattended by the roadside?"
D: Elegant: Zilu arranged false ministers for a nobleman's funeral. The Master rebuked him: 'Whom are we deceiving — Heaven? I'd rather die in your hands, honestly, than in the hands of pretend officials.'
D: Distilled: Honest simplicity in death is preferable to fraudulent grandeur — pretending to be more than you are deceives no one who matters.
Notes: Zilu's well-intentioned deception: arranging the funeral protocols of a high official for someone who held no office. Confucius rejects it as a violation of Li (propriety) — you cannot claim rituals above your actual station. Qi qi tian hu — 'would I deceive Heaven?' Connects to FUALL: institutional forms must match institutional reality. Claiming unearned status is a form of the erosion pattern.

D: Original: Tsze-kung said, 'There is a beautiful gem here. Should I lay it up in a case and keep it? or should I seek for a good price and sell it?' The Master said, 'Sell it! Sell it! But I would wait for one to offer the price.'
D: Refashioned: Zigong said, "Here is a beautiful gem. Should I store it in a case and keep it? Or seek a good price and sell it?" The Master said, "Sell it! Sell it! But I would wait for the right buyer."
D: Elegant: A beautiful gem — hide it or sell it? Sell it, said the Master. But wait for the right buyer.
D: Distilled: Talent should not be hoarded but offered to the world — though only to those worthy of receiving it.
Notes: The 'gem' is Confucius's own talent and virtue, which has gone unrecognized by rulers. Gu zhi zai, gu zhi zai, wo dai gu zhe ye — 'Sell it! Sell it! But I wait for a buyer.' Confucius wants to serve but will not debase himself by serving just anyone. Connects to FUALL: the intellectual's dilemma — seeking to contribute to public life while maintaining standards about whom to serve.

D: Original:
The Master was wishing to go and live among the nine wild tribes of the east.
Some one said, 'They are rude. How can you do such a thing?' The Master said, 'If a superior man dwelt among them, what rudeness would there be?'
D: Refashioned: The Master expressed a wish to go live among the nine barbarian tribes of the East. Someone said, "They are uncultured. How could you bear it?" The Master said, "If a Junzi dwelt among them, what lack of culture would there be?"
D: Elegant: They are uncultured, they said. If a Junzi lived there, he replied, what would be uncultured?
D: Distilled: Culture is not a fixed property of a place but something a cultivated person carries with them and transmits wherever they go.
Notes: Jiu Yi — the nine barbarian tribes of the east. Junzi ju zhi, he lou zhi you — 'if the Junzi dwelt there, what rudeness would remain?' The claim: civilization is portable. It resides in persons, not in geography. A single cultivated person can transform a community. Connects directly to FUALL's transmission concept: civilization is carried by individuals, not by institutions alone.

D: Original: The Master said, 'I returned from Wei to Lu, and then the music was reformed, and the pieces in the Royal songs and Praise songs all found their proper places.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "After I returned from Wei to Lu, the music was set in order — the court songs and the hymns were each restored to their proper place."
D: Elegant: He returned from Wei to Lu, and the music was set right — every song restored to its proper place.
D: Distilled: Restoring cultural order begins with restoring the proper arrangement of inherited works — returning each element to its rightful place.
Notes: Confucius's editorial work on the Shi Jing (Book of Songs) — traditionally he is credited with selecting and organizing the 305 odes into their final canonical form. Ya (court songs) and Song (hymns of praise) — two of the four sections of the Shi Jing. The act of restoration is itself a form of transmission: not creating new works but ensuring the existing ones are properly preserved and arranged. Connects to FUALL: transmission includes curation — deciding what to preserve and how to organize it.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Abroad, to serve the high ministers and nobles; at home, to serve one's father and elder brothers; in all duties to the dead, not to dare not to exert one's self; and not to be overcome of wine:-- which one of these things do I attain to?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "In public, to serve one's superiors. At home, to serve one's parents and elders. In duties to the dead, to give one's utmost. And to not be overcome by wine. Which of these do I actually manage?"
D: Elegant: Serve your superiors, your parents, the dead — and hold your drink. Which of these, he asked, do I actually manage?
D: Distilled: The seemingly simple duties of public service, family devotion, honoring the dead, and personal discipline are harder to maintain than anyone admits.
Notes: Four mundane duties presented as ongoing challenges even for the Master. The final item — 'not being overcome by wine' — is deliberately deflating, moving from cosmic duty to personal discipline. Confucius's characteristic self-deprecation: even basic virtues are hard to sustain consistently. Connects to FUALL: moral life is tested in the daily and ordinary, not just in crisis moments.

D: Original: The Master standing by a stream, said, 'It passes on just like this, not ceasing day or night!'
D: Refashioned: The Master stood by a river and said, "Everything passes like this — flowing on, day and night, without ceasing."
D: Elegant: By the river: 'It flows on like this — never stopping, day or night.'
D: Distilled: Time flows without ceasing, and this relentless passage is the condition against which all human effort takes place.
Notes: Shi zhe ru si fu, bu she zhou ye — 'it passes on like this, not ceasing day or night.' One of the most famous lines in Chinese literature. The river as metaphor for the passage of time and the impermanence of all things. Connects to FUALL: the urgency of transmission — if wisdom is not actively passed on, the river of time carries it away.

D: Original: The Master said, 'I have not seen one who loves virtue as he loves beauty.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "I have never met anyone who loves virtue as much as they love beauty."
D: Elegant: I have yet to see someone who loves virtue as passionately as they love beauty.
D: Distilled: Humanity's instinctive devotion to beauty far exceeds its devotion to virtue — and that gap is the central moral challenge.
Notes: Wu wei jian hao de ru hao se zhe ye — 'I have not seen one who loves virtue (De) as they love beauty (Se).' The comparison is not between virtue and vice but between virtue and aesthetics — the natural human attraction to beauty is stronger than the cultivated attraction to goodness. Connects to FUALL: the Temple and Academy must make virtue as compelling as beauty — the challenge of moral education.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The prosecution of learning may be compared to what may happen in raising a mound. If there want but one basket of earth to complete the work, and I stop, the stopping is my own work. It may be compared to throwing down the earth on the level ground. Though but one basketful is thrown at a time, the advancing with it is my own going forward.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Learning is like building a mound of earth. If you stop when only one more basket would complete it, the stopping is your own choice. It is also like leveling ground — even if only one basket of earth has been thrown down, the advance is your own doing."
D: Elegant: One basket short of finishing the mound — and you stop. That's your choice. One basket laid on flat ground — and you've begun. That's also your choice.
D: Distilled: Whether you advance or give up is always your own decision — and the difference between completion and failure can be a single step.
Notes: Two metaphors for human agency in learning: (1) stopping one step short of completion = your failure, not fate's; (2) taking a single step forward = your achievement. The responsibility is entirely the learner's. Jin, wu wang ye; zhi, wu zhi ye — 'advancing is my own going forward; stopping is my own stopping.' Connects to FUALL: agency and responsibility for one's own intellectual and moral development.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Never flagging when I set forth anything to him;-- ah! that is Hui.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "The one who never flagged when I set forth anything to him — that was Yan Hui."
D: Elegant: Hui never flagged. Whatever I taught, he received it fully.
D: Distilled: The ideal student's defining quality is inexhaustible receptivity — never tiring of what is being offered.
Notes: Yu zhi bu dai — 'spoken to without flagging/wearying.' Yan Hui's quality: tireless receptivity. Not brilliance but endurance in learning. Connects to FUALL: the transmission chain depends on students who do not tire of receiving.

D: Original: The Master said of Yen Yuan, 'Alas! I saw his constant advance. I never saw him stop in his progress.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said of Yan Yuan, "How sad! I watched him advance constantly. I never once saw him stop."
D: Elegant: I saw him advance without ceasing. I never saw him stop. And then he was gone.
D: Distilled: The tragedy of Yan Hui's death was the loss of someone who represented the ideal of unceasing moral progress.
Notes: Wu jian qi jin ye, wei jian qi zhi ye — 'I saw his advancing, never saw him stop.' Yan Hui's death (481 BCE) was one of the great sorrows of Confucius's life (see 11.8-10). The praise is for constant forward motion — the embodied ideal of lifelong learning. Connects to FUALL: the transmission tragedy — when the best student dies, the chain is weakened irreparably.

D: Original: The Master said, 'There are cases in which the blade springs, but the plant does not go on to flower! There are cases where it flowers, but no fruit is subsequently produced!'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "There are seedlings that sprout but never flower. There are plants that flower but never bear fruit."
D: Elegant: Some sprout but never bloom. Some bloom but never fruit. Promise fulfilled is rarer than promise shown.
D: Distilled: Beginning well is common; following through to completion is rare — most potential goes unrealized.
Notes: Miao er bu xiu, xiu er bu shi — 'sprouts but doesn't flower; flowers but doesn't fruit.' The agricultural metaphor for unfulfilled human potential. Likely spoken with Yan Hui in mind — who showed every promise but died before full fruition. Connects to FUALL: the gap between potential and realization is one of the permanent tragedies of the human condition.

D: Original: The Master said, 'A youth is to be regarded with respect. How do we know that his future will not be equal to our present? If he reach the age of forty or fifty, and has not made himself heard of, then indeed he will not be worth being regarded with respect.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "A young person deserves respect. How do we know they will not surpass us? But if they reach forty or fifty and have still not made their mark, then they no longer deserve that particular form of respect."
D: Elegant: Respect the young — they may surpass you. But if they reach fifty and have made nothing of themselves, respect them for something else.
D: Distilled: Youth deserves the respect of its potential, but potential that goes unrealized past middle age can no longer trade on future promise.
Notes: Hou sheng ke wei — 'the rising generation is to be respected.' Yan zhi qi lai zhe zhi bu ru jin ye — 'how do we know the future will not be equal to the present?' But potential has an expiration date: by forty or fifty, one must have actualized something. Connects to FUALL: the intergenerational transmission depends on the young actually developing their potential, not merely possessing it.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Can men refuse to assent to the words of strict admonition? But it is reforming the conduct because of them which is valuable. Can men refuse to be pleased with words of gentle advice? But it is unfolding their aim which is valuable. If a man be pleased with these words, but does not unfold their aim, and assents to those, but does not reform his conduct, I can really do nothing with him.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "An army of a great state can lose its commander. But even a common person cannot be stripped of their will."
D: Elegant: You can steal a general from an army. You cannot steal the will from even the humblest person.
D: Distilled: External power can be taken by force, but a person's inner resolve is beyond anyone's reach.
Notes: San jun ke duo shuai ye, pi fu bu ke duo zhi ye — 'the three armies can lose their commander; an ordinary person cannot be robbed of their will.' The military metaphor: physical force can overcome physical defense, but inner determination is in a different category entirely. Connects to FUALL's four power domains: the Ideational domain (will, conviction) is the most inviolable — it cannot be conquered from outside.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Hold faithfulness and sincerity as first principles. Have no friends not equal to yourself. When you have faults, do not fear to abandon them.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Make faithfulness and sincerity your first principles. Do not befriend those who pull you downward. When you have faults, do not hesitate to correct them."
D: Elegant: First principles: faithfulness and sincerity. Keep worthy company. Fix your faults without delay.
D: Distilled: The foundations of character are faithfulness, good company, and prompt self-correction.
Notes: Near-verbatim repetition of 1.8. Zhu zhong xin, wu you bu ru ji zhe, guo ze wu dan gai — faithfulness (Xin) and sincerity (Zhong) as foundational; quality of companionship; and self-correction. The repetition itself is significant — these are principles Confucius returned to throughout his life. Connects to FUALL: core principles are worth restating because they are worth remembering.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The commander of the forces of a large state may be carried off, but the will of even a common man cannot be taken from him.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "An army of a great state can lose its commander. But even a common person cannot be stripped of their will."
D: Elegant: You can steal a general from an army. You cannot steal the will from even the humblest person.
D: Distilled: External power can be taken by force, but a person's inner resolve is beyond anyone's reach.
Notes: San jun ke duo shuai ye, pi fu bu ke duo zhi ye — 'the three armies can lose their commander; an ordinary person cannot be robbed of their will.' The military metaphor: physical force can overcome physical defense, but inner determination is in a different category entirely. Connects to FUALL's four power domains: the Ideational domain (will, conviction) is the most inviolable — it cannot be conquered from outside.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'Dressed himself in a tattered robe quilted with hemp, yet standing by the side of men dressed in furs, and not ashamed;-- ah! it is Yu who is equal to this!
'"He dislikes none, he covets nothing;-- what can he do but what is good!"'
Tsze-lu kept continually repeating these words of the ode, when the Master said, 'Those things are by no means sufficient to constitute (perfect) excellence.'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, "Wearing a worn, patched robe yet standing next to people in fine furs without shame — that is You, Zilu."
The Odes say, 'He envies nothing, he covets nothing — how can he fail to do good?'
Zilu kept repeating this verse proudly. The Master said, "That alone is not enough to constitute excellence."
D: Elegant: Zilu wore rags beside fur-clad men without flinching. Impressive — but not sufficient. Freedom from envy is not the same as positive virtue.
D: Distilled: Not being ashamed or envious is admirable but insufficient — true excellence requires active cultivation, not merely the absence of vice.
Notes: Zilu's character: courageous, unpretentious, free from envy — but Confucius pushes further. Bu qi bu qiu, he yong bu zang — 'neither jealous nor covetous, how can he not be good?' (Ode 33). Confucius warns against resting in a negative achievement. Connects to FUALL: the distinction between negative virtue (absence of vice) and positive virtue (active cultivation of good) — see also 1.15.

D: Original: The Master said, 'When the year becomes cold, then we know how the pine and the cypress are the last to lose their leaves.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "Only when the year turns cold do we see that the pine and cypress are the last to lose their leaves."
D: Elegant: When winter comes, the pine and cypress are the last standing. Character is tested by cold.
D: Distilled: True character reveals itself only under adversity — the equivalent of seeing which trees hold their leaves when winter arrives.
Notes: Sui han ran hou zhi song bai zhi hou diao ye — one of the most famous Confucian metaphors. The pine and cypress (evergreens in China) represent moral constancy under duress. The 'cold year' = political disorder, personal crisis, social pressure. Character is invisible in good times; only hardship reveals who holds firm. Connects to FUALL: the erosion pattern is itself the test — those who maintain their principles during civilizational decline are the pines and cypresses.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The wise are free from perplexities; the virtuous from anxiety; and the bold from fear.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "The wise are free from perplexity. The virtuous are free from anxiety. The courageous are free from fear."
D: Elegant: Wisdom dispels confusion. Virtue dispels worry. Courage dispels fear.
D: Distilled: The three liberations are from confusion through wisdom, from anxiety through virtue, and from fear through courage.
Notes: Zhi zhe bu huo, ren zhe bu you, yong zhe bu ju — three virtues paired with three freedoms. Zhi (wisdom), Ren (benevolence), Yong (courage) = the Three Virtues of Confucianism. Each conquers a specific weakness. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom framework: Reality (overcomes confusion), Right (overcomes anxiety), Reason (provides the courage to act).

D: Original: The Master said, 'There are some with whom we may study in common, but we shall find them unable to go along with us to principles. Perhaps we may go on with them to principles, but we shall find them unable to get established in those along with us. Or if we may get so established along with them, we shall find them unable to weigh occurring events along with us.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, "There are those with whom we may study together but who cannot follow us to principles. There are those who can reach principles with us but who cannot stand firm in them with us. And there are those who can stand firm with us but who cannot exercise judgment alongside us in difficult cases."
D: Elegant: Some can study but not reach principles. Some reach principles but cannot hold firm. Some hold firm but cannot judge. The full path has many falling-off points.
D: Distilled: The path from study to principle to firmness to judgment is long, and at each stage, companions fall away — few complete the full journey.
Notes: Four ascending levels of intellectual and moral companionship: (1) studying together, (2) reaching principles, (3) standing firm in principles, (4) exercising judgment in difficult cases (quan — 'weighing'). Each stage is harder than the last, and each loses more people. Connects to FUALL: the progression from Academy (study) to Wisdom (judgment) loses participants at every stage.

D: Original:
How the flowers of the aspen-plum flutter and turn! Do I not think of you? But your house is distant.
The Master said, 'It is the want of thought about it. How is it distant?'
D: Refashioned:
"The flowers of the plum tree flutter and turn! Do I not think of you? But your home is so far away."
The Master said, "He did not truly think of her. If he did, how could the distance matter?"
D: Elegant: He says he thinks of her but complains of the distance. If he truly thought of her, distance would be nothing.
D: Distilled: When desire is genuine, obstacles become irrelevant — the person who complains about the distance does not truly want to arrive.
Notes: A poem from the Book of Songs about longing. Confucius applies it philosophically: genuine desire overcomes all obstacles. Wei zhi si ye, fu he yuan zhi you — 'it is the lack of thinking; how could it be far?' The same principle as 7.29 (Is virtue far? I desire it, and it is here). Connects to FUALL: the most common excuse for not pursuing wisdom is that it is difficult or distant — but genuine desire closes any gap.

D: Original: Book 10: Heang Tang (In His Village)

Confucius, in his village, looked simple and sincere, and as if he were not able to speak.
When he was in the prince's ancestorial temple, or in the court, he spoke minutely on every point, but cautiously.
D: Refashioned:
In his own village, Confucius appeared simple and sincere, almost as if he could not speak at all.
But in the ancestral temple or at court, he spoke precisely on every point, though always with appropriate caution.
D: Elegant: At home: quiet, almost mute. At court: precise, measured, careful. He adapted his speech to the setting.
D: Distilled: The wise person calibrates their speech to the setting — reserved in casual company, precise where precision matters.
Notes: Opens Book 10 — a unique book consisting entirely of descriptions of Confucius's daily conduct, dress, and habits. Xiang dang (in his village) vs. zong miao chao ting (ancestral temple/court). The contrast: Confucius was not always formal. His behavior adapted to context. Li (propriety) is not rigidity but sensitivity to setting. Connects to FUALL: Book 10 as a whole shows that the Junzi ideal is lived in daily details, not just grand principles.

D: Original:
When he was waiting at court, in speaking with the great officers of the lower grade, he spake freely, but in a straightforward manner; in speaking with those of the higher grade, he did so blandly, but precisely.
When the ruler was present, his manner displayed respectful uneasiness; it was grave, but self-possessed.
D: Refashioned:
When waiting at court, in conversation with lower-ranking officials he spoke freely and directly. With higher-ranking officials, he was more careful but precise.
When the ruler was present, he showed respectful attentiveness — serious, but composed.
D: Elegant: With subordinates: open. With superiors: careful. With the ruler: reverent but composed. Each rank received its proper regard.
D: Distilled: Propriety means adjusting your manner to the rank and role of the person you address — neither obsequious nor casual, but calibrated.
Notes: Three levels of court behavior: xia da fu (lower officers) — free and direct; shang da fu (upper officers) — blandly precise; jun (ruler) — respectfully uneasy but self-possessed. Li (propriety) as relational calibration. Connects to FUALL: social institutions function through appropriate gradations of formality.

D: Original:
When the prince called him to employ him in the reception of a visitor, his countenance appeared to change, and his legs to move forward with difficulty.
He inclined himself to the other officers among whom he stood, moving his left or right arm, as their position required, but keeping the skirts of his robe before and behind evenly adjusted.
He hastened forward, with his arms like the wings of a bird.
When the guest had retired, he would report to the prince, 'The visitor is not turning round any more.'
D: Refashioned:
When the prince called on Confucius to receive a visitor, his face would change and he would move forward with deliberate, careful steps.
He would incline himself to the other officers, adjusting his position left or right as protocol required, keeping his robes precisely arranged.
As he hastened forward, his arms spread like a bird's wings.
When the guest departed, he would report to the prince, "The visitor has left and is not turning back."
D: Elegant: Receiving a state guest: his face changed, his steps became measured, his robes precise. He moved like a bird in flight and reported the guest's departure to the last detail.
D: Distilled: Diplomatic protocol demands total physical and mental presence — every gesture, step, and word must be precisely calibrated.
Notes: Detailed description of Confucius's conduct during formal reception of state guests. The physical details — changing countenance, careful steps, bird-like arm movement, precise reporting — show Li (propriety) expressed through the body. Bin (guest reception) was one of the Five Rites. Connects to FUALL: ritual conduct is embodied knowledge, not abstract principle.

D: Original:
When he entered the palace gate, he seemed to bend his body, as if it were not sufficient to admit him.
When he was standing, he did not occupy the middle of the gate-way; when he passed in or out, he did not tread upon the threshold.
When he was passing the vacant place of the prince, his countenance appeared to change, and his legs to bend under him, and his words came as if he hardly had breath to utter them.
He ascended the reception hall, holding up his robe with both his hands, and his body bent; holding in his breath also, as if he dared not breathe.
When he came out from the audience, as soon as he had descended one step, he began to relax his countenance, and had a satisfied look. When he had got to the bottom of the steps, he advanced rapidly to his place, with his arms like wings, and on occupying it, his manner still showed respectful uneasiness.
D: Refashioned:
When he entered the palace gate, he would bend his body as if the gate were too small.
He did not stand in the center of the gateway or step on the threshold.
Passing the ruler's empty seat, his face would change, his steps slow, and he would speak as if barely able to draw breath.
Ascending the reception hall, he held up his robe with both hands, his body bent, holding his breath as if he dared not breathe.
On leaving, as soon as he descended one step, his face relaxed and he quickened his pace, returning to his place with his arms like wings, his manner still showing respectful care.
D: Elegant: He bent at the gate, stilled at the threshold, held his breath ascending, and only relaxed one step down. Every movement was a sentence of respect.
D: Distilled: Physical conduct in sacred and official spaces should express reverence through every gesture — entering, ascending, departing, and returning to one's place.
Notes: Micro-choreography of court behavior. The bodily language of respect: making oneself small at the gate, avoiding the center (reserved for the ruler), holding breath in the presence chamber. The progressive relaxation upon leaving shows that the tension was genuine, not performed. Connects to FUALL: Li as embodied practice — propriety is not rules memorized but dispositions trained into the body.

D: Original:
When he was carrying the scepter of his ruler, he seemed to bend his body, as if he were not able to bear its weight. He did not hold it higher than the position of the hands in making a bow, nor lower than their position in giving anything to another. His countenance seemed to change, and look apprehensive, and he dragged his feet along as if they were held by something to the ground.
In presenting the presents with which he was charged, he wore a placid appearance.
At his private audience, he looked highly pleased.
D: Refashioned:
When carrying the ruler's jade scepter, he would bend as if unable to bear its weight. He held it no higher than a bow and no lower than the position of offering a gift. His face appeared apprehensive, and he moved his feet as if dragged along the ground.
When presenting diplomatic gifts, he wore a placid expression.
At his private audience, he appeared delighted.
D: Elegant: With the scepter: bent, apprehensive, shuffling. Presenting gifts: serene. In private audience: delighted. Each moment received its proper emotion.
D: Distilled: Carrying the symbols of authority demands visible gravity; fulfilling official duties demands composure; and private interaction permits genuine warmth.
Notes: The jade scepter (gui) — a ritual object symbolizing the ruler's authority. Confucius's bodily response to carrying it — bending, shuffling, appearing burdened — is not physical weakness but ritual performance of the weight of delegated authority. The three settings (carrying authority, official duty, private audience) each have their own appropriate emotional register. Connects to FUALL: the symbols of authority should be treated as heavy, not light.

D: Original:
The superior man did not use a deep purple, or a puce colour, in the ornaments of his dress.
Even in his undress, he did not wear anything of a red or reddish colour.
In warm weather, he had a single garment either of coarse or fine texture, but he wore it displayed over an inner garment.
Over lamb's fur he wore a garment of black; over fawn's fur one of white; and over fox's fur one of yellow.
The fur robe of his undress was long, with the right sleeve short.
He required his sleeping dress to be half as long again as his body.
When staying at home, he used thick furs of the fox or the badger.
When he put off mourning, he wore all the appendages of the girdle.
His under-garment, except when it was required to be of the curtain shape, was made of silk cut narrow above and wide below.
He did not wear lamb's fur or a black cap, on a visit of condolence.
On the first day of the month he put on his court robes, and presented himself at court.
D: Refashioned:
The Junzi did not use deep purple or maroon for dress ornaments, nor wear red or reddish colors even casually.
In warm weather, he wore a single garment of coarse or fine texture over an inner layer.
Black over lambskin, white over fawnskin, yellow over foxskin.
His casual fur robe was long, with a shorter right sleeve.
His sleeping garment was half again as long as his body.
At home, he used thick fox or badger fur.
After mourning, he wore all his normal accessories.
Except for formal curtain-shaped garments, his robes were cut narrow above and wide below.
He did not wear lambskin or a black cap to visit the bereaved.
On the first day of each month, he put on his court robes and presented himself at court.
D: Elegant: Every garment had its rule: the right fabric for the right fur, the right color for the right occasion, the right cut for the right context. Dress was not vanity — it was discipline made visible.
D: Distilled: Attention to dress is not superficiality but a form of moral discipline — what you wear reflects your respect for the occasion, the company, and yourself.
Notes: Extensive description of Confucius's dress codes. The principles: no casual use of formal colors (purple, maroon = expensive dyes reserved for ceremony); appropriate matching of outer garments to inner furs; specific adjustments for mourning, court, and home. Li (propriety) extended to the body's covering. Connects to FUALL: institutional life requires visible markers of role and occasion — dress as a technology of social legibility.

D: Original:
When fasting, he thought it necessary to have his clothes brightly clean and made of linen cloth.
When fasting, he thought it necessary to change his food, and also to change the place where he commonly sat in the apartment.
D: Refashioned:
When fasting, he wore bright, clean clothes made of linen.
When fasting, he changed his diet and moved to a different part of his dwelling.
D: Elegant: Fasting meant clean linen, different food, a different seat. The whole environment shifted to match the inner purpose.
D: Distilled: Spiritual preparation requires physical changes — different clothes, different food, different surroundings signal a different state of mind.
Notes: Zhai (fasting/purification before sacrifice). The changes are environmental: clean clothes, altered diet, relocated seating. The principle: inner preparation is supported by outer changes. The body and its environment are recruited to reinforce the mental/spiritual shift. Connects to FUALL's Temple institution: ritual preparation involves the whole person, not just the mind.

D: Original:
He did not dislike to have his rice finely cleaned, nor to have his minced meat cut quite small.
He did not eat rice which had been injured by heat or damp and turned sour, nor fish or flesh which was gone. He did not eat what was discoloured, or what was of a bad flavour, nor anything which was ill-cooked, or was not in season.
He did not eat meat which was not cut properly, nor what was served without its proper sauce.
Though there might be a large quantity of meat, he would not allow what he took to exceed the due proportion for the rice. It was only in wine that he laid down no limit for himself, but he did not allow himself to be confused by it.
He did not partake of wine and dried meat bought in the market.
He was never without ginger when he ate.
He did not eat much.
When he had been assisting at the prince's sacrifice, he did not keep the flesh which he received overnight. The flesh of his family sacrifice he did not keep over three days. If kept over three days, people could not eat it.
When eating, he did not converse. When in bed, he did not speak.
Although his food might be coarse rice and vegetable soup, he would offer a little of it in sacrifice with a grave, respectful air.
D: Refashioned:
He did not object to finely cleaned rice or finely cut meat.
He did not eat rice that had gone sour, fish or meat that had spoiled, food that was discolored or smelled bad, food that was poorly cooked, or food that was out of season.
He did not eat meat that was improperly cut or served without the right sauce.
He kept meat in proportion to rice, never letting it dominate.
In wine alone, he set no fixed limit — but he never allowed it to muddle his judgment.
He did not consume wine or dried meat bought from the market.
He always had ginger with his meals.
He did not eat to excess.
Sacrificial meat from the prince's ceremony was not kept overnight. Family sacrificial meat was not kept more than three days.
While eating, he did not converse. In bed, he did not speak.
Even if the food was only coarse rice and vegetable broth, he would offer a portion in sacrifice with a grave and respectful manner.
D: Elegant: Everything about food had its rule: freshness, proportion, timing, sauce, season. He ate with care, sacrificed with gravity, and never let wine cloud his mind. Even coarse rice received a moment of reverence.
D: Distilled: Mindful eating — attention to quality, proportion, timing, and ritual offering — is a daily practice of discipline and gratitude.
Notes: The most detailed passage on Confucius's eating habits. Key principles: quality (no spoiled food), proportion (meat subordinate to grain), temperance (wine but not excess), ritual (offering a portion in sacrifice even from humble meals), silence (eating and sleeping are not times for conversation). Shi bu yan, qin bu yu — 'while eating do not converse, while in bed do not speak.' The passage shows Li extended to the most mundane activities. Connects to FUALL: discipline in daily habits is the foundation of discipline in larger matters.

D: Original: If his mat was not straight, he did not sit on it.
D: Refashioned: If his mat was not straight, he did not sit on it.
D: Elegant: If the mat was crooked, he did not sit.
D: Distilled: Even the smallest disorder in your environment is worth correcting before you proceed — attention to detail is a moral habit.
Notes: Xi bu zheng, bu zuo — one of the shortest and most famous Confucian sayings. The crooked mat is a metaphor for any small disorder that should be corrected rather than tolerated. Li begins with attending to what is immediately in front of you. Connects to FUALL: civilizational order begins with personal order — macro disorder is the accumulation of tolerated micro disorders.

D: Original:
When the villagers were drinking together, on those who carried staffs going out, he went out immediately after.
When the villagers were going through their ceremonies to drive away pestilential influences, he put on his court robes and stood on the eastern steps.
D: Refashioned:
When villagers gathered for a drinking ceremony, he waited for the elders with walking staffs to leave first, then followed.
When the villagers performed their ritual to drive away evil influences, he put on his court robes and stood on the eastern steps of his house.
D: Elegant: At village gatherings, he let the elders leave first. During the exorcism rites, he stood formally on his steps in court dress. Propriety, even at home.
D: Distilled: Respect for elders and reverence during communal rituals should be maintained even in informal village settings.
Notes: Xiang ren yin jiu — village drinking ceremony. Nuo — the communal exorcism ritual to drive away pestilence (performed at year's end). Confucius stands in court robes during the Nuo — honoring the spirits of his ancestors who might be disturbed by the exorcism. Li maintained in the village context, not just at court. Connects to FUALL: ritual obligation extends to community life, not just institutional settings.

D: Original:
When he was sending complimentary inquiries to any one in another State, he bowed twice as he escorted the messenger away.
Chi K'ang having sent him a present of physic, he bowed and received it, saying, 'I do not know it. I dare not taste it.'
D: Refashioned:
When sending greetings to someone in another state, he bowed twice as he sent the messenger off.
When Ji Kang sent him medicine, he bowed and accepted it, saying, "I do not know this medicine. I dare not taste it yet."
D: Elegant: He bowed twice when sending a messenger abroad. Receiving medicine: 'I don't know it. I dare not taste it.' Caution even in accepting gifts.
D: Distilled: Courtesy in sending messages and caution in accepting unfamiliar gifts both reflect the same underlying principle: thoughtful attentiveness to every exchange.
Notes: Two vignettes of Li in interpersonal dealings. The double bow to the departing messenger = respect for the person being contacted. The caution about unknown medicine = honest acknowledgment of ignorance rather than polite consumption of something potentially harmful. Connects to FUALL: integrity includes admitting what you don't know, even when social pressure favors pretending otherwise.

D: Original: The stable being burned down, when he was at court, on his return he said, 'Has any man been hurt?' He did not ask about the horses.
D: Refashioned: When the stable burned down while he was at court, the Master returned and asked, "Was anyone hurt?" He did not ask about the horses.
D: Elegant: The stable burned. He asked about the people. Not the horses.
D: Distilled: In any crisis, the first question should always be about human welfare, not property.
Notes: Shang ren hu, bu wen ma — 'hurt anyone? Did not ask about the horses.' One of the most frequently cited examples of Confucius's Ren (benevolence). The instinctive prioritization of human life over property. In a society where horses were extremely valuable, this was a notable moral choice. Connects to FUALL: the hierarchy of value — human beings above property, always.

D: Original:
When the prince sent him a gift of cooked meat, he would adjust his mat, first taste it, and then give it away to others. When the prince sent him a gift of undressed meat, he would have it cooked, and offer it to the spirits of his ancestors. When the prince sent him a gift of a living animal, he would keep it alive.
When he was in attendance on the prince and joining in the entertainment, the prince only sacrificed. He first tasted everything.
When he was ill and the prince came to visit him, he had his head to the east, made his court robes be spread over him, and drew his girdle across them.
When the prince's order called him, without waiting for his carriage to be yoked, he went at once.
D: Refashioned:
When the prince sent him cooked meat, he would adjust his mat, taste it first, and share it with others. When the prince sent raw meat, he would cook it and offer it to his ancestors. When the prince sent a live animal, he would keep it alive.
When attending the prince at a meal, while the prince made his offering, Confucius would taste everything first.
When ill and visited by the prince, he would face east, have his court robes draped over him, and draw his sash across.
When summoned by the prince, he would set out immediately without waiting for his carriage to be readied.
D: Elegant: Cooked meat from the prince: taste and share. Raw meat: cook and sacrifice. A live animal: keep it alive. A summons: go immediately, on foot if necessary.
D: Distilled: Every type of gift from authority requires its own proper response, and every summons requires immediate obedience — the form of the response is itself a communication.
Notes: Detailed protocols for receiving gifts from the ruler and responding to his presence. Each type of gift has its own appropriate treatment: cooked meat (share), raw meat (ancestral offering), live animals (preserve life). The response to illness — facing east, court robes draped — maintains the fiction of readiness even when bedridden. The immediate departure without waiting for the carriage = treating the ruler's summons as superseding all personal convenience. Connects to FUALL: Li as relational protocol — every relationship has its proper forms of exchange.

D: Original: When he entered the ancestral temple of the State, he asked about everything.
D: Refashioned: When he entered the state's ancestral temple, he asked about everything.
D: Elegant: In the ancestral temple, he asked about everything — again.
D: Distilled: Asking is a form of reverence that should be practiced every time, not just the first time.
Notes: Repetition of 3.15, reinforcing the principle: ru tai miao, mei shi wen — entering the grand temple, asking about everything. The fact that this is repeated in Book 10 (daily conduct) shows it was a consistent practice, not a one-time event. Connects to FUALL: genuine inquiry is a permanent disposition, not a phase.

D: Original:
When any of his friends died, if he had no relations who could be depended on for the necessary offices, he would say, 'I will bury him.'
When a friend sent him a present, though it might be a carriage and horses, he did not bow.
The only present for which he bowed was that of the flesh of sacrifice.
D: Refashioned:
When a friend died who had no family to handle the burial, Confucius would say, "I will take care of the funeral."
When a friend sent a gift — even a carriage and horses — he would not bow.
The only gift for which he bowed was sacrificial meat.
D: Elegant: For a friendless dead, he said: I will bury him. For a gift of horses, he did not bow. For sacrificial meat alone, he bowed.
D: Distilled: The highest respect is reserved for what is sacred, not for what is expensive — and the deepest kindness is offered where no one else will provide it.
Notes: Three vignettes: (1) taking responsibility for a friend's burial when no family is available — the ultimate act of friendship; (2) not bowing for expensive gifts — material wealth does not command deference; (3) bowing for sacrificial meat — sacred offerings outrank material gifts. Connects to FUALL: the hierarchy of value — the sacred above the material, human obligation above convenience.

D: Original:
In bed, he did not lie like a corpse. At home, he did not put on any formal deportment.
When he saw any one in a mourning dress, though it might be an acquaintance, he would change countenance; when he saw any one wearing the cap of full dress, or a blind person, though he might be in his undress, he would salute them in a ceremonious manner.
To any person in mourning he bowed forward to the crossbar of his carriage; he bowed in the same way to any one bearing the tables of population.
When he was at an entertainment where there was an abundance of provisions set before him, he would change countenance and rise up.
On a sudden clap of thunder, or a violent wind, he would change countenance.
D: Refashioned:
In bed, he did not lie rigidly. At home, he did not maintain formal deportment.
When he saw someone in mourning — even a casual acquaintance — he would change his expression. Seeing someone in ceremonial dress or a blind person, even informally, he would show proper respect.
To anyone in mourning or carrying census records, he bowed to the crossbar of his carriage.
At a feast with abundant provisions, his expression would change and he would rise.
At a sudden clap of thunder or violent wind, his expression would change.
D: Elegant: In bed: relaxed. At home: informal. But at the sight of mourning, ceremony, blindness, thunder — his expression changed instantly. Respect and reverence were reflexive.
D: Distilled: True propriety operates reflexively — responding to grief, ceremony, vulnerability, and natural power with immediate, unstudied changes of demeanor.
Notes: Book 10's portrait of Confucius in daily life. The changes of expression (bian se) are involuntary — they show Li internalized to the level of reflex. The triggers: mourning (human grief), ceremony (social authority), blindness (vulnerability), abundant food (gratitude), thunder/wind (cosmic power). Each produces an appropriate emotional shift. Connects to FUALL: virtue is not a decision made each time but a trained disposition that responds automatically to the morally relevant features of each situation.

D: Original:
When he was about to mount his carriage, he would stand straight, holding the cord.
When he was in the carriage, he did not turn his head quite round, he did not talk hastily, he did not point with his hands.
D: Refashioned:
When about to mount his carriage, he would stand straight and hold the mounting cord.
In the carriage, he did not turn his head fully around, did not speak hastily, and did not point with his hands.
D: Elegant: Stand straight before mounting. In the carriage: no craning, no hasty speech, no pointing. Composure, even in transit.
D: Distilled: Dignity and self-control should be maintained even during routine travel — the cultivated person is never off-duty.
Notes: Li in transit. Bu nei gu (did not look behind), bu ji yan (did not speak hastily), bu qin zhi (did not point). Every setting has its proper deportment. The principle: there is no moment when propriety does not apply. Connects to FUALL: moral conduct is not situation-dependent — it is constant.

D: Original:
Seeing the countenance, it instantly rises. It flies round, and by and by settles.
The Master said, 'There is the hen-pheasant on the hill bridge. At its season! At its season!' Tsze-lu made a motion to it. Thrice it smelt him and then rose.
D: Refashioned:
A hen-pheasant was startled, flew up, circled, and settled again.
The Master said, "Look at that pheasant on the hill bridge — how perfectly timed! How perfectly timed!" Zilu made a gesture toward it. The pheasant looked at him three times and flew away.
D: Elegant: A pheasant rose, circled, and settled. 'How timely,' said the Master. Zilu reached for it. The bird looked at him three times — and flew.
D: Distilled: Natural things act with perfect timing and cannot be captured by grasping — wisdom, like the pheasant, must be approached with patience, not seized by force.
Notes: The closing passage of Book 10 — enigmatic and often debated. The pheasant as metaphor: it acts in its own season (shi zai), cannot be captured through clumsy approach, and departs when it senses aggression. The Master admires its natural timing; Zilu tries to grab it and fails. Connects to FUALL: wisdom, like the pheasant, must be patiently received, not forcefully seized. The Way operates in its own time.

D: Original: Book 11: Hsien Tsin (The Forerunners)

The Master said, 'The men of former times, in the matters of ceremonies and music were rustics, it is said, while the men of these latter times, in ceremonies and music, are accomplished gentlemen.
'If I have occasion to use those things, I follow the men of former times.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'In the old days, people who practiced ritual and music were considered rough and unpolished. Today, those who practice them are called refined gentlemen. If I had to choose, I would follow the old ways.'
D: Elegant: The ancients brought sincerity to ceremony; the moderns bring polish. Confucius chose sincerity.
D: Distilled: Authenticity in practice matters more than sophistication in performance.
Notes: Li (禮, ritual propriety) and Yue (樂, music) — the twin pillars of Confucian cultural education. Confucius consistently valued substance over surface. Connects to FUALL erosion pattern: as institutions age, form replaces function.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'Of those who were with me in Ch'an and Ts'ai, there are none to be found to enter my door.'
Distinguished for their virtuous principles and practice, there were Yen Yuan, Min Tsze-ch'ien, Zan Po-niu, and Chung-kung; for their ability in speech, Tsai Wo and Tsze-kung; for their adminis- trative talents, Zan Yu and Chi Lu; for their literary acquirements, Tsze-yu and Tsze-hsia.
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'None of the disciples who were with me in the hardships of Ch'en and Ts'ai still come to my door.' He distinguished them by their strengths: Yan Yuan, Min Ziqian, Ran Boniu, and Zhonggong for virtue; Zai Wo and Zigong for eloquence; Ran You and Zilu for administration; Ziyou and Zixia for scholarship.
D: Elegant: Confucius named his best students by what they were best at — virtue, speech, governance, learning — and mourned that the ones who had suffered alongside him no longer came.
D: Distilled: A teacher's proudest legacy is not what he taught but who endured the hard seasons with him.
Notes: Ch'en and Ts'ai — two states where Confucius and his followers were besieged and nearly starved (~489 BCE). The four categories of excellence (virtue, speech, governance, scholarship) map roughly to FUALL's educational institutions: Temple (virtue), Agora (speech), Academy/Guilds (governance), Academy (scholarship). Yan Yuan (Yan Hui) is Confucius's most beloved disciple.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Hui gives me no assistance. There is nothing that I say in which he does not delight.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Hui is no help to me at all. He delights in everything I say.'
D: Elegant: The student who agrees with everything teaches the teacher nothing.
D: Distilled: A disciple who never challenges you never sharpens you.
Notes: Yan Hui (Yan Yuan) — Confucius's favorite student, praised elsewhere for his virtue but here gently noted for his uncritical admiration. This passage anticipates Mill's argument in On Liberty Ch. 2: beliefs untested by opposition become dead dogma. Connects to Reason in the FUALL framework — wisdom requires dialectic, not just transmission.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Filial indeed is Min Tsze-ch'ien! Other people say nothing of him different from the report of his parents and brothers.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Min Ziqian is truly filial. No one can find any gap between what his parents and brothers say of him and what others observe.'
D: Elegant: True filial piety leaves no gap between the family's report and the world's witness.
D: Distilled: When private character matches public reputation, virtue is genuine.
Notes: Min Ziqian (Min Sun) — one of Confucius's top disciples for virtue. Xiao (孝, filial piety) — foundational Confucian virtue. The passage emphasizes consistency between private and public character, a key test of the Junzi (君子).

D: Original: Nan Yung was frequently repeating the lines about a white scepter stone. Confucius gave him the daughter of his elder brother to wife.
D: Refashioned: Nan Rong kept reciting the poem about the white jade scepter — how a flaw in jade can be polished away, but a flaw in speech cannot. Confucius gave him his niece in marriage.
D: Elegant: He who meditates on the irreversibility of careless words earns the sage's trust.
D: Distilled: A man who guards his speech reveals his character.
Notes: The poem referenced is from the Book of Odes (Shijing), Ode 256 (Bai Gui). Nan Rong (Nan-kung Kuo) — a disciple noted for caution. Confucius's decision to marry his niece to this man shows he valued careful speech (Xin, 信) as a marker of trustworthy character.

D: Original: Chi K'ang asked which of the disciples loved to learn. Confucius replied to him, 'There was Yen Hui; he loved to learn. Unfortunately his appointed time was short, and he died. Now there is no one who loves to learn, as he did.'
D: Refashioned: Chi Kang asked which disciple loved learning. Confucius replied, 'Yan Hui loved learning. But his life was cut short. Now there is no one who truly loves learning as he did.'
D: Elegant: The one who truly loved learning died young. Since then, the seat has been empty.
D: Distilled: The rarest quality in a student is not talent but a genuine love of learning itself.
Notes: Chi K'ang — head of the powerful Ji family in Lu. Yan Hui died around age 32. Confucius gave nearly the same answer to Duke Ai in 6.3. The repetition underscores how singular Yan Hui was. Connects to FUALL's Academy: the institution fails not for lack of students but for lack of students who love learning for its own sake.

D: Original:
When Yen Yuan died, Yen Lu begged the carriage of the Master to sell and get an outer shell for his son's coffin.
The Master said, 'Every one calls his son his son, whether he has talents or has not talents. There was Li; when he died, he had a coffin but no outer shell. I would not walk on foot to get a shell for him, because, having followed in the rear of the great officers, it was not proper that I should walk on foot.'
D: Refashioned: When Yan Yuan died, his father Yan Lu asked the Master to sell his carriage to pay for an outer coffin. The Master said, 'Talented or not, every father calls his son his son. When my own son Li died, he had an inner coffin but no outer one. I would not go on foot to provide one, because having served among the great officers, it would not be proper for me to walk.'
D: Elegant: Confucius refused to sell his carriage for a coffin — not from coldness, but because ritual demanded that a man in his position ride, even in grief.
D: Distilled: Grief does not override propriety; the rules exist precisely for the moments when feeling would sweep them away.
Notes: Yan Lu — Yan Hui's father, also a disciple of Confucius. Kong Li (Bo Yu) — Confucius's own son, who died before him. Li (禮) governs even mourning: the tension between personal grief and ritual obligation is a core Confucian theme. Confucius models that Li is not cold formalism but the structure that holds society together when emotions run highest.

D: Original: When Yen Yuan died, the Master said, 'Alas! Heaven is destroying me! Heaven is destroying me!'
D: Refashioned: When Yan Yuan died, the Master cried out, 'Heaven is destroying me! Heaven is destroying me!'
D: Elegant: At the death of his best student, Confucius did not quote the sages. He cried out against Heaven.
D: Distilled: Even the sage breaks when the irreplaceable is lost.
Notes: Tian (天, Heaven) — in Confucian usage, the moral order of the universe, not a personal deity. Confucius's outcry suggests he saw Yan Hui as essential to the transmission of the Dao. His death was not merely personal loss but civilizational loss — the chain of transmission was broken. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: wisdom depends on living carriers.

D: Original:
When Yen Yuan died, the Master bewailed him exceedingly, and the disciples who were with him said, 'Master, your grief is excessive?'
'Is it excessive?' said he.
'If I am not to mourn bitterly for this man, for whom should I mourn?'
D: Refashioned: When Yan Yuan died, the Master wept bitterly. His disciples said, 'Master, your grief is excessive.' He replied, 'Is it excessive? If I do not mourn bitterly for this man, for whom should I mourn?'
D: Elegant: 'Is it excessive?' he said. 'If not for this man — then for whom?'
D: Distilled: Some losses deserve grief that breaks convention.
Notes: The disciples' concern reflects Li (propriety) — mourning has prescribed limits. Confucius's response asserts that some losses genuinely warrant exceeding those limits. This is a rare moment where Ren (仁, humaneness) overrides Li (禮, ritual form) — a hierarchy Confucius affirms elsewhere (3.3).

D: Original:
When Yen Yuan died, the disciples wished to give him a great funeral, and the Master said, 'You may not do so.'
The disciples did bury him in great style.
The Master said, 'Hui behaved towards me as his father. I have not been able to treat him as my son. The fault is not mine; it belongs to you, O disciples.'
D: Refashioned: When Yan Yuan died, the disciples wanted to give him an extravagant funeral. The Master said, 'You should not.' They buried him lavishly anyway. The Master said, 'Hui treated me as a father. I was unable to treat him as a son. The fault is not mine — it is yours, my disciples.'
D: Elegant: The disciples gave Yan Hui the funeral Confucius forbade. He said: 'Hui treated me as his father. I could not treat him as my son. That failure is yours, not mine.'
D: Distilled: Honoring someone against their principles dishonors them.
Notes: The irony: the disciples' lavish funeral violated the simplicity Yan Hui lived by. Confucius could not control his own students, mirroring the broader theme that even a great teacher cannot force virtue. Li (禮) demands funerals appropriate to station, not to affection.

D: Original: Chi Lu asked about serving the spirits of the dead. The Master said, 'While you are not able to serve men, how can you serve their spirits?' Chi Lu added, 'I venture to ask about death?' He was answered, 'While you do not know life, how can you know about death?'
D: Refashioned: Zilu asked about serving the spirits of the dead. The Master said, 'You cannot even serve the living — how would you serve the dead?' Zilu then asked about death. The Master said, 'You do not yet understand life — how can you understand death?'
D: Elegant: 'You cannot serve the living — how will you serve the dead? You do not understand life — how will you understand death?'
D: Distilled: Master what comes before you before reaching for what lies beyond.
Notes: One of the most famous passages in the Analects. Confucius's pragmatic agnosticism: he does not deny spirits or an afterlife but insists on priority — get the human world right first. This is the core of Confucian humanism and directly parallels FUALL's Reality principle: start with what is knowable and immediate. Zilu (Zhong You) — bold, impulsive disciple, often corrected by Confucius.

D: Original:
The disciple Min was standing by his side, looking bland and precise; Tsze-lu, looking bold and soldierly; Zan Yu and Tsze-kung, with a free and straightforward manner. The Master was pleased.
He said, 'Yu, there!-- he will not die a natural death.'
D: Refashioned: Min Ziqian stood by the Master looking composed and precise. Zilu looked bold and soldierly. Ran You and Zigong had an easy, open manner. The Master was pleased. But of Zilu he said, 'That one — he will not die a natural death.'
D: Elegant: Confucius looked at his students with pleasure — the composed one, the bold one, the open ones. Of the bold one he said: 'He will not die a natural death.'
D: Distilled: The teacher who knows his students best can see which virtues will save them and which will destroy them.
Notes: Zilu (子路) did in fact die violently in 480 BCE, killed during a political coup in Wei, refusing to flee. His death confirmed Confucius's prediction. The passage illustrates Zhi (智, wisdom) as the ability to read character and foresee consequences.

D: Original:
Some parties in Lu were going to take down and rebuild the Long Treasury.
Min Tsze-ch'ien said, 'Suppose it were to be repaired after its old style;-- why must it be altered and made anew?'
The Master said, 'This man seldom speaks; when he does, he is sure to hit the point.'
D: Refashioned: Officials in Lu proposed tearing down and rebuilding the Long Treasury. Min Ziqian said, 'Why not simply repair it as it was? Why must it be completely rebuilt?' The Master said, 'This man rarely speaks, but when he does, he hits the mark.'
D: Elegant: 'Why rebuild what can be repaired?' The Master said: 'He seldom speaks — but when he does, he is right.'
D: Distilled: The wisest counsel is often the simplest: repair before you replace.
Notes: Min Ziqian's conservatism here reflects a core Confucian instinct: do not destroy functioning institutions to build new ones. The Long Treasury (Chang Fu) was a state building in Lu. This maps directly to FUALL's framework: institutional reform should preserve what works rather than razing and rebuilding.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'What has the lute of Yu to do in my door?'
The other disciples began not to respect Tsze-lu. The Master said, 'Yu has ascended to the hall, though he has not yet passed into the inner apartments.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'What is Zilu's rough lute-playing doing in my school?' The other disciples began to look down on Zilu. The Master corrected them: 'Yu has ascended to the hall, though he has not yet entered the inner room.'
D: Elegant: Confucius mocked Zilu's rough music, then rebuked the students who took it too far: 'He has reached the hall — he simply hasn't entered the inner chamber yet.'
D: Distilled: Criticizing someone's progress is not permission to dismiss their achievement.
Notes: The metaphor of hall (tang) vs. inner chamber (shi) became a standard Chinese idiom for stages of mastery. Confucius models the teacher's dilemma: honest critique can be weaponized by others. Zilu's lute-playing was martial and rough, reflecting his character — competent but unrefined.

D: Original:
Tsze-kung asked which of the two, Shih or Shang, was the superior. The Master said, 'Shih goes beyond the due mean, and Shang does not come up to it.'
'Then,' said Tsze-kung, 'the superiority is with Shih, I suppose.'
The Master said, 'To go beyond is as wrong as to fall short.'
D: Refashioned: Zigong asked who was better, Shi (Zizhang) or Shang (Zixia). The Master said, 'Shi overshoots the mark; Shang falls short of it.' Zigong said, 'Then Shi is the better?' The Master said, 'To overshoot is as wrong as to fall short.'
D: Elegant: 'Overshooting is as wrong as falling short.' Excess and deficiency are equally far from the center.
D: Distilled: Going too far is no closer to right than not going far enough.
Notes: Zhongyong (中庸, the Doctrine of the Mean) — the principle that virtue lies in the center, not the extreme. This passage is one of its clearest expressions. Directly parallels Aristotle's Golden Mean (Nicomachean Ethics II.6). Connects to FUALL Ethics framework: virtue is calibration, not maximization.

D: Original:
The head of the Chi family was richer than the duke of Chau had been, and yet Ch'iu collected his imposts for him, and increased his wealth.
The Master said, 'He is no disciple of mine. My little children, beat the drum and assail him.'
D: Refashioned: The head of the Ji family was wealthier than the Duke of Zhou had ever been, and yet Ran Qiu collected taxes for him, making him richer still. The Master said, 'He is no disciple of mine. Students, you may beat the drum and attack him.'
D: Elegant: When his own disciple helped a rich man grow richer through unjust taxation, Confucius disowned him publicly.
D: Distilled: Serving power against justice forfeits your claim to wisdom.
Notes: Ran Qiu (Ran You, 冉求) — one of Confucius's administrative disciples who served the powerful Ji family. The Duke of Zhou (周公) — the sage-regent who Confucius idealized. Yi (義, righteousness) demands that ability serve justice, not merely power. Confucius's fury here is rare and striking — one of the few times he publicly denounces a student.

D: Original:
Ch'ai is simple.
Shan is dull.
Shih is specious.
Yu is coarse.
D: Refashioned: Gao Chai is simple-minded. Zeng Shen is slow. Zizhang is showy. Zilu is coarse.
D: Elegant: Four disciples, four flaws: simple, slow, showy, coarse.
D: Distilled: A good teacher sees each student's specific limitation, not a generic one.
Notes: Gao Chai (Ch'ai), Zeng Shen (Shan/Zengzi), Zizhang (Shih), Zilu (Yu). Each flaw is distinct and requires a different corrective — the passage illustrates Confucius's individualized pedagogy, a core principle of FUALL's Academy: education must be calibrated to the student.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'There is Hui! He has nearly attained to perfect virtue. He is often in want.
'Ts'ze does not acquiesce in the appointments of Heaven, and his goods are increased by him. Yet his judgments are often correct.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Hui has nearly reached perfect virtue, yet he is often poor. Zigong does not accept the decrees of Heaven and speculates in goods — but his judgments are often correct.'
D: Elegant: Yan Hui was nearly perfect and nearly penniless. Zigong rejected fate, chased profit — and was usually right about the market.
D: Distilled: Virtue and worldly success follow different logics; being right about one says nothing about the other.
Notes: Ren (仁, perfect virtue/benevolence) vs. material success. Yan Hui embodies the Confucian ideal of virtue independent of fortune. Zigong (端木赐) was famously wealthy — a successful merchant and diplomat. 'Does not acquiesce in the appointments of Heaven' (Ming, 命) — Zigong resists fatalism. The tension between virtue and capability is a recurring FUALL theme.

D: Original: Tsze-chang asked what were the characteristics of the GOOD man. The Master said, 'He does not tread in the footsteps of others, but moreover, he does not enter the chamber of the sage.'
D: Refashioned: Zizhang asked about the characteristics of a truly good person. The Master said, 'He does not merely follow in the footsteps of others, but neither does he enter the sage's inner chamber.'
D: Elegant: The good man is original but not yet a sage — he walks his own path without reaching the deepest room.
D: Distilled: Goodness is independence of character; wisdom is something further still.
Notes: Confucius distinguishes between the 'good person' (shan ren, 善人) and the sage (sheng, 聖). The good person has natural moral instinct but lacks the depth that comes from rigorous study and self-cultivation. Connects to FUALL's hierarchy: natural goodness is necessary but not sufficient for wisdom.

D: Original: The Master said, 'If, because a man's discourse appears solid and sincere, we allow him to be a good man, is he really a superior man? or is his gravity only in appearance?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Just because a man's words sound solid and sincere, can we be sure he is a superior person? Perhaps his gravity is only a performance.'
D: Elegant: Solid words may mask a hollow character. Gravity can be worn like a costume.
D: Distilled: Sounding wise and being wise are different accomplishments.
Notes: Junzi (君子, superior person) cannot be identified by speech alone. This echoes 1.3 ('Fine words and an insinuating appearance are seldom associated with true virtue'). Connects to FUALL's Reason: truth requires verification beyond surface presentation.

D: Original: Tsze-lu asked whether he should immediately carry into practice what he heard. The Master said, 'There are your father and elder brothers to be consulted;-- why should you act on that principle of immediately carrying into practice what you hear?' Zan Yu asked the same, whether he should immediately carry into practice what he heard, and the Master answered, 'Immediately carry into practice what you hear.' Kung-hsi Hwa said, 'Yu asked whether he should carry immediately into practice what he heard, and you said, "There are your father and elder brothers to be consulted." Ch'iu asked whether he should immediately carry into practice what he heard, and you said, "Carry it immediately into practice." I, Ch'ih, am perplexed, and venture to ask you for an explanation.' The Master said, 'Ch'iu is retiring and slow; therefore, I urged him forward. Yu has more than his own share of energy; therefore I kept him back.'
D: Refashioned: Zilu asked whether he should immediately act on what he learned. The Master said, 'Your father and elder brothers are still alive to consult — why rush?' Ran You asked the same question, and the Master said, 'Yes, act on it immediately.' When Gongxi Hua pointed out the contradiction, the Master explained: 'Ran You is hesitant and slow, so I push him forward. Zilu has more energy than he needs, so I hold him back.'
D: Elegant: To the impulsive student: 'Wait.' To the hesitant student: 'Act now.' The teaching is the same — find the center — but the medicine must fit the patient.
D: Distilled: Wisdom gives opposite advice to opposite temperaments, because the destination is the same.
Notes: This is the clearest illustration of Confucius's pedagogical method: individualized instruction based on character assessment. Zilu's excess energy (勇) needs restraint; Ran You's timidity needs encouragement. The Zhongyong (Mean) is reached from different directions. Connects to FUALL's Academy: effective education is not one-size-fits-all.

D: Original: The Master was put in fear in K'wang and Yen Yuan fell behind. The Master, on his rejoining him, said, 'I thought you had died.' Hui replied, 'While you were alive, how should I presume to die?'
D: Refashioned: The Master was in danger in Kuang, and Yan Yuan fell behind the group. When they reunited, the Master said, 'I thought you had died.' Yan Hui replied, 'While you are alive, how could I presume to die?'
D: Elegant: 'I thought you were dead.' 'While you live, Master, how could I presume to die?'
D: Distilled: The student's devotion: my life has no right to end before yours does.
Notes: The Kuang incident (~496 BCE) — Confucius was mistaken for Yang Huo and surrounded by hostile forces. Yan Hui's reply expresses absolute loyalty, but also the Confucian idea that the student's life is bound to the teacher's mission. The transmission of Dao depends on living carriers.

D: Original:
Chi Tsze-zan asked whether Chung Yu and Zan Ch'iu could be called great ministers.
The Master said, 'I thought you would ask about some extraordinary individuals, and you only ask about Yu and Ch'iu!
'What is called a great minister, is one who serves his prince according to what is right, and when he finds he cannot do so, retires.
'Now, as to Yu and Ch'iu, they may be called ordinary ministers.'
Tsze-zan said, 'Then they will always follow their chief;-- will they?'
The Master said, 'In an act of parricide or regicide, they would not follow him.'
D: Refashioned: Ji Ziran asked whether Zilu and Ran Qiu could be called great ministers. The Master said, 'I expected you to ask about extraordinary men, and you ask about those two? A great minister serves his ruler according to what is right, and resigns when he cannot. Yu and Qiu are merely ordinary ministers.' Ji Ziran asked, 'Then they would always follow orders?' The Master said, 'They would not follow orders to commit parricide or regicide.'
D: Elegant: A great minister serves by principle and resigns when principle demands it. An ordinary minister follows orders — up to the point of murder.
D: Distilled: The test of a minister is not obedience but the line he will not cross.
Notes: Yi (義, righteousness) as the limit on loyalty. Confucius sets a hierarchy: principle above service, but even ordinary ministers have a floor — they will not participate in the ultimate crimes. Ji Ziran — a member of the powerful Ji family. Connects to FUALL's domains of power: the ideational domain (principles) must constrain the social domain (political loyalty).

D: Original:
Tsze-lu got Tsze-kao appointed governor of Pi.
The Master said, 'You are injuring a man's son.'
Tsze-lu said, 'There are (there) common people and officers; there are the altars of the spirits of the land and grain. Why must one read books before he can be considered to have learned?'
The Master said, 'It is on this account that I hate your glib-tongued people.'
D: Refashioned: Zilu arranged for his young student Zigao to be appointed governor of Pi. The Master said, 'You are ruining someone else's son.' Zilu protested, 'There are people to govern and altars to tend — why must one read books to be considered educated?' The Master said, 'This is exactly why I hate glib talkers.'
D: Elegant: Zilu put an unready student in office and called it learning by doing. Confucius called it ruining a man's son.
D: Distilled: Placing the unformed in positions of power is not education — it is negligence.
Notes: Pi — a district under the Ji family's control. Zigao (Gao Chai) — described in 11.17 as 'simple-minded.' Zilu's argument ('why must one read books?') is anti-intellectual pragmatism. Confucius's sharp rejection shows he valued preparation before power. Connects to FUALL's Academy: governance requires training before responsibility.

D: Original:
Tsze-lu, Tsang Hsi, Zan Yu, and Kung-hsi Hwa were sitting by the Master.
He said to them, 'Though I am a day or so older than you, do not think of that.
'From day to day you are saying, "We are not known." If some ruler were to know you, what would you like to do?'
Tsze-lu hastily and lightly replied, 'Suppose the case of a State of ten thousand chariots; let it be straitened between other large States; let it be suffering from invading armies; and to this let there be added a famine in corn and in all vegetables:-- if I were intrusted with the government of it, in three years' time I could make the people to be bold, and to recognise the rules of righteous conduct.' The Master smiled at him.
Turning to Yen Yu, he said, 'Ch'iu, what are your wishes?' Ch'iu replied, 'Suppose a state of sixty or seventy li square, or one of fifty or sixty, and let me have the government of it;-- in three years' time, I could make plenty to abound among the people. As to teaching them the principles of propriety, and music, I must wait for the rise of a superior man to do that.'
'What are your wishes, Ch'ih,' said the Master next to Kung- hsi Hwa. Ch'ih replied, 'I do not say that my ability extends to these things, but I should wish to learn them. At the services of the ancestral temple, and at the audiences of the princes with the sovereign, I should like, dressed in the dark square-made robe and the black linen cap, to act as a small assistant.'
Last of all, the Master asked Tsang Hsi, 'Tien, what are your wishes?' Tien, pausing as he was playing on his lute, while it was yet twanging, laid the instrument aside, and rose. 'My wishes,' he said, 'are different from the cherished purposes of these three gentlemen.' 'What harm is there in that?' said the Master; 'do you also, as well as they, speak out your wishes.' Tien then said, 'In this, the last month of spring, with the dress of the season all complete, along with five or six young men who have assumed the cap, and six or seven boys, I would wash in the I, enjoy the breeze among the rain altars, and return home singing.' The Master heaved a sigh and said, 'I give my approval to Tien.'
The three others having gone out, Tsang Hsi remained behind, and said, 'What do you think of the words of these three friends?' The Master replied, 'They simply told each one his wishes.'
Hsi pursued, 'Master, why did you smile at Yu?'
He was answered, 'The management of a State demands the rules of propriety. His words were not humble; therefore I smiled at him.'
Hsi again said, 'But was it not a State which Ch'iu proposed for himself?' The reply was, 'Yes; did you ever see a territory of sixty or seventy li or one of fifty or sixty, which was not a State?'
Once more, Hsi inquired, 'And was it not a State which Ch'ih proposed for himself?' The Master again replied, 'Yes; who but princes have to do with ancestral temples, and with audiences but the sovereign? If Ch'ih were to be a small assistant in these services, who could be a great one?
D: Refashioned:
Zilu, Zeng Xi, Ran You, and Gongxi Hua sat with the Master. He said, 'Forget that I am a bit older. You all say no one recognizes you. If a ruler knew your worth, what would you do?'

Zilu answered quickly: 'Give me a large state, squeezed between enemies, suffering invasion and famine. In three years I could make its people brave and principled.' The Master smiled.

Ran You said: 'Give me a small territory. In three years I could bring prosperity — though for teaching propriety and music, I would need a wiser man.'

Gongxi Hua said: 'I don't claim such abilities, but I would like to learn. At ancestral ceremonies and diplomatic audiences, I would serve as a junior assistant in proper robes.'

Finally the Master asked Zeng Xi, who was still playing his lute. He set it down and said, 'My wish is different from theirs.' The Master said, 'No harm in that — speak freely.' Zeng Xi said, 'In late spring, wearing the season's clothes, I would go with a few young men and boys to bathe in the Yi River, enjoy the breeze at the Rain Dance altars, and walk home singing.'
