The Master sighed deeply and said, 'I am with Zeng Xi.'

Afterward, Zeng Xi asked why the Master had smiled at Zilu. 'Governing a state requires propriety. His words lacked humility, so I smiled.' About Ran You: 'Was that not also a state he described?' About Gongxi Hua: 'Ancestral ceremonies and diplomatic audiences — who but a ruler handles those? If Hua would be a minor assistant there, who could be the major one?'
D: Elegant: Three disciples described ambitions of governance. The fourth said he would bathe in the river, feel the breeze, and walk home singing. Confucius sighed and said, 'I am with him.'
D: Distilled: The deepest ambition is not to rule the world but to live in harmony with it.
Notes: The most celebrated passage in the Analects. Zeng Xi (Zeng Dian, 曾皙) — father of Zengzi. His answer describes the Spring Purification Festival. Confucius's approval has been debated for centuries: is it Daoist quietism? Aesthetic appreciation? Most likely, Zeng Xi embodies the harmony (He, 和) that is the goal of all Confucian cultivation — a person so aligned with the Dao that governance becomes unnecessary because life itself is properly ordered. The Yi River bathing and Rain Dance altars reference the ancient purification rite (xi). Connects to FUALL's Wisdom: the highest aim is not power over the world but right relationship with it.

D: Original: Book 12: Yen Yuan

Yen Yuan asked about perfect virtue. The Master said, 'To subdue one's self and return to propriety, is perfect virtue. If a man can for one day subdue himself and return to propriety, all under heaven will ascribe perfect virtue to him. Is the practice of perfect virtue from a man himself, or is it from others?'
Yen Yuan said, 'I beg to ask the steps of that process.' The Master replied, 'Look not at what is contrary to propriety; listen not to what is contrary to propriety; speak not what is contrary to propriety; make no movement which is contrary to propriety.' Yen Yuan then said, 'Though I am deficient in intelligence and vigour, I will make it my business to practise this lesson.'
D: Refashioned:
Yan Yuan asked about perfect virtue (Ren). The Master said, 'To master yourself and return to propriety — that is Ren. If a person can do this for even a single day, the whole world will recognize his virtue. Achieving Ren depends on yourself, not on others.'

Yan Yuan asked for the practical steps. The Master said, 'Do not look at what is contrary to propriety. Do not listen to what is contrary to propriety. Do not speak what is contrary to propriety. Do not act contrary to propriety.' Yan Yuan said, 'Though I am not clever, I will devote myself to this.'
D: Elegant: 'Master yourself and return to propriety — that is perfect virtue. Look not, listen not, speak not, act not contrary to what is right.' The student said: 'I will devote myself to this.'
D: Distilled: Perfect virtue is self-mastery expressed through disciplined conduct in every sense — sight, hearing, speech, and action.
Notes: Ke ji fu li (克己復禮) — 'subdue the self and return to ritual' — one of the most important formulations in Chinese philosophy. The 'four don'ts' (si wu, 四勿) became a foundational discipline. Li (禮) here is not mere etiquette but the entire system of right conduct. Ren (仁) is achieved through Li — the two are inseparable. Connects to FUALL's Right: virtue is not feeling but practice.

D: Original: Chung-kung asked about perfect virtue. The Master said, 'It is, when you go abroad, to behave to every one as if you were receiving a great guest; to employ the people as if you were assisting at a great sacrifice; not to do to others as you would not wish done to yourself; to have no murmuring against you in the country, and none in the family.' Chung-kung said, 'Though I am deficient in intelligence and vigour, I will make it my business to practise this lesson.'
D: Refashioned: Zhonggong asked about perfect virtue. The Master said, 'When you go out in public, behave as though you were receiving a distinguished guest. When you employ people, act as though you were conducting a great sacrifice. Do not do to others what you would not want done to yourself. Then neither in the state nor in the family will there be resentment against you.' Zhonggong said, 'Though I am not clever, I will devote myself to this.'
D: Elegant: Treat every public act as a sacred ceremony. Do not impose on others what you would refuse yourself. Master this, and no one — in state or family — will resent you.
D: Distilled: The negative Golden Rule: do not do to others what you would not want done to yourself.
Notes: Ji suo bu yu, wu shi yu ren (己所不欲，勿施於人) — the 'Silver Rule' or negative formulation of the Golden Rule. This appears again in 15.23. Zhonggong (Ran Yong) — one of the top disciples for virtue. The comparison to 'receiving a guest' and 'conducting a sacrifice' elevates daily life to ritual significance. Connects to FUALL's Right: ethics is enacted in how you treat people, not in abstract principles.

D: Original:
Sze-ma Niu asked about perfect virtue.
The Master said, 'The man of perfect virtue is cautious and slow in his speech.'
'Cautious and slow in his speech!' said Niu;-- 'is this what is meant by perfect virtue?' The Master said, 'When a man feels the difficulty of doing, can he be other than cautious and slow in speaking?'
D: Refashioned: Sima Niu asked about perfect virtue. The Master said, 'The person of Ren is cautious and slow in speech.' Sima Niu said, 'Cautious and slow in speech — is that all Ren means?' The Master said, 'When you understand how difficult right action is, how can you be anything but cautious in what you say?'
D: Elegant: 'The virtuous are slow to speak.' 'Is that all?' 'When you know how hard it is to do right, you stop talking so freely about it.'
D: Distilled: The person who understands how hard right action is speaks carefully about what he will do.
Notes: Sima Niu (司馬牛) — a disciple whose brother Huan Tui attempted to assassinate Confucius. Confucius tailors his answer: Sima Niu was reportedly impulsive and talkative. Ren (仁) is defined differently for each questioner — the same virtue, calibrated to the student's weakness. Connects to FUALL's Reason: words are commitments, and careless speech undermines credibility.

D: Original:
Sze-ma Niu asked about the superior man. The Master said, 'The superior man has neither anxiety nor fear.'
'Being without anxiety or fear!' said Nui;-- 'does this constitute what we call the superior man?'
The Master said, 'When internal examination discovers nothing wrong, what is there to be anxious about, what is there to fear?'
D: Refashioned: Sima Niu asked about the superior person. The Master said, 'The superior person has neither anxiety nor fear.' Sima Niu said, 'No anxiety, no fear — is that what makes a superior person?' The Master said, 'When self-examination reveals nothing wrong, what is there to be anxious about or afraid of?'
D: Elegant: No anxiety, no fear. Not because nothing threatens — but because self-examination finds nothing wrong.
D: Distilled: Freedom from anxiety comes not from safety but from a clean conscience.
Notes: Junzi (君子) — the Confucian ideal person. Inner integrity produces psychological freedom. The logic: if you have done right, external threats cannot produce real shame. This parallels Socrates' claim at his trial that a good man cannot be harmed. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom: inner alignment produces outer composure.

D: Original:
Sze-ma Niu, full of anxiety, said, 'Other men all have their brothers, I only have not.'
Tsze-hsia said to him, 'There is the following saying which I have heard:--
'"Death and life have their determined appointment; riches and honours depend upon Heaven."
'Let the superior man never fail reverentially to order his own conduct, and let him be respectful to others and observant of propriety:-- then all within the four seas will be his brothers. What has the superior man to do with being distressed because he has no brothers?'
D: Refashioned: Sima Niu said anxiously, 'Everyone else has brothers. I alone have none.' Zixia said to him, 'I have heard it said: life and death are determined by fate; wealth and rank depend on Heaven. If the superior person is unfailingly respectful in conduct and observant of propriety, then everyone within the four seas is his brother. Why should a superior person worry about having no brothers?'
D: Elegant: 'Life and death are fated; wealth and rank depend on Heaven. Be reverent and proper, and all within the four seas become your brothers.'
D: Distilled: When you cannot choose your family, let your character choose your community.
Notes: Sima Niu's brother Huan Tui was a would-be assassin and traitor — hence his grief about 'having no brothers.' 'Within the four seas, all are brothers' (si hai zhi nei, jie xiong di ye, 四海之內，皆兄弟也) became one of the most famous phrases in Chinese culture. Zixia (Bu Shang) — a disciple known for scholarship. Ming (命, fate/destiny) — acceptance of what cannot be controlled, paired with effort toward what can be.

D: Original: Tsze-chang asked what constituted intelligence. The Master said, 'He with whom neither slander that gradually soaks into the mind, nor statements that startle like a wound in the flesh, are successful, may be called intelligent indeed. Yea, he with whom neither soaking slander, nor startling statements, are successful, may be called farseeing.'
D: Refashioned: Zizhang asked what constitutes true intelligence. The Master said, 'When slander that soaks in gradually and accusations that sting like flesh wounds both fail to move you — that is intelligence. Indeed, when neither soaking slander nor stinging accusations can sway you, you may be called far-seeing.'
D: Elegant: True intelligence is immunity to both slow poison and sudden wounds — the slander that seeps in and the accusation that strikes like a blade.
D: Distilled: The clearest thinker is the one unmoved by both gradual manipulation and sudden shock.
Notes: Ming (明, intelligence/clarity) — not mere cleverness but clarity of judgment under pressure. The two threats are distinct: slow infiltration (rumor, propaganda) and sudden shock (accusation, crisis). Resisting both requires Zhi (智, wisdom). Connects to FUALL's Reason: discernment means filtering signal from noise regardless of delivery method.

D: Original:
Tsze-kung asked about government. The Master said, 'The requisites of government are that there be sufficiency of food, sufficiency of military equipment, and the confidence of the people in their ruler.'
Tsze-kung said, 'If it cannot be helped, and one of these must be dispensed with, which of the three should be foregone first?' 'The military equipment,' said the Master.
Tsze-kung again asked, 'If it cannot be helped, and one of the remaining two must be dispensed with, which of them should be foregone?' The Master answered, 'Part with the food. From of old, death has been the lot of all men; but if the people have no faith in their rulers, there is no standing for the state.'
D: Refashioned: Zigong asked about government. The Master said, 'A government needs three things: sufficient food, sufficient arms, and the trust of the people.' Zigong asked, 'If you had to give up one, which would go first?' 'Arms.' 'And if you had to give up another?' 'Food. People have always died — but without trust, a state cannot stand.'
D: Elegant: Arms can be surrendered. Food can be scarce. But without the people's trust, there is no state at all.
D: Distilled: A government can survive the loss of resources but not the loss of trust.
Notes: One of the most politically significant passages in the Analects. Xin (信, trust/faithfulness) is placed above material security and military power. The hierarchy — trust > food > arms — inverts conventional realist assumptions about power. Connects directly to FUALL's domains of power: the ideational domain (trust, legitimacy) is more fundamental than physical or resource domains.

D: Original:
Chi Tsze-ch'ang said, 'In a superior man it is only the substantial qualities which are wanted;-- why should we seek for ornamental accomplishments?'
Tsze-kung said, 'Alas! Your words, sir, show you to be a superior man, but four horses cannot overtake the tongue.
Ornament is as substance; substance is as ornament. The hide of a tiger or a leopard stripped of its hair, is like the hide of a dog or a goat stripped of its hair.'
D: Refashioned: Ji Zicheng said, 'What matters in a superior person is substance, not refinement. Why bother with cultural polish?' Zigong replied, 'What a pity — your words betray you, sir. Four horses cannot chase down a reckless tongue. Refinement matters as much as substance, and substance as much as refinement. Strip the fur from a tiger or leopard and its hide looks no different from a dog's.'
D: Elegant: Strip the pattern from a tiger's skin and it looks like any dog's hide. Substance without culture is unrecognizable; culture without substance is fraud.
D: Distilled: Without refinement, even genuine substance is indistinguishable from mediocrity.
Notes: Wen (文, culture/refinement) vs. Zhi (質, substance/nature). Confucius addresses this balance in 6.18: 'When substance exceeds refinement, you get crudeness; when refinement exceeds substance, you get pedantry.' Zigong's tiger metaphor argues that the two are inseparable. Connects to FUALL's educational model: substance (Academy content) needs form (Temple ritual, Agora discourse) to be transmissible.

D: Original:
The Duke Ai inquired of Yu Zo, saying, 'The year is one of scarcity, and the returns for expenditure are not sufficient;-- what is to be done?'
Yu Zo replied to him, 'Why not simply tithe the people?'
'With two tenths, said the duke, 'I find it not enough;-- how could I do with that system of one tenth?'
Yu Zo answered, 'If the people have plenty, their prince will not be left to want alone. If the people are in want, their prince cannot enjoy plenty alone.'
D: Refashioned: Duke Ai asked You Ruo, 'It is a year of scarcity and I cannot cover expenses. What should I do?' You Ruo said, 'Why not reduce the tax to a tithe?' The Duke said, 'I cannot manage even with the current double tithe — how could I manage with less?' You Ruo replied, 'When the people have plenty, how can their ruler be left in want? When the people are in want, how can their ruler enjoy plenty alone?'
D: Elegant: 'When the people prosper, how can their ruler lack? When the people starve, how can their ruler feast?' Tax less and the people will produce more.
D: Distilled: A ruler's prosperity cannot be separated from his people's; tax less and both will have more.
Notes: You Ruo (有若) — a disciple who physically resembled Confucius. The 'tithe' (che, 徹) is a 10% tax from the Zhou system. Duke Ai of Lu was taxing at 20%. You Ruo's economic argument anticipates supply-side reasoning: reducing the burden on producers increases total output. Connects to FUALL's domains of power: resource power depends on a healthy relationship between rulers and the productive base.

D: Original:
Tsze-chang having asked how virtue was to be exalted, and delusions to be discovered, the Master said, 'Hold faithfulness and sincerity as first principles, and be moving continually to what is right;-- this is the way to exalt one's virtue.
'You love a man and wish him to live; you hate him and wish him to die. Having wished him to live, you also wish him to die. This is a case of delusion.
'"It may not be on account of her being rich, yet you come to make a difference."'
D: Refashioned: Zizhang asked how to cultivate virtue and detect delusion. The Master said, 'Make faithfulness and sincerity your foundation, and constantly move toward what is right — that is how to cultivate virtue. As for delusion: when you love someone, you want them to live forever; when you hate them, you want them dead. To wish someone both alive and dead — that is delusion.'
D: Elegant: Cultivate virtue through faithfulness, sincerity, and constant movement toward what is right. Detect delusion by noticing when you wish the same person both alive and dead.
D: Distilled: Delusion is wanting contradictory things from the same person; virtue is the steady discipline of not indulging it.
Notes: Zhong (忠, faithfulness) and Xin (信, sincerity) as the practical foundation of De (德, virtue). The delusion example is psychological: contradictory emotions toward the same person reveal that your judgment is driven by feeling, not principle. Connects to FUALL's Reason: self-awareness of emotional contradiction is a prerequisite for clear thinking.

D: Original:
The Duke Ching, of Ch'i, asked Confucius about government.
Confucius replied, 'There is government, when the prince is prince, and the minister is minister; when the father is father, and the son is son.'
'Good!' said the duke; 'if, indeed; the prince be not prince, the minister not minister, the father not father, and the son not son, although I have my revenue, can I enjoy it?'
D: Refashioned: Duke Jing of Qi asked Confucius about government. Confucius replied, 'Government works when the ruler acts as a ruler, the minister as a minister, the father as a father, and the son as a son.' The Duke said, 'Exactly! If the ruler is not a ruler, the minister not a minister, the father not a father, and the son not a son — then even if I have grain, how can I eat it in peace?'
D: Elegant: Let the ruler be a ruler, the minister a minister, the father a father, the son a son. When names match reality, governance works. When they don't, even grain in the storehouse cannot feed you.
D: Distilled: Good government begins when everyone fulfills the role they claim to hold.
Notes: Zhengming (正名, rectification of names) in its political form. Duke Jing of Qi (齊景公) — his own court was in disarray, making this advice especially pointed. The principle: social order depends on people actually performing the duties attached to their titles. When roles become empty, the whole system breaks down. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: institutional decay begins when titles detach from responsibilities.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'Ah! it is Yu, who could with half a word settle litigations!'
Tsze-lu never slept over a promise.
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Zilu could settle a legal dispute with half a word!' And indeed, Zilu never broke a promise overnight.
D: Elegant: He could settle a lawsuit with half a word — because no one doubted his word for a moment.
D: Distilled: When a person's trustworthiness is absolute, even half a word carries the force of law.
Notes: Zilu's (子路) character: decisive, trustworthy, a man of his word. Xin (信, trustworthiness) as the foundation of social authority. The passage connects legal authority to personal character — people accept a ruling when they trust the judge.

D: Original: The Master said, 'In hearing litigations, I am like any other body. What is necessary, however, is to cause the people to have no litigations.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'In hearing legal cases, I am no different from anyone else. What matters is to create conditions where people have no need to bring cases at all.'
D: Elegant: The goal of justice is not better verdicts but fewer disputes.
D: Distilled: The best legal system is one that makes itself unnecessary.
Notes: Wu song (無訟, no litigation) — the Confucian ideal of a society so well-ordered through moral education that legal disputes rarely arise. This is governance through De (德, virtue) rather than Fa (法, law). Connects to FUALL's framework: education (paideia) reduces the need for enforcement.

D: Original: Tsze-chang asked about government. The Master said, 'The art of governing is to keep its affairs before the mind without weariness, and to practise them with undeviating consistency.'
D: Refashioned: Zizhang asked about government. The Master said, 'Keep the affairs of governance constantly in mind without growing weary, and carry them out with unwavering consistency.'
D: Elegant: Govern by keeping the work in mind without exhaustion, and executing it without deviation.
D: Distilled: Good government is sustained attention and consistent execution — nothing more, nothing less.
Notes: The administrative virtue: ju zhi wu juan (居之無倦, dwell in it without weariness). Government is not brilliance but endurance. Connects to FUALL's Guilds: competent governance is a craft requiring discipline, not charisma.

D: Original: The Master said, 'By extensively studying all learning, and keeping himself under the restraint of the rules of propriety, one may thus likewise not err from what is right.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Study broadly, and restrain yourself with the rules of propriety. In this way, you will not go astray.'
D: Elegant: Learn widely; constrain yourself with propriety. Breadth without discipline wanders; discipline without breadth narrows.
D: Distilled: Broad learning kept in check by principled conduct is the path that does not wander.
Notes: Bo xue yu wen, yue zhi yi li (博學於文，約之以禮) — a formulation repeated from 6.27. Wen (文, culture/learning) provides range; Li (禮, propriety) provides structure. Connects to FUALL's Academy: education needs both breadth of knowledge and a framework of principles.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The superior man seeks to perfect the admirable qualities of men, and does not seek to perfect their bad qualities. The mean man does the opposite of this.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person develops the best in others and does not develop the worst. The inferior person does the opposite.'
D: Elegant: The superior person cultivates what is admirable in others. The inferior person cultivates what is deplorable.
D: Distilled: Character is revealed by whether you bring out the best or worst in others.
Notes: Junzi (君子) vs. Xiaoren (小人, petty person) — one of the Analects' most consistent contrasts. Cheng ren zhi mei (成人之美, completing others' goodness). Connects to FUALL's educational mission: the teacher's task is to develop the student's strengths, not exploit weaknesses.

D: Original: Chi K'ang asked Confucius about government. Confucius replied, 'To govern means to rectify. If you lead on the people with correctness, who will dare not to be correct?'
D: Refashioned: Ji Kang asked Confucius about government. Confucius replied, 'To govern means to rectify. If you lead with correctness, who will dare to be incorrect?'
D: Elegant: 'To govern is to rectify.' Lead correctly, and no one dares do otherwise.
D: Distilled: The word 'govern' means 'to set right' — and it begins with the governor.
Notes: Zheng (政, government) and Zheng (正, to rectify) are homophones in Chinese — Confucius exploits this to make a philosophical point through etymology. Ji Kang (季康子) — the most powerful minister in Lu. The message: reform begins at the top. Connects to FUALL's principle that institutional health flows from leadership character.

D: Original: Chi K'ang, distressed about the number of thieves in the state, inquired of Confucius how to do away with them. Confucius said, 'If you, sir, were not covetous, although you should reward them to do it, they would not steal.'
D: Refashioned: Ji Kang, worried about the number of thieves in his state, asked Confucius how to stop them. Confucius said, 'If you yourself were not greedy, you could pay people to steal and they would refuse.'
D: Elegant: 'If you were not covetous, you could offer rewards for theft and no one would take them.'
D: Distilled: A corrupt leader who complains about corruption is looking at the mirror and blaming the reflection.
Notes: Confucius diagnoses theft as a systemic problem rooted in leadership. When rulers model greed, the people follow. De (德, virtue) flows downward: the moral condition of the state mirrors the moral condition of its rulers. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: institutional corruption always begins at the top.

D: Original: Chi K'ang asked Confucius about government, saying, 'What do you say to killing the unprincipled for the good of the principled?' Confucius replied, 'Sir, in carrying on your government, why should you use killing at all? Let your evinced desires be for what is good, and the people will be good. The relation between superiors and inferiors, is like that between the wind and the grass. The grass must bend, when the wind blows across it.'
D: Refashioned: Ji Kang asked Confucius, 'What about executing the lawless to encourage the lawful?' Confucius replied, 'Sir, why would you need to kill anyone to govern? If your own desires are set on what is good, the people will be good. The ruler's character is the wind; the people's character is the grass. When the wind blows, the grass bends.'
D: Elegant: The ruler is the wind. The people are the grass. Set yourself toward good, and the people will bend that way without a single execution.
D: Distilled: You do not need to kill people into goodness — you need to lead them toward it.
Notes: Feng (風, wind) and Cao (草, grass) — one of the Analects' most famous metaphors. Ji Kang proposes rule by fear; Confucius counters with rule by moral example (De, 德). This is the core Confucian alternative to Legalism (Fa Jia, 法家), which relied on punishment. Connects to FUALL's domains of power: ideational power (moral example) is more durable than physical power (coercion).

D: Original:
Tsze-chang asked, 'What must the officer be, who may be said to be distinguished?'
The Master said, 'What is it you call being distinguished?'
Tsze-chang replied, 'It is to be heard of through the State, to be heard of throughout his clan.'
The Master said, 'That is notoriety, not distinction.
'Now the man of distinction is solid and straightforward, and loves righteousness. He examines people's words, and looks at their countenances. He is anxious to humble himself to others. Such a man will be distinguished in the country; he will be distinguished in his clan.
'As to the man of notoriety, he assumes the appearance of virtue, but his actions are opposed to it, and he rests in this character without any doubts about himself. Such a man will be heard of in the country; he will be heard of in the clan.'
D: Refashioned: Zizhang asked, 'What must an officer be to be called distinguished?' The Master said, 'What do you mean by distinguished?' Zizhang said, 'To be widely known throughout the state and clan.' The Master said, 'That is notoriety, not distinction. A truly distinguished person is solid, straightforward, and loves righteousness. He reads people's words and faces carefully and is anxious to defer to others. Such a person will be genuinely distinguished in both state and clan. The notorious person puts on a show of virtue while acting against it, and rests comfortably in this pretense. He will be widely heard of — but not truly distinguished.'
D: Elegant: Notoriety is being known everywhere. Distinction is being solid, straight, and humble — and being known for that. The first is a performance. The second is a character.
D: Distilled: Being famous and being distinguished are different things; one depends on publicity, the other on substance.
Notes: Wen (聞, to be heard of/famous) vs. Da (達, to be distinguished/to truly arrive). Confucius makes a distinction between celebrity and genuine accomplishment. Yi (義, righteousness) is the marker that separates the two. Connects to FUALL's Agora: public discourse should elevate substance over spectacle.

D: Original:
Fan Ch'ih rambling with the Master under the trees about the rain altars, said, 'I venture to ask how to exalt virtue, to correct cherished evil, and to discover delusions.'
The Master said, 'Truly a good question!
'If doing what is to be done be made the first business, and success a secondary consideration;-- is not this the way to exalt virtue? To assail one's own wickedness and not assail that of others;-- is not this the way to correct cherished evil? For a morning's anger to disregard one's own life, and involve that of his parents;-- is not this a case of delusion?'
D: Refashioned: Fan Chi, walking with the Master near the Rain Dance altars, asked, 'How does one cultivate virtue, correct hidden evil, and detect delusion?' The Master said, 'Good questions! Put duty first and reward second — is that not how to cultivate virtue? Attack your own faults, not the faults of others — is that not how to correct evil? To throw away your life and endanger your parents over a moment's anger — is that not delusion?'
D: Elegant: Cultivate virtue: duty first, reward second. Correct evil: attack your own faults, not others'. Detect delusion: notice when a moment's anger would cost you everything.
D: Distilled: Virtue is duty before reward; correction is self-scrutiny before accusation; clarity is checking your anger before it checks you.
Notes: Fan Chi (樊遲) — a disciple who appears often asking practical questions. The Rain Dance altars (wu yu tan) — ritual sites for rain ceremonies. The three questions map to De (virtue cultivation), self-correction, and emotional regulation. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom framework: Reality (see your own faults), Reason (prioritize correctly), Right (control destructive impulses).

D: Original:
Fan Ch'ih asked about benevolence. The Master said, 'It is to love all men.' He asked about knowledge. The Master said, 'It is to know all men.'
Fan Ch'ih did not immediately understand these answers.
The Master said, 'Employ the upright and put aside all the crooked;-- in this way the crooked can be made to be upright.'
Fan Ch'ih retired, and, seeing Tsze-hsia, he said to him, 'A Little while ago, I had an interview with our Master, and asked him about knowledge. He said, 'Employ the upright, and put aside all the crooked;-- in this way, the crooked will be made to be upright.' What did he mean?'
Tsze-hsia said, 'Truly rich is his saying!
'Shun, being in possession of the kingdom, selected from among all the people, and employed Kao-yao, on which all who were devoid of virtue disappeared. T'ang, being in possession of the kingdom, selected from among all the people, and employed I Yin, and all who were devoid of virtue disappeared.'
D: Refashioned:
Fan Chi asked about benevolence. The Master said, 'Love all people.' He asked about knowledge. The Master said, 'Know all people.' Fan Chi did not understand. The Master explained, 'Elevate the upright and set aside the crooked — this way, even the crooked become straight.'

Later, Fan Chi asked Zixia what the Master meant. Zixia said, 'What a rich saying! When Shun ruled, he chose Gao Yao from among all the people, and the unvirtuous disappeared. When Tang ruled, he chose Yi Yin, and the same thing happened.'
D: Elegant: Love all people — that is benevolence. Know all people — that is knowledge. Elevate the upright over the crooked, and even the crooked straighten themselves.
D: Distilled: Place good people in positions of influence and the system corrects itself.
Notes: Ren (仁) = love; Zhi (智) = discernment of character. The practical application: leadership selection reforms society more effectively than punishment. Shun and Gao Yao — legendary sage-rulers. Tang — founder of the Shang dynasty. Yi Yin — his chief minister. The principle: institutional health depends on putting the right people in the right positions. Connects to FUALL's framework: the Academy and Guilds must identify and elevate competence.

D: Original: Tsze-kung asked about friendship. The Master said, 'Faithfully admonish your friend, and skillfully lead him on. If you find him impracticable, stop. Do not disgrace yourself.'
D: Refashioned: Zigong asked about friendship. The Master said, 'Advise your friend honestly and guide him skillfully. If he will not listen, stop. Do not humiliate yourself.'
D: Elegant: Counsel your friend faithfully. Guide him with skill. If he refuses — stop. Do not degrade yourself in pursuit of his improvement.
D: Distilled: Honest friendship has limits: when counsel is refused, persistence becomes self-degradation.
Notes: Zhong gao (忠告, faithful admonishment) — a duty of friendship, but bounded by the friend's receptivity. Confucius recognizes that insisting past the point of receptivity harms the relationship and the adviser's dignity. Xin (信) between friends requires mutuality.

D: Original: The philosopher Tsang said, 'The superior man on grounds of culture meets with his friends, and by their friendship helps his virtue.'
D: Refashioned: Zengzi said, 'The superior person uses culture to bring friends together, and uses friendship to support virtue.'
D: Elegant: Culture draws friends together; friendship sustains virtue. Each feeds the other.
D: Distilled: True friendship is built on shared culture and directed toward mutual improvement.
Notes: Zengzi (曾子, Zeng Shen) — Confucius's student who became one of the principal transmitters of his teaching. Yi wen hui you (以文會友, using culture to gather friends) — the model of intellectual community. Connects to FUALL's Agora: discourse communities sustained by shared commitment to learning.

D: Original: Book 13: Tsze-Lu

Tsze-lu asked about government. The Master said, 'Go before the people with your example, and be laborious in their affairs.'
He requested further instruction, and was answered, 'Be not weary (in these things).'
D: Refashioned: Zilu asked about government. The Master said, 'Lead by example, and work hard for the people.' Zilu asked for more. The Master said, 'Do not grow weary of it.'
D: Elegant: Lead by example. Work hard. Do not tire. That is the whole of governance.
D: Distilled: Governing well is leading by example and never stopping.
Notes: Xian zhi, lao zhi (先之，勞之) — 'go before them, toil for them.' Government as service, not command. The additional instruction — 'do not tire' (wu juan, 無倦) — echoes 12.14. Confucius's political philosophy is less about policy than about the character of the leader.

D: Original:
Chung-kung, being chief minister to the Head of the Chi family, asked about government. The Master said, 'Employ first the services of your various officers, pardon small faults, and raise to office men of virtue and talents.'
Chung-kung said, 'How shall I know the men of virtue and talent, so that I may raise them to office?' He was answered, 'Raise to office those whom you know. As to those whom you do not know, will others neglect them?'
D: Refashioned: Zhonggong, serving as chief minister to the Ji family, asked about governing. The Master said, 'Delegate to your officers, forgive small mistakes, and promote people of virtue and talent.' Zhonggong asked, 'How do I identify the talented and virtuous to promote?' The Master said, 'Promote those you know. Those you don't know — others will bring them to your attention.'
D: Elegant: Delegate, forgive small faults, promote the virtuous. 'But how do I find them?' Promote the ones you know. The ones you don't know will be brought forward by others.
D: Distilled: You cannot discover all talent yourself; promote what you can see and others will surface the rest.
Notes: Practical governance advice: ju xian cai (舉賢才, promote the virtuous and talented). Confucius addresses the common objection ('how can I know them all?') with pragmatic realism. Connects to FUALL's Guilds: institutional competence depends on talent identification at every level.

D: Original:
Tsze-lu said, 'The ruler of Wei has been waiting for you, in order with you to administer the government. What will you consider the first thing to be done?'
The Master replied, 'What is necessary is to rectify names.'
'So, indeed!' said Tsze-lu. 'You are wide of the mark! Why must there be such rectification?'
The Master said, 'How uncultivated you are, Yu! A superior man, in regard to what he does not know, shows a cautious reserve.
'If names be not correct, language is not in accordance with the truth of things. If language be not in accordance with the truth of things, affairs cannot be carried on to success.
'When affairs cannot be carried on to success, proprieties and music will not flourish. When proprieties and music do not flourish, punishments will not be properly awarded. When punishments are not properly awarded, the people do not know how to move hand or foot.
'Therefore a superior man considers it necessary that the names he uses may be spoken appropriately, and also that what he speaks may be carried out appropriately. What the superior man requires, is just that in his words there may be nothing incorrect.'
D: Refashioned:
Zilu said, 'The ruler of Wei is waiting for you to help govern. What would you do first?' The Master said, 'Rectify the names.' Zilu laughed: 'How impractical! Why is that necessary?' The Master said, 'How uncultivated you are, Yu. A superior person is cautious about what he does not understand.

If names are not correct, language does not accord with truth. If language does not accord with truth, affairs cannot be carried out successfully. When affairs fail, propriety and music decay. When propriety and music decay, punishments miss their mark. When punishments miss their mark, the people do not know how to act.

Therefore, the superior person insists that names be used correctly and that what he says can be carried out. In his words, there must be nothing careless.'
D: Elegant: If names are wrong, language lies. If language lies, nothing works. If nothing works, culture decays. If culture decays, justice fails. If justice fails, the people are lost. — So the first act of governance is to call things what they are.
D: Distilled: All political dysfunction begins with the corruption of language.
Notes: Zhengming (正名, rectification of names) — one of the most influential concepts in the Analects. Confucius argues that political and moral order depends on linguistic accuracy: when words lose their proper meanings, the entire chain of governance breaks. This directly anticipates Orwell's 'Politics and the English Language' and connects to FUALL's Reason: clear thinking requires honest language. The cascade (names → language → affairs → Li → punishment → the people) is one of the most systematic arguments in the Analects.

D: Original:
Fan Ch'ih requested to be taught husbandry. The Master said, 'I am not so good for that as an old husbandman.' He requested also to be taught gardening, and was answered, 'I am not so good for that as an old gardener.'
Fan Ch'ih having gone out, the Master said, 'A small man, indeed, is Fan Hsu!
If a superior love propriety, the people will not dare not to be reverent. If he love righteousness, the people will not dare not to submit to his example. If he love good faith, the people will not dare not to be sincere. Now, when these things obtain, the people from all quarters will come to him, bearing their children on their backs;-- what need has he of a knowledge of husbandry?'
D: Refashioned: Fan Chi asked to learn farming. The Master said, 'An old farmer would be better at that.' He asked to learn gardening. 'An old gardener would be better.' After Fan Chi left, the Master said, 'What a small-minded man. If a ruler loves propriety, the people will be reverent. If he loves righteousness, they will follow his lead. If he loves good faith, they will be sincere. When that happens, people from everywhere will come carrying their children to his domain. What need does such a ruler have for farming skills?'
D: Elegant: Fan Chi wanted to learn farming. Confucius told him to find a farmer. Then, privately: 'If leaders cultivate propriety, righteousness, and good faith, the people will come to them from everywhere. Why would such a leader need to know how to plow?'
D: Distilled: A leader's job is not to do every task but to create conditions under which all tasks get done.
Notes: Confucius is not dismissing practical labor but insisting on the priority of moral leadership. Li (禮), Yi (義), and Xin (信) — the ruler's three tools. Fan Chi (樊遲) is called 'small-minded' (xiao ren) not for wanting to farm but for confusing the leader's role with the laborer's. Connects to FUALL's educational institutions: the Academy trains leaders, the Guilds train practitioners — both are necessary, but they serve different functions.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Though a man may be able to recite the three hundred odes, yet if, when intrusted with a governmental charge, he knows not how to act, or if, when sent to any quarter on a mission, he cannot give his replies unassisted, notwithstanding the extent of his learning, of what practical use is it?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'A man may be able to recite all three hundred Odes, but if he cannot apply his learning when given a government assignment, or cannot handle a diplomatic mission independently — what practical use is all that learning?'
D: Elegant: Three hundred poems memorized — and unable to govern a province or handle a diplomat. What was the learning for?
D: Distilled: Knowledge that cannot be applied to real responsibilities is not yet understanding.
Notes: The Shijing (詩經, Book of Odes/Poetry) — 305 poems that formed the core curriculum of Zhou education. Confucius consistently valued application over memorization. Connects to FUALL's Academy: learning must produce competence, not merely erudition.

D: Original: The Master said, 'When a prince's personal conduct is correct, his government is effective without the issuing of orders. If his personal conduct is not correct, he may issue orders, but they will not be followed.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'When a ruler's personal conduct is correct, things get done without orders. When his personal conduct is not correct, he may give orders, but no one will follow them.'
D: Elegant: If the ruler is right, no orders are needed. If the ruler is wrong, no orders are obeyed.
D: Distilled: Authority flows from personal example, not from the power to command.
Notes: Qi shen zheng (其身正) — 'when one's person is correct.' A restatement of the core Confucian principle of government by moral example (De, 德). Nearly identical to 13.13. Connects to FUALL's ideational domain of power: legitimate authority derives from demonstrated character.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The governments of Lu and Wei are brothers.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The governments of Lu and Wei are like brothers.'
D: Elegant: Lu and Wei are brothers — both equally dysfunctional.
D: Distilled: Related states often share the same failures.
Notes: Lu (魯) — Confucius's home state. Wei (衛) — a state he visited frequently. Both were descended from the founders of the Zhou dynasty (the Duke of Zhou and Kang Shu, respectively, who were brothers). Both states had weak rulers dominated by powerful families. The comparison is rueful: shared ancestry produced shared dysfunction.

D: Original: The Master said of Ching, a scion of the ducal family of Wei, that he knew the economy of a family well. When he began to have means, he said, 'Ha! here is a collection!' When they were a little increased, he said, 'Ha! this is complete!' When he had become rich, he said, 'Ha! this is admirable!'
D: Refashioned: The Master said of Jing, a prince of Wei, that he understood household management well. When he first acquired means, he said, 'This is a start!' As they grew, he said, 'This is adequate!' When he became wealthy, he said, 'This is splendid!'
D: Elegant: A prince of Wei with growing wealth said: 'A start... enough... splendid!' — the rare art of contentment at every stage.
D: Distilled: The person who finds satisfaction at each stage of sufficiency has mastered what wealth cannot teach.
Notes: Jing (荊) — a prince of Wei known for frugal household management. Contentment (zhi zu, 知足) is a Confucian and Daoist virtue. The passage models the right relationship with wealth: appreciation without craving more.

D: Original:
When the Master went to Wei, Zan Yu acted as driver of his carriage.
The Master observed, 'How numerous are the people!'
Yu said, 'Since they are thus numerous, what more shall be done for them?' 'Enrich them,' was the reply.
'And when they have been enriched, what more shall be done?' The Master said, 'Teach them.'
D: Refashioned: When the Master traveled to Wei with Ran You driving, he observed, 'How populous this place is!' Ran You asked, 'Since the people are numerous, what should be done for them?' The Master said, 'Make them prosperous.' 'And after that?' 'Educate them.'
D: Elegant: First enrich the people. Then educate them. Prosperity before instruction — because empty stomachs cannot learn.
D: Distilled: Material security is the precondition for education, not its competitor.
Notes: Fu zhi (富之, enrich them) → Jiao zhi (教之, teach them). The sequence matters: Confucius does not skip material welfare to jump to moral instruction. This three-stage model (population → prosperity → education) is a practical theory of state development. Connects directly to FUALL's framework: the educational institutions (Academy, Temple, Guilds) require a foundation of material stability to function.

D: Original: The Master said, 'If there were (any of the princes) who would employ me, in the course of twelve months, I should have done something considerable. In three years, the government would be perfected.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'If any ruler would employ me, within a year I could accomplish something substantial. In three years, the work would be complete.'
D: Elegant: Give me one year for a foundation. Give me three and the work is done.
D: Distilled: Real reform takes not forever but a committed period — if the reformer is empowered to act.
Notes: Confucius's confidence in his own abilities stands in contrast to his lifelong inability to find a ruler willing to employ him fully. The timeline — one year for visible progress, three for completion — is practical, not utopian. Confucius was roughly 56 when he said this, still hoping for political appointment.

D: Original: The Master said, '"If good men were to govern a country in succession for a hundred years, they would be able to transform the violently bad, and dispense with capital punishments." True indeed is this saying!'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The saying goes: "If good people governed a country for a hundred consecutive years, they could transform even the violent and abolish capital punishment." How true that is!'
D: Elegant: A century of good governance can transform even the violent and make executions unnecessary. The ancients said it — and they were right.
D: Distilled: Sustained moral leadership over generations can transform a society's worst elements.
Notes: The hundred-year timeline for transformation through De (virtue) rather than Fa (punishment). Confucius endorses an ancient saying — the reform of society is generational work, not a single leader's achievement. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: decline is gradual, and so is restoration.

D: Original: The Master said, 'If a truly royal ruler were to arise, it would still require a generation, and then virtue would prevail.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Even if a truly royal ruler arose, it would still take a full generation for virtue to prevail.'
D: Elegant: Even a sage-king needs a generation before virtue prevails.
D: Distilled: Even the best ruler cannot accelerate moral transformation beyond a generation.
Notes: A correction of the idealistic view that a single great leader can instantly reform society. Thirty years (一世, one generation) is Confucius's minimum estimate. This realism distinguishes Confucian thinking from utopianism. Connects to FUALL's framework: institutional rebuilding is generational work.

D: Original: The Master said, 'If a minister make his own conduct correct, what difficulty will he have in assisting in government? If he cannot rectify himself, what has he to do with rectifying others?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'If a leader corrects his own conduct, what difficulty will he have in governing? If he cannot correct himself, how can he correct others?'
D: Elegant: If you can govern yourself, governing others is easy. If you cannot — do not try.
D: Distilled: The authority to correct others begins with correcting yourself.
Notes: Zheng ji (正己, rectify oneself) before zheng ren (正人, rectify others). Nearly identical in substance to 12.17 and 13.6. The repetition across multiple books shows this is not incidental but foundational. Connects to FUALL's principle that institutional reform begins with personal reform.

D: Original: The disciple Zan returning from the court, the Master said to him, 'How are you so late?' He replied, 'We had government business.' The Master said, 'It must have been family affairs. If there had been government business, though I am not now in office, I should have been consulted about it.'
D: Refashioned: When Ran You returned late from court, the Master asked why. Ran You said, 'Government business.' The Master said, 'You mean family business. If it had been state business, I would have been consulted — even though I hold no office.'
D: Elegant: Ran You called it government business. Confucius corrected him: 'If it were government business, I would have been consulted. You mean the family's private affairs.'
D: Distilled: When powerful families call their private interests 'government business,' the state has already been captured.
Notes: The Ji family (季氏) controlled Lu's actual governance while the Duke was nominal head of state. Confucius's correction distinguishes zheng (政, government affairs) from shi (事, private/family affairs). This is a critique of oligarchic capture — when a faction's interests are disguised as state interests. Connects to FUALL's domains of power: social power (elite families) capturing the ideational domain (legitimate governance).

D: Original:
The Duke Ting asked whether there was a single sentence which could make a country prosperous. Confucius replied, 'Such an effect cannot be expected from one sentence.
'There is a saying, however, which people have-- "To be a prince is difficult; to be a minister is not easy."
'If a ruler knows this,-- the difficulty of being a prince,-- may there not be expected from this one sentence the prosperity of his country?'
The duke then said, 'Is there a single sentence which can ruin a country?' Confucius replied, 'Such an effect as that cannot be expected from one sentence. There is, however, the saying which people have-- "I have no pleasure in being a prince, but only in that no one can offer any opposition to what I say!"
'If a ruler's words be good, is it not also good that no one oppose them? But if they are not good, and no one opposes them, may there not be expected from this one sentence the ruin of his country?'
D: Refashioned:
Duke Ding asked whether a single saying could make a country prosper. Confucius said, 'No single saying can guarantee that. But there is a saying: "Being a ruler is difficult; being a minister is not easy." If a ruler truly understood this difficulty, that understanding might come close to making a country prosper.'

The Duke then asked whether a single saying could ruin a country. Confucius replied, 'No single saying can guarantee that either. But there is a saying: "My only pleasure in being ruler is that no one opposes what I say." If a ruler says good things and no one opposes them — fine. But if he says bad things and no one opposes them, that could come close to ruining a country.'
D: Elegant: One saying to prosper: 'Being a ruler is hard.' One saying to ruin: 'No one may oppose me.' The first breeds humility; the second breeds tyranny.
D: Distilled: A ruler who knows governance is hard will govern well; a ruler who silences opposition will govern into ruin.
Notes: Duke Ding of Lu (魯定公, r. 509-495 BCE). The second saying — 'no one opposes me' — is Confucius's most direct critique of autocracy. The logic: unchecked authority eliminates the feedback loops that prevent catastrophic error. Connects directly to FUALL's Lib/Auth spectrum and to Mill's On Liberty: suppression of dissent is self-destructive for the suppressor.

D: Original:
The Duke of Sheh asked about government.
The Master said, 'Good government obtains, when those who are near are made happy, and those who are far off are attracted.'
D: Refashioned: The Duke of She asked about government. The Master said, 'Good government is when those who are near are happy, and those who are far away are drawn to come.'
D: Elegant: Make the near happy and the far will come on their own.
D: Distilled: The best advertisement for a government is the contentment of the people already under it.
Notes: The Duke of She (葉公) — a powerful local official in Chu. Jin zhe yue, yuan zhe lai (近者悅，遠者來) — a concise theory of soft power. Good governance attracts; bad governance repels. Connects to FUALL's ideational domain of power: legitimacy attracts loyalty voluntarily.

D: Original: Tsze-hsia, being governor of Chu-fu, asked about government. The Master said, 'Do not be desirous to have things done quickly; do not look at small advantages. Desire to have things done quickly prevents their being done thoroughly. Looking at small advantages prevents great affairs from being accomplished.'
D: Refashioned: Zixia, serving as governor of Jufu, asked about government. The Master said, 'Do not desire speed. Do not fixate on small gains. Wanting things done quickly prevents thoroughness. Fixating on small gains prevents great accomplishments.'
D: Elegant: Haste prevents thoroughness. Small gains prevent great ones. The governor who chases either achieves neither.
D: Distilled: The pursuit of quick wins is the enemy of lasting achievement.
Notes: Wu yu su (無欲速, do not desire speed) — a warning against short-term thinking in governance. Zixia (子夏, Bu Shang) — a disciple known for scholarship. Connects to FUALL's framework: institutional building is slow work; rushing it produces fragile structures.

D: Original:
The Duke of Sheh informed Confucius, saying, 'Among us here there are those who may be styled upright in their conduct. If their father have stolen a sheep, they will bear witness to the fact.'
Confucius said, 'Among us, in our part of the country, those who are upright are different from this. The father conceals the misconduct of the son, and the son conceals the misconduct of the father. Uprightness is to be found in this.'
D: Refashioned: The Duke of She told Confucius, 'Among my people, there are those so upright that if a father steals a sheep, the son will testify against him.' Confucius replied, 'Among my people, uprightness means something different. Fathers conceal the faults of sons, and sons conceal the faults of fathers. True uprightness lies in this.'
D: Elegant: The Duke boasted of sons who inform on fathers. Confucius replied: 'Among us, fathers and sons protect each other. That is where uprightness lives.'
D: Distilled: A society that demands children betray their parents has confused legal compliance with moral integrity.
Notes: One of the most debated passages in the Analects. Confucius places family loyalty (Xiao, 孝) above abstract legal obligation. The tension between family bonds and civic duty is unresolved and deliberate — Confucius is not endorsing cover-ups but asserting that a legal system that destroys familial trust is worse than the crimes it punishes. This connects to FUALL's framework: the family is the foundational social institution; a state that undermines it undermines its own foundation.

D: Original: Fan Ch'ih asked about perfect virtue. The Master said, 'It is, in retirement, to be sedately grave; in the management of business, to be reverently attentive; in intercourse with others, to be strictly sincere. Though a man go among rude, uncultivated tribes, these qualities may not be neglected.'
D: Refashioned: Fan Chi asked about perfect virtue. The Master said, 'In private, be quietly dignified. In business, be conscientiously attentive. In dealing with others, be scrupulously honest. These qualities should not be abandoned even among uncivilized peoples.'
D: Elegant: Dignity in private. Diligence in work. Honesty with others. Hold these even among the uncivilized.
D: Distilled: The standards of conduct for a virtuous person do not change based on the company they keep.
Notes: Ren (仁) defined through three practical contexts: private life, professional life, and social relations. The universality clause — 'even among rude tribes' — makes this a cross-cultural ethical claim. Connects to FUALL's Right: moral principles are not culturally relative at the foundational level.

D: Original:
Tsze-kung asked, saying, 'What qualities must a man possess to entitle him to be called an officer? The Master said, 'He who in his conduct of himself maintains a sense of shame, and when sent to any quarter will not disgrace his prince's commission, deserves to be called an officer.'
Tsze-kung pursued, 'I venture to ask who may be placed in the next lower rank?' And he was told, 'He whom the circle of his relatives pronounce to be filial, whom his fellow-villagers and neighbours pronounce to be fraternal.'
Again the disciple asked, 'I venture to ask about the class still next in order.' The Master said, 'They are determined to be sincere in what they say, and to carry out what they do. They are obstinate little men. Yet perhaps they may make the next class.'
Tsze-kung finally inquired, 'Of what sort are those of the present day, who engage in government?' The Master said 'Pooh! they are so many pecks and hampers, not worth being taken into account.'
D: Refashioned: Zigong asked what qualities entitle a person to be called an officer. The Master said, 'One who maintains a sense of shame in his conduct, and when sent abroad does not disgrace his mission.' Zigong asked about the next rank. 'One whom his relatives call filial and his neighbors call respectful.' And the next? 'One who insists on keeping his word and finishing what he starts — a stubborn, narrow person, but perhaps acceptable.' And the current generation of officials? 'Pah! Mere measuring cups — hardly worth counting.'
D: Elegant: The best officer acts with shame and honor. The next is filial and respectful. The next merely keeps his word. And today's officials? Measuring cups. Not worth counting.
D: Distilled: The hierarchy of public service: integrity, then character, then mere reliability — and below even that, today's hollow officials.
Notes: Chi (恥, sense of shame) as the essential quality of public service. Confucius's hierarchy descends from principled conduct to filial duty to mere reliability to worthlessness. Dou shao (斗筲, measuring cups and baskets) — containers of trivial capacity. The contempt for contemporary officials is sharp. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: when institutional standards degrade, the people who fill positions degrade with them.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Since I cannot get men pursuing the due medium, to whom I might communicate my instructions, I must find the ardent and the cautiously-decided. The ardent will advance and lay hold of truth; the cautiously-decided will keep themselves from what is wrong.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Since I cannot find people who follow the middle way to teach, I must settle for the ardent and the cautious. The ardent will rush forward to seize truth. The cautious will at least keep themselves from doing wrong.'
D: Elegant: Unable to find students of the middle way, Confucius settled for two types: the impulsive who charge toward truth, and the cautious who at least avoid error.
D: Distilled: When you cannot find balanced people, work with the bold who reach for truth and the careful who avoid wrong.
Notes: Zhonghang (中行, the middle way) — the ideal temperament. Kuang (狂, the ardent/impulsive) and Juan (狷, the cautiously restrained) — second-best alternatives. Confucius's pragmatism: the perfect student is rare; work with what you have. Connects to FUALL's educational philosophy: meet people where they are.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'The people of the south have a saying-- "A man without constancy cannot be either a wizard or a doctor." Good!
'Inconstant in his virtue, he will be visited with disgrace.'
The Master said, 'This arises simply from not attending to the prognostication.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The southerners have a saying: "A man without constancy cannot be a shaman or a doctor." True! "One who is inconstant in virtue will bring disgrace upon himself." The Master added, 'This comes from not heeding the signs.'
D: Elegant: Even shamans and doctors require constancy. What hope has a person of inconsistent virtue?
D: Distilled: Constancy is the minimum requirement for any practice — spiritual, medical, or moral.
Notes: Heng (恆, constancy/perseverance) — a virtue Confucius considers essential. The quote is from the Yi Jing (易經, Book of Changes), Hexagram 32 (Heng). The passage links moral constancy to practical competence: if you cannot sustain effort, you cannot master anything.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The superior man is affable, but not adulatory; the mean man is adulatory, but not affable.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person is agreeable but not sycophantic. The inferior person is sycophantic but not agreeable.'
D: Elegant: The superior person is harmonious without flattering. The inferior person flatters without being harmonious.
D: Distilled: Agreeableness is not agreement; the person who flatters everyone agrees with no one.
Notes: He (和, harmony) vs. Tong (同, conformity/sycophancy) — a key Confucian distinction. Being harmonious means integrating diverse perspectives; being sycophantic means surrendering your own. Connects to FUALL's Agora: productive discourse requires honest engagement, not performative agreement.

D: Original: Tsze-kung asked, saying, 'What do you say of a man who is loved by all the people of his neighborhood?' The Master replied, 'We may not for that accord our approval of him.' 'And what do you say of him who is hated by all the people of his neighborhood?' The Master said, 'We may not for that conclude that he is bad. It is better than either of these cases that the good in the neighborhood love him, and the bad hate him.'
D: Refashioned: Zigong asked, 'What do you think of a man loved by everyone in his neighborhood?' The Master said, 'That is not sufficient.' 'And one hated by everyone?' 'That is not sufficient either. Better if the good people love him and the bad people hate him.'
D: Elegant: Loved by all? Suspicious. Hated by all? Incomplete. Best: loved by the good, hated by the bad.
D: Distilled: Universal approval is as suspect as universal hostility; the right test is which people approve and which do not.
Notes: Confucius rejects both populism and contrarianism. The correct measure of character is differential approval: good people should recognize you and bad people should be discomforted by you. Connects to FUALL's Reason: consensus is not truth; the quality of your supporters matters more than their quantity.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The superior man is easy to serve and difficult to please. If you try to please him in any way which is not accordant with right, he will not be pleased. But in his employment of men, he uses them according to their capacity. The mean man is difficult to serve, and easy to please. If you try to please him, though it be in a way which is not accordant with right, he may be pleased. But in his employment of men, he wishes them to be equal to everything.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person is easy to serve but hard to please. Try to please him through anything contrary to right, and he will not be pleased. But he employs people according to their capacities. The inferior person is hard to serve but easy to please. Please him through wrong means and he is delighted. But he demands that his employees be good at everything.'
D: Elegant: The superior person is easy to serve because he expects only what you can give. The inferior person is hard to serve because he demands everything while accepting anything to be flattered.
D: Distilled: Good leaders are hard to flatter but fair in their expectations; bad leaders are easy to flatter but impossible to satisfy.
Notes: Junzi (君子) vs. Xiaoren (小人) in management style. The superior person uses people according to their capacity (qi qi, 器器) — a management principle. The inferior person demands universal competence, which is both unrealistic and tyrannical. Connects to FUALL's Guilds: good leadership matches people to roles.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The superior man has a dignified ease without pride. The mean man has pride without a dignified ease.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person has dignified composure without arrogance. The inferior person has arrogance without dignified composure.'
D: Elegant: Composure without arrogance is strength. Arrogance without composure is weakness pretending to be strength.
D: Distilled: True dignity needs no arrogance; arrogance betrays the absence of true dignity.
Notes: Tai (泰, dignified ease) vs. Jiao (驕, pride/arrogance). The distinction is internal: genuine confidence (grounded in character) produces calm. Insecurity produces compensatory display. Connects to FUALL's Junzi ideal: character produces presence; pretension produces posturing.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The firm, the enduring, the simple, and the modest are near to virtue.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Firmness, endurance, simplicity, and modesty are near to virtue.'
D: Elegant: Firm, enduring, simple, modest — not yet virtue itself, but its nearest neighbors.
D: Distilled: The qualities closest to virtue are firmness, endurance, simplicity, and restraint.
Notes: Gang yi mu ne (剛毅木訥) — four qualities that are near Ren (仁) but not identical to it. Confucius values raw character over polish. These are prerequisites, not accomplishments. Connects to FUALL's framework: virtue is built on a foundation of basic character traits.

D: Original: Tsze-lu asked, saying, 'What qualities must a man possess to entitle him to be called a scholar?' The Master said, 'He must be thus,-- earnest, urgent, and bland:-- among his friends, earnest and urgent; among his brethren, bland.'
D: Refashioned: Zilu asked what qualities a person needed to be called a scholar. The Master said, 'He must be earnest and encouraging with friends, and warm with brothers — earnest and encouraging among friends, warm among brothers.'
D: Elegant: Among friends: earnest and challenging. Among family: warm and generous. That is the scholar.
D: Distilled: A scholar is demanding with friends and gentle with family — matching his manner to the relationship.
Notes: Shi (士, scholar-official) — the class Confucius was training. Qie qie, si si (切切偲偲) — earnest mutual encouragement. Yi yi (怡怡) — warmth and harmony. Different relationships require different emotional registers. Connects to FUALL's educational institutions: the Academy demands rigor, the Temple provides warmth.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Let a good man teach the people seven years, and they may then likewise be employed in war.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Let good men train the people for seven years, and they may then be employed in military service.'
D: Elegant: Seven years of moral training before military service. Discipline the soul before arming the body.
D: Distilled: A people must be educated in virtue before they can be trusted with weapons.
Notes: The seven-year timeline for civic education before military service. Confucius subordinates military power to moral preparation. Connects to FUALL's domains of power: physical power (military) must be grounded in ideational power (moral education). Also connects to 13.30.

D: Original: The Master said, 'To lead an uninstructed people to war, is to throw them away.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'To send untrained people into war is to throw them away.'
D: Elegant: To lead the untrained into battle is not warfare — it is waste.
D: Distilled: Sending unprepared people to fight is not courage but cruelty.
Notes: Yi bu jiao min zhan, shi wei qi zhi (以不教民戰，是謂棄之) — 'to use the uninstructed people in war is to discard them.' Paired with 13.29: first educate, then (and only then) deploy. Connects to FUALL's principle that institutions must prepare people before placing demands on them.

D: Original: Book 14: Hsien Wan

Hsien asked what was shameful. The Master said, 'When good government prevails in a state, to be thinking only of salary; and, when bad government prevails, to be thinking, in the same way, only of salary;-- this is shameful.'
D: Refashioned: Xian asked what was shameful. The Master said, 'When government is good, to think only of your salary. When government is bad, to think only of your salary. In either case — that is shameful.'
D: Elegant: Good government or bad, thinking only of your paycheck is shameful either way.
D: Distilled: The person who serves only for compensation is a mercenary regardless of the regime.
Notes: Xian (原憲, Yuan Si) — a disciple known for poverty and integrity. Lu (祿, salary/emolument) as the measure of a shallow official. Confucius does not oppose compensation but opposes making it the sole motivation. Connects to FUALL's framework: public service requires orientation beyond self-interest.

D: Original:
'When the love of superiority, boasting, resentments, and covetousness are repressed, this may be deemed perfect virtue.'
The Master said, 'This may be regarded as the achievement of what is difficult. But I do not know that it is to be deemed perfect virtue.'
D: Refashioned: Someone said, 'If a person suppresses his desire for superiority, boasting, resentment, and covetousness, can he be called virtuous?' The Master said, 'That is certainly difficult to achieve. But I do not know that it constitutes perfect virtue.'
D: Elegant: Suppressing vice is hard. But hardship alone is not virtue — it is only the absence of vice.
D: Distilled: Not doing wrong is difficult, but difficulty alone does not make it virtue.
Notes: Ren (仁) requires more than the suppression of bad impulses; it requires the active cultivation of goodness. Confucius distinguishes between negative achievement (restraint) and positive achievement (benevolence). Connects to FUALL's ethics: virtue is not merely avoiding harm but actively pursuing good.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The scholar who cherishes the love of comfort is not fit to be deemed a scholar.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'A scholar who loves comfort does not deserve to be called a scholar.'
D: Elegant: A scholar who loves comfort has chosen the wrong profession.
D: Distilled: The pursuit of truth and the pursuit of comfort are incompatible commitments.
Notes: Shi (士, scholar) — the class Confucius was training for public service. The rejection of comfort (an ju, 安居) echoes 4.14 and 15.31. Connects to FUALL's Academy: intellectual seriousness requires willingness to sacrifice material ease.

D: Original: The Master said, 'When good government prevails in a state, language may be lofty and bold, and actions the same. When bad government prevails, the actions may be lofty and bold, but the language may be with some reserve.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Under good government, both speech and action should be bold. Under bad government, actions should still be bold, but speech should be more guarded.'
D: Elegant: In good times, speak and act boldly. In bad times, act boldly still — but guard your tongue.
D: Distilled: Courage in action is always required; courage in speech depends on whether the state can tolerate it.
Notes: A pragmatic concession: Confucius does not demand suicidal candor under tyranny, but he does demand that conduct never be compromised. The distinction between yan (言, speech) and xing (行, action) under different political conditions shows political realism. Connects to FUALL's Lib/Auth spectrum: free speech is a function of political environment.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The virtuous will be sure to speak correctly, but those whose speech is good may not always be virtuous. Men of principle are sure to be bold, but those who are bold may not always be men of principle.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The virtuous will certainly speak well, but those who speak well are not necessarily virtuous. People of principle will certainly be brave, but the brave are not necessarily people of principle.'
D: Elegant: Virtue always produces good speech; good speech does not always indicate virtue. Principle always produces courage; courage does not always indicate principle.
D: Distilled: Virtue implies eloquence and courage, but neither eloquence nor courage implies virtue.
Notes: A logical point about necessary vs. sufficient conditions. De (virtue) → Yan (good speech) and De → Yong (courage), but not the reverse. Connects to FUALL's Reason: surface indicators (eloquence, bravery) are unreliable measures of underlying character.

D: Original: Nan-kung Kwo, submitting an inquiry to Confucius, said, 'I was skillful at archery, and Ao could move a boat along upon the land, but neither of them died a natural death. Yu and Chi personally wrought at the toils of husbandry, and they became possessors of the kingdom.' The Master made no reply; but when Nan-kung Kwo went out, he said, 'A superior man indeed is this! An esteemer of virtue indeed is this!'
D: Refashioned: Nangong Kuo observed to Confucius, 'Yi was a great archer and Ao could drag a boat across dry land, yet neither died a natural death. Yu and Hou Ji worked the land with their own hands and gained the whole kingdom.' The Master said nothing. But after Nangong Kuo left, he said, 'What a superior person! What a lover of virtue!'
D: Elegant: The strongmen died violent deaths. The farmers inherited the kingdom. Confucius said nothing — then praised the man who noticed.
D: Distilled: Those who rely on force perish by it; those who cultivate virtue inherit what force could never hold.
Notes: Yi (羿) — legendary archer who overthrew a dynasty. Ao (奡/寒浞) — strongman. Both died violently. Yu (禹) — founder of the Xia dynasty, famous for flood control. Hou Ji (后稷) — ancestor of the Zhou, god of agriculture. The contrast: force vs. cultivation, conquest vs. service. Connects to FUALL's domains of power: physical power is less durable than ideational power.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Superior men, and yet not always virtuous, there have been, alas! But there never has been a mean man, and, at the same time, virtuous.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'There have been superior people who were not always virtuous, alas. But there has never been an inferior person who was virtuous.'
D: Elegant: Even the best sometimes fall short of virtue. But the petty never reach it at all.
D: Distilled: A superior person may occasionally fail at virtue; an inferior person never achieves it.
Notes: Junzi (君子) can fail; Xiaoren (小人) cannot succeed. The asymmetry is important: moral capacity is a prerequisite, not a guarantee. Connects to FUALL's educational philosophy: character development creates the potential for virtue, but does not guarantee it.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Can there be love which does not lead to strictness with its object? Can there be loyalty which does not lead to the instruction of its object?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Can there be love that does not demand the best from its object? Can there be loyalty that does not instruct?'
D: Elegant: Love that does not challenge is not love. Loyalty that does not teach is not loyalty.
D: Distilled: Real love demands growth; real loyalty demands honesty.
Notes: Ai (愛, love) and Zhong (忠, loyalty) both require active engagement, not passive support. Confucius rejects sentimental love and blind loyalty alike. Connects to FUALL's Right: genuine care for others requires the courage to correct them.

D: Original: The Master said, 'In preparing the governmental notifications, P'i Shan first made the rough draft; Shi-shu examined and discussed its contents; Tsze-yu, the manager of Foreign intercourse, then polished the style; and, finally, Tsze-ch'an of Tung-li gave it the proper elegance and finish.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'When the government of Zheng drafted official documents, Pi Shan wrote the first draft, Shi Shu reviewed the content, Ziyu the diplomat polished the style, and Zichan of Dongli gave it final elegance.'
D: Elegant: Draft, review, polish, refine — four hands shaping one document. That is how Zheng governed its communications.
D: Distilled: Excellence in governance is a collaborative process, not a solo performance.
Notes: Zichan (子產, Gongsun Qiao) — prime minister of Zheng (died 522 BCE), one of the historical figures Confucius most admired. The four-stage drafting process shows institutional competence: each person contributes their specific skill. Connects to FUALL's Guilds: competent governance requires specialized roles working in sequence.

D: Original:
Some one asked about Tsze-ch'an. The Master said, 'He was a kind man.'
He asked about Tsze-hsi. The Master said, 'That man! That man!'
He asked about Kwan Chung. 'For him,' said the Master, 'the city of Pien, with three hundred families, was taken from the chief of the Po family, who did not utter a murmuring word, though, to the end of his life, he had only coarse rice to eat.'
D: Refashioned: Someone asked about Zichan. The Master said, 'He was a kind man.' About Zixi? 'That man! That man!' About Guan Zhong? 'He was the kind of man who could seize the city of Pian with its three hundred families from the Bo clan, and the chief of Bo, reduced to eating coarse rice for the rest of his life, never uttered a word of complaint against him.'
D: Elegant: Zichan: kind. Zixi: unworthy of comment. Guan Zhong: so just that the man he ruined never complained.
D: Distilled: The ultimate measure of a statesman is whether even those he defeats acknowledge his fairness.
Notes: Zichan (子產) — minister of Zheng (see 14.9). Zixi (子西) — minister of Chu, dismissed with contempt. Guan Zhong (管仲) — prime minister of Qi under Duke Huan, a figure Confucius both criticized (for lack of ritual propriety) and praised (for his political achievements). The anecdote about Bo's chief shows Guan Zhong's governance was so fair that even its losers accepted it. Connects to FUALL's ideational domain: legitimate authority is accepted even by those it constrains.

D: Original: The Master said, 'To be poor without murmuring is difficult. To be rich without being proud is easy.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'To be poor without complaining is hard. To be rich without being arrogant is easy.'
D: Elegant: Poverty without bitterness is hard. Wealth without arrogance is easy. Most people get both wrong.
D: Distilled: The harder discipline is cheerfulness in poverty, not humility in wealth.
Notes: Confucius inverts the expected ranking: we assume the rich face harder moral challenges, but contentment in poverty requires deeper character. Connects to 1.15 where Confucius and Zigong discuss being poor yet joyful vs. rich yet courteous.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Mang Kung-ch'o is more than fit to be chief officer in the families of Chao and Wei, but he is not fit to be great officer to either of the States Tang or Hsieh.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Meng Gongchuo is more than qualified to manage the great households of Zhao or Wei, but unfit to serve as minister to the smaller states of Teng or Xue.'
D: Elegant: Some people are perfect for large, stable systems and completely wrong for small, demanding ones. Know the difference.
D: Distilled: Competence in one context does not guarantee competence in another; scale and scope matter.
