Notes: Meng Gongchuo (孟公綽) — a minister in Lu known for integrity but limited ambition. Zhao and Wei — large stable families requiring steady management. Teng and Xue — small states requiring versatile, dynamic governance. The passage illustrates Confucius's emphasis on matching talent to role. Connects to FUALL's Guilds: institutional design must match personnel to appropriate scope.

D: Original:
Tsze-lu asked what constituted a COMPLETE man. The Master said, 'Suppose a man with the knowledge of Tsang Wu-chung, the freedom from covetousness of Kung-ch'o, the bravery of Chwang of Pien, and the varied talents of Zan Ch'iu; add to these the accomplishments of the rules of propriety and music:-- such a one might be reckoned a COMPLETE man.'
He then added, 'But what is the necessity for a complete man of the present day to have all these things? The man, who in the view of gain, thinks of righteousness; who in the view of danger is prepared to give up his life; and who does not forget an old agreement however far back it extends:-- such a man may be reckoned a COMPLETE man.'
D: Refashioned: Zilu asked what makes a complete person. The Master said, 'Combine the wisdom of Zang Wuzhong, the selflessness of Meng Gongchuo, the courage of Zhuang of Bian, and the versatile skills of Ran Qiu, then refine them with propriety and music — that would be a complete person.' Then he added, 'But does a complete person today really need all that? Someone who thinks of righteousness when he sees profit, is ready to give his life in danger, and honors old commitments no matter how much time has passed — he might qualify.'
D: Elegant: The ideal: wisdom, selflessness, courage, and skill, refined by culture. The realistic standard: choose justice over profit, face death without flinching, and keep your oldest promises.
D: Distilled: A complete person is not the one with every excellence but the one who acts rightly when it costs something.
Notes: Cheng ren (成人, the complete person) — Confucius's ideal of integrated human development. Zang Wuzhong — known for wisdom. Meng Gongchuo — for unselfishness. Zhuang of Bian — for bravery. Ran Qiu — for administrative skill. The 'reduced' modern standard focuses on three tests: justice vs. profit (Yi, 義), courage in danger (Yong, 勇), and fidelity to commitments (Xin, 信). Connects to FUALL's Wisdom: the complete person integrates Reality, Reason, and Right.

D: Original:
The Master asked Kung-ming Chia about Kung- shu Wan, saying, 'Is it true that your master speaks not, laughs not, and takes not?'
Kung-ming Chia replied, 'This has arisen from the reporters going beyond the truth.-- My master speaks when it is the time to speak, and so men do not get tired of his speaking. He laughs when there is occasion to be joyful, and so men do not get tired of his laughing. He takes when it is consistent with righteousness to do so, and so men do not get tired of his taking.' The Master said, 'So! But is it so with him?'
D: Refashioned: The Master asked Gongming Jia about Gongshu Wen, 'Is it true your master never speaks, never laughs, never takes anything?' Gongming Jia said, 'That reputation goes too far. My master speaks when the time is right, so people don't tire of his speech. He laughs when there's reason for joy, so people don't tire of his laughter. He takes only when it is just, so people don't tire of his taking.' The Master said, 'Is that really so? Is that truly how he is?'
D: Elegant: He speaks when it is time, laughs when there is cause, takes when it is just — and no one tires of any of it. 'Is it really so?' Confucius wondered.
D: Distilled: The highest reputation is earned not by abstinence but by perfect timing — speaking, enjoying, and accepting only when the moment is right.
Notes: Gongshu Wen (公叔文子) — a minister of Wei, posthumously titled 'Wen' (cultured). The passage illustrates the Zhongyong principle: excellence is not suppression but calibration. Confucius's skeptical closing question ('Is it so?') suggests such perfect calibration is extremely rare.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Tsang Wu-chung, keeping possession of Fang, asked of the duke of Lu to appoint a successor to him in his family. Although it may be said that he was not using force with his sovereign, I believe he was.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Zang Wuzhong held the city of Fang while negotiating with the Duke of Lu to appoint a successor to his family line. People say he wasn't coercing his sovereign. I believe he was.'
D: Elegant: He held a city hostage while 'asking' the duke for a favor. People called it diplomacy. Confucius called it coercion.
D: Distilled: Leverage disguised as a request is still coercion.
Notes: Zang Wuzhong (臧武仲) — a minister of Lu praised for wisdom elsewhere (14.13) but criticized here for political manipulation. Using military assets as negotiating leverage against one's own sovereign violates the minister-ruler relationship. Confucius distinguishes between apparent and actual dynamics of power.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The duke Wan of Tsin was crafty and not upright. The duke Hwan of Ch'i was upright and not crafty.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Duke Wen of Jin was crafty but not upright. Duke Huan of Qi was upright but not crafty.'
D: Elegant: One duke was clever but dishonest. The other was honest but unsubtle. Confucius preferred the honest one.
D: Distilled: Uprightness without cunning is preferable to cunning without uprightness.
Notes: Duke Wen of Jin (晉文公, r. 636-628 BCE) and Duke Huan of Qi (齊桓公, r. 685-643 BCE) — the two greatest hegemons of the Spring and Autumn period. Both achieved dominance, but by different means. Confucius's preference for Duke Huan (upright/zheng 正) over Duke Wen (crafty/gui 詭) reflects his consistent prioritization of moral character over political effectiveness.

D: Original:
Tsze-lu said, 'The Duke Hwan caused his brother Chiu to be killed, when Shao Hu died with his master, but Kwan Chung did not die. May not I say that he was wanting in virtue?'
The Master said, 'The Duke Hwan assembled all the princes together, and that not with weapons of war and chariots:-- it was all through the influence of Kwan Chung. Whose beneficence was like his? Whose beneficence was like his?'
D: Refashioned: Zilu said, 'When Duke Huan killed his brother Jiu, Shao Hu died alongside his master, but Guan Zhong did not. Was Guan Zhong lacking in virtue?' The Master said, 'Duke Huan united all the princes without resorting to military force — that was entirely Guan Zhong's achievement. Who else showed such benevolence? Who else showed such benevolence?'
D: Elegant: Guan Zhong survived his lord's murder and served the murderer — and united the realm without war. Confucius asked: 'Whose benevolence was greater than his?'
D: Distilled: The greatest loyalty is sometimes not dying with your lord but living to achieve what your lord could not.
Notes: Guan Zhong (管仲) — see also 14.18. Duke Huan killed Prince Jiu (Guan Zhong's original master). Shao Hu (召忽) died with Jiu; Guan Zhong defected to Duke Huan and became his chief minister. Confucius's defense of Guan Zhong is surprising: he prioritizes Guan Zhong's civilizational achievement (unifying the princes, preventing barbarian invasion) over personal loyalty. Ren (仁) here is measured by consequences, not by formal fidelity. Connects to FUALL's framework: macro-level impact can outweigh micro-level moral conventions.

D: Original:
Tsze-kung said, 'Kwan Chung, I apprehend, was wanting in virtue. When the Duke Hwan caused his brother Chiu to be killed, Kwan Chung was not able to die with him. Moreover, he became prime minister to Hwan.'
The Master said, 'Kwan Chung acted as prime minister to the Duke Hwan, made him leader of all the princes, and united and rectified the whole kingdom. Down to the present day, the people enjoy the gifts which he conferred. But for Kwan Chung, we should now be wearing our hair unbound, and the lappets of our coats buttoning on the left side.
'Will you require from him the small fidelity of common men and common women, who would commit suicide in a stream or ditch, no one knowing anything about them?'
D: Refashioned: Zigong said, 'Guan Zhong was surely lacking in virtue — when Duke Huan killed Prince Jiu, Guan Zhong did not die with him but instead became Huan's prime minister.' The Master said, 'Guan Zhong helped Duke Huan become leader of all the princes and brought order to the whole realm. To this day, the people enjoy the benefits he secured. Without Guan Zhong, we would be wearing our hair unbound and fastening our clothes on the left — we would have been conquered by barbarians. Would you expect from him the petty fidelity of a common man and woman, dying unknown in a ditch?'
D: Elegant: Without Guan Zhong, we would be wearing barbarian dress. Should he have died in a ditch for personal honor instead of saving a civilization?
D: Distilled: Personal loyalty is a virtue, but preserving a civilization outranks it.
Notes: Pi fa zuo ren (被髮左衽, unbound hair and left-lapelled clothes) — markers of non-Chinese 'barbarian' culture. Confucius argues that Guan Zhong's defection from Prince Jiu preserved Chinese civilization itself, making personal loyalty a lesser concern. This is one of the most consequentialist arguments in the Analects. Connects to FUALL's hierarchy: civilizational survival (the Dao) outweighs individual moral convention.

D: Original:
The great officer, Hsien, who had been family- minister to Kung-shu Wan, ascended to the prince's court in company with Wan.
The Master, having heard of it, said, 'He deserved to be considered WAN (the accomplished).'
D: Refashioned: Gongshu Wen's former household steward, Xian, rose to serve in the prince's court alongside Wen himself. When Confucius heard this, he said, 'Wen truly earned his posthumous title of "Accomplished."'
D: Elegant: He elevated his own servant to stand beside him at court. For that alone, he earned the title 'Accomplished.'
D: Distilled: True greatness is measured by your willingness to elevate those beneath you.
Notes: Gongshu Wen (公叔文子) — see 14.14. The posthumous title Wen (文, cultured/accomplished) was the highest cultural honor. Confucius affirms that elevating talent regardless of origin — even one's own servant — justifies the title. Connects to FUALL's educational principle: merit should determine advancement, not birth.

D: Original:
The Master was speaking about the unprincipled course of the duke Ling of Wei, when Ch'i K'ang said, 'Since he is of such a character, how is it he does not lose his State?'
Confucius said, 'The Chung-shu Yu has the superintendence of his guests and of strangers; the litanist, T'o, has the management of his ancestral temple; and Wang-sun Chia has the direction of the army and forces:-- with such officers as these, how should he lose his State?'
D: Refashioned: The Master was discussing Duke Ling of Wei's unprincipled rule. Ji Kang asked, 'If he is so bad, why hasn't he lost his state?' Confucius replied, 'Because Zhongshu Yu manages his diplomatic guests, the litanist Tuo manages the ancestral temple, and Wangsun Jia commands the military. With officers like these, how would he lose his state?'
D: Elegant: A bad ruler survives when his ministers are good enough to carry the state despite him.
D: Distilled: Incompetent leaders are sustained by competent subordinates — which delays reform but preserves the state.
Notes: Duke Ling of Wei (衛靈公) — frequently criticized for moral failures but his state functioned because of capable ministers. The passage makes a structural point: institutional competence can compensate for leadership failure, at least temporarily. Connects to FUALL's framework: institutions can buffer against poor leadership, but this is a stopgap, not a solution.

D: Original: The Master said, 'He who speaks without modesty will find it difficult to make his words good.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'He who speaks without modesty will find it difficult to live up to his words.'
D: Elegant: Speak without humility and your words will outrun your ability to honor them.
D: Distilled: Immodest promises become debts you cannot repay.
Notes: Yan zhi bu zuo (言之不怍, speaking without shame/modesty). Connects to Xin (信, trustworthiness): extravagant claims erode credibility. A variation on the theme of 4.22 and 4.24.

D: Original:
Chan Ch'ang murdered the Duke Chien of Ch'i.
Confucius bathed, went to court, and informed the duke Ai, saying, 'Chan Hang has slain his sovereign. I beg that you will undertake to punish him.'
The duke said, 'Inform the chiefs of the three families of it.'
Confucius retired, and said, 'Following in the rear of the great officers, I did not dare not to represent such a matter, and my prince says, "Inform the chiefs of the three families of it."'
He went to the chiefs, and informed them, but they would not act. Confucius then said, 'Following in the rear of the great officers, I did not dare not to represent such a matter.'
D: Refashioned: Chen Heng murdered Duke Jian of Qi. Confucius bathed, went to court, and reported to Duke Ai of Lu: 'Chen Heng has slain his sovereign. I request that you punish him.' The Duke said, 'Report it to the three ministers.' Confucius withdrew and said, 'Since I hold rank behind the great officers, I could not fail to report such a thing. And my prince tells me to report it to the ministers.' He went to them, but they refused to act. Confucius said again, 'Since I hold rank behind the great officers, I could not fail to report it.'
D: Elegant: Confucius reported a regicide. The duke deferred to his ministers. The ministers refused. Confucius had done his duty — and everyone else had failed theirs.
D: Distilled: When those in power refuse to act on injustice, the person who reported it is not the one who failed.
Notes: Chen Heng (陳恆/田恆, also Chen Chengzi) murdered Duke Jian of Qi in 481 BCE. Confucius's formal bathing before court shows the gravity of the report. Duke Ai's deflection to the Ji, Meng, and Shu families (the 'three families' who controlled Lu) shows the duke's powerlessness. The passage illustrates institutional paralysis: everyone knows what should be done, no one does it. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: when nominal authority and actual power diverge, justice becomes impossible.

D: Original: Tsze-lu asked how a ruler should be served. The Master said, 'Do not impose on him, and, moreover, withstand him to his face.'
D: Refashioned: Zilu asked how one should serve a ruler. The Master said, 'Do not deceive him — and oppose him to his face when necessary.'
D: Elegant: Never deceive your ruler. And when he is wrong — tell him to his face.
D: Distilled: Honest service means never lying to power and never fearing to confront it.
Notes: Wu qi ye, er fan zhi (勿欺也，而犯之) — 'do not cheat him, and moreover confront him.' The two duties of a minister: honesty (no deception) and courage (direct opposition when needed). This is the Confucian model of loyal dissent. Connects to FUALL's Lib/Auth spectrum: healthy governance requires institutionalized opposition.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The progress of the superior man is upwards; the progress of the mean man is downwards.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person progresses upward. The inferior person progresses downward.'
D: Elegant: The superior person rises. The inferior person sinks. Both are always moving.
D: Distilled: Character is a direction, not a position — you are always either climbing or descending.
Notes: Shang da (上達, reaching upward) vs. Xia da (下達, reaching downward). The 'upward' direction is toward the Dao, virtue, and truth; 'downward' is toward material gain and base impulses. Connects to FUALL's framework: human development is directional — cultivation or degradation, never stasis.

D: Original: The Master said, 'In ancient times, men learned with a view to their own improvement. Now-a-days, men learn with a view to the approbation of others.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'In ancient times, people learned for their own improvement. Nowadays, people learn to impress others.'
D: Elegant: The ancients learned for themselves. We learn for our audience.
D: Distilled: Learning driven by self-improvement produces wisdom; learning driven by others' approval produces performance.
Notes: Wei ji zhi xue (為己之學, learning for oneself) vs. Wei ren zhi xue (為人之學, learning for others). One of Confucius's most frequently cited observations. The distinction maps directly to FUALL's Academy: education aimed at genuine understanding vs. credentialism. Also connects to the erosion pattern: when learning becomes performative, its substance degrades.

D: Original:
Chu Po-yu sent a messenger with friendly inquiries to Confucius.
Confucius sat with him, and questioned him. 'What,' said he, 'is your master engaged in?' The messenger replied, 'My master is anxious to make his faults few, but he has not yet succeeded.' He then went out, and the Master said, 'A messenger indeed! A messenger indeed!'
D: Refashioned: Qu Boyu sent a messenger to Confucius. The Master sat with the messenger and asked, 'What is your master working on these days?' The messenger replied, 'My master is trying to reduce his faults, but has not yet succeeded.' After the messenger left, the Master said, 'What a messenger! What a messenger!'
D: Elegant: 'He is trying to reduce his faults but has not yet succeeded.' Confucius said: 'What a messenger!' — praising both the answer and the man who sent it.
D: Distilled: The person still working to reduce his faults has already surpassed most who think they have none.
Notes: Qu Boyu (蘧伯玉) — a minister of Wei admired by Confucius (see 15.6). The messenger's answer is perfectly calibrated: honest about ongoing imperfection, which itself demonstrates virtue. Confucius praises both the message and the messenger — the envoy reflects the master's quality.

D: Original: The Master said, 'He who is not in any particular office, has nothing to do with plans for the administration of its duties.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'A person who holds no office should not concern himself with the plans of that office.'
D: Elegant: Not your office, not your business.
D: Distilled: Stay in your lane: do not plan for responsibilities that are not yours.
Notes: Bu zai qi wei, bu mou qi zheng (不在其位，不謀其政) — repeated from 8.14. This is about institutional boundaries: each role has its scope, and overstepping creates disorder. Connects to FUALL's Guilds: competent institutions require clear jurisdictions.

D: Original: The philosopher Tsang said, 'The superior man, in his thoughts, does not go out of his place.'
D: Refashioned: Zengzi said, 'The superior person, in his thoughts, does not go beyond his position.'
D: Elegant: Think within your role. The mind that wanders past its station loses its footing.
D: Distilled: Disciplined thought stays within the bounds of one's actual responsibilities.
Notes: Si bu chu qi wei (思不出其位) — quoted from the Yi Jing (Book of Changes), Hexagram 52 (Gen, Keeping Still). Zengzi (曾子) applies it to mental discipline: do not scheme about matters outside your jurisdiction. Complements 14.27.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The superior man is modest in his speech, but exceeds in his actions.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person is modest in speech but goes beyond in action.'
D: Elegant: Promise less. Deliver more. That is the superior person's way.
D: Distilled: Under-promise and over-deliver.
Notes: Chi yan er guo xing (恥言而過行) — 'ashamed when words exceed actions.' Another formulation of the Confucian emphasis on xing (行, action) over yan (言, speech). Connects to 4.22, 4.24, and 14.21.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'The way of the superior man is threefold, but I am not equal to it. Virtuous, he is free from anxieties; wise, he is free from perplexities; bold, he is free from fear.
Tsze-kung said, 'Master, that is what you yourself say.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The way of the superior person has three dimensions, and I have not mastered any of them: the virtuous person is free from anxiety; the wise person is free from confusion; the courageous person is free from fear.' Zigong said, 'Master, you are describing yourself.'
D: Elegant: Virtue without anxiety. Wisdom without confusion. Courage without fear. 'I have mastered none of these,' said the Master. His student replied: 'You are describing yourself.'
D: Distilled: The three freedoms of the complete person: freedom from anxiety through virtue, from confusion through wisdom, from fear through courage.
Notes: Ren (仁), Zhi (智), Yong (勇) — the three cardinal virtues (san da de, 三達德), also in the Zhongyong (Doctrine of the Mean). Confucius's self-deprecation and Zigong's gentle correction illustrate the master-student relationship. The three map to FUALL's Wisdom: Ren → Right, Zhi → Reason, Yong → the courage to act on Reality.

D: Original: Tsze-kung was in the habit of comparing men together. The Master said, 'Tsze must have reached a high pitch of excellence! Now, I have not leisure for this.'
D: Refashioned: Zigong made a habit of comparing people. The Master said, 'Zigong must have achieved great excellence himself! I certainly don't have the leisure for such comparisons.'
D: Elegant: Zigong loved ranking other people. Confucius said dryly: 'He must have perfected himself already — I certainly haven't found the time to judge others.'
D: Distilled: The person who spends time ranking others has stopped working on himself.
Notes: Fang ren (方人, comparing/judging others) — Confucius considers this a waste of energy better directed at self-cultivation. The ironic praise ('Tsze must have reached a high pitch of excellence!') is a gentle rebuke. Connects to 14.25: look inward, not outward.

D: Original: The Master said, 'I will not be concerned at men's not knowing me; I will be concerned at my own want of ability.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'I will not worry about others not knowing me. I will worry about my own lack of ability.'
D: Elegant: Do not worry about being unknown. Worry about being unworthy of being known.
D: Distilled: The only recognition problem worth solving is the gap between what you are and what you should be.
Notes: Bu huan ren zhi bu ji zhi (不患人之不己知) — a theme repeated throughout the Analects (1.1, 1.16, 4.14, 15.18). Confucius consistently redirects concern from reputation to capability. Connects to FUALL's framework: internal development precedes external recognition.

D: Original: The Master said, 'He who does not anticipate attempts to deceive him, nor think beforehand of his not being believed, and yet apprehends these things readily (when they occur);-- is he not a man of superior worth?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The person who does not assume he will be deceived or disbelieved, yet perceives deception and disbelief quickly when they occur — is he not truly worthy?'
D: Elegant: Do not expect to be deceived — but recognize deception instantly when it comes.
D: Distilled: Wisdom is not suspicion but alertness: trust without naivety.
Notes: The balance between openness and discernment. Confucius rejects both paranoia (anticipating deception) and naivety (failing to detect it). Zhi (智, wisdom) operates in real time, not through preconceived suspicion. Connects to FUALL's Reality: see what is actually there, not what you fear or hope to see.

D: Original:
Wei-shang Mau said to Confucius, 'Ch'iu, how is it that you keep roosting about? Is it not that you are an insinuating talker?'
Confucius said, 'I do not dare to play the part of such a talker, but I hate obstinacy.'
D: Refashioned: Weisheng Mou said to Confucius, 'Qiu, why do you keep wandering from place to place? Are you just a smooth talker?' Confucius said, 'I would not dare to be a smooth talker. I simply hate stubbornness.'
D: Elegant: 'Why do you keep wandering?' 'I hate stubbornness' — meaning: the stubbornness of a world that refuses to change.
D: Distilled: Restlessness in the face of injustice is not instability — it is refusal to accept a broken world.
Notes: Weisheng Mou (微生畝) — possibly a recluse criticizing Confucius's political ambitions. Ning (佞, glib speech) is what he accuses Confucius of; gu (固, stubbornness/obstinacy) is what Confucius claims to oppose. The exchange captures the tension between Confucius's activism and the Daoist/recluse criticism that he should withdraw from a corrupt world.

D: Original: The Master said, 'A horse is called a ch'i, not because of its strength, but because of its other good qualities.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'A fine horse is praised not for its strength but for its character.'
D: Elegant: A horse is called excellent not for its strength but for its virtue. The same is true of people.
D: Distilled: True excellence is character, not raw power.
Notes: Qi (驥, an excellent horse) — valued for De (德, virtue/character qualities) not Li (力, strength). The analogy applies to people: ability matters less than the qualities that govern how ability is used. Connects to FUALL's framework: power without virtue is destructive.

D: Original:
Some one said, 'What do you say concerning the principle that injury should be recompensed with kindness?'
The Master said, 'With what then will you recompense kindness?
'Recompense injury with justice, and recompense kindness with kindness.'
D: Refashioned: Someone asked, 'What do you think of repaying injury with kindness?' The Master said, 'Then how would you repay kindness? Repay injury with justice. Repay kindness with kindness.'
D: Elegant: Repay injury with justice, not kindness — or you will have nothing left to repay kindness with.
D: Distilled: If you answer cruelty with generosity, you devalue generosity; answer cruelty with justice and reserve generosity for those who deserve it.
Notes: Yi de bao yuan (以德報怨, repay grievance with virtue) — a proposal Confucius rejects. His counter: yi zhi bao yuan, yi de bao de (以直報怨，以德報德, repay grievance with uprightness, repay virtue with virtue). This is often contrasted with Jesus's 'turn the other cheek' and with Laozi's Daodejing Ch. 63 which endorses repaying injury with kindness. Confucius's position is practical justice, not revenge and not pacifism. Connects to FUALL's Right: justice is proportional response.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'Alas! there is no one that knows me.'
Tsze-kung said, 'What do you mean by thus saying-- that no one knows you?' The Master replied, 'I do not murmur against Heaven. I do not grumble against men. My studies lie low, and my penetration rises high. But there is Heaven;-- that knows me!'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'No one understands me.' Zigong asked, 'How can you say no one understands you?' The Master replied, 'I do not blame Heaven. I do not blame people. I study what is near at hand and reach toward what is high. If anyone knows me, it is Heaven.'
D: Elegant: 'No one knows me.' 'Then who does?' 'I do not blame heaven or men. I study the low and penetrate the high. Heaven knows me.'
D: Distilled: When no one on earth understands your work, the only consolation is that the work itself is right.
Notes: Xia xue er shang da (下學而上達, study what is below and comprehend what is above) — learning from concrete particulars to reach universal principles. Tian (天, Heaven) as the ultimate witness when human recognition fails. This is one of Confucius's most personal and poignant statements. Connects to FUALL's framework: the thinker oriented toward truth may not be understood in his own time.

D: Original:
The Kung-po Liao, having slandered Tsze-lu to Chi-sun, Tsze-fu Ching-po informed Confucius of it, saying, 'Our master is certainly being led astray by the Kung-po Liao, but I have still power enough left to cut Liao off, and expose his corpse in the market and in the court.'
The Master said, 'If my principles are to advance, it is so ordered. If they are to fall to the ground, it is so ordered. What can the Kung-po Liao do where such ordering is concerned?'
D: Refashioned: Gongbo Liao slandered Zilu to the Ji family. Zifu Jingbo told Confucius and offered to have Gongbo Liao killed. The Master said, 'If the Dao is to prevail, that is Heaven's doing. If the Dao is to fail, that is also Heaven's doing. What can Gongbo Liao do against Heaven's will?'
D: Elegant: An ally offered to kill the slanderer. Confucius refused: 'If the Way is to prevail, Heaven ordains it. If it is to fail, Heaven ordains that too. What can one slanderer do against fate?'
D: Distilled: The success or failure of truth depends on forces larger than any individual enemy.
Notes: Ming (命, fate/destiny/Heaven's will) — Confucius accepts that the outcome of his mission is not entirely in human hands. This is not passivity but a refusal to use violence to advance the Dao. The offer to kill Gongbo Liao is rejected — the Dao cannot be advanced through murder. Connects to FUALL's framework: truth's triumph is not guaranteed by force but by its alignment with what is real.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'Some men of worth retire from the world.
Some retire from particular states.
Some retire because of disrespectful looks.
Some retire because of contradictory language.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Some worthy people withdraw from the world entirely. Some withdraw from particular states. Some withdraw because of contemptuous looks. Some withdraw because of hostile words.'
D: Elegant: The worthy withdraw in stages: first from the world, then from a state, then from disdain, then from insult. Each threshold has its own breaking point.
D: Distilled: There are degrees of withdrawal, each triggered by a different level of indignity.
Notes: The four levels of withdrawal (bi shi, bi di, bi se, bi yan — 辟世、辟地、辟色、辟言). Confucius catalogs the recluse tradition without endorsing it — he acknowledges these are reasonable responses while choosing to remain engaged himself. Connects to FUALL's framework: the tension between engagement and withdrawal when institutions are corrupt.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Those who have done this are seven men.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Seven men have done this.'
D: Elegant: Seven men have chosen withdrawal. Confucius counts them without joining them.
D: Distilled: The sage honors those who withdrew while choosing not to follow.
Notes: The 'seven men' are traditionally identified as various recluses, though the exact identities are debated. The passage is a continuation of 14.39. Confucius respects withdrawal as a legitimate response but does not adopt it.

D: Original: Tsze-lu happening to pass the night in Shih-man, the gatekeeper said to him, 'Whom do you come from?' Tsze-lu said, 'From Mr. K'ung.' 'It is he,-- is it not?'-- said the other, 'who knows the impracticable nature of the times and yet will be doing in them.'
D: Refashioned: Zilu stopped for the night at the Stone Gate. The gatekeeper asked, 'Where are you from?' Zilu said, 'From Master Kong.' The gatekeeper said, 'Ah — the one who knows the times are hopeless and keeps trying anyway?'
D: Elegant: 'The one who knows it's hopeless and keeps going?' Even the gatekeeper understood Confucius's defining trait.
D: Distilled: To know the world cannot be saved and to keep working to save it — that is the mark of the sage.
Notes: Zhi qi bu ke er wei zhi (知其不可而為之) — 'knows it cannot be done yet does it anyway.' This phrase, attributed to an anonymous gatekeeper, became one of the most famous descriptions of Confucius. It captures the core of his project: moral commitment independent of predicted outcomes. Connects directly to FUALL's framework: the civilizational project is undertaken not because success is guaranteed but because the work itself is right.

D: Original:
The Master was playing, one day, on a musical stone in Wei, when a man, carrying a straw basket, passed the door of the house where Confucius was, and said, 'His heart is full who so beats the musical stone.'
A little while after, he added, 'How contemptible is the one-ideaed obstinacy those sounds display! When one is taken no notice of, he has simply at once to give over his wish for public employment. "Deep water must be crossed with the clothes on; shallow water may be crossed with the clothes held up."'
The Master said, 'How determined is he in his purpose! But this is not difficult!'
D: Refashioned: The Master was playing a stone chime in Wei when a man carrying a basket passed by and said, 'Whoever is playing that chime has a full heart.' After listening more, he added, 'How stubborn and one-track! If no one recognizes you, just stop. As the poem says: if the water is deep, wade through fully clothed; if shallow, hitch up your skirts.' The Master said, 'How decisive! But that is the easy path.'
D: Elegant: A stranger heard Confucius's music and diagnosed his soul: 'Full of purpose, but stubborn. If no one listens — stop.' Confucius replied: 'How resolute. But giving up is the easy part.'
D: Distilled: Withdrawing from an indifferent world is decisive — but persisting is harder and better.
Notes: The basket-carrier represents the Daoist/recluse position: adapt to circumstances, do not insist. The poem quoted is from Shijing Ode 34 (Bei Feng). Confucius acknowledges the logic but rejects the conclusion: the Dao demands persistence. Connects to 14.41: knowing it's hopeless and continuing anyway.

D: Original:
Tsze-chang said, 'What is meant when the Shu says that Kao-tsung, while observing the usual imperial mourning, was for three years without speaking?'
The Master said, 'Why must Kao-tsung be referred to as an example of this? The ancients all did so. When the sovereign died, the officers all attended to their several duties, taking instructions from the prime minister for three years.'
D: Refashioned: Zizhang asked about King Gaozong of Yin, who reportedly did not speak for three years while mourning. The Master said, 'Why single out Gaozong? All the ancients did this. When a sovereign died, all officials continued in their duties, taking direction from the prime minister for three years.'
D: Elegant: Three years of silence in mourning was not one king's eccentricity — it was the ancient standard. The system ran itself while the new ruler observed.
D: Distilled: In a well-designed system, the leader's temporary absence does not cause collapse.
Notes: Gaozong (高宗, Wu Ding) — a Shang dynasty king (c. 1250 BCE). The Shu (書經, Book of Documents) records his three-year silence. The 'three years' mourning' was a standard Confucian practice (see also 17.21). The institutional point: governance should be robust enough to function during leadership transitions. Connects to FUALL's framework: institutional resilience matters more than individual leadership.

D: Original: The Master said, 'When rulers love to observe the rules of propriety, the people respond readily to the calls on them for service.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'When rulers love propriety, the people respond readily to calls for service.'
D: Elegant: When rulers honor propriety, the people serve willingly. Compliance follows respect.
D: Distilled: People serve willingly when they see their rulers taking obligations seriously.
Notes: Shang hao li (上好禮) — 'when those above love Li.' Voluntary compliance vs. forced obedience. The mechanism: when people see their leaders observing proper conduct, they trust the system enough to participate. Connects to FUALL's ideational domain of power: legitimacy produces voluntary cooperation.

D: Original: Tsze-lu asked what constituted the superior man. The Master said, 'The cultivation of himself in reverential carefulness.' 'And is this all?' said Tsze-lu. 'He cultivates himself so as to give rest to others,' was the reply. 'And is this all?' again asked Tsze-lu. The Master said, 'He cultivates himself so as to give rest to all the people. He cultivates himself so as to give rest to all the people:-- even Yao and Shun were still solicitous about this.'
D: Refashioned: Zilu asked what makes a superior person. The Master said, 'Cultivating himself with reverential care.' 'Is that all?' 'Cultivating himself so as to bring peace to others.' 'Is that all?' 'Cultivating himself so as to bring peace to all the people. Even Yao and Shun found this difficult.'
D: Elegant: First, cultivate yourself with reverence. Then, bring peace to those around you. Then, to all people. Even the sage-kings found that last step hard.
D: Distilled: Self-cultivation expands outward — from personal discipline to peace for others to peace for all — and even the greatest rulers struggled with the final circle.
Notes: Xiu ji yi jing (修己以敬) → xiu ji yi an ren (修己以安人) → xiu ji yi an bai xing (修己以安百姓). Three concentric circles of responsibility: self → intimates → all people. Yao (堯) and Shun (舜) — the sage-kings who represent the highest ideal of governance. Even they found universal peace difficult. Connects to FUALL's framework: wisdom begins with self-cultivation but aims at civilizational benefit.

D: Original: Yuan Zang was squatting on his heels, and so waited the approach of the Master, who said to him, 'In youth not humble as befits a junior; in manhood, doing nothing worthy of being handed down; and living on to old age:-- this is to be a pest.' With this he hit him on the shank with his staff.
D: Refashioned: Yuan Rang squatted casually, waiting for the Master. Confucius said, 'In youth you were not respectful. In manhood you achieved nothing worth passing on. Now in old age you go on living — you are a pest.' And he rapped him on the shins with his staff.
D: Elegant: Disrespectful in youth, useless in manhood, and still alive in old age. Confucius called him a pest and hit him with a stick.
D: Distilled: A life that produces nothing at any stage and persists regardless is a burden, not an achievement.
Notes: Yuan Rang (原壤) — traditionally identified as an old acquaintance of Confucius, not a disciple. Squatting (ji ju, 踞) was considered extremely disrespectful. Confucius's physical rebuke is unique in the Analects. The three failures (youth without humility, adulthood without contribution, old age without legacy) represent a complete waste of human potential.

D: Original:
A youth of the village of Ch'ueh was employed by Confucius to carry the messages between him and his visitors. Some one asked about him, saying, 'I suppose he has made great progress.'
The Master said, 'I observe that he is fond of occupying the seat of a full-grown man; I observe that he walks shoulder to shoulder with his elders. He is not one who is seeking to make progress in learning. He wishes quickly to become a man.'
D: Refashioned: Yuan Rang squatted casually, waiting for the Master. Confucius said, 'In youth you were not respectful. In manhood you achieved nothing worth passing on. Now in old age you go on living — you are a pest.' And he rapped him on the shins with his staff.
D: Elegant: Disrespectful in youth, useless in manhood, and still alive in old age. Confucius called him a pest and hit him with a stick.
D: Distilled: A life that produces nothing at any stage and persists regardless is a burden, not an achievement.
Notes: Yuan Rang (原壤) — traditionally identified as an old acquaintance of Confucius, not a disciple. Squatting (ji ju, 踞) was considered extremely disrespectful. Confucius's physical rebuke is unique in the Analects. The three failures (youth without humility, adulthood without contribution, old age without legacy) represent a complete waste of human potential.

D: Original: Book 15: Wei Ling Kung

The Duke Ling of Wei asked Confucius about tactics. Confucius replied, 'I have heard all about sacrificial vessels, but I have not learned military matters.' On this, he took his departure the next day.
When he was in Chan, their provisions were exhausted, and his followers became so ill that they were unable to rise.
Tsze-lu, with evident dissatisfaction, said, 'Has the superior man likewise to endure in this way?' The Master said, 'The superior man may indeed have to endure want, but the mean man, when he is in want, gives way to unbridled license.'
D: Refashioned: Duke Ling of Wei asked Confucius about military tactics. Confucius replied, 'I know about sacrificial vessels, but I have never studied warfare.' He left the next day. In the state of Chen, their provisions ran out, and his followers became too weak to stand. Zilu said bitterly, 'Must the superior person endure this too?' The Master said, 'The superior person endures hardship with composure. The inferior person, in hardship, loses all restraint.'
D: Elegant: Asked about war, Confucius said he knew only ritual — and left. When famine came, his student asked: 'Must the superior person suffer like this?' 'Yes. But the inferior person, suffering, loses himself.'
D: Distilled: The difference between the superior and inferior person is not whether they suffer but whether suffering breaks their character.
Notes: Duke Ling of Wei (衛靈公) — see 14.20. Confucius refuses to advise on military matters, consistent with his emphasis on De (virtue) over force. The subsequent famine in Chen tests his own teaching: Junzi gu qiong (君子固窮) — 'the superior person is steadfast in hardship.' Xiaoren qiong si lan (小人窮斯濫) — 'the petty person in hardship runs wild.' Connects to FUALL's Wisdom: principles must hold under pressure or they are not principles.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'Ts'ze, you think, I suppose, that I am one who learns many things and keeps them in memory?'
Tsze-kung replied, 'Yes,-- but perhaps it is not so?'
'No,' was the answer; 'I seek a unity all-pervading.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Zigong, do you think I merely learn many things and remember them?' Zigong replied, 'Yes — is that not so?' The Master said, 'No. I seek one principle that threads through everything.'
D: Elegant: 'You think I am a man of many facts? No. I seek the one thread that connects them all.'
D: Distilled: The goal of learning is not accumulation but integration — finding the single principle that unifies everything.
Notes: Yi yi guan zhi (一以貫之) — 'one thread running through all.' This is Confucius's most explicit statement about the unity of his teaching. The 'one thread' is traditionally interpreted as Zhong Shu (忠恕, loyalty and reciprocity) or simply Ren (仁). Connects directly to FUALL's framework: the project seeks a unified understanding (Wisdom = Reality + Reason + Right) rather than a collection of separate insights.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Yu, those who know virtue are few.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Yu, those who truly know virtue are few.'
D: Elegant: Those who truly know virtue are few. The Master names no exceptions.
D: Distilled: Genuine understanding of virtue is rare — even among those who pursue it.
Notes: Zhi de zhe xian (知德者鮮) — 'those who know virtue are rare.' Addressed to Zilu (Yu). A sobering assessment from a teacher surrounded by students: even among the dedicated, deep understanding is uncommon.

D: Original: The Master said, 'May not Shun be instanced as having governed efficiently without exertion? What did he do? He did nothing but gravely and reverently occupy his royal seat.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Shun may be the best example of governing without effort. What did he do? He simply sat on his throne with gravity and reverence, facing south — and everything was in order.'
D: Elegant: Shun governed without exertion. He sat with gravity, faced south, and the world ordered itself around him.
D: Distilled: The highest governance is so well-founded that the ruler's mere presence is sufficient.
Notes: Wu wei er zhi (無為而治) — 'governing through non-action.' Shun (舜) — the sage-king who exemplifies effortless governance. Nan mian (南面, facing south) — the ritual posture of the Chinese emperor. The concept parallels Daoist wu wei but with a Confucian emphasis: Shun's 'effortlessness' was the product of appointing the right people and maintaining proper institutions. Connects to FUALL's framework: the best-designed institutions require minimal intervention.

D: Original:
Tsze-chang asked how a man should conduct himself, so as to be everywhere appreciated.
The Master said, 'Let his words be sincere and truthful, and his actions honourable and careful;-- such conduct may be practised among the rude tribes of the South or the North. If his words be not sincere and truthful and his actions not honourable and careful, will he, with such conduct, be appreciated, even in his neighborhood?
'When he is standing, let him see those two things, as it were, fronting him. When he is in a carriage, let him see them attached to the yoke. Then may he subsequently carry them into practice.'
Tsze-chang wrote these counsels on the end of his sash.
D: Refashioned: Zizhang asked how to be valued wherever one goes. The Master said, 'Let your words be sincere and truthful, your actions honorable and careful — and this conduct will work even among uncivilized peoples. If your words lack sincerity and your actions lack honor, will you even be valued in your own neighborhood? When standing, imagine these principles right before you. When riding in a carriage, see them attached to the yoke. Then you can practice them anywhere.' Zizhang wrote these words on his sash.
D: Elegant: Sincerity in speech, honor in action — these work everywhere, even among barbarians. Zizhang wrote it on his sash so he would never forget.
D: Distilled: Universal respect is earned by two things: meaning what you say and doing what is right.
Notes: Zhong xin (忠信, faithfulness and sincerity) and Du jing (篤敬, honorable carefulness) — Confucius's recipe for universal applicability. The visual advice (see them before you, attached to the yoke) is a practical mnemonic technique. Zizhang writing on his sash (shen, 紳) is one of the Analects' most vivid images of a student internalizing a teaching. Connects to FUALL's Right: genuine ethics are cross-cultural.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'Truly straightforward was the historiographer Yu. When good government prevailed in his State, he was like an arrow. When bad government prevailed, he was like an arrow.
A superior man indeed is Chu Po-yu! When good government prevails in his state, he is to be found in office. When bad government prevails, he can roll his principles up, and keep them in his breast.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The historian Yu was truly straight — like an arrow whether the government was good or bad. And Qu Boyu is a truly superior person — in office when government is good, able to roll up his principles and keep them safe when it is bad.'
D: Elegant: The historian was an arrow in all weather. Qu Boyu served when it was right and withdrew when it wasn't — rolling up his principles like a scroll to preserve them.
D: Distilled: One form of integrity is unwavering directness; another is knowing when to serve and when to withdraw.
Notes: Shi Yu (史魚) — a historiographer of Wei known for uncompromising integrity. Qu Boyu (蘧伯玉) — a minister of Wei who knew when to serve and when to withdraw (see 14.26). Confucius praises both models: the arrow (constant directness) and the scroll (strategic preservation). Both are forms of integrity. Connects to FUALL's framework: wisdom includes knowing when to act and when to preserve.

D: Original: The Master said, 'When a man may be spoken with, not to speak to him is to err in reference to the man. When a man may not be spoken with, to speak to him is to err in reference to our words. The wise err neither in regard to their man nor to their words.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'When someone can be spoken to and you do not speak, you lose the person. When someone cannot be spoken to and you speak anyway, you waste your words. The wise person wastes neither people nor words.'
D: Elegant: Silence when speech is needed loses people. Speech when silence is needed wastes words. The wise lose neither.
D: Distilled: Wisdom is knowing when to speak and when to stay silent — and never getting it backward.
Notes: Shi ren (失人, losing a person) vs. Shi yan (失言, losing/wasting words). Zhi (智) here is social intelligence — reading situations correctly. Connects to FUALL's Agora: effective discourse requires discernment about audience and timing.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The determined scholar and the man of virtue will not seek to live at the expense of injuring their virtue. They will even sacrifice their lives to preserve their virtue complete.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The truly committed scholar and the person of virtue will not preserve their lives at the expense of their principles. They will sacrifice their lives to keep their virtue intact.'
D: Elegant: The virtuous do not purchase life by selling principle. They pay with life to preserve it.
D: Distilled: There are things worth dying for, and the integrity of your principles is one of them.
Notes: Sha shen cheng ren (殺身成仁) — 'kill the body to accomplish Ren.' One of the most demanding statements in the Analects. Confucius asserts that Ren (仁) is worth more than physical survival. This parallels Socrates' position at his trial. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom: the highest commitment is to principles that transcend individual survival.

D: Original: Tsze-kung asked about the practice of virtue. The Master said, 'The mechanic, who wishes to do his work well, must first sharpen his tools. When you are living in any state, take service with the most worthy among its great officers, and make friends of the most virtuous among its scholars.'
D: Refashioned: Zigong asked about the practice of virtue. The Master said, 'A craftsman who wants to do good work must first sharpen his tools. In whatever state you live, serve the worthiest of its great officers and befriend the most virtuous of its scholars.'
D: Elegant: Sharpen your tools before the work: serve the worthiest officials, befriend the most virtuous scholars. That is how you practice virtue.
D: Distilled: Virtue is practiced by choosing the best people to work for and the best people to learn from.
Notes: Gong yu shan qi shi, bi xian li qi qi (工欲善其事，必先利其器) — 'The craftsman who would do good work must first sharpen his tools.' A famous Chinese proverb originating here. The 'tools' of virtue are relationships: good mentors and good friends. Connects to FUALL's framework: the quality of your intellectual community determines the quality of your development.

D: Original:
Yen Yuan asked how the government of a country should be administered.
The Master said, 'Follow the seasons of Hsia.
'Ride in the state carriage of Yin.
'Wear the ceremonial cap of Chau.
'Let the music be the Shao with its pantomimes.
Banish the songs of Chang, and keep far from specious talkers. The songs of Chang are licentious; specious talkers are dangerous.'
D: Refashioned: Yan Yuan asked how a country should be governed. The Master said, 'Follow the Xia calendar. Use the Yin state carriage. Wear the Zhou ceremonial cap. Let the music be the Shao. Banish the songs of Zheng and keep away from smooth talkers. The songs of Zheng are licentious; smooth talkers are dangerous.'
D: Elegant: Take the best from each dynasty: Xia's calendar, Yin's carriage, Zhou's cap, Shun's music. Banish licentious songs and slick speakers.
D: Distilled: The best system takes the best practices from every era and guards against corruption in music and speech.
Notes: Confucius constructs an ideal state by selecting the best institutional practices from the three dynasties: Xia (calendar/agriculture), Yin/Shang (state carriage/transportation), Zhou (ceremonial cap/ritual). The Shao (韶) music was created under the sage-king Shun. The songs of Zheng (鄭聲) were considered morally corrupting. Ning ren (佞人, specious/smooth talkers) — people who corrupt discourse. Connects to FUALL's framework: institutional design should preserve what works from every tradition while filtering out what corrupts.

D: Original: The Master said, 'If a man take no thought about what is distant, he will find sorrow near at hand.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'If a person gives no thought to distant consequences, he will find trouble close at hand.'
D: Elegant: Those who will not think ahead will soon find trouble at their door.
D: Distilled: Neglecting the future guarantees that the future will arrive as a crisis.
Notes: Ren wu yuan lü, bi you jin you (人無遠慮，必有近憂) — 'Without distant thinking, there must be near worries.' One of the most widely quoted Chinese proverbs. Connects to FUALL's Reason: foresight is a core component of wisdom.

D: Original: The Master said, 'It is all over! I have not seen one who loves virtue as he loves beauty.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'It is hopeless. I have never met anyone who loves virtue the way people love beauty.'
D: Elegant: I have never met a person who desires virtue the way he desires beauty. It is hopeless.
D: Distilled: Humanity's failure is not that it cannot recognize virtue but that it does not desire virtue as strongly as it desires pleasure.
Notes: Hao de ru hao se (好德如好色) — 'loving virtue as one loves beauty/sexual attractiveness.' Confucius's lament about the asymmetry between moral aspiration and natural desire. This is repeated from 9.18. Connects to FUALL's Reality: the human condition involves a persistent gap between what we know is right and what we naturally want.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Was not Tsang Wan like one who had stolen his situation? He knew the virtue and the talents of Hui of Liu-hsia, and yet did not procure that he should stand with him in court.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Zang Wen was like a man who stole his own position. He knew the virtue and talent of Hui of Liuxia, yet failed to bring him into government alongside himself.'
D: Elegant: He knew the best man for the job and left him out. Confucius said: that is stealing your own office.
D: Distilled: Knowing who is best and failing to elevate them is a form of corruption equal to stealing the position yourself.
Notes: Zang Wenzhong (臧文仲) — a minister of Lu. Hui of Liuxia (柳下惠) — a famous sage known for integrity. Zang is criticized not for incompetence but for failing to promote someone more capable. Qie wei (竊位, stealing a position) — occupying a role while preventing the most qualified from serving. Connects to FUALL's Guilds: the duty of good leadership is to identify and elevate talent.

D: Original: The Master said, 'He who requires much from himself and little from others, will keep himself from being the object of resentment.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Demand much from yourself and little from others, and you will keep resentment far away.'
D: Elegant: Hard on yourself, easy on others — that is how you avoid resentment.
D: Distilled: The person who demands more from himself than from others never becomes the object of bitterness.
Notes: Gong zi hou er bo ze yu ren (躬自厚而薄責於人) — 'be thick in self-expectation and thin in blaming others.' A practical recipe for harmonious relationships. Connects to FUALL's Right: moral authority comes from holding yourself to higher standards than you impose on others.

D: Original: The Master said, 'When a man is not in the habit of saying-- "What shall I think of this? What shall I think of this?" I can indeed do nothing with him!'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'If a person never asks himself, "What should I make of this? What should I make of this?" — then I truly can do nothing with him.'
D: Elegant: The student who never asks 'What should I think about this?' is beyond teaching.
D: Distilled: The unteachable person is not the one who lacks intelligence but the one who never questions.
Notes: Ru zhi he, ru zhi he (如之何，如之何) — 'how about this, what about this.' The minimum requirement for learning is reflective questioning. Without it, no amount of teaching can take hold. Connects to FUALL's Academy: education fails not from lack of teachers but from lack of genuine inquiry in students.

D: Original: The Master said, 'When a number of people are together, for a whole day, without their conversation turning on righteousness, and when they are fond of carrying out the suggestions of a small shrewdness;-- theirs is indeed a hard case.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'When people spend the whole day together without their conversation ever touching on righteousness, and delight in showing off petty cleverness — theirs is a hopeless case.'
D: Elegant: A whole day of talk without a word about what is right, filled instead with displays of small cunning — these people are beyond help.
D: Distilled: People who never discuss what is right, only what is clever, have abandoned the only conversation that matters.
Notes: Yi (義, righteousness) as the necessary content of meaningful discourse. Xiao hui (小慧, petty cleverness) as its substitute. The passage condemns discourse without moral substance. Connects to FUALL's Agora: public discourse that avoids questions of right and wrong degenerates into entertainment.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The superior man in everything considers righteousness to be essential. He performs it according to the rules of propriety. He brings it forth in humility. He completes it with sincerity. This is indeed a superior man.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person considers righteousness essential in everything. He performs it through propriety, expresses it in humility, and completes it with faithfulness. This is what it means to be a superior person.'
D: Elegant: Righteousness is the root. Propriety is the method. Humility is the manner. Faithfulness is the seal. That is the superior person.
D: Distilled: The superior person's formula: righteousness as foundation, propriety as form, humility as style, faithfulness as proof.
Notes: Yi (義) → Li (禮) → Sun (遜, humility) → Xin (信). Four stages of virtuous action: know what is right, do it properly, present it humbly, follow through faithfully. This is one of the most systematic descriptions of the Junzi in the Analects. Connects to FUALL's Right: virtue is not a single trait but an integrated practice.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The superior man is distressed by his want of ability. He is not distressed by men's not knowing him.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person is troubled by his own lack of ability, not by others failing to recognize him.'
D: Elegant: The superior person's worry: 'Am I good enough?' Not: 'Do they know how good I am?'
D: Distilled: Focus on your competence, not your reputation.
Notes: Bing wu neng (病無能, distressed by lack of ability). Another variation on the theme of 1.1, 1.16, 4.14, 14.32. The Junzi's orientation is always internal. Connects to FUALL's Academy: genuine learning is self-directed, not approval-seeking.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The superior man dislikes the thought of his name not being mentioned after his death.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person dreads dying without having left a legacy.'
D: Elegant: The superior person's deepest fear: dying without having left something worth remembering.
D: Distilled: The real fear is not death but dying before your work matters.
Notes: Ji mo wu wen (疾沒世而名不稱) — 'distressed at departing this world with no name.' This is not vanity but concern for legacy — the desire that one's life contribute something to the chain of transmission. Connects to FUALL's framework: the project's anxiety is that wisdom will not be transmitted.

D: Original: The Master said, 'What the superior man seeks, is in himself. What the mean man seeks, is in others.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person seeks what he needs within himself. The inferior person seeks it in others.'
D: Elegant: Look within for what you need — the inferior person looks everywhere else.
D: Distilled: Self-reliance in the search for virtue distinguishes the superior from the inferior person.
Notes: Qiu zhu ji (求諸己) vs. Qiu zhu ren (求諸人). The Junzi's locus of control is internal; the Xiaoren's is external. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom: genuine understanding is developed internally, not borrowed from authority.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The superior man is dignified, but does not wrangle. He is sociable, but not a partizan.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person is dignified without being contentious. He is sociable without being partisan.'
D: Elegant: Dignified but not combative. Social but not factional. That is the superior person.
D: Distilled: True character is firm without being aggressive and open without being tribal.
Notes: Qun er bu dang (群而不黨) — 'associates but does not form factions.' The Junzi participates in community without becoming partisan. Connects to FUALL's Agora: productive discourse requires engagement without tribalism.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The superior man does not promote a man simply on account of his words, nor does he put aside good words because of the man.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person does not promote someone merely because of their words, nor does he dismiss good words because of who said them.'
D: Elegant: Do not elevate a person for speaking well. Do not reject wisdom because the speaker is flawed.
D: Distilled: Judge arguments by their merit and people by their actions — never confuse the two.
Notes: Bu yi yan ju ren, bu yi ren fei yan (不以言舉人，不以人廢言). Separate the evaluation of ideas from the evaluation of persons. This is a foundational principle of intellectual integrity. Connects to FUALL's Reason: truth is independent of its source.

D: Original: Tsze-kung asked, saying, 'Is there one word which may serve as a rule of practice for all one's life?' The Master said, 'Is not RECIPROCITY such a word? What you do not want done to yourself, do not do to others.'
D: Refashioned: Zigong asked, 'Is there a single word that can serve as a guide for one's entire life?' The Master said, 'Perhaps the word "reciprocity." Do not do to others what you would not want done to yourself.'
D: Elegant: One word for a lifetime: reciprocity. Do not inflict on others what you would refuse for yourself.
D: Distilled: The single principle sufficient for a lifetime: do not do to others what you would not want done to you.
Notes: Shu (恕, reciprocity) — Confucius's candidate for the one unifying ethical principle. Ji suo bu yu, wu shi yu ren (己所不欲，勿施於人) — the 'Silver Rule,' also in 12.2. The negative formulation ('do not do') is deliberate: it sets a floor rather than a ceiling, leaving room for individual judgment about positive action. This parallels the Golden Rule in Matthew 7:12 and Hillel's formulation in the Talmud. Connects to FUALL's Right: the minimum ethical principle is universally applicable.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'In my dealings with men, whose evil do I blame, whose goodness do I praise, beyond what is proper? If I do sometimes exceed in praise, there must be ground for it in my examination of the individual.
'This people supplied the ground why the three dynasties pursued the path of straightforwardness.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'In dealing with people, do I blame anyone too harshly or praise anyone too generously beyond what is warranted? If I do sometimes praise excessively, there is always a basis for it that I have verified. The common people today are the same people whose honesty gave the three dynasties their straight path.'
D: Elegant: If I praise, there is evidence. If I blame, there is cause. The people who walk the earth today are the same honest stock that sustained three dynasties.
D: Distilled: Judgment must be grounded in evidence, and faith in ordinary people is justified by the track record of civilization.
Notes: The 'three dynasties' (san dai, 三代) — Xia, Shang, Zhou — are the Confucian golden age of righteous governance. Confucius grounds his faith in human potential in historical evidence: these same people sustained great civilizations. Connects to FUALL's framework: the project's faith in human capacity is not naive but historically informed.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Even in my early days, a historiographer would leave a blank in his text, and he who had a horse would lend him to another to ride. Now, alas! there are no such things.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'In my younger days, a historian would leave a blank where he was uncertain, and a man who owned a horse would lend it to another. Now such things no longer exist.'
D: Elegant: Once, historians admitted uncertainty and horse-owners shared. Now neither happens.
D: Distilled: The decline of intellectual honesty and personal generosity are symptoms of the same civilizational erosion.
Notes: Two seemingly unrelated examples linked by a common thread: declining integrity. The historian's blank space (que wen, 闕文) represents intellectual honesty — admitting what you do not know. The horse-lending represents generosity and trust. Both have disappeared. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: the degradation of character shows up in both intellectual and social life simultaneously.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Specious words confound virtue. Want of forbearance in small matters confounds great plans.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Clever-sounding words corrupt virtue. Lack of patience in small matters ruins great plans.'
D: Elegant: Clever words corrupt virtue. Impatience with small things destroys great ones.
D: Distilled: Two destroyers of excellence: persuasive nonsense and impatience with detail.
Notes: Qiao yan luan de (巧言亂德) — see 1.3, 17.17. Xiao bu ren ze luan da mou (小不忍則亂大謀) — 'inability to endure small things disrupts great plans.' Two distinct warnings: one about discourse (bad speech corrupts character), one about temperament (impatience destroys strategy). Connects to FUALL's Reason and Right: both require discipline against seductive shortcuts.

D: Original: The Master said, 'When the multitude hate a man, it is necessary to examine into the case. When the multitude like a man, it is necessary to examine into the case.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'When the crowd hates someone, you must examine the case yourself. When the crowd loves someone, you must also examine the case yourself.'
D: Elegant: The crowd's hatred requires investigation. The crowd's love requires it equally.
D: Distilled: Popular opinion — whether for or against — is never a substitute for independent judgment.
Notes: Zhong wu zhi, bi cha yan; zhong hao zhi, bi cha yan (眾惡之，必察焉；眾好之，必察焉). Confucius demands independent verification against mob sentiment in both directions. This echoes 13.24. Connects to FUALL's Reason: truth is not determined by consensus.

D: Original: The Master said, 'A man can enlarge the principles which he follows; those principles do not enlarge the man.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'A person can enlarge the principles he follows. But principles do not enlarge the person.'
D: Elegant: You can make the Way greater. The Way cannot make you greater — that is your work alone.
D: Distilled: The Dao does not elevate you; you elevate the Dao through how you live it.
Notes: Ren neng hong dao, fei dao hong ren (人能弘道，非道弘人) — 'people can enlarge the Way; the Way does not enlarge people.' Confucius insists on human agency: the Dao is not a force that automatically improves those who follow it; it requires active human effort to be realized. Connects to FUALL's framework: wisdom is achieved through human effort, not received passively from tradition.

D: Original: The Master said, 'To have faults and not to reform them,-- this, indeed, should be pronounced having faults.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'To have faults and not correct them — that is the real fault.'
D: Elegant: The fault is not in failing. The fault is in refusing to fix it.
D: Distilled: An uncorrected fault is the only real fault.
Notes: Guo er bu gai, shi wei guo yi (過而不改，是謂過矣). One of the most quoted lines from the Analects. The distinction between error (inevitable) and persistence in error (a moral failing). Connects to FUALL's framework: self-correction is the engine of all improvement.

D: Original: The Master said, 'I have been the whole day without eating, and the whole night without sleeping:-- occupied with thinking. It was of no use. The better plan is to learn.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'I once went the whole day without eating and the whole night without sleeping, occupied with thinking. It was useless. Better to learn.'
D: Elegant: A day without food, a night without sleep, all spent thinking — and nothing gained. Better to learn from others.
D: Distilled: Pure thought without input is sterile; learning must feed reflection.
Notes: Si vs. Xue (思 vs. 學) — thinking vs. learning. This complements 2.15: 'Learning without thought is labor lost; thought without learning is perilous.' Together they establish Confucius's epistemology: both input (learning) and processing (thought) are necessary; neither alone is sufficient. Connects to FUALL's Academy: genuine understanding requires both study and reflection.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The object of the superior man is truth. Food is not his object. There is plowing;-- even in that there is sometimes want. So with learning;-- emolument may be found in it. The superior man is anxious lest he should not get truth; he is not anxious lest poverty should come upon him.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person's object is truth, not food. Farming may lead to hunger; learning may lead to salary. But the superior person worries about truth, not about poverty.'
D: Elegant: The superior person pursues truth, not livelihood. Poverty may come; truth must not be lost.
D: Distilled: Better to be poor and oriented toward truth than comfortable and oriented toward survival.
Notes: Mou dao bu mou shi (謀道不謀食) — 'plan for the Way, not for food.' The Junzi's priority hierarchy: Dao above material security. This does not mean Confucius was indifferent to poverty (see 13.9: 'enrich them, then teach them') but that the scholar's personal orientation must be toward truth. Connects to FUALL's Academy: the intellectual vocation requires accepting material uncertainty.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'When a man's knowledge is sufficient to attain, and his virtue is not sufficient to enable him to hold, whatever he may have gained, he will lose again.
'When his knowledge is sufficient to attain, and he has virtue enough to hold fast, if he cannot govern with dignity, the people will not respect him.
'When his knowledge is sufficient to attain, and he has virtue enough to hold fast; when he governs also with dignity, yet if he try to move the people contrary to the rules of propriety:-- full excellence is not reached.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'When knowledge is sufficient to attain a position but virtue is insufficient to hold it, you will lose whatever you gain. When knowledge and virtue are both sufficient, but you cannot govern with dignity, the people will not respect you. When you have knowledge, virtue, and dignity, but try to move the people in ways that violate propriety, full excellence is still not reached.'
D: Elegant: Knowledge gains. Virtue holds. Dignity commands respect. Propriety completes the work. Miss any one and excellence falls short.
D: Distilled: Mastery requires four things in sequence: knowledge to attain, virtue to hold, dignity to command, and propriety to complete.
Notes: A four-stage model of leadership excellence: Zhi (知, knowledge) → De (德, virtue) → Zhuang (莊, dignity) → Li (禮, propriety). Each is necessary but insufficient alone. The sequence matters: you can have all three prior qualities and still fail at the final step. Connects to FUALL's framework: complete leadership integrates intellectual, moral, social, and cultural competence.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The superior man cannot be known in little matters; but he may be intrusted with great concerns. The small man may not be intrusted with great concerns, but he may be known in little matters.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person cannot be fully known from small tasks, but can be entrusted with great responsibilities. The inferior person cannot be entrusted with great responsibilities, but can be observed in small tasks.'
D: Elegant: You cannot judge a great person by small tasks, nor entrust a small person with great ones. Scale reveals character.
D: Distilled: Great people are tested by great responsibilities; small people are exposed by them.
Notes: Junzi bu ke xiao zhi, er ke da shou (君子不可小知，而可大受). The principle: scope of assessment must match scope of capability. Small tasks may not reveal a great person's strengths; great tasks will certainly reveal a small person's limitations. Connects to FUALL's Guilds: matching assessment scale to capability level.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Virtue is more to man than either water or fire. I have seen men die from treading on water and fire, but I have never seen a man die from treading the course of virtue.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Virtue is more essential to people than water or fire. I have seen people die from stepping into water or fire, but I have never seen anyone die from stepping into the path of virtue.'
D: Elegant: Water and fire can kill you. Virtue never has. Yet people fear virtue more than either.
D: Distilled: People fear the demands of virtue more than physical danger, even though virtue has never destroyed anyone.
Notes: De (virtue) compared to the basic necessities (water and fire). Confucius argues that virtue is both more essential and less dangerous than material necessities, yet people resist it more. The paradox: we fear what cannot harm us and embrace what can.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Let every man consider virtue as what devolves on himself. He may not yield the performance of it even to his teacher.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Let every person consider virtue their own personal responsibility. In this matter, you need not defer even to your teacher.'
D: Elegant: In the matter of virtue, do not yield — not even to your teacher.
D: Distilled: Virtue is the one domain where deference to authority is not a virtue.
Notes: Dang ren bu rang yu shi (當仁不讓於師) — 'when it comes to Ren, do not yield even to your teacher.' A striking statement from a teacher: in the pursuit of virtue, the student's own responsibility is primary. No authority figure can substitute for personal moral agency. Connects to FUALL's framework: wisdom must ultimately be individually owned, not borrowed from institutions.

D: Original: The Master said, 'The superior man is correctly firm, and not firm merely.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person is firm in principle, not merely stubborn.'
D: Elegant: Principled firmness is not the same as stubbornness. One has a reason; the other has only rigidity.
D: Distilled: True firmness is loyalty to principle; mere stubbornness is loyalty to position.
Notes: Zhen er bu liang (貞而不諒) — 'upright but not inflexibly loyal to a single position.' The Junzi's firmness is in values (Yi, 義), not in arbitrary positions. Connects to FUALL's Reason: genuine conviction is flexible in method but fixed in principle.

D: Original: The Master said, 'A minister, in serving his prince, reverently discharges his duties, and makes his emolument a secondary consideration.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'In serving his ruler, a minister attends reverently to his duties and treats his salary as secondary.'
D: Elegant: Serve with reverence. Let compensation be an afterthought.
D: Distilled: The public servant's priority is duty, not pay.
Notes: Shi jun, jing qi shi er hou qi shi (事君，敬其事而後其食). Restates the theme of 14.1: service must be motivated by duty, not by salary. Connects to FUALL's framework: institutional health depends on people oriented toward mission rather than compensation.

D: Original: The Master said, 'In teaching there should be no distinction of classes.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'In education, there should be no class distinctions.'
D: Elegant: Teach everyone. No exceptions. No classes.
D: Distilled: Education should be universal, not reserved for any social class.
Notes: You jiao wu lei (有教無類) — 'in teaching, there are no categories.' One of Confucius's most revolutionary principles. In his era, education was restricted to the aristocracy. Confucius accepted students from all backgrounds, charging only a token fee (a bundle of dried meat). This is the foundation of FUALL's educational philosophy: the Academy must be open to all.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Those whose courses are different cannot lay plans for one another.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Those who follow different paths cannot make plans together.'
D: Elegant: If your ways diverge, your plans cannot converge.
D: Distilled: People who disagree on fundamentals cannot collaborate on specifics.
Notes: Dao bu tong, bu xiang wei mou (道不同，不相為謀). The Dao (道, Way) here refers to fundamental values and direction, not trivial preferences. Cooperation requires shared foundational commitments. Connects to FUALL's framework: the project's institutions require shared commitment to Wisdom as a precondition for productive work.

D: Original: The Master said, 'In language it is simply required that it convey the meaning.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'In language, it is simply required that it convey the meaning.'
D: Elegant: Language has one job: to convey meaning. Everything else is ornament.
D: Distilled: The purpose of language is clarity, not decoration.
Notes: Ci da er yi yi (辭達而已矣) — 'words arrive at meaning and that is all.' This is not anti-literary but anti-ornamental: language should serve communication, not display. Connects to FUALL's Reason: clear thinking requires clear expression.

D: Original:
The Music-master, Mien, having called upon him, when they came to the steps, the Master said, 'Here are the steps.' When they came to the mat for the guest to sit upon, he said, 'Here is the mat.' When all were seated, the Master informed him, saying, 'So and so is here; so and so is here.'
The Music-master, Mien, having gone out, Tsze-chang asked, saying. 'Is it the rule to tell those things to the Music- master?'
The Master said, 'Yes. This is certainly the rule for those who lead the blind.'
D: Refashioned: When the blind Music-master Mian came to visit, the Master guided him at every step. 'Here are the stairs. Here is the mat. So-and-so is seated here; so-and-so there.' After Mian left, Zizhang asked, 'Is this the proper way to assist a music-master?' The Master said, 'Yes. This is how one guides those who cannot see.'
D: Elegant: Confucius guided the blind musician step by step — stairs, mat, who sat where. 'Is this the rule?' his student asked. 'Yes. This is how you help those who cannot see.'
D: Distilled: True courtesy is not ceremony but practical helpfulness — telling people what they need to know in the moment they need it.
Notes: Music-master Mian (師冕) — court musicians were traditionally blind. Confucius's detailed guidance (stairs, mat, seating) is an example of Li (禮) as practical compassion, not empty formality. The passage illustrates that propriety at its best is attentive care for others' actual needs. Connects to FUALL's Right: ritual is the mechanism through which care becomes action.

D: Original: Book 16: Ke She

The head of the Chi family was going to attack Chwan-yu.
Zan Yu and Chi-lu had an interview with Confucius, and said, 'Our chief, Chi, is going to commence operations against Chwan-yu.'
Confucius said, 'Ch'iu, is it not you who are in fault here?
