'Now, in regard to Chwan-yu, long ago, a former king appointed its ruler to preside over the sacrifices to the eastern Mang; moreover, it is in the midst of the territory of our State; and its ruler is a minister in direct connexion with the sovereign:-- What has your chief to do with attacking it?'
Zan Yu said, 'Our master wishes the thing; neither of us two ministers wishes it.'
Confucius said, 'Ch'iu, there are the words of Chau Zan,-- "When he can put forth his ability, he takes his place in the ranks of office; when he finds himself unable to do so, he retires from it. How can he be used as a guide to a blind man, who does not support him when tottering, nor raise him up when fallen?"
'And further, you speak wrongly. When a tiger or rhinoceros escapes from his cage; when a tortoise or piece of jade is injured in its repository:-- whose is the fault?'
Zan Yu said, 'But at present, Chwan-yu is strong and near to Pi; if our chief do not now take it, it will hereafter be a sorrow to his descendants.'
Confucius said. 'Ch'iu, the superior man hates that declining to say-- "I want such and such a thing," and framing explanations for the conduct.
'I have heard that rulers of States and chiefs of families are not troubled lest their people should be few, but are troubled lest they should not keep their several places; that they are not troubled with fears of poverty, but are troubled with fears of a want of contented repose among the people in their several places. For when the people keep their several places, there will be no poverty; when harmony prevails, there will be no scarcity of people; and when there is such a contented repose, there will be no rebellious upsettings.
'So it is.-- Therefore, if remoter people are not submissive, all the influences of civil culture and virtue are to be cultivated to attract them to be so; and when they have been so attracted, they must be made contented and tranquil.
'Now, here are you, Yu and Ch'iu, assisting your chief. Remoter people are not submissive, and, with your help, he cannot attract them to him. In his own territory there are divisions and downfalls, leavings and separations, and, with your help, he cannot preserve it.
'And yet he is planning these hostile movements within the State.-- I am afraid that the sorrow of the Chi-sun family will not be on account of Chwan-yu, but will be found within the screen of their own court.'
D: Refashioned:
The Ji family planned to attack Zhuanyu. Ran You and Zilu met with Confucius and said, 'Our chief is about to take military action against Zhuanyu.' Confucius said, 'Qiu, is this not your fault? A former king appointed Zhuanyu's ruler to preside over sacrifices. It lies within our own state. Its ruler is a minister connected to the sovereign. What business has your chief attacking it?'

Ran You said, 'Our master wants it — neither of us ministers supports it.' Confucius replied, 'Qiu! There is an old saying: "When you can serve, take your place; when you cannot, step down." How can a guide who fails to support the blind when they stumble be of any use? And further — you speak wrongly. When a tiger escapes its cage or jade is broken in its box, whose fault is it? The keeper's.

Ran You said, 'But Zhuanyu is strong and close to Pi. If we don't take it now, it will trouble our descendants.' Confucius said, 'Qiu! The superior person detests those who refuse to say "I want this" and instead invent justifications.

I have heard that rulers should worry not about having too few people but about inequality; not about poverty but about discontent. When people have their proper places, there is no poverty. When there is harmony, there is no shortage of people. When people are content, there is no rebellion.

If distant peoples will not submit, attract them through culture and virtue. Once they come, keep them content. But you two are helping your chief, and the distant peoples will not come, and the state itself is fracturing — and yet he plans war within his own borders! I fear the Ji family's trouble will come not from Zhuanyu but from within their own walls.'
D: Elegant: The tiger has escaped the cage. The jade is shattered in its box. And you ask who is to blame? — The keeper. The Ji family's real enemy is not across the border but behind its own walls.
D: Distilled: Rulers who manufacture external threats to distract from internal decay will find the decay was the real threat all along.
Notes: One of the longest and most politically substantive passages in the Analects. Zhuanyu (顓臾) — a small dependent state within Lu's territory. The Ji family (季氏) — the most powerful of the three families controlling Lu. Confucius's argument against the attack is multi-layered: legal (Zhuanyu has legitimate status), moral (the Ji ministers are complicit), strategic (internal division is the real danger). The maxim 'bu huan gua er huan bu jun' (不患寡而患不均, worry not about scarcity but about inequality) became a foundational Chinese political principle. Connects to FUALL's domains of power: when social power (the Ji family) overrides legitimate authority (the Duke), the system is already failing.

D: Original:
Confucius said, 'When good government prevails in the empire, ceremonies, music, and punitive military expeditions proceed from the son of Heaven. When bad government prevails in the empire, ceremonies, music, and punitive military expeditions proceed from the princes. When these things proceed from the princes, as a rule, the cases will be few in which they do not lose their power in ten generations. When they proceed from the Great officers of the princes, as a rule, the cases will be few in which they do not lose their power in five generations. When the subsidiary ministers of the great officers hold in their grasp the orders of the state, as a rule, the cases will be few in which they do not lose their power in three generations.
'When right principles prevail in the kingdom, government will not be in the hands of the Great officers.
'When right principles prevail in the kingdom, there will be no discussions among the common people.'
D: Refashioned:
Confucius said, 'When the realm is well-governed, ceremonies, music, and military campaigns originate from the sovereign. When it is badly governed, they originate from the regional lords. When lords control these things, power is typically lost within ten generations. When their ministers control them, within five. When their ministers' subordinates hold power, within three.

When right principles prevail, government does not fall into the hands of great officers. When right principles prevail, the common people have nothing to dispute about.'
D: Elegant: Power that originates from the sovereign lasts ten generations. From the nobles, five. From their subordinates, three. The further power drifts from its source, the faster it decays.
D: Distilled: The more dispersed legitimate authority becomes from its proper source, the faster the system collapses.
Notes: A theory of political entropy: legitimate authority degrades as it passes to lower levels. The progression (son of Heaven → princes → great officers → subsidiary ministers) describes the historical decay of the Zhou dynasty. Each transfer of power halves the time before collapse. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: institutional legitimacy decays when authority migrates from its proper locus.

D: Original: Confucius said, 'The revenue of the state has left the ducal House now for five generations. The government has been in the hands of the Great officers for four generations. On this account, the descendants of the three Hwan are much reduced.'
D: Refashioned: Confucius said, 'Revenue has left the ducal house for five generations now. Government has been in the hands of the great officers for four. This is why the descendants of the three Huan families are in decline.'
D: Elegant: Five generations since the duke lost revenue. Four since he lost governance. The great families who seized power are now declining in turn.
D: Distilled: Usurped power decays just as the legitimate power it replaced did.
Notes: The three Huan families (三桓) — Ji, Meng, and Shu — descendants of Duke Huan of Lu, who controlled the state. Confucius observes that the usurpers are themselves decaying, illustrating the principle from 16.2: illegitimate power is inherently unstable. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: the rot of institutional capture is self-limiting — the captors eventually decay too.

D: Original: Confucius said, 'There are three friendships which are advantageous, and three which are injurious. Friendship with the upright; friendship with the sincere; and friendship with the man of much observation:-- these are advantageous. Friendship with the man of specious airs; friendship with the insinuatingly soft; and friendship with the glib-tongued:-- these are injurious.'
D: Refashioned: Confucius said, 'Three friendships benefit you, and three harm you. Friendship with the upright, the sincere, and the well-informed benefits you. Friendship with the flattering, the two-faced, and the glib-tongued harms you.'
D: Elegant: Beneficial friends: the honest, the sincere, the well-read. Harmful friends: the flattering, the slippery, the smooth-talking.
D: Distilled: Your friends shape your character; choose the honest over the pleasant.
Notes: San yi you, san sun you (三益友，三損友). The taxonomy of friendship is based not on personal warmth but on moral and intellectual quality. Zhi (直, upright), Liang (諒, sincere), and Duo wen (多聞, well-informed) are the beneficial types. Pian pi (便辟, specious), Shan rou (善柔, insinuatingly soft), and Pian ning (便佞, glib) are the harmful types. Connects to FUALL's Agora: the quality of your discourse community determines the quality of your thinking.

D: Original: Confucius said, 'There are three things men find enjoyment in which are advantageous, and three things they find enjoyment in which are injurious. To find enjoyment in the discriminating study of ceremonies and music; to find enjoyment in speaking of the goodness of others; to find enjoyment in having many worthy friends:-- these are advantageous. To find enjoyment in extravagant pleasures; to find enjoyment in idleness and sauntering; to find enjoyment in the pleasures of feasting:-- these are injurious.'
D: Refashioned: Confucius said, 'Three pleasures benefit you, and three harm you. Finding pleasure in studying ritual and music, in speaking of others' goodness, and in having many worthy friends benefits you. Finding pleasure in extravagance, in idleness, and in feasting harms you.'
D: Elegant: Beneficial pleasures: learning, praising goodness, worthy friends. Harmful pleasures: luxury, idleness, feasting.
D: Distilled: The pleasures that build you up are learning, generosity, and good company; the ones that break you down are indulgence, laziness, and excess.
Notes: San yi le, san sun le (三益樂，三損樂). The taxonomy of pleasure parallels the taxonomy of friendship in 16.4. Confucius does not condemn pleasure itself but distinguishes constructive from destructive forms. Li yue (禮樂, ritual and music) as beneficial pleasure connects learning to joy. Connects to FUALL's framework: the educational institutions should be sources of genuine pleasure, not mere obligation.

D: Original: Confucius said, 'There are three errors to which they who stand in the presence of a man of virtue and station are liable. They may speak when it does not come to them to speak;-- this is called rashness. They may not speak when it comes to them to speak;-- this is called concealment. They may speak without looking at the countenance of their superior;-- this is called blindness.'
D: Refashioned: Confucius said, 'Three errors are common when in the presence of a person of authority. Speaking before it is your turn — that is rashness. Not speaking when it is your turn — that is concealment. Speaking without reading the other person's expression — that is blindness.'
D: Elegant: Three errors in the presence of the powerful: speaking too soon (rashness), not speaking at all (concealment), speaking without reading the room (blindness).
D: Distilled: In the presence of authority, the three failures are premature speech, withheld speech, and tone-deaf speech.
Notes: Zao (躁, rashness), Yin (隱, concealment), Gu (瞽, blindness). These are errors of timing and social awareness, not of content. All three relate to Zhi (智, wisdom) as social intelligence — knowing when and how to speak. Connects to FUALL's Agora: effective discourse requires not just having something to say but knowing when and how to say it.

D: Original: Confucius said, 'There are three things which the superior man guards against. In youth, when the physical powers are not yet settled, he guards against lust. When he is strong and the physical powers are full of vigor, he guards against quarrelsomeness. When he is old, and the animal powers are decayed, he guards against covetousness.'
D: Refashioned: Confucius said, 'The superior person guards against three things. In youth, when his energies are unsettled, he guards against lust. In his prime, when his energies are at full strength, he guards against combativeness. In old age, when his energies are declining, he guards against acquisitiveness.'
D: Elegant: Youth: guard against lust. Prime: guard against aggression. Old age: guard against greed. Each stage has its specific danger.
D: Distilled: The dangers shift with age — desire, then dominance, then accumulation — and each requires its own vigilance.
Notes: San jie (三戒, three warnings). The developmental psychology is practical: each life stage presents a characteristic temptation tied to physical energy levels. Se (色, lust/desire), Dou (鬥, combativeness), De (得, acquisitiveness). Connects to FUALL's framework: self-knowledge must include awareness of how your vulnerabilities change over time.

D: Original:
Confucius said, 'There are three things of which the superior man stands in awe. He stands in awe of the ordinances of Heaven. He stands in awe of great men. He stands in awe of the words of sages.
'The mean man does not know the ordinances of Heaven, and consequently does not stand in awe of them. He is disrespectful to great men. He makes sport of the words of sages.'
D: Refashioned: Confucius said, 'The superior person stands in awe of three things: the will of Heaven, great people, and the words of sages. The inferior person, not knowing Heaven's will, does not stand in awe of it. He is disrespectful to great people and mocks the words of sages.'
D: Elegant: The superior person reveres Heaven's will, great people, and the words of sages. The inferior person, knowing none of these, fears none of them.
D: Distilled: The capacity for awe distinguishes the wise from the foolish; those who revere nothing understand nothing.
Notes: San wei (三畏, three objects of awe). Tian ming (天命, Heaven's mandate/ordinances) — the moral order of the universe. Da ren (大人, great people) — those who embody the Dao. Sheng ren zhi yan (聖人之言, words of sages) — the accumulated wisdom tradition. The Xiaoren's failure is not rebellion but ignorance: he does not know these things exist, so he cannot respect them. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom: genuine understanding begins with recognition that some things are greater than oneself.

D: Original: Confucius said, 'Those who are born with the possession of knowledge are the highest class of men. Those who learn, and so, readily, get possession of knowledge, are the next. Those who are dull and stupid, and yet compass the learning, are another class next to these. As to those who are dull and stupid and yet do not learn;-- they are the lowest of the people.'
D: Refashioned: Confucius said, 'Those born with knowledge are the highest class. Those who learn and acquire knowledge are next. Those who are dull but persist in learning come after them. Those who are dull and refuse to learn — they are the lowest.'
D: Elegant: Born knowing: highest. Learning to know: next. Struggling to learn: honorable. Refusing to learn: lowest.
D: Distilled: The ranking of people is not by natural intelligence but by willingness to learn.
Notes: Sheng er zhi zhi (生而知之) — 'born knowing.' Confucius believed such people existed but were extremely rare (he did not claim to be one — see 7.20). The hierarchy's real message is that effort (xue, 學) matters more than innate capacity. The lowest class is defined not by stupidity but by refusal to learn. Connects to FUALL's Academy: the only disqualifying trait is the refusal to engage.

D: Original: Confucius said, 'The superior man has nine things which are subjects with him of thoughtful consideration. In regard to the use of his eyes, he is anxious to see clearly. In regard to the use of his ears, he is anxious to hear distinctly. In regard to his countenance, he is anxious that it should be benign. In regard to his demeanor, he is anxious that it should be respectful. In regard to his speech, he is anxious that it should be sincere. In regard to his doing of business, he is anxious that it should be reverently careful. In regard to what he doubts about, he is anxious to question others. When he is angry, he thinks of the difficulties (his anger may involve him in). When he sees gain to be got, he thinks of righteousness.'
D: Refashioned: Confucius said, 'The superior person gives thoughtful attention to nine things: In seeing, he aims for clarity. In hearing, for accuracy. In expression, for warmth. In manner, for respect. In speech, for sincerity. In work, for reverent care. In doubt, for questioning others. In anger, for considering the consequences. When seeing a chance for gain, for thinking of what is right.'
D: Elegant: Nine disciplines: clear sight, sharp hearing, warm expression, respectful manner, sincere speech, careful work, honest questioning, tempered anger, righteous restraint before gain.
D: Distilled: The superior person applies disciplined attention to every faculty: eyes, ears, face, body, tongue, hands, doubts, anger, and desire.
Notes: Jiu si (九思, nine subjects of thoughtful consideration). This is one of the most comprehensive self-cultivation checklists in the Analects. Each pairs a human faculty or situation with its proper discipline. The ninth — yi yi (義, righteousness when seeing gain) — echoes 14.13 and 19.1. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom: each dimension of human capacity requires its own form of disciplined attention.

D: Original:
Confucius said, 'Contemplating good, and pursuing it, as if they could not reach it; contemplating evil, and shrinking from it, as they would from thrusting the hand into boiling water:-- I have seen such men, as I have heard such words.
'Living in retirement to study their aims, and practising righteousness to carry out their principles:-- I have heard these words, but I have not seen such men.'
D: Refashioned: Confucius said, 'Pursuing good as if you could never reach it, shrinking from evil as from boiling water — I have seen such people and heard such words. Living in seclusion to examine one's aims, practicing righteousness to fulfill one's principles — I have heard such words, but I have never seen such people.'
D: Elegant: Chasing good as if it were always out of reach, recoiling from evil as from boiling water — such people exist. But those who live in quiet seclusion practicing what they preach? I have heard of them. I have never met one.
D: Distilled: People who flee evil are common enough; people who quietly practice righteousness in seclusion are almost mythical.
Notes: The asymmetry: moral urgency (pursuit of good, avoidance of evil) is observable; consistent private practice of principles is almost never seen. Confucius's skepticism about recluses (yin ju, 隱居) who claim to practice righteousness in isolation — he has 'heard' the claim but 'never seen' it verified. Connects to FUALL's framework: virtue must be publicly demonstrated, not merely privately claimed.

D: Original:
The duke Ching of Ch'i had a thousand teams, each of four horses, but on the day of his death, the people did not praise him for a single virtue. Po-i and Shu-ch'i died of hunger at the foot of the Shau-yang mountain, and the people, down to the present time, praise them.
'Is not that saying illustrated by this?'
D: Refashioned: Duke Jing of Qi had a thousand teams of horses, but when he died, no one praised him for a single virtue. Bo Yi and Shu Qi starved to death on Mount Shouyang, and people praise them to this day. Is this not what the saying means?
D: Elegant: A duke with four thousand horses: no one praised his virtue. Two men who starved on a mountain: praised for millennia. Wealth leaves no legacy; character does.
D: Distilled: Wealth is forgotten at death; virtue is remembered across centuries.
Notes: Duke Jing of Qi (齊景公) — see 12.11, 18.3. Bo Yi (伯夷) and Shu Qi (叔齊) — brothers who refused to serve the Zhou dynasty after it overthrew the Shang, and starved rather than eat Zhou grain on Mount Shouyang. They are Confucian exemplars of principled refusal. The 'saying' referenced is likely from 12.10 (about virtue vs. wealth). Connects to FUALL's framework: legacy is determined by character, not by resources.

D: Original:
Ch'an K'ang asked Po-yu, saying, 'Have you heard any lessons from your father different from what we have all heard?'
Po-yu replied, 'No. He was standing alone once, when I passed below the hall with hasty steps, and said to me, "Have you learned the Odes?" On my replying "Not yet," he added, "If you do not learn the Odes, you will not be fit to converse with." I retired and studied the Odes.
'Another day, he was in the same way standing alone, when I passed by below the hall with hasty steps, and said to me, 'Have you learned the rules of Propriety?' On my replying 'Not yet,' he added, 'If you do not learn the rules of Propriety, your character cannot be established.' I then retired, and learned the rules of Propriety.
'I have heard only these two things from him.'
Ch'ang K'ang retired, and, quite delighted, said, 'I asked one thing, and I have got three things. I have heard about the Odes. I have heard about the rules of Propriety. I have also heard that the superior man maintains a distant reserve towards his son.'
D: Refashioned: Chen Kang asked Confucius's son Boyu, 'Have you received any special instruction from your father that we haven't?' Boyu replied, 'No. Once, when he was standing alone and I hurried past, he asked, "Have you studied the Odes?" I said not yet. He said, "If you don't study the Odes, you will have nothing to speak with." So I went and studied the Odes. Another time, he asked, "Have you studied the rules of Propriety?" I said not yet. He said, "If you don't study Propriety, your character cannot be established." So I went and studied Propriety. I have heard only these two lessons from him.' Chen Kang went away delighted, saying, 'I asked one question and got three answers: about the Odes, about Propriety, and about how a father keeps a proper distance from his son.'
D: Elegant: Confucius's son received no special teaching — just two commands: study the Odes, study Propriety. A visitor asked one question and learned three things: the value of poetry, the value of ritual, and that a sage keeps his own son at arm's length.
D: Distilled: The sage's son received no privilege — only the same two disciplines everyone needs: poetry and propriety.
Notes: Boyu (伯魚, Kong Li) — Confucius's son. Chen Kang (陳亢, also Chen Ziqin) — a disciple. The two pillars of Confucian education: Shi (詩, the Odes/Poetry) for cultivating speech and emotional intelligence, and Li (禮, Propriety) for establishing character. The 'three things' (san yi, 三益): Odes, Propriety, and the principle that a father should not show favoritism to his own son in teaching. Connects to FUALL's Academy: education must be universal and impartial, even within the teacher's own family.

D: Original: The wife of the prince of a state is called by him FU ZAN. She calls herself HSIAO T'UNG. The people of the State call her CHUN FU ZAN, and, to the people of other States, they call her K'WA HSIAO CHUN. The people of other states also call her CHUN FU ZAN.
D: Refashioned: The wife of a state's ruler is called 'Fu Ren' by her husband. She calls herself 'Xiao Tong.' The people call her 'Jun Fu Ren.' To people of other states, she is called 'Gua Xiao Jun.' People of other states also call her 'Jun Fu Ren.'
D: Elegant: A ruler's wife has five names depending on who speaks and to whom — each title precisely calibrated to relationship and context.
D: Distilled: Proper naming reflects proper relationships; every title carries a specific social meaning.
Notes: A passage about Zhengming (正名, rectification of names) applied to court protocol. The different titles for the same person (Fu Ren, Xiao Tong, Jun Fu Ren, Gua Xiao Jun) encode relationships of authority, humility, and diplomatic courtesy. This is Li (禮) at its most formal.

D: Original: Book 17: Yang Ho

Yang Ho wished to see Confucius, but Confucius would not go to see him. On this, he sent a present of a pig to Confucius, who, having chosen a time when Ho was not at home, went to pay his respects for the gift. He met him, however, on the way.
Ho said to Confucius, 'Come, let me speak with you.' He then asked, 'Can he be called benevolent who keeps his jewel in his bosom, and leaves his country to confusion?' Confucius replied, 'No.' 'Can he be called wise, who is anxious to be engaged in public employment, and yet is constantly losing the opportunity of being so?' Confucius again said, 'No.' 'The days and months are passing away; the years do not wait for us.' Confucius said, 'Right; I will go into office.'
D: Refashioned: Yang Huo wanted to see Confucius, but Confucius would not visit him. So Yang Huo sent a pig as a gift, knowing protocol required a return visit. Confucius chose a time when Yang Huo was out to make his reciprocal call — but met him on the road. Yang Huo said, 'Come, I want to speak with you. Can a man who hoards his talent while his country falls apart be called benevolent? No. Can a man who wants to serve but keeps missing his chance be called wise? No. Time is passing. The years will not wait.' Confucius said, 'Very well. I will take office.'
D: Elegant: Yang Huo trapped Confucius with a gift and a question: 'Can a man who lets his country fall apart be called benevolent?' Confucius conceded: 'I will serve.'
D: Distilled: Even the sage can be cornered by his own principles: if you believe in service, you cannot forever refuse to serve.
Notes: Yang Huo (陽貨, also Yang Hu) — a powerful and controversial steward of the Ji family who effectively seized control of Lu around 505 BCE. This passage is rich with irony: Yang Huo uses Confucius's own values (Ren, Zhi) against him. The pig-gift creates a ritual obligation. Confucius's 'I will take office' is likely diplomatic rather than sincere — he never actually served Yang Huo. Connects to FUALL's framework: Li (禮) can be weaponized, creating obligations the recipient cannot refuse without violating his own principles.

D: Original: The Master said, 'By nature, men are nearly alike; by practice, they get to be wide apart.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'By nature, people are similar. Through practice and habit, they grow far apart.'
D: Elegant: Nature makes us alike. Habit makes us different.
D: Distilled: Human beings are born similar; it is practice that creates the vast differences between them.
Notes: Xing xiang jin ye, xi xiang yuan ye (性相近也，習相遠也). One of the most influential statements in Chinese philosophy. Xing (性, nature) is the starting point; Xi (習, practice/habit) is the differentiating force. This is the Confucian case for education: since nature is roughly equal, differences are produced by cultivation and environment. Connects directly to FUALL's framework: the educational institutions exist because human potential is shaped by practice, not predetermined by nature.

D: Original: The Master said, 'There are only the wise of the highest class, and the stupid of the lowest class, who cannot be changed.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Only the wisest and the most foolish never change.'
D: Elegant: The highest wisdom and the deepest foolishness have one thing in common: neither can be moved.
D: Distilled: Only the very best and the very worst are beyond the reach of education.
Notes: Wei shang zhi yu xia yu bu yi (唯上知與下愚不移). A qualification of 17.2: while most people are shaped by practice, the extreme ends are fixed. This sets the boundaries of education's reach: it works for the vast middle but cannot elevate the utterly closed or further enlighten the already complete.

D: Original:
The Master, having come to Wu-ch'ang, heard there the sound of stringed instruments and singing.
Well pleased and smiling, he said, 'Why use an ox knife to kill a fowl?'
Tsze-yu replied, 'Formerly, Master, I heard you say,-- "When the man of high station is well instructed, he loves men; when the man of low station is well instructed, he is easily ruled."'
The Master said, 'My disciples, Yen's words are right. What I said was only in sport.'
D: Refashioned: When the Master arrived in Wucheng and heard the sound of stringed instruments and singing, he smiled and said, 'Why use an ox-cleaver to kill a chicken?' Ziyu replied, 'Master, I once heard you say: "When the educated person learns the Way, he loves others. When the common person learns the Way, he is easy to govern."' The Master said, 'My students, Ziyu is right. I was joking.'
D: Elegant: Confucius teased his student for teaching music in a small town. The student quoted the Master's own words back at him. Confucius laughed: 'You are right. I was joking.'
D: Distilled: The teacher who mocks the application of his own principles in small places has forgotten that principles have no minimum scale.
Notes: Wucheng (武城) — a small district governed by Ziyu (子游, Yan Yan). The 'ox-cleaver for a chicken' (ge ji yan yong niu dao, 割雞焉用牛刀) became a famous Chinese idiom for using excessive means. Ziyu's response is sharp: the Dao applies everywhere, regardless of scale. Confucius's gracious retreat demonstrates intellectual honesty. Connects to FUALL's framework: the educational mission applies to every community, not just the major centers.

D: Original:
Kung-shan Fu-zao, when he was holding Pi, and in an attitude of rebellion, invited the Master to visit him, who was rather inclined to go.
Tsze-lu was displeased, and said, 'Indeed, you cannot go! Why must you think of going to see Kung-shan?'
The Master said, 'Can it be without some reason that he has invited ME? If any one employ me, may I not make an eastern Chau?'
D: Refashioned: Gongshan Furao, who held the city of Pi in rebellion, invited Confucius to join him. The Master was inclined to go. Zilu was displeased: 'You cannot go! Why would you even consider visiting Gongshan?' The Master said, 'Would he invite me without reason? If anyone employs me, I could create an Eastern Zhou.'
D: Elegant: A rebel invited him. Confucius was tempted: 'If anyone employs me, I could rebuild the Zhou dynasty here in the east.'
D: Distilled: The reformer's temptation: any platform, even a rebel's, might serve the cause.
Notes: Gongshan Furao (公山弗擾) — a retainer of the Ji family who seized Pi in rebellion (~502 BCE). Confucius's willingness to consider the invitation reveals his desperate desire for a political platform. 'Eastern Zhou' (dong Zhou, 東周) — Confucius dreamed of restoring the golden age of early Zhou governance. Zilu's objection is practical: you cannot build legitimate governance on a rebel's foundation. Connects to FUALL's framework: the tension between pragmatic opportunity and principled restraint.

D: Original: Tsze-chang asked Confucius about perfect virtue. Confucius said, 'To be able to practise five things everywhere under heaven constitutes perfect virtue.' He begged to ask what they were, and was told, 'Gravity, generosity of soul, sincerity, earnestness, and kindness. If you are grave, you will not be treated with disrespect. If you are generous, you will win all. If you are sincere, people will repose trust in you. If you are earnest, you will accomplish much. If you are kind, this will enable you to employ the services of others.
D: Refashioned: Zizhang asked about perfect virtue. Confucius said, 'To practice five things everywhere under heaven constitutes perfect virtue.' Zizhang asked what they were. 'Gravity, generosity, sincerity, earnestness, and kindness. Gravity prevents disrespect. Generosity wins people over. Sincerity earns trust. Earnestness produces results. Kindness enables you to employ others.'
D: Elegant: Five universals of virtue: gravity earns respect, generosity wins loyalty, sincerity earns trust, earnestness achieves results, kindness enables service.
D: Distilled: Perfect virtue is five things practiced everywhere: be serious, generous, honest, diligent, and kind.
Notes: Wu mei (五美): Gong (恭, gravity), Kuan (寬, generosity), Xin (信, sincerity), Min (敏, earnestness), Hui (惠, kindness). Each virtue is paired with its practical benefit. This is Ren (仁) broken down into operational components. Connects to FUALL's Right: virtue is not abstract but consists of specific, practicable qualities.

D: Original:
Pi Hsi inviting him to visit him, the Master was inclined to go.
Tsze-lu said, 'Master, formerly I have heard you say, "When a man in his own person is guilty of doing evil, a superior man will not associate with him." Pi Hsi is in rebellion, holding possession of Chung-mau; if you go to him, what shall be said?'
The Master said, 'Yes, I did use these words. But is it not said, that, if a thing be really hard, it may be ground without being made thin? Is it not said, that, if a thing be really white, it may be steeped in a dark fluid without being made black?
'Am I a bitter gourd! How can I be hung up out of the way of being eaten?'
D: Refashioned: Pi Xi, holding Zhongmou in rebellion, invited the Master to visit. The Master was inclined to go. Zilu objected: 'Master, you once said that the superior person will not associate with one who commits evil in his own person. Pi Xi holds Zhongmou in rebellion — how can you go?' The Master said, 'Yes, I said that. But is it not also said: what is truly hard cannot be worn thin by grinding? What is truly white cannot be stained by dyeing? Am I a bitter gourd, meant to hang on the wall and never be eaten?'
D: Elegant: 'Am I a bitter gourd, hung on the wall, never to be tasted?' The Master longed to be used — even by imperfect employers.
D: Distilled: A talent that refuses every imperfect opportunity becomes an ornament rather than an instrument.
Notes: Pi Xi (佛肸) — a rebel holding Zhongmou in the state of Jin. Confucius uses two metaphors: the ungrindable stone (moral resilience) and the bitter gourd (the waste of unused talent). His frustration is existential: he cannot find a ruler worthy of his principles, yet refusing all opportunities means his teaching goes unimplemented. Connects to 14.41: knowing it's hopeless and trying anyway.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'Yu, have you heard the six words to which are attached six becloudings?' Yu replied, 'I have not.'
'Sit down, and I will tell them to you.
'There is the love of being benevolent without the love of learning;-- the beclouding here leads to a foolish simplicity. There is the love of knowing without the love of learning;-- the beclouding here leads to dissipation of mind. There is the love of being sincere without the love of learning;-- the beclouding here leads to an injurious disregard of consequences. There is the love of straightforwardness without the love of learning;-- the beclouding here leads to rudeness. There is the love of boldness without the love of learning;-- the beclouding here leads to insubordination. There is the love of firmness without the love of learning;-- the beclouding here leads to extravagant conduct.'
D: Refashioned:
The Master said, 'Yu, have you heard of the six virtues and their six corruptions?' Zilu said he had not. 'Sit down and I will tell you.

Love of benevolence without love of learning leads to foolish credulity. Love of knowledge without love of learning leads to aimless speculation. Love of honesty without love of learning leads to hurtful bluntness. Love of directness without love of learning leads to rudeness. Love of courage without love of learning leads to recklessness. Love of firmness without love of learning leads to rash extremism.'
D: Elegant: Every virtue, without learning, becomes its own corruption: benevolence becomes naivety, knowledge becomes distraction, honesty becomes cruelty, directness becomes rudeness, courage becomes recklessness, firmness becomes fanaticism.
D: Distilled: Every virtue has a shadow, and learning is the only thing that keeps virtue from becoming vice.
Notes: Liu yan liu bi (六言六蔽, six words and six becloudings). This is one of the most psychologically sophisticated passages in the Analects. Each virtue (Ren 仁, Zhi 知, Xin 信, Zhi 直, Yong 勇, Gang 剛) becomes destructive without the discipline of Xue (學, learning). Learning is not a virtue in itself but the corrective that keeps all virtues calibrated. Connects directly to FUALL's framework: Wisdom requires all three components (Reality, Reason, Right) — any one without the others produces distortion.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'My children, why do you not study the Book of Poetry?
'The Odes serve to stimulate the mind.
'They may be used for purposes of self-contemplation.
'They teach the art of sociability.
'They show how to regulate feelings of resentment.
'From them you learn the more immediate duty of serving one's father, and the remoter one of serving one's prince.
'From them we become largely acquainted with the names of birds, beasts, and plants.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'My students, why do you not study the Book of Poetry? The Odes stimulate the mind. They can be used for self-reflection. They teach sociability. They show how to express resentment properly. From them you learn the immediate duty to your father and the broader duty to your ruler. You also learn the names of many birds, beasts, and plants.'
D: Elegant: Study poetry: it awakens, reflects, socializes, channels anger, teaches duty, and names the world. No other text does so many things at once.
D: Distilled: Poetry does six things no other study can: it stimulates, mirrors, connects, disciplines emotion, teaches duty, and names the world.
Notes: Shi ke yi xing (詩可以興) — the Shijing (詩經, Book of Poetry/Odes) as the foundation of education. The six functions: Xing (興, stimulate), Guan (觀, observe/reflect), Qun (群, socialize), Yuan (怨, express grievance properly), Shi (事, serve father and prince), and Zhi (知, know the natural world). This is Confucius's theory of liberal education: the humanities are not ornamental but foundational. Connects to FUALL's Academy: literature is the primary vehicle for emotional, social, and intellectual development.

D: Original: The Master said to Po-yu, 'Do you give yourself to the Chau-nan and the Shao-nan. The man who has not studied the Chau-nan and the Shao-nan, is like one who stands with his face right against a wall. Is he not so?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said to his son Boyu, 'Have you studied the first sections of the Odes — the Zhounan and Shaonan? A person who has not studied them is like someone standing with his face against a wall — he can see nothing.'
D: Elegant: Without the Odes, you are staring at a wall. You can see nothing.
D: Distilled: A person uneducated in the foundational texts is blind to the world.
Notes: Zhounan (周南) and Shaonan (召南) — the first two sections of the Shijing, dealing with domestic life, marriage, and proper conduct. Boyu (伯魚) — Confucius's son (see 16.13). The wall metaphor is vivid: without literary education, you have no perspective, no depth, no angle of vision.

D: Original: The Master said, '"It is according to the rules of propriety," they say.-- "It is according to the rules of propriety," they say. Are gems and silk all that is meant by propriety? "It is music," they say.-- "It is music," they say. Are bells and drums all that is meant by music?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'They keep saying "propriety, propriety" — but is propriety just a matter of jade and silk? They keep saying "music, music" — but is music just bells and drums?'
D: Elegant: Propriety is not jade and silk. Music is not bells and drums. Both are far deeper than their outward forms.
D: Distilled: Ritual and music are not their physical trappings; reducing them to objects is missing the point entirely.
Notes: Li (禮) and Yue (樂) — the two pillars of Confucian culture. Confucius protests against reducing them to material forms (jade, silk, bells, drums). The inner meaning of Li is right relationship; the inner meaning of Yue is harmony. When people equate these with their physical instruments, the substance is lost. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: when institutions are reduced to their outward forms, their purpose has already been forgotten.

D: Original: The Master said, 'He who puts on an appearance of stern firmness, while inwardly he is weak, is like one of the small, mean people;-- yea, is he not like the thief who breaks through, or climbs over, a wall?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'A person who projects stern firmness while being inwardly weak is like a petty thief — one who breaks through walls or climbs over fences.'
D: Elegant: Outward toughness hiding inner weakness is not leadership — it is burglary.
D: Distilled: Projecting strength to conceal weakness is not virtue but a form of theft.
Notes: Se li nei ren (色厲內荏, stern outside, weak inside). The comparison to a thief (chuan yu, 穿窬, one who tunnels or climbs walls) is deliberately degrading: the person who performs strength while lacking it is committing a kind of fraud. Connects to FUALL's framework: authenticity is foundational — performance without substance is a form of deception.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Your good, careful people of the villages are the thieves of virtue.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'The carefully agreeable people of the villages are the thieves of virtue.'
D: Elegant: The village conformists who offend no one and stand for nothing — they are virtue's pickpockets.
D: Distilled: The person who is pleasant to everyone and principled about nothing is the most dangerous enemy of virtue.
Notes: Xiang yuan, de zhi zei ye (鄉原，德之賊也) — 'the village worthy is the thief of virtue.' One of Confucius's sharpest sayings. The xiang yuan (鄉原/鄉愿) is the person who goes along with everyone, appears moral, but actually has no convictions. Mencius later elaborated this concept extensively (Mencius 7B.37). Connects to FUALL's framework: the most dangerous threat to moral seriousness is not open vice but agreeable emptiness.

D: Original: The Master said, 'To tell, as we go along, what we have heard on the way, is to cast away our virtue.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'To repeat what you hear on the road as you walk along is to throw away your virtue.'
D: Elegant: Gossip is virtue's hemorrhage — it bleeds out as you walk.
D: Distilled: Repeating unverified information as you go is a steady erosion of your moral credibility.
Notes: Dao ting tu shuo (道聽途說) — 'heard on the road, told on the path.' This became a standard Chinese idiom for spreading rumors and gossip. De zhi qi (德之棄, casting away virtue). Unreflective repetition of hearsay damages both truth and character. Connects to FUALL's Reason: information must be verified before transmission.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'There are those mean creatures! How impossible it is along with them to serve one's prince!
'While they have not got their aims, their anxiety is how to get them. When they have got them, their anxiety is lest they should lose them.
'When they are anxious lest such things should be lost, there is nothing to which they will not proceed.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'What contemptible creatures! How impossible it is to serve one's ruler alongside them! Before they have what they want, they are consumed with getting it. Once they have it, they are consumed with fear of losing it. And when they fear losing it, there is nothing they will not do.'
D: Elegant: Before they get power, they scheme to get it. After they get it, they scheme to keep it. Between the two, there is nothing they won't do.
D: Distilled: People driven by acquisition and fear of loss will commit any act to satisfy either impulse.
Notes: Bi fu (鄙夫, mean/contemptible person). The psychological portrait: anxiety about getting and anxiety about losing produce identical moral degradation. The bi fu cannot serve alongside a ruler because his self-interest will override any public duty. Connects to FUALL's domains of power: when personal interest captures political roles, governance collapses.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'Anciently, men had three failings, which now perhaps are not to be found.
'The high-mindedness of antiquity showed itself in a disregard of small things; the high-mindedness of the present day shows itself in wild license. The stern dignity of antiquity showed itself in grave reserve; the stern dignity of the present day shows itself in quarrelsome perverseness. The stupidity of antiquity showed itself in straightforwardness; the stupidity of the present day shows itself in sheer deceit.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'In the old days, people had three characteristic failings, which may no longer exist in their original form. Ancient high-mindedness meant disregarding petty things — today's version is wild license. Ancient dignity meant grave reserve — today's version is quarrelsome pettiness. Ancient simplicity meant straightforwardness — today's version is outright deceit.'
D: Elegant: Ancient boldness ignored trifles; modern boldness ignores decency. Ancient gravity was reserved; modern gravity is quarrelsome. Ancient simplicity was direct; modern simplicity is dishonest.
D: Distilled: Every generation degrades the virtues it inherits: boldness becomes recklessness, dignity becomes aggression, simplicity becomes deceit.
Notes: Confucius describes a three-part moral decay where each classical virtue has degenerated into a parody of itself. Kuang (狂, high-mindedness) → dang (蕩, license). Jin (矜, dignity) → jiao (忿戾, quarrelsome). Yu (愚, simplicity) → zha (詐, deceit). Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: over time, the form of a virtue persists while its substance degrades.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Fine words and an insinuating appearance are seldom associated with virtue.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Smooth words and a flattering appearance are rarely associated with virtue.'
D: Elegant: Smooth words and a pleasing face: virtue's usual disguise — but usually a disguise for its absence.
D: Distilled: The more polished the presentation, the more suspect the character.
Notes: Qiao yan ling se, xian yi ren (巧言令色，鮮矣仁). Repeated from 1.3 — one of only a few sayings duplicated in the Analects. The repetition underscores how central this warning is to Confucius's teaching. Connects to FUALL's Reason: surface performance is inversely correlated with depth of character.

D: Original: The Master said, 'I hate the manner in which purple takes away the luster of vermilion. I hate the way in which the songs of Chang confound the music of the Ya. I hate those who with their sharp mouths overthrow kingdoms and families.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'I hate the way purple displaces vermilion. I hate the way the songs of Zheng corrupt proper music. I hate those who use sharp tongues to overthrow states and families.'
D: Elegant: Purple usurps red. Vulgar songs displace proper music. Sharp tongues topple kingdoms. Confucius hated all three — the corruption of the authentic by the counterfeit.
D: Distilled: The counterfeit that displaces the genuine — in color, music, or speech — is the deepest form of corruption.
Notes: Zi (紫, purple) displacing Zhu (朱, vermilion/red) — in Zhou court protocol, vermilion was the proper color of ritual garments; purple was an upstart imitation. Zheng sheng (鄭聲, songs of Zheng) — morally corrupting music. Li kou (利口, sharp tongues) — demagogues. All three examples are about counterfeits replacing originals. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: institutional decay happens when imitations successfully displace the genuine article.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'I would prefer not speaking.'
Tsze-kung said, 'If you, Master, do not speak, what shall we, your disciples, have to record?'
The Master said, 'Does Heaven speak? The four seasons pursue their courses, and all things are continually being produced, but does Heaven say anything?'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'I would prefer not to speak at all.' Zigong said, 'If you do not speak, Master, what will your disciples have to transmit?' The Master said, 'Does Heaven speak? The four seasons run their course, all things are born and grow, but does Heaven say anything?'
D: Elegant: 'I would prefer silence.' 'Then what will we transmit?' 'Does Heaven speak? The seasons turn, life grows — and Heaven says nothing.'
D: Distilled: The deepest truths may not require words; nature teaches without speaking.
Notes: Tian he yan zai (天何言哉) — 'what does Heaven say?' This is Confucius's most Daoist-sounding statement, echoing Laozi's concept of wu wei and the Dao that cannot be spoken. But in context, Confucius is not abandoning language — he is noting that the deepest teachings may be transmitted through example and practice rather than verbal instruction. Connects to FUALL's framework: some knowledge is transmitted through practice (Li) rather than proposition.

D: Original: Zu Pei wished to see Confucius, but Confucius declined, on the ground of being sick, to see him. When the bearer of this message went out at the door, (the Master) took his lute and sang to it, in order that Pei might hear him.
D: Refashioned: Ru Bei wanted to see Confucius. Confucius declined, claiming illness. But as soon as the messenger left the door, the Master picked up his lute and sang — making sure Ru Bei could hear.
D: Elegant: Confucius told the visitor he was too sick to receive him — then picked up his lute and sang loudly enough for the visitor to hear the lie.
D: Distilled: Sometimes the honest thing is to make your refusal unmistakable rather than politely ambiguous.
Notes: Ru Bei (孺悲) — the visitor's identity is uncertain; some commentators suggest he was a person Confucius wished to rebuke. By singing audibly after claiming illness, Confucius deliberately reveals his own excuse as false — an act of transparent discourtesy that communicates his disapproval more powerfully than any explanation. This is Li (禮) used paradoxically: violating protocol to make a moral point.

D: Original:
Tsai Wo asked about the three years' mourning for parents, saying that one year was long enough.
'If the superior man,' said he, 'abstains for three years from the observances of propriety, those observances will be quite lost. If for three years he abstains from music, music will be ruined.
'Within a year the old grain is exhausted, and the new grain has sprung up, and, in procuring fire by friction, we go through all the changes of wood for that purpose. After a complete year, the mourning may stop.'
The Master said, 'If you were, after a year, to eat good rice, and wear embroidered clothes, would you feel at ease?' 'I should,' replied Wo.
The Master said, 'If you can feel at ease, do it. But a superior man, during the whole period of mourning, does not enjoy pleasant food which he may eat, nor derive pleasure from music which he may hear. He also does not feel at ease, if he is comfortably lodged. Therefore he does not do what you propose. But now you feel at ease and may do it.'
Tsai Wo then went out, and the Master said, 'This shows Yu's want of virtue. It is not till a child is three years old that it is allowed to leave the arms of its parents. And the three years' mourning is universally observed throughout the empire. Did Yu enjoy the three years' love of his parents?'
D: Refashioned:
Zai Wo argued that one year of mourning for parents was sufficient. 'If a gentleman abstains from propriety for three years, propriety will be lost. If he abstains from music for three years, music will be ruined. After a year, the old grain is consumed and new grain has grown. The seasonal cycle is complete. One year is enough.'

The Master asked, 'If you ate good rice and wore fine clothes after just one year, would you feel comfortable?' Zai Wo said, 'Yes.' The Master said, 'If you feel comfortable, then do it. But the superior person during mourning finds no pleasure in good food, no enjoyment in music, and no comfort in fine lodgings. That is why he does not do what you propose. Since you feel comfortable, go ahead.'

After Zai Wo left, the Master said, 'What a lack of virtue in Yu! A child does not leave its parents' arms until it is three years old. The three-year mourning period is observed throughout the world. Did Yu not receive three years of love from his parents?'
D: Elegant: Zai Wo wanted to mourn for one year. Confucius asked: 'Would you feel comfortable eating well after one year?' 'Yes.' 'Then do it — but know that a virtuous person would not.' After he left: 'Did he not receive three years of love from his parents?'
D: Distilled: The mourning period mirrors the years of helpless dependence we owed our parents — and cutting it short reveals the depth of our ingratitude.
Notes: San nian zhi sang (三年之喪, three years' mourning) — one of the most debated Confucian practices. Zai Wo (宰我/宰予) — a disciple known for eloquence and for challenging Confucius. His rationalist argument (propriety decays, the grain cycle completes) is countered by Confucius's emotional argument: the three years mirror infancy's dependence. Xiao (孝, filial piety) — the mourning period is the child's reciprocal debt to parents. Confucius's method here is Socratic: he lets Zai Wo condemn himself. Connects to FUALL's framework: some obligations are grounded not in utility but in reciprocity and gratitude.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Hard is it to deal with him, who will stuff himself with food the whole day, without applying his mind to anything good! Are there not gamesters and chess players? To be one of these would still be better than doing nothing at all.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'How hard it is to deal with the person who eats all day without applying his mind to anything worthwhile. Are there not board games and chess? Even those would be better than nothing.'
D: Elegant: Even playing chess is better than spending the day eating and thinking about nothing.
D: Distilled: Any mental exercise, even a game, is better than a mind that refuses to engage with anything.
Notes: Bo yi (博弈, board games and chess) — games requiring strategy. Confucius does not endorse gaming but uses it as a floor: anything that exercises the mind is better than complete mental idleness. The passage is about the minimum standard of human engagement. Connects to FUALL's Academy: the first step is simply getting people to think about something.

D: Original: Tsze-lu said, 'Does the superior man esteem valour?' The Master said, 'The superior man holds righteousness to be of highest importance. A man in a superior situation, having valour without righteousness, will be guilty of insubordination; one of the lower people having valour without righteousness, will commit robbery.'
D: Refashioned: Zilu asked, 'Does the superior person esteem courage?' The Master said, 'The superior person holds righteousness supreme. A person in authority who has courage without righteousness will be guilty of rebellion. A common person who has courage without righteousness will become a robber.'
D: Elegant: Courage without righteousness is rebellion in a leader and robbery in a commoner. Righteousness comes first.
D: Distilled: Courage without moral direction is dangerous regardless of social position.
Notes: Yi (義, righteousness) over Yong (勇, courage). The hierarchy is absolute: courage is only a virtue when governed by righteousness. Without Yi, courage in a ruler produces tyranny and in a subject produces crime. Connects to FUALL's Right: moral orientation must precede and constrain every other quality.

D: Original:
Tsze-kung said, 'Has the superior man his hatreds also?' The Master said, 'He has his hatreds. He hates those who proclaim the evil of others. He hates the man who, being in a low station, slanders his superiors. He hates those who have valour merely, and are unobservant of propriety. He hates those who are forward and determined, and, at the same time, of contracted understanding.'
The Master then inquired, 'Ts'ze, have you also your hatreds?' Tsze-kung replied, 'I hate those who pry out matters, and ascribe the knowledge to their wisdom. I hate those who are only not modest, and think that they are valourous. I hate those who make known secrets, and think that they are straightforward.'
D: Refashioned: Zigong asked, 'Does the superior person also have things he hates?' The Master said, 'Yes. He hates those who publicize others' faults. He hates those in low positions who slander their superiors. He hates the bold who lack propriety. He hates the decisive but narrow-minded.' The Master then asked, 'Zigong, do you also have your hatreds?' Zigong replied, 'I hate those who spy out information and call it wisdom. I hate those who mistake rudeness for courage. I hate those who expose secrets and call it honesty.'
D: Elegant: The Master's hatreds: gossips, insubordinate slanderers, reckless brutes, and stubborn fools. Zigong's: spies who claim insight, bullies who claim courage, and tattlers who claim integrity.
D: Distilled: Even the virtuous have hatreds — what they hate reveals what they value.
Notes: A rich exchange about legitimate moral aversion. Both Confucius's and Zigong's lists target the same pattern: people who disguise vice as virtue. Calling espionage 'wisdom,' rudeness 'courage,' or betrayal 'honesty' — each is a corruption of Zhengming (正名, correct naming). Connects to FUALL's Reason: moral clarity requires seeing through self-serving relabelings of vice as virtue.

D: Original: The Master said, 'Of all people, girls and servants are the most difficult to behave to. If you are familiar with them, they lose their humility. If you maintain a reserve towards them, they are discontented.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Women and servants are the most difficult to manage. If you are familiar with them, they become presumptuous. If you keep your distance, they become resentful.'
D: Elegant: Familiarity breeds presumption; distance breeds resentment. Some relationships defy easy calibration.
D: Distilled: Some social dynamics resist every strategy: closeness and distance both produce problems.
Notes: This is the most controversial passage in the Analects in modern scholarship. Many scholars read it as reflecting the patriarchal norms of 5th-century BCE China rather than a timeless moral teaching. Others interpret 'women' (nü zi, 女子) as referring specifically to palace women and concubines, and 'servants' (xiao ren, 小人) as household staff — making it a comment on household management, not a universal claim about gender. The passage describes a genuine management dilemma about calibrating formality and warmth.

D: Original: The Master said, 'When a man at forty is the object of dislike, he will always continue what he is.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'If a person is still disliked at forty, that is how he will always remain.'
D: Elegant: If at forty you are still disliked, you are not going to change.
D: Distilled: By forty, character is mostly fixed; the person who is still objectionable will likely stay that way.
Notes: Si shi er jian wu yan (四十而見惡焉) — by age forty, character is set. This is descriptive rather than prescriptive: Confucius is not condemning change but observing its declining probability. Connects to 2.4 where Confucius describes his own development in decades (thirty: established; forty: no doubts; etc.).

D: Original: Book 18: Wei Tsze (The Viscount of Wei)

The Viscount of Wei withdrew from the court. The Viscount of Chi became a slave to Chau. Pi-kan remonstrated with him and died.
Confucius said, 'The Yin dynasty possessed these three men of virtue.'
D: Refashioned: The Viscount of Wei withdrew from court. The Viscount of Ji became a slave. Bi Gan remonstrated with the tyrant and was killed. Confucius said, 'The Yin dynasty had these three men of virtue.'
D: Elegant: One withdrew. One submitted to slavery. One spoke out and died. Confucius said: 'Three men of virtue.'
D: Distilled: Virtue under tyranny takes many forms: withdrawal, endurance, and martyrdom are all legitimate responses.
Notes: The Viscount of Wei (微子), the Viscount of Ji (箕子), and Bi Gan (比干) — three noble relatives of King Zhou (紂), the last and most tyrannical ruler of the Shang/Yin dynasty. Each responded to tyranny differently: Wei withdrew, Ji submitted to slavery, Bi Gan protested and was executed. Confucius validates all three responses. Connects to FUALL's framework: there is no single correct response to institutional corruption — integrity can express itself through flight, endurance, or confrontation.

D: Original: Hui of Liu-hsia being chief criminal judge, was thrice dismissed from his office. Some one said to him, 'Is it not yet time for you, sir, to leave this?' He replied, 'Serving men in an upright way, where shall I go to, and not experience such a thrice- repeated dismissal? If I choose to serve men in a crooked way, what necessity is there for me to leave the country of my parents?'
D: Refashioned: Hui of Liuxia served as chief judge and was dismissed three times. Someone said, 'Is it not time to leave?' He replied, 'If I serve people uprightly, where could I go and not be dismissed? If I were willing to serve dishonestly, why would I need to leave my own country?'
D: Elegant: 'Serve honestly and be dismissed everywhere, or serve dishonestly and never need to leave.' He chose to stay and be dismissed.
D: Distilled: The honest person who keeps getting fired is not the one with the problem.
Notes: Hui of Liuxia (柳下惠) — a famous judge of Lu, repeatedly dismissed for refusing to compromise his principles. His logic is airtight: if integrity causes dismissal everywhere, the problem is systemic, not personal. If you abandon integrity, you can stay anywhere — but the staying is worthless. Connects to FUALL's framework: institutional corruption punishes integrity, but abandoning integrity to survive within the institution is worse than dismissal.

D: Original: The duke Ching of Ch'i, with reference to the manner in which he should treat Confucius, said, 'I cannot treat him as I would the chief of the Chi family. I will treat him in a manner between that accorded to the chief of the Chi, and that given to the chief of the Mang family.' He also said, 'I am old; I cannot use his doctrines.' Confucius took his departure.
D: Refashioned: Duke Jing of Qi, considering how to treat Confucius, said, 'I cannot give him the treatment due the head of the Ji family. I will treat him somewhere between that and the level of the Meng family.' He also said, 'I am old; I cannot put his doctrines to use.' Confucius departed.
D: Elegant: The duke admitted he could not give Confucius top rank and was too old to use his ideas. So Confucius left.
D: Distilled: When a ruler admits he cannot implement your teaching, there is nothing left to do but leave.
Notes: Duke Jing of Qi (齊景公) — see 12.11 and 16.12. His half-hearted offer and admission of inability represent the frustration Confucius faced repeatedly: rulers who admired his ideas but lacked the will or capacity to implement them. Connects to FUALL's framework: ideas without institutional commitment remain ideas.

D: Original: The people of Ch'i sent to Lu a present of female musicians, which Chi Hwan received, and for three days no court was held. Confucius took his departure.
D: Refashioned: The people of Qi sent a gift of female musicians to Lu. Ji Huan accepted them, and for three days the court did not convene. Confucius left.
D: Elegant: Qi sent dancing girls. The court shut down for three days. Confucius packed and left.
D: Distilled: When pleasure captures governance, the person of principle has no reason to stay.
Notes: Ji Huan (季桓子) — head of the Ji family, the de facto ruler of Lu. The female musicians (nü yue, 女樂) were a deliberate diplomatic strategy by Qi to distract Lu's government. Confucius's departure was precipitated by this final evidence that Lu's rulers were unserious. This occurred around 497 BCE, beginning Confucius's 14-year exile. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: when entertainment displaces governance, the system has failed.

D: Original:
The madman of Ch'u, Chieh-yu, passed by Confucius, singing and saying, 'O FANG! O FANG! How is your virtue degenerated! As to the past, reproof is useless; but the future may still be provided against. Give up your vain pursuit. Give up your vain pursuit. Peril awaits those who now engage in affairs of government.'
Confucius alighted and wished to converse with him, but Chieh-yu hastened away, so that he could not talk with him.
D: Refashioned: Jieyu, the madman of Chu, passed by Confucius singing: 'O Phoenix! O Phoenix! How your virtue has declined! The past cannot be corrected, but the future may still be saved. Give up! Give up! Those who serve in government today court danger.' Confucius got down from his carriage to speak with him, but Jieyu hurried away before they could talk.
D: Elegant: A madman sang to Confucius: 'O Phoenix, your virtue is fading. Give up — government now is peril.' Confucius ran after him, but the madman was already gone.
D: Distilled: Sometimes the clearest assessment of a situation comes from those considered mad — and the sage cannot even catch them to respond.
Notes: Jieyu (接舆) — the 'madman of Chu,' a famous recluse figure who appears in both the Analects and the Zhuangzi. The phoenix (feng, 鳳) is Confucius's symbol — a mythical bird that appears only in times of good governance. Jieyu's song is both warning and lament: the age of good governance has passed. Confucius's attempt to engage and Jieyu's refusal dramatize the split between activism and withdrawal. Connects to FUALL's framework: the tension between the activist who insists on engagement and the critic who sees the futility.

D: Original:
Ch'ang-tsu and Chieh-ni were at work in the field together, when Confucius passed by them, and sent Tsze-lu to inquire for the ford.
Ch'ang-tsu said, 'Who is he that holds the reins in the carriage there?' Tsze-lu told him, 'It is K'ung Ch'iu.' 'Is it not K'ung Ch'iu of Lu?' asked he. 'Yes,' was the reply, to which the other rejoined, 'He knows the ford.'
Tsze-lu then inquired of Chieh-ni, who said to him, 'Who are you, sir?' He answered, 'I am Chung Yu.' 'Are you not the disciple of K'ung Ch'iu of Lu?' asked the other. 'I am,' replied he, and then Chieh-ni said to him, 'Disorder, like a swelling flood, spreads over the whole empire, and who is he that will change its state for you? Than follow one who merely withdraws from this one and that one, had you not better follow those who have withdrawn from the world altogether?' With this he fell to covering up the seed, and proceeded with his work, without stopping.
Tsze-lu went and reported their remarks, when the Master observed with a sigh, 'It is impossible to associate with birds and beasts, as if they were the same with us. If I associate not with these people,-- with mankind,-- with whom shall I associate? If right principles prevailed through the empire, there would be no use for me to change its state.'
D: Refashioned:
Changju and Jieni were plowing together when Confucius passed by and sent Zilu to ask for directions to the river ford. Changju asked, 'Who is riding in that carriage?' 'Kong Qiu.' 'Of Lu?' 'Yes.' 'Then he already knows the ford.' Zilu asked Jieni instead. Jieni said, 'Who are you?' 'Zhong You.' 'The disciple of Kong Qiu of Lu?' 'Yes.' Jieni said, 'Disorder floods the whole world. Who is going to change it? Rather than following a man who merely avoids this ruler or that, you would do better following those of us who have abandoned the world altogether.' And he went back to his plowing.

Zilu reported this to the Master, who sighed: 'I cannot live among birds and beasts as though they were my companions. If I do not associate with humanity, with whom should I associate? If the Way prevailed in the world, I would have no need to try to change it.'
D: Elegant: The hermits told Zilu: 'The world is flooded with chaos. Who will fix it? Follow us into withdrawal.' Confucius sighed: 'I cannot live with birds and beasts. If the Way prevailed, I would not need to change anything.'
D: Distilled: Withdrawal from a broken world is logical — but someone must stay and work within humanity, because birds and beasts will not build civilization.
Notes: Changju (長沮) and Jieni (桀溺) — hermit farmers who represent the Daoist/recluse position. Their argument: the world is beyond repair; withdrawal is the only sane response. Confucius's counter: human beings must live among human beings; withdrawal abandons the responsibility to try. 'If the Dao prevailed, I would not need to change it' (天下有道，丘不與易也) — his activism is not ambition but duty born from necessity. This is the definitive statement of Confucian engagement vs. Daoist withdrawal. Connects directly to FUALL's framework: the civilizational project exists because withdrawal, however tempting, solves nothing.

D: Original:
Tsze-lu, following the Master, happened to fall behind, when he met an old man, carrying across his shoulder on a staff a basket for weeds. Tsze-lu said to him, 'Have you seen my master, sir!' The old man replied, 'Your four limbs are unaccustomed to toil; you cannot distinguish the five kinds of grain:-- who is your master?' With this, he planted his staff in the ground, and proceeded to weed.
Tsze-lu joined his hands across his breast, and stood before him.
The old man kept Tsze-lu to pass the night in his house, killed a fowl, prepared millet, and feasted him. He also introduced to him his two sons.
Next day, Tsze-lu went on his way, and reported his adventure. The Master said, 'He is a recluse,' and sent Tsze-lu back to see him again, but when he got to the place, the old man was gone.
Tsze-lu then said to the family, 'Not to take office is not righteous. If the relations between old and young may not be neglected, how is it that he sets aside the duties that should be observed between sovereign and minister? Wishing to maintain his personal purity, he allows that great relation to come to confusion. A superior man takes office, and performs the righteous duties belonging to it. As to the failure of right principles to make progress, he is aware of that.'
D: Refashioned:
Zilu, traveling behind the Master, met an old man carrying a basket of weeds on a staff. Zilu asked, 'Have you seen my master?' The old man replied, 'You can't even use your four limbs properly or tell the five grains apart — what kind of master do you have?' He stuck his staff in the ground and began weeding. Zilu stood respectfully with folded hands.

The old man invited Zilu to stay the night, killed a chicken, cooked millet, and introduced his two sons. The next day Zilu continued his journey and reported to the Master. Confucius said, 'He is a recluse,' and sent Zilu back — but the old man was gone.

Zilu said to the household: 'Not taking office is a failure of duty. If the bonds between old and young cannot be neglected, how can the obligations between ruler and subject be set aside? In trying to keep his own hands clean, he has abandoned the great social order. The superior person takes office and fulfills the duties that come with it. As for the Dao not prevailing — he is already aware of that.'
D: Elegant: The old man mocked Zilu, fed him, and disappeared. Zilu's message to his empty house: 'You kept your hands clean by abandoning every obligation. Purity at the cost of duty is not virtue.'
D: Distilled: Personal purity purchased by abandoning social responsibility is selfishness disguised as virtue.
Notes: The old man (he ren, 荷蓧丈人) — another hermit-farmer in the Daoist/recluse tradition. The encounter follows 18.5 and 18.6 in a sequence of confrontations between Confucian engagement and recluse withdrawal. Zilu's speech is the Confucian rebuttal: if you recognize the obligation between old and young (within the family), you cannot deny the obligation between ruler and subject (within the state). Private virtue that ignores public duty is incomplete. Connects to FUALL's framework: the educational institutions require active participation; withdrawal is a failure of the mission.

D: Original:
The men who have retired to privacy from the world have been Po-i, Shu-ch'i, Yu-chung, I-yi, Chu-chang, Hui of Liu-hsia, and Shao-lien.
The Master said, 'Refusing to surrender their wills, or to submit to any taint in their persons;-- such, I think, were Po-i and Shu-ch'i.
'It may be said of Hui of Liu-hsia, and of Shao-lien, that they surrendered their wills, and submitted to taint in their persons, but their words corresponded with reason, and their actions were such as men are anxious to see. This is all that is to be remarked in them.
'It may be said of Yu-chung and I-yi, that, while they hid themselves in their seclusion, they gave a license to their words; but, in their persons, they succeeded in preserving their purity, and, in their retirement, they acted according to the exigency of the times.
'I am different from all these. I have no course for which I am predetermined, and no course against which I am predetermined.'
D: Refashioned: Those who withdrew entirely from the world include Bo Yi, Shu Qi, Yu Zhong, Yi Yi, Zhu Zhang, Hui of Liuxia, and Shao Lian. The Master said, 'Bo Yi and Shu Qi refused to compromise their will or taint their persons. Hui of Liuxia and Shao Lian did compromise their will and accept humiliation, but their words accorded with reason and their actions were carefully considered. Yu Zhong and Yi Yi lived in seclusion and spoke freely, but maintained personal purity and acted according to the demands of their times. I am different from all of them. I have no fixed course I must follow and no fixed course I must avoid.'
D: Elegant: Some refused to bend. Some bent and bore the shame. Some hid and spoke freely. Confucius said: 'I am different. I have no predetermined course — for or against.'
D: Distilled: The sage's flexibility is not indecision but freedom from any fixed strategy — adapting to what the moment requires.
Notes: Wu ke wu bu ke (無可無不可) — 'nothing I am predetermined for, nothing I am predetermined against.' This is Confucius's statement of ultimate adaptability: he is not committed to any single strategy (engagement, withdrawal, compromise, defiance) but responds to each situation as it demands. Bo Yi and Shu Qi — purists. Hui of Liuxia and Shao Lian — pragmatists. Yu Zhong and Yi Yi — recluses. Confucius transcends all three categories. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom: the highest understanding is responsive to Reality rather than locked into any single approach.

D: Original:
The grand music master, Chih, went to Ch'i.
Kan, the master of the band at the second meal, went to Ch'u. Liao, the band master at the third meal, went to Ts'ai. Chueh, the band master at the fourth meal, went to Ch'in.
Fang-shu, the drum master, withdrew to the north of the river.
Wu, the master of the hand drum, withdrew to the Han.
Yang, the assistant music master, and Hsiang, master of the musical stone, withdrew to an island in the sea.
D: Refashioned: The grand music-master Zhi went to Qi. The bandmaster for the second meal went to Chu. The one for the third meal went to Cai. The one for the fourth meal went to Qin. The drum-master withdrew north of the river. The hand-drum master withdrew to the Han. The assistant music-master and the stone-chime master withdrew to an island in the sea.
D: Elegant: One by one, the musicians of Lu scattered — to Qi, Chu, Cai, Qin, beyond the rivers, to an island in the sea. The court's music dispersed with them.
D: Distilled: When the musicians leave, the culture is already dead.
Notes: The dispersal of Lu's court musicians symbolizes the collapse of ritual culture. Music (Yue, 樂) was central to Confucian governance — its disintegration signaled institutional decay. Each musician's departure to a different state shows the fragmentation of what was once a unified cultural system. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: when the carriers of culture scatter, the culture itself dissolves.

D: Original: The duke of Chau addressed his son, the duke of Lu, saying, 'The virtuous prince does not neglect his relations. He does not cause the great ministers to repine at his not employing them. Without some great cause, he does not dismiss from their offices the members of old families. He does not seek in one man talents for every employment.'
D: Refashioned: The Duke of Zhou told his son, the Duke of Lu: 'The virtuous ruler does not neglect his own relatives. He does not give his great ministers cause to resent being overlooked. He does not dismiss members of old families without serious reason. He does not expect one person to be competent at everything.'
D: Elegant: Four rules for ruling: do not neglect your kin, do not alienate your ministers, do not dismiss old families lightly, and do not expect one person to do everything.
D: Distilled: Good governance maintains relationships, retains institutional memory, and distributes responsibilities realistically.
Notes: The Duke of Zhou (周公, Zhou Gong Dan) — the sage-regent whom Confucius most admired, founder of the state of Lu. His advice to his son (the first Duke of Lu, Bo Qin) is practical statecraft: maintain alliances, preserve institutional continuity, and match talent to roles. 'Not seeking in one man talents for every employment' (wu qiu bei yu yi ren, 無求備於一人) anticipates modern management theory. Connects to FUALL's Guilds: institutional design must distribute capability, not concentrate it.

D: Original: To Chau belonged the eight officers, Po-ta, Po- kwo, Chung-tu, Chung-hwu, Shu-ya, Shu-hsia, Chi-sui, and Chi-kwa.
D: Refashioned: The Zhou dynasty had eight distinguished officers: Boda, Boguo, Zhongtu, Zhonghu, Shuye, Shuxia, Jisui, and Jikua.
D: Elegant: Eight officers of Zhou — four pairs of brothers, each serving with distinction.
D: Distilled: The Zhou dynasty's strength was visible in the quality and number of its officers.
Notes: The eight officers (ba shi, 八士) are traditionally identified as four pairs of twins or brothers. Their listing serves as evidence of the Zhou dynasty's excellence in human capital — it attracted and developed talent in abundance. The passage is brief but its placement after the Duke of Zhou's advice (18.10) makes its point: the right principles produce the right people.

D: Original: Book 19: Tsze-Chang

Tsze-chang said, 'The scholar, trained for public duty, seeing threatening danger, is prepared to sacrifice his life. When the opportunity of gain is presented to him, he thinks of righteousness. In sacrificing, his thoughts are reverential. In mourning, his thoughts are about the grief which he should feel. Such a man commands our approbation indeed.'
