D: Refashioned: Zizhang said, 'The true scholar, seeing danger, is ready to give his life. Seeing opportunity for gain, he thinks of righteousness. In sacrifice, he thinks of reverence. In mourning, he thinks of grief. This is the person we can approve.'
D: Elegant: Danger: offer your life. Gain: think of justice. Sacrifice: be reverent. Mourning: grieve truly. That is the officer worth approving.
D: Distilled: A worthy person is tested at four points: danger, gain, worship, and grief — and responds with courage, justice, reverence, and genuine feeling.
Notes: Zizhang (子張) — a disciple known for ambition and sometimes criticized for showiness (19.15-16). His description of the shi (士, scholar-officer) uses four crisis moments to test character. Yi (義) governs the response to gain; Jing (敬) governs sacrifice; Ai (哀) governs mourning. Connects to FUALL's Right: character is revealed in crisis moments, not in comfortable times.

D: Original: Tsze-chang said, 'When a man holds fast to virtue, but without seeking to enlarge it, and believes right principles, but without firm sincerity, what account can be made of his existence or non-existence?'
D: Refashioned: Zizhang said, 'A person who holds to virtue without trying to expand it, and believes in principles without deep conviction — what difference does his existence make?'
D: Elegant: To hold virtue without expanding it and believe without conviction — this is existing without mattering.
D: Distilled: Passive virtue and uncommitted belief are indistinguishable from having neither.
Notes: Zhi de bu hong (執德不弘, holding virtue without broadening it). Zizhang criticizes static virtue — having principles but not developing or applying them. The question 'what account can be made of his existence or non-existence?' is sharply existential. Connects to FUALL's framework: wisdom must be actively cultivated, not merely held.

D: Original: The disciples of Tsze-hsia asked Tsze-chang about the principles that should characterize mutual intercourse. Tsze- chang asked, 'What does Tsze-hsia say on the subject?' They replied, 'Tsze-hsia says:-- "Associate with those who can advantage you. Put away from you those who cannot do so."' Tsze-chang observed, 'This is different from what I have learned. The superior man honours the talented and virtuous, and bears with all. He praises the good, and pities the incompetent. Am I possessed of great talents and virtue?-- who is there among men whom I will not bear with? Am I devoid of talents and virtue?-- men will put me away from them. What have we to do with the putting away of others?'
D: Refashioned: Zixia's students asked Zizhang about the principles of social relations. Zizhang asked, 'What does Zixia say?' They said, 'Zixia says: associate with those who can help you; avoid those who cannot.' Zizhang said, 'That is different from what I have learned. The superior person honors the talented but accepts everyone. He praises the good and pities the incompetent. If I am truly talented, whom can I not tolerate? If I lack talent, others will avoid me — I will not need to avoid them.'
D: Elegant: Zixia says: befriend the useful, avoid the useless. Zizhang counters: if I am great, I can bear anyone; if I am not, they will flee from me. Either way, exclusion is not my business.
D: Distilled: The truly capable person does not need to sort others — his own qualities do the sorting.
Notes: A debate between two Confucian schools of thought on social relations. Zixia (子夏) advocates selective association (strategic networking). Zizhang (子張) advocates universal tolerance based on self-confidence. Both positions have merit; the tension between selectivity and inclusiveness is unresolved. Connects to FUALL's Agora: intellectual communities must balance standards with openness.

D: Original: Tsze-hsia said, 'Even in inferior studies and employments there is something worth being looked at; but if it be attempted to carry them out to what is remote, there is a danger of their proving inapplicable. Therefore, the superior man does not practise them.'
D: Refashioned: Zixia said, 'Even minor studies have something worth examining. But pushed too far, they risk becoming impractical. Therefore the superior person does not pursue them.'
D: Elegant: Minor studies have minor value. But pursued too far, they become obstacles to what matters.
D: Distilled: Specialization in trivia is worse than ignorance of it, because it crowds out what is important.
Notes: Xiao dao (小道, minor ways/studies) — technical or specialized knowledge that has limited scope. Zixia warns against the danger of over-investment in narrow expertise at the expense of broad understanding. Connects to FUALL's Academy: education must prioritize foundational understanding over specialized knowledge.

D: Original: Tsze-hsia said, 'He, who from day to day recognises what he has not yet, and from month to month does not forget what he has attained to, may be said indeed to love to learn.'
D: Refashioned: Zixia said, 'The person who each day recognizes what he does not yet know, and each month does not forget what he has learned, can truly be said to love learning.'
D: Elegant: Each day: know what you don't know. Each month: don't forget what you've learned. That is what loving to learn looks like.
D: Distilled: True love of learning is the daily expansion of awareness and the monthly retention of what was gained.
Notes: Ri zhi qi suo wu (日知其所亡, daily know what you lack). Hao xue (好學, loving learning) — the quality Confucius prized most and attributed fully only to Yan Hui (6.3, 11.6). Zixia's formulation makes it operational: it is a daily and monthly practice, not a disposition. Connects to FUALL's Academy: learning is a disciplined habit, not a natural talent.

D: Original: Tsze-hsia said, 'There are learning extensively, and having a firm and sincere aim; inquiring with earnestness, and reflecting with self-application:-- virtue is in such a course.'
D: Refashioned: Zixia said, 'Learn broadly, hold your aim firmly, inquire earnestly, and reflect on what is near at hand — virtue is found in this course.'
D: Elegant: Broad study, firm purpose, earnest inquiry, and close reflection — together, these are virtue.
D: Distilled: Virtue emerges from the combination of wide learning, steady purpose, honest questioning, and practical reflection.
Notes: Bo xue, du zhi, qie wen, jin si (博學、篤志、切問、近思) — four components of intellectual virtue. This is one of the clearest methodological statements in the Analects. 'Reflecting on what is near' (jin si, 近思) means applying abstract learning to concrete, immediate situations. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom framework: Reality (近思), Reason (博學, 切問), and Right (篤志) integrated through practice.

D: Original: Tsze-hsia said, 'Mechanics have their shops to dwell in, in order to accomplish their works. The superior man learns, in order to reach to the utmost of his principles.'
D: Refashioned: Zixia said, 'Craftsmen have workshops where they develop their skills. The superior person learns in order to reach the highest level of his principles.'
D: Elegant: The craftsman perfects his art in his workshop. The superior person perfects his principles through learning.
D: Distilled: Learning is the workshop where character is built.
Notes: Bai gong ju si yi cheng qi shi (百工居肆以成其事) — craftsmen dwell in workshops to complete their work. Junzi xue yi zhi qi dao (君子學以致其道) — the superior person learns to reach his Way. The analogy elevates learning to the status of craft: it requires a space, sustained effort, and practical output. Connects to FUALL's Guilds: both technical and moral competence are built through disciplined practice.

D: Original: Tsze-hsia said, 'The mean man is sure to gloss his faults.'
D: Refashioned: Zixia said, 'The inferior person always finds a way to gloss over his faults.'
D: Elegant: The petty person's specialty: making his faults look like anything but faults.
D: Distilled: The inferior person's defining skill is the ability to repackage his failures as something else.
Notes: Xiao ren zhi guo ye bi wen (小人之過也必文, the petty person's faults are always glossed over). Wen (文) here means to ornament or disguise. Self-deception about one's own faults is the mark of the Xiaoren. Contrasts with 15.29: 'To have faults and not reform them — that is the real fault.' Connects to FUALL's Reason: intellectual honesty begins with honest self-assessment.

D: Original: Tsze-hsia said, 'The superior man undergoes three changes. Looked at from a distance, he appears stern; when approached, he is mild; when he is heard to speak, his language is firm and decided.'
D: Refashioned: Zixia said, 'The superior person appears three ways. From a distance, he looks stern. Close up, he is warm. When he speaks, his words are firm and clear.'
D: Elegant: At a distance: stern. Up close: warm. When he speaks: precise. Three impressions of one person, each true.
D: Distilled: The superior person's authority, warmth, and clarity are not contradictions — they are the same character seen from different angles.
Notes: San bian (三變, three changes/impressions). The Junzi's appearance shifts with proximity: commanding at a distance (wei, 望之儼然), approachable close up (ji zhi ye wen, 即之也溫), and precise in speech (ting qi yan ye li, 聽其言也厲). Genuine character integrates qualities that seem contradictory. Connects to FUALL's framework: depth of character produces coherent complexity, not inconsistency.

D: Original: Tsze-hsia said, 'The superior man, having obtained their confidence, may then impose labours on his people. If he have not gained their confidence, they will think that he is oppressing them. Having obtained the confidence of his prince, one may then remonstrate with him. If he have not gained his confidence, the prince will think that he is vilifying him.'
D: Refashioned: Zixia said, 'The superior person must gain the trust of the people before imposing burdens on them. Without their trust, they will feel oppressed. He must gain the trust of his ruler before offering criticism. Without the ruler's trust, the ruler will think he is being attacked.'
D: Elegant: Before you burden the people, earn their trust — or they will call it oppression. Before you criticize your ruler, earn his trust — or he will call it attack.
D: Distilled: The right to demand and the right to criticize are both earned through trust, never assumed.
Notes: Xin er hou lao (信而後勞, trust then labor) and xin er hou jian (信而後諫, trust then remonstrate). Xin (信, trust/faithfulness) is the prerequisite for both governance and counsel. Without it, the same actions (taxation, criticism) are experienced as oppression and insult. Connects to FUALL's domains of power: legitimacy (ideational domain) must precede the exercise of authority.

D: Original: Tsze-hsia said, 'When a person does not transgress the boundary line in the great virtues, he may pass and repass it in the small virtues.'
D: Refashioned: Zixia said, 'As long as a person does not transgress the boundary in great virtues, he may cross back and forth in the small ones.'
D: Elegant: Hold the line on the great virtues. On the small ones, there is room to move.
D: Distilled: Moral flexibility in small matters is permissible only when the fundamental principles are never compromised.
Notes: Da de bu yu xian (大德不踰閑, great virtue does not exceed the boundary). Xiao de chu ru ke ye (小德出入可也, small virtue may go in and out). A practical principle of moral prioritization: not every rule carries equal weight. Connects to FUALL's Right: ethical judgment requires distinguishing between foundational principles and secondary rules.

D: Original:
Tsze-yu said, 'The disciples and followers of Tsze-hsia, in sprinkling and sweeping the ground, in answering and replying, in advancing and receding, are sufficiently accomplished. But these are only the branches of learning, and they are left ignorant of what is essential.-- How can they be acknowledged as sufficiently taught?'
Tsze-hsia heard of the remark and said, 'Alas! Yen Yu is wrong. According to the way of the superior man in teaching, what departments are there which he considers of prime importance, and delivers? what are there which he considers of secondary importance, and allows himself to be idle about? But as in the case of plants, which are assorted according to their classes, so he deals with his disciples. How can the way of a superior man be such as to make fools of any of them? Is it not the sage alone, who can unite in one the beginning and the consummation of learning?'
D: Refashioned: Ziyu said, 'Zixia's students are good enough at sweeping floors, greeting guests, and knowing when to advance or retreat. But these are only the branches of learning — they lack the roots. How can they be called properly educated?' Zixia heard this and replied, 'Ziyu is wrong. In the Way of the superior person, what is taught first and what is set aside for later? It is like plants sorted by kind — each in its proper order. How can the Way of the superior person deceive any student? Only the sage can unite the beginning and end of learning.'
D: Elegant: Ziyu said: 'Your students know etiquette but not substance.' Zixia replied: 'Learning has a natural sequence, like sorting plants by type. Only a sage can teach it all at once.'
D: Distilled: The debate between roots and branches in education is false — both matter, and the sequence depends on the student.
Notes: A debate between two of Confucius's leading students about pedagogy. Ziyu (子游) criticizes Zixia's students for mastering forms (etiquette) without substance. Zixia defends a sequential approach: basics first, depth later. Both positions reflect real tensions in education. Ben (本, roots) vs. Mo (末, branches) is a perennial educational debate. Connects to FUALL's Academy: curriculum design must balance foundational skills with deeper understanding.

D: Original: Tsze-hsia said, 'The officer, having discharged all his duties, should devote his leisure to learning. The student, having completed his learning, should apply himself to be an officer.'
D: Refashioned: Zixia said, 'The official who has fulfilled his duties should spend his remaining time in learning. The student who has completed his studies should devote himself to public service.'
D: Elegant: After duty, study. After study, serve. Learning and service feed each other.
D: Distilled: Service and learning are not alternatives but phases of the same life: each prepares for the other.
Notes: Shi you yu li ze xue; xue you yu li ze shi (仕而優則學；學而優則仕). One of the most quoted passages in Chinese educational history. The relationship between study and service is reciprocal: practice informs learning, learning improves practice. Connects to FUALL's framework: the Academy and the Guilds are not separate tracks but alternating phases.

D: Original: Tsze-hsia said, 'Mourning, having been carried to the utmost degree of grief, should stop with that.'
D: Refashioned: Zixia said, 'Mourning should be carried to the utmost depth of grief — and then stop there.'
D: Elegant: Grieve fully. Then stop. Mourning that exceeds genuine feeling becomes performance.
D: Distilled: Mourning should reach the depth of real grief and go no further.
Notes: Sang zhi ai er zhi (喪致乎哀而止). Li (禮) governs mourning: it must express genuine emotion but not exceed it. Excessive mourning becomes display rather than feeling. The principle: ritual should channel emotion, not replace it or amplify it beyond what is real.

D: Original: Tsze-hsia said, 'My friend Chang can do things which are hard to be done, but yet he is not perfectly virtuous.'
D: Refashioned: Zixia said, 'My friend Zizhang can do difficult things, but he has not yet achieved complete virtue.'
D: Elegant: Zizhang can do what is hard. That is not the same as doing what is right.
D: Distilled: Capability and virtue are different accomplishments; one does not guarantee the other.
Notes: Zizhang (子張) — praised for ambition and ability but repeatedly noted as lacking in Ren (仁). The criticism is specific: difficulty of execution is not the measure of moral quality. Connects to FUALL's framework: competence (Guilds) and character (Temple) are both necessary.

D: Original: The philosopher Tsang said, 'How imposing is the manner of Chang! It is difficult along with him to practise virtue.'
D: Refashioned: Zengzi said, 'How imposing Zizhang's manner is! It is hard to practice virtue alongside him.'
D: Elegant: Zizhang's manner is so grand that everyone around him feels small. That is not virtue — it is a barrier to it.
D: Distilled: An imposing manner that intimidates rather than inspires makes virtue harder for everyone nearby.
Notes: Zengzi's criticism of Zizhang complements Zixia's in 19.15. The imposing manner (tang tang, 堂堂) — impressive but creating distance. The Junzi should make virtue accessible, not intimidating. Connects to FUALL's Temple: the institution that transmits moral culture must be inviting, not forbidding.

D: Original: The philosopher Tsang said, 'I heard this from our Master:-- "Men may not have shown what is in them to the full extent, and yet they will be found to do so, on occasion of mourning for their parents."'
D: Refashioned: Zengzi said, 'I heard this from our Master: "People may not fully reveal themselves in daily life, yet they will do so when mourning their parents."'
D: Elegant: A person's true depth emerges in grief for a parent. Until then, you may not know what is in them.
D: Distilled: Mourning reveals the full measure of a person's emotional capacity and filial depth.
Notes: Ren wei you zi zhi zhe ye, bi ye qin sang hu (人未有自致者也，必也親喪乎). Grief for parents as the ultimate revealer of character. Xiao (孝, filial piety) is the deepest emotional bond, and mourning is when it becomes fully visible. Connects to FUALL's framework: some qualities only emerge under genuine emotional pressure.

D: Original: The philosopher Tsang said, 'I have heard this from our Master:-- "The filial piety of Mang Chwang, in other matters, was what other men are competent to, but, as seen in his not changing the ministers of his father, nor his father's mode of government, it is difficult to be attained to."'
D: Refashioned: Zengzi said, 'I heard from our Master: "The filial piety of Meng Zhuang was in most things what any person could manage. But in not changing his father's ministers or his father's policies — that is hard to match."'
D: Elegant: Meng Zhuang's filial piety was ordinary in most things. What was extraordinary: he changed neither his father's ministers nor his father's methods.
D: Distilled: The hardest form of filial piety is not emotional devotion but institutional continuity — preserving your father's system after his death.
Notes: Meng Zhuang (孟莊子) — a minister of Lu. Not changing his father's ministers (bu gai fu zhi chen, 不改父之臣) or policies (bu gai fu zhi zheng, 不改父之政) for three years was the ideal of filial governance. This connects to 1.11 and 4.20. The difficulty is restraining the new leader's impulse to reshape everything. Connects to FUALL's framework: institutional continuity is a virtue — the urge to remake everything is often vanity disguised as reform.

D: Original: The chief of the Mang family having appointed Yang Fu to be chief criminal judge, the latter consulted the philosopher Tsang. Tsang said, 'The rulers have failed in their duties, and the people consequently have been disorganised, for a long time. When you have found out the truth of any accusation, be grieved for and pity them, and do not feel joy at your own ability.'
D: Refashioned: When Yang Fu was appointed chief judge by the Meng family, he consulted Zengzi. Zengzi said, 'The rulers have failed in their duties, and the people have been disorganized for a long time. When you discover the truth in a criminal case, feel grief and compassion, not satisfaction at your own cleverness.'
D: Elegant: When you uncover the truth, grieve for the criminal. He is a product of rulers who failed him. Do not congratulate yourself.
D: Distilled: A just judge feels sorrow, not pride, when the guilty are convicted — because their guilt reflects the system's failure.
Notes: Yang Fu (陽膚) — a student of Zengzi appointed as judge. Zengzi's advice is remarkable: criminal behavior reflects systemic failure (rulers abandoning duty), so the judge should feel compassion, not triumph. Ai jin er wu xi (哀矜而勿喜, grieve for them and do not rejoice). Connects to FUALL's framework: institutional failure produces individual pathology; punishment without systemic reform is incomplete.

D: Original: Tsze-kung said, 'Chau's wickedness was not so great as that name implies. Therefore, the superior man hates to dwell in a low-lying situation, where all the evil of the world will flow in upon him.'
D: Refashioned: Zigong said, 'Zhou's wickedness was not as extreme as his reputation suggests. This is why the superior person avoids being in a morally low position — because all the world's evil flows down to that spot.'
D: Elegant: King Zhou was bad, but not as bad as legend claims. His real mistake was occupying the low ground — where all the world's blame collects.
D: Distilled: Once your reputation sinks below a threshold, you become the magnet for every accusation, deserved or not.
Notes: Zhou (紂) — the last king of the Shang dynasty, the archetypal tyrant. Zigong's point is sophisticated: once someone becomes the designated villain, all subsequent evil is attributed to them regardless of actual culpability. Xia liu (下流, low-lying position) — a metaphor for moral reputation as geography: blame flows downhill. Connects to FUALL's Reason: reputations distort historical judgment; critical examination of received narratives is essential.

D: Original: Tsze-kung said, 'The faults of the superior man are like the eclipses of the sun and moon. He has his faults, and all men see them; he changes again, and all men look up to him.'
D: Refashioned: Zigong said, 'The faults of the superior person are like eclipses of the sun or moon. When he errs, everyone sees it. When he corrects himself, everyone looks up to him again.'
D: Elegant: The superior person's faults are solar eclipses — visible to all. His corrections are sunrise — and all look up again.
D: Distilled: A great person's mistakes are public events, but so are his corrections — and the corrections earn more respect than perfection would.
Notes: Ri yue zhi shi (日月之食, eclipse of sun and moon). The metaphor works on multiple levels: the eclipse is dramatic and visible, but temporary and followed by restoration. Public error openly corrected builds more trust than hidden perfection. Connects to FUALL's framework: transparency in failure, combined with genuine correction, is the strongest foundation for moral authority.

D: Original:
Kung-sun Ch'ao of Wei asked Tsze-kung, saying, 'From whom did Chung-ni get his learning?'
Tsze-kung replied, 'The doctrines of Wan and Wu have not yet fallen to the ground. They are to be found among men. Men of talents and virtue remember the greater principles of them, and others, not possessing such talents and virtue, remember the smaller. Thus, all possess the doctrines of Wan and Wu. Where could our Master go that he should not have an opportunity of learning them? And yet what necessity was there for his having a regular master?'
D: Refashioned: Gongsun Chao of Wei asked Zigong, 'From whom did Confucius learn?' Zigong replied, 'The teachings of Kings Wen and Wu have not vanished from the earth. They survive among the people. The talented remember the greater principles; the less talented remember the smaller ones. There is nowhere the Master could go without finding something to learn from. And what need had he for a single fixed teacher?'
D: Elegant: The teachings of the ancient kings survive scattered among the people. Confucius learned from all of them. Why would he need a single teacher?
D: Distilled: A great mind does not need one master — the whole world is his classroom.
Notes: Wen Wu zhi dao (文武之道, the Way of Kings Wen and Wu) — the cultural inheritance of the Zhou dynasty. Zigong's answer is brilliant: Confucius had no single teacher because wisdom is distributed throughout humanity. He learned from everyone. Wu chang shi (無常師, no fixed teacher) — this principle became central to Chinese intellectual culture. Connects to FUALL's framework: wisdom is not owned by any single institution but collected from every source.

D: Original:
Shu-sun Wu-shu observed to the great officers in the court, saying, 'Tsze-kung is superior to Chung-ni.'
Tsze-fu Ching-po reported the observation to Tsze-kung, who said, 'Let me use the comparison of a house and its encompassing wall. My wall only reaches to the shoulders. One may peep over it, and see whatever is valuable in the apartments.
'The wall of my Master is several fathoms high. If one do not find the door and enter by it, he cannot see the ancestral temple with its beauties, nor all the officers in their rich array.
'But I may assume that they are few who find the door. Was not the observation of the chief only what might have been expected?'
D: Refashioned: Shusun Wushu said to the court officials, 'Zigong is superior to Confucius.' Zifu Jingbo reported this to Zigong. Zigong said, 'Let me use the analogy of a house and its surrounding wall. My wall reaches only to the shoulder — you can peek over it and see whatever is inside. My Master's wall is many fathoms high. Unless you find the gate and enter, you cannot see the beauty of the ancestral temple or the splendor of the court within. But perhaps few people find the gate. No wonder that official said what he did.'
D: Elegant: My wall is shoulder-high — anyone can peek over it. The Master's wall is many fathoms tall. Unless you find the gate, you cannot see what is inside. Few find the gate.
D: Distilled: Shallow people are easily appreciated because they are easily seen; the truly great are invisible to those who cannot find the way in.
Notes: Shusun Wushu (叔孫武叔) — a minister of Lu who repeatedly disparaged Confucius. Zigong's wall metaphor (gong qiang, 宮牆) became famous: the shallow person is transparent and therefore seems impressive; the deep person requires effort to understand. 'Few find the gate' (de qi men er ru, 得其門而入) — genuine understanding of great minds requires dedicated effort. Connects to FUALL's framework: wisdom is not self-evident; it requires the education to perceive it.

D: Original: Shu-sun Wu-shu having spoken revilingly of Chung-ni, Tsze-kung said, 'It is of no use doing so. Chung-ni cannot be reviled. The talents and virtue of other men are hillocks and mounds which may be stepped over. Chung-ni is the sun or moon, which it is not possible to step over. Although a man may wish to cut himself off from the sage, what harm can he do to the sun or moon? He only shows that he does not know his own capacity.
D: Refashioned: When Shusun Wushu spoke abusively of Confucius, Zigong said, 'It is useless. Confucius cannot be diminished. Other men's talents are hills you can step over. Confucius is the sun and moon — you cannot step over them. A man may wish to cut himself off from the sun, but what harm does he do to it? He only shows that he does not know his own scale.'
D: Elegant: Others are hills you can step over. Confucius is the sun. You may refuse to look at it, but that says nothing about the sun.
D: Distilled: Refusing to look at the sun does not diminish the sun — it only reveals your own smallness.
Notes: Ri yue (日月, sun and moon) — Zigong's metaphor for Confucius's stature. The point is epistemological: criticism of the truly great reveals only the critic's limitations. Bu zhi liang (不知量, not knowing one's own measure). Connects to FUALL's framework: the inability to appreciate greatness is a failure of the perceiver, not of the perceived.

D: Original:
Ch'an Tsze-ch'in, addressing Tsze-kung, said, 'You are too modest. How can Chung-ni be said to be superior to you?'
Tsze-kung said to him, 'For one word a man is often deemed to be wise, and for one word he is often deemed to be foolish. We ought to be careful indeed in what we say.
'Our Master cannot be attained to, just in the same way as the heavens cannot be gone up to by the steps of a stair.
'Were our Master in the position of the ruler of a State or the chief of a Family, we should find verified the description which has been given of a sage's rule:-- he would plant the people, and forthwith they would be established; he would lead them on, and forthwith they would follow him; he would make them happy, and forthwith multitudes would resort to his dominions; he would stimulate them, and forthwith they would be harmonious. While he lived, he would be glorious. When he died, he would be bitterly lamented. How is it possible for him to be attained to?'
D: Refashioned: Chen Ziqin said to Zigong, 'You are being too modest. How can Confucius really be your superior?' Zigong replied, 'A person can be judged wise or foolish by a single word — so we must be careful what we say. Our Master is as unreachable as the heavens. Were he to rule a state or a great family, the description of the sage-king would come true: he would plant the people and they would stand; lead them and they would follow; bring them peace and they would flock to him; inspire them and they would unite. In life he was glorious; in death, deeply mourned. How can anyone be compared to him?'
D: Elegant: Zigong said: 'The Master is as unreachable as Heaven itself. If he ruled, the people would stand, follow, flock, and unite. Glorious in life. Mourned in death. How can he be matched?'
D: Distilled: The greatest teacher's potential was never fully realized because no ruler was wise enough to employ him — but his greatness remains undeniable.
Notes: Chen Ziqin (陳子禽, also Chen Kang) — the same disciple who asked Boyu about special instruction in 16.13. Zigong's final defense of Confucius closes Book 19 with the most exalted description of the Master in the Analects. The hypothetical ('were he to rule...') is poignant: Confucius's political philosophy was never implemented because no ruler had the wisdom to empower him. Connects to FUALL's framework: the gap between what wisdom could achieve and what the world permits it to achieve is one of the central tragedies of civilization.

D: Original: Book 20: Yao Yueh (Yao Said)

Yao said, 'Oh! you, Shun, the Heaven-determined order of succession now rests in your person. Sincerely hold fast the due Mean. If there shall be distress and want within the four seas, the Heavenly revenue will come to a perpetual end.'
Shun also used the same language in giving charge to Yu.
T'ang said, 'I the child Li, presume to use a dark-coloured victim, and presume to announce to Thee, O most great and sovereign God, that the sinner I dare not pardon, and thy ministers, O God, I do not keep in obscurity. The examination of them is by thy mind, O God. If, in my person, I commit offences, they are not to be attributed to you, the people of the myriad regions. If you in the myriad regions commit offences, these offences must rest on my person.'
Chau conferred great gifts, and the good were enriched.
'Although he has his near relatives, they are not equal to my virtuous men. The people are throwing blame upon me, the One man.'
He carefully attended to the weights and measures, examined the body of the laws, restored the discarded officers, and the good government of the kingdom took its course.
He revived States that had been extinguished, restored families whose line of succession had been broken, and called to office those who had retired into obscurity, so that throughout the kingdom the hearts of the people turned towards him.
What he attached chief importance to, were the food of the people, the duties of mourning, and sacrifices.
By his generosity, he won all. By his sincerity, he made the people repose trust in him. By his earnest activity, his achievements were great. By his justice, all were delighted.
D: Refashioned:
Yao said, 'Shun! The mandate of Heaven now rests with you. Hold faithfully to the center. If the people within the four seas fall into hardship, Heaven's favor will end forever.' Shun passed the same charge to Yu.

Tang said, 'I, your humble servant Li, dare to sacrifice this dark ox and declare before the supreme God: I will not pardon the guilty, nor will I conceal your servants, O God — let your own judgment decide. If I myself offend, do not blame the people. If the people offend, the blame rests on me.'

The Zhou dynasty gave great gifts, and the good were enriched. 'Though the king has close relatives, they are not equal to persons of virtue.' If the people blame anyone, let them blame me alone.

He carefully standardized weights and measures, reviewed the laws, restored officials who had been wrongly dismissed, and good governance spread throughout the realm. He revived fallen states, restored broken family lines, and called back those who had withdrawn from public life — and the hearts of the people everywhere turned to him.

What he valued most: the people's food, mourning rites, and sacrifices. By generosity, he won the people. By sincerity, he earned their trust. By diligence, he achieved great things. By justice, all were content.
D: Elegant: Three dynasties, one charge: hold the center, serve the people, take responsibility for all failure. By generosity, win them. By sincerity, earn trust. By diligence, achieve. By justice, bring contentment.
D: Distilled: The mandate of Heaven across all dynasties is the same: hold to the center, take blame personally, and govern through generosity, sincerity, diligence, and justice.
Notes: This is the most historically ambitious passage in the Analects, linking the founding charges of three dynasties: Yao to Shun (mythical), Tang of the Shang, and the Duke of Zhou. Yun zhi zhong (允執其中, sincerely hold fast the mean) — the doctrine of the Mean as the supreme principle of governance. Tang's prayer accepting personal responsibility for his people's sins parallels Moses and other founder figures. The four virtues of governance (Kuan 寬, Xin 信, Min 敏, Gong 公) summarize the Confucian ideal of leadership. Connects to FUALL's framework: the mission of civilization is transmitted across generations through a consistent set of principles — the erosion pattern occurs when this transmission breaks.

D: Original:
Tsze-chang asked Confucius, saying, 'In what way should a person in authority act in order that he may conduct government properly?' The Master replied, 'Let him honour the five excellent, and banish away the four bad, things;-- then may he conduct government properly.' Tsze-chang said, 'What are meant by the five excellent things?' The Master said, 'When the person in authority is beneficent without great expenditure; when he lays tasks on the people without their repining; when he pursues what he desires without being covetous; when he maintains a dignified ease without being proud; when he is majestic without being fierce.'
Tsze-chang said, 'What is meant by being beneficent without great expenditure?' The Master replied, 'When the person in authority makes more beneficial to the people the things from which they naturally derive benefit;-- is not this being beneficent without great expenditure? When he chooses the labours which are proper, and makes them labour on them, who will repine? When his desires are set on benevolent government, and he secures it, who will accuse him of covetousness? Whether he has to do with many people or few, or with things great or small, he does not dare to indicate any disrespect;-- is not this to maintain a dignified ease without any pride? He adjusts his clothes and cap, and throws a dignity into his looks, so that, thus dignified, he is looked at with awe;-- is not this to be majestic without being fierce?'
Tsze-chang then asked, 'What are meant by the four bad things?' The Master said, 'To put the people to death without having instructed them;-- this is called cruelty. To require from them, suddenly, the full tale of work, without having given them warning;-- this is called oppression. To issue orders as if without urgency, at first, and, when the time comes, to insist on them with severity;-- this is called injury. And, generally, in the giving pay or rewards to men, to do it in a stingy way;-- this is called acting the part of a mere official.'
D: Refashioned:
Zizhang asked Confucius how a person in authority should govern. The Master said, 'Honor the five excellences and banish the four evils.'

'What are the five excellences?' 'Be generous without being wasteful — benefit the people through what naturally benefits them. Impose labor without causing resentment — choose appropriate work at appropriate times. Desire good governance without being greedy — seek benevolence and you will have it. Be dignified without being arrogant — be proper regardless of scale. Be impressive without being intimidating — dress well and carry yourself with gravity.'

'What are the four evils?' 'Executing people without having educated them — that is cruelty. Demanding results without warning — that is oppression. Setting lax deadlines then enforcing them strictly — that is injury. Being stingy in paying what is owed — that is the behavior of a petty official.'
D: Elegant: Five excellences: generous, fair, principled, dignified, commanding. Four evils: punishing the untaught, demanding the unwarned, enforcing the unannounced, paying grudgingly. The first is cruelty; the last is bureaucratic pettiness.
D: Distilled: Good governance is generous, fair, principled, dignified, and commanding; bad governance punishes without teaching, demands without warning, and pays without grace.
Notes: Wu mei (五美, five excellences) and Si e (四惡, four evils). The five excellences are practical virtues of governance: each pairs a positive quality with its natural excess. The four evils are systematic abuses: each involves a failure of process (no education before punishment, no warning before demand, no consistency in deadlines, no generosity in compensation). This is one of the most practical governance passages in the Analects. Connects to FUALL's Guilds: institutional competence requires fair processes, adequate preparation, and consistent standards.

D: Original:
The Master said, 'Without recognising the ordinances of Heaven, it is impossible to be a superior man.
'Without an acquaintance with the rules of Propriety, it is impossible for the character to be established.
'Without knowing the force of words, it is impossible to know men.'
D: Refashioned: The Master said, 'Without understanding Heaven's will, one cannot be a superior person. Without knowing the rules of propriety, one cannot establish character. Without understanding the power of words, one cannot know people.'
D: Elegant: Three foundations of the superior person: know Heaven's will. Know propriety. Know words. Without them: no character, no judgment, no standing.
D: Distilled: The three requirements for being a complete person: understand fate, practice propriety, and discern the truth in what people say.
Notes: Bu zhi ming, wu yi wei junzi (不知命，無以為君子也). Bu zhi li, wu yi li (不知禮，無以立也). Bu zhi yan, wu yi zhi ren (不知言，無以知人也). The final passage of the Analects establishes three foundations: Ming (命, fate/Heaven's mandate) — understanding the moral order one operates within. Li (禮, propriety) — the practical framework for character. Yan (言, words) — the ability to judge people through their speech. This triad connects to FUALL's Wisdom: Reality (understanding Heaven/fate/what is), Right (propriety/how to act), and Reason (discerning truth in language). The Analects closes with the same integration the FUALL framework seeks: wisdom as the unity of understanding, conduct, and judgment.
